item.title: Morning Bid: No quiet on the eastern front
item.description:
March 6 - Everything Mike Dolan and the ROI team are excited to read, watch and listen to over the weekend.
From the Editor
Hello Morning Bid readers!
The war in Iran could prove to be one of the most momentous geopolitical events in decades, with long-term ramifications for global alliances and energy markets . It also represents the biggest risk to global supply chains since the Covid-19 pandemic.
The joint U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran that began last Saturday , killing Iranian Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, were met with fierce retaliation by Tehran, which launched ŌĆŗstrikes across the region.
Yet as events unfolded this week, investors often appeared like the proverbial deer caught in the headlights. With some notable exceptions, mostly in Asia, prices in many asset classes moved far less than one would have expected ŌĆō and ŌĆīsometimes in unexpected directions .
Today's spike in energy prices may change that, however.
Major oil benchmarks are on pace for the largest weekly jumps since RussiaŌĆÖs invasion of Ukraine in 2022. Brent crude has topped $87 per barrel with WTI eclipsing $84, following big jumps on Friday morning. These represent increases of over 20% and 25%, respectively, on the week, and spikes of over 40% in the year to date.
The scale of the current disruption is enormous: the vital Strait of Hormuz has essentially been closed , hundreds of oil tankers are stranded , Qatar has shut its liquefied natural gas production, Saudi Arabia has suspended production at a key refinery and Iraq has shut its crude production.
One might have expected prices to already be closer to ŌĆŗthree digits. But this morning's big moves suggest the market's optimism about a quick resolution to the crisis is now being tested .
Certain regions are struggling more than others. Asian refineries that depend on the Middle East for nearly 60% of their crude supplies are struggling to ŌĆŗfind alternatives. Some plants have already reduced operations. Asian jet fuel prices skyrocketed over 70% on Wednesday , hitting a record high. European gas prices have also shot up.
(For a look at how the conflict is impacting various ŌüĀenergy markets, check out ROI columnist Gavin MaguireŌĆÖs chart-based overview .)
President Donald TrumpŌĆÖs response to the energy situation has been mixed. In reference to increasing gasoline prices, he said on Thursday , ŌĆ£They'll drop very rapidly when this is over, and if they rise, they rise."
Yet the U.S. is also now temporary loosening restrictions ŌĆŗon Indian purchases of Russian oil to ease the squeeze on IndiaŌĆÖs refiners, and the U.S. Treasury also considered taking action in the oil futures markets to tamp down prices, though it has reportedly ruled that out for now .
Additionally, the president said earlier in the week that the U.S. would offer insurance coverage and ŌĆŗnavy escorts to help open the blocked Strait of Hormuz ŌĆō but this plan is likely to be too little, too late .
The impact on other asset classes has been notably varied.
Asian stocks markets ŌĆō home to many countries that import the majority of their energy from the Middle East ŌĆō have been rocked. In particular, South Korea's Kospi ŌĆō one of the best-performing equity indices in the first two months of the year ŌĆō was headed for its largest weekly fall in six years , around 10.5%.
European equities have also weakened since the conflict began , though far less significantly, and U.S. equities performed much better than their global peers, as the worldŌĆÖs biggest energy producer is well-insulated from shortage concerns and benefitted from ŌĆŗa bounce in technology stocks .
The dollar, one of the bigger winners during the conflict, is on track to rise roughly 1.5% this week ŌĆō and this may not be down entirely to safe haven demand .
Meanwhile, Treasury yields rose as fears about the potentially meaningful inflationary impact of an energy crisis offset investorsŌĆÖ flight to ŌĆŗsafety.
While gold prices were up on Friday, the supposed safe haven is actually down on the week. This could represent investors selling to cover losses in other areas but could also highlight the speculation that has helped fuel bullionŌĆÖs massive runup in the past year.
Later today, markets may briefly turn their focus away from fear ŌĆīof war only to ŌüĀrun back into the fear of a potential AI jobs apocalypse , with the release of U.S. February payrolls.
But all eyes will eventually return to the Middle East. No matter how ŌĆō or when ŌĆō this conflict ends, it has the potential to shift how the world thinks about defense, energy security, and resource nationalism. At a time when the AI revolution is demanding ever-greater amounts of energy, metals and minerals, thatŌĆÖs a huge deal.
For more stories on how the Middle East conflict may impact financial markets and commodities, check out Reuters Open Interest . You can learn:
As we head into the weekend, check out the ROI teamŌĆÖs ŌĆŗrecommendations for what you should read, listen to, and watch to stay ŌĆŗinformed and ready for the week ahead.
IŌĆÖd love to hear from ŌüĀyou, so please reach out to me at anna.szymanski@thomsonreuters.com , opens new tab .
This weekend, we're reading...
JAMIE MCGEEVER, ROI Markets Columnist: Evan Feigenbaum, at the Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, dissects China's response ŌĆō or, more accurately, non-response ŌĆō to U.S. attacks on Venezuela and Iran, two of ChinaŌĆÖs supposed allies. Confirmation, as if any were needed, that Beijing DoesnŌĆÖt Think Like Washington , opens new tab .
RON BOUSSO, ROI Energy Columnist: This excellent U.S. congressional report , opens new tab , drafted after last year's 12-day IsraelŌĆōIran war, clearly lays out the history and ŌĆŗstrategic importance of the Strait of Hormuz.
ANDY HOME, ROI Metals Columnist: Amanda van Dyke, founder of the Critical Minerals Hub, offers insightful analysis , opens new tab of how the Iran war is burning through not only Western missile stockpiles but ŌĆŗalso the critical metals that make them ŌüĀfunction.
MIKE DOLAN, ROI Finance & Markets Columnist: This VoxEU column , opens new tab from the Centre for Economic Policy Research uses the 2021ŌĆō2022 boom and bust of non-fungible tokens (NFTs) as an "unprecedented real-time laboratory for bubble economics." The authors show how hard it is to measure or realize returns in illiquid markets for unique assets and how transaction prices can mislead, with clear lessons for regulators of digital and private assets.
GAVIN MAGUIRE, ROI Global Energy Transition Columnist: This excellent Reuters explainer summarises what's in China's latest five-year plan (2026ŌĆō2030). Easy to miss amid the chaos surrounding the Iran war, the plan is likely to have a material impact on global markets and key technology trends ŌüĀfor the rest of ŌĆŗthe 2020s.
We're listening to...
CLYDE RUSSELL, ROI Asia Commodities and Energy Columnist: Gulf Intelligence's Daily Energy Markets podcast , opens new tab provides essential context on the current Gulf oil situation and China's decision to order ŌĆŗits major refiners to suspend fuel imports.
And we're watching...
ANNA SZYMANSKI, ROI Editor-in-Charge: The Morning Bid podcast ŌĆō where you can catch Mike Dolan five days a week ŌĆō is now available on YouTube , opens new tab . Check out the Sunday edition each week where I join Mike to discuss whatŌĆÖs in store for the week ahead in markets.
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every ŌĆŗweekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the authors. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Israel backing Iranian Kurdish plans to seize Iran border areas, sources say
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran to seize towns near the frontier, ŌĆŗaccording to three sources familiar with Israel's talks with the factions.
The notion of an offensive by Iranian Kurdish forces based in Iraq gained attention on Friday when U.S. ŌĆīPresident Donald Trump told Reuters it would be "wonderful" if they crossed the border.
A Kurdish insurgency could have serious consequences for Iran as it defends itself against the air campaign. The militias have consulted with the U.S. about how and whether to attack Iran's security forces, Reuters has reported.
Israel has been holding its own talks with Iranian Kurdish insurgent groups based in the semi-autonomous region of Iraqi Kurdistan for around a year, two Iranian Kurdish sources ŌĆŗsaid, while an Israeli source said talks had been "long-term".
The two Iranian Kurdish sources have direct knowledge of the armed dissident groups and the source from Israel has direct knowledge of its engagement ŌĆŗwith them. All spoke on condition of anonymity.
Israel's government and the Israeli military did not immediately respond to requests for comment, and Israel has not commented ŌüĀpublicly on such engagement during the current war.
An initial goal of the Kurdish factions would be seizing Iranian territory along the border, the three sources said. One of the Kurdish sources said their aim ŌĆŗwas to seize the towns of Oshnavieh and Piranshahr, among others.
These sources said thousands of fighters were gathering on the Iraqi side of the border and preparing to launch an offensive within a week, something Reuters ŌĆŗwas not able to confirm.
Independent estimates put the militias' combined strength at 5,000-8,000.
They possess only light arms, according to the Kurdish sources. But while they might not have the firepower to mount a significant bid for self-rule, with U.S. and Israeli help they could cause trouble on the border.
The Israeli source said Israel did not expect them to be able to overthrow their government, but that backing them could erode Iran's control over its hinterlands and distract its Islamic Revolutionary Guard ŌĆŗCorps.
Five long-standing Iranian dissident groups announced an alliance just at the end of last month.
It includes the Kurdistan Free Life Party (PJAK), the Democratic Party of Iranian Kurdistan (PDKI) and the Kurdistan Freedom Party (PAK), which have all participated in ŌĆŗinsurgencies and maintain fighters in Iraq.
It is not clear that they will get any support from their ethnic brethren in Iraq, however; Iraqi Kurdistan's political leadership has publicly denied any plan to send fighters or get involved in Iran, ŌĆīdespite reports of ŌüĀoutside pressure to do so.
The Israeli source cautioned that there was pushback from the Iraqi Kurds, and without their practical support it would be hard for the Iranian Kurds to mobilise. Trump's lack of clarity on how long the war could last had also led to hesitancy.
Iran has been attacking Kurdish armed groups inside Iraq, along with U.S. bases in the area, and on Friday warned Iraqi Kurdistan that it would retaliate against any deployment of hostile forces on the frontier.
The three sources said Kurds within Iran had been providing targeting intelligence on the border areas to the U.S. and Israel.
Israeli ŌĆŗanalyst Jonathan Spyer said Israel was seeking to "destroy the ŌĆŗregime by any means available".
But Danny Citrinowicz, ŌüĀan Iran expert and former Israeli intelligence officer, said an insurgency in Iran did not have broad support among Iraqi and Iranian Kurds:
"I think they're all waiting to see if the regime will hold on or not."
Turkish and Iraqi officials, neither keen to support separatism among an ethnic group spread across parts of Iraq, ŌĆŗTurkey, Syria and Iran, have also expressed reservations about any insurgency in Iran.
Citrinowicz said supporting an uprising might backfire on the U.S. and Israel by ŌĆŗfanning nationalism.
Israel has maintained discreet ŌüĀmilitary, intelligence and business ties with various Kurdish groups since the 1960s, viewing them as a buffer against shared adversaries.
The two Kurdish sources said the factions were in closer coordination with the U.S. than with Israel, but that any cross-border offensive would require air support from both.
One of those sources said they had not yet received weapons, but would request air defence systems, drones, small arms, and artillery support.
Kurdish groups have a long history of working ŌüĀwith the U.S., ŌĆŗbut recent incidents have strained ties.
One of the Iranian Kurdish sources said Kurdish leaders had concerns about being "betrayed" like the ŌĆŗKurdish groups in northern Syria, who had been forced to cede territory after long serving as the primary U.S. partner in the area.
The source said Iranian Kurdish leaders had requested guarantees from the U.S., without saying what they were.
Both Iranian Kurdish sources said ŌĆŗthe factions' goal would be to establish a semi-autonomous region in a federal Iran, similar to the model in Iraq.

item.title: Exclusive: US pressing Sri Lanka not to repatriate Iranian crew and survivors from sunken ship, memo says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is pressing Sri Lanka's government not to repatriate the survivors ŌĆŗfrom the Iranian warship it sank this week, as well as the crew of a second Iranian ship ŌĆīthat is in Sri Lankan custody, according to an internal State Department cable seen by Reuters on Friday.
A U.S. submarine sank the IRIS Dena warship in the Indian Ocean about 19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city of Galle on Wednesday, killing dozens of sailors and dramatically widening ŌĆŗWashington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.
On Thursday, Sri Lanka began offloading 208 crew members from a second Iranian ship, the ŌĆŗnaval auxiliary vessel IRIS Booshehr, which had found itself stranded in Sri Lanka's exclusive economic zone ŌüĀbut outside its maritime boundary.
President Anura Kumara Dissanayake said his island nation had a "humanitarian responsibility" to take in the crew.
The torpedoing ŌĆŗof the Dena - which U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth described as "quiet death" - was the first such action by the United States since ŌĆŗWorld War Two and a clear sign of the Iran conflict's widening geographic scope.
The internal State Department cable, which was dated March 6 and has not been previously reported, said Jayne Howell, the charge d'affaires at the U.S. embassy in Colombo, had emphasized to Sri Lanka's government that neither ŌĆŗthe Booshehr crew nor the 32 Dena survivors should be repatriated to Iran.
It said "Sri Lankan authorities should minimize Iranian attempts to ŌĆŗuse the detainees for propaganda."
The State Department did not immediately respond to a request for comment. Representatives for Dissanayake's office and Sri Lanka's foreign ministry ŌĆīwere not ŌüĀimmediately available for comment.
The cable said Howell also told the Israeli ambassador to India and Sri Lanka there was no plan to repatriate the crew to Iran. The envoy asked Howell whether there was any engagement with the crew to encourage "defection", the cable said.
A representative for the Israeli embassy in New Delhi did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
On Wednesday, Sri Lanka's deputy ŌĆŗminister for health and mass ŌĆŗmedia, Hansaka Wijemuni, told Reuters ŌüĀthat Tehran had asked Colombo for help repatriating the bodies of those killed aboard the Dena, but a timeframe to do so has not yet been determined.
The Dena had taken part in naval ŌĆŗexercises organised by India in the Bay of Bengal last month and was returning to ŌĆŗIran when it ŌüĀwas struck by a U.S. torpedo.
A U.S. official, speaking on condition of anonymity, told Reuters the Dena was armed when it was hit and the United States did not provide a warning before carrying out the strike.
The State Department cable said the second vessel, the Booshehr, ŌüĀwill ŌĆŗremain in Sri Lankan custody for the duration of the conflict.
Sri Lankan authorities ŌĆŗsaid on Friday that they were escorting the Booshehr to a harbor on the eastern coast and moving most of its crew to a navy camp near Colombo.

item.title: Exclusive: Turkey asks Britain's MI6 to step up protection of Syria's Sharaa, sources say; Ankara denies report
item.description:
ISTANBUL/DAMASCUS/LONDON, March 6 (Reuters) - TurkeyŌĆÖs intelligence agency asked ŌĆŗits British counterpart MI6 last month to take a larger role in protecting Syrian President Ahmed al-Sharaa after recent assassination plots, according to five people ŌĆīfamiliar with the matter.
After this story was published, Turkey denied that its intelligence agency MIT had made any such request to MI6.
The request highlights efforts by foreign allies to shore up a country still shaken by sporadic violence 15 months after the overthrow of President Bashar al-Assad, with the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran now rattling the wider region.
Those allies see Sharaa as crucial to preventing a relapse into sectarian fighting or civil war, after ŌĆŗ14 years of civil conflict drove millions of refugees abroad and allowed Islamic State to control swathes of Syria.
The militants last month stepped up attacks on military and ŌĆŗsecurity personnel across Syria and declared Sharaa, a former rebel, their "number one foe".
It was unclear what specifically TurkeyŌĆÖs National Intelligence Organization, or MIT, had ŌüĀasked of MI6, or what new role, if any, MI6 had taken up.
The Turkish presidency said in a statement issued by its communications directorate that MIT maintains effective cooperation in the ŌĆŗfight against terrorism with the international intelligence community and security units in Syria, but that this story did not reflect the truth.
"Contrary to what is claimed in the report in question, ŌĆŗit is not true that MIT has made any request to MI6 regarding the protection of the Syrian President or sought to assume such a role," it said.
Turkey, Britain and the U.S. last year threw their backing behind Sharaa to try to reunite and rebuild his country of 26 million. London and Washington have scrapped most sanctions on Syria and on Hayat Tahrir al-Sham (HTS), the Islamist group he ŌĆŗonce led.
The sources who spoke to Reuters requested anonymity owing to the sensitivity of the matter.
MIT, the Turkish foreign ministry, Britain's foreign office and Syria's defence and interior ministries did ŌĆŗnot comment when contacted ahead of publication.
The sources, including Syrian and foreign officials, all cited rising anxiety over a series of reported Islamic State plots to kill Sharaa.
A Turkish source said that MIT, which has played ŌĆīa key ŌüĀrole in helping the new government to establish itself, appealed to MI6 for more support after one such incident last month. A senior Syrian security source said the request came after a ŌĆ£high-risk assassination plotŌĆØ, adding that MIT, MI6 and Syrian authorities were constantly sharing intelligence.
Details of the plot were unclear.
A separate Western intelligence source briefed on the matter believed Turkey wanted to introduce a Western presence in Damascus to provide something of a buffer between the agencies of Turkey and Israel, currently at loggerheads.
Last year, Sharaa and two senior cabinet ministers ŌĆŗwere targeted by Islamic State in five ŌĆŗfoiled assassination attempts, according to the U.N. ŌüĀOffice of Counter-Terrorism. In November, Reuters reported that Syrian authorities had foiled two of the attempts .
Describing Sharaa as a "watchdog" of the global anti-Islamic State coalition, the group mounted six attacks on Syrian authorities last month in what it called a ŌĆ£new phase".
On Thursday, Damascus openly acknowledged for the first ŌĆŗtime that it coordinates with MIT, saying they had cooperated to foil an Islamic State attack in the capital.
Turkish security sources said ŌĆŗMIT had identified a team ŌüĀof three preparing remote bomb attacks, enabling Syrian counterparts to prevent an "imminent assault".
A U.S. diplomat briefed on the matter said MIT's request to MI6 had been prompted by the Islamic State resurgence.
The Western intelligence source said the two agencies could intensify joint planning and technical operations, but that no decision had been made on whether to send British personnel to Damascus.
A Syrian security source said a ŌüĀphysical British ŌĆŗpresence would be "highly risky". They said MI6 had been discussed at a meeting in Damascus on February 26 between ŌĆŗa delegation headed by Britain's special envoy for Syria, Ann Snow, and Syria's deputy interior minister, Major General Abdulqader Tahan.
Sharaa was a commander of Al Qaeda's Nusra Front in Syria before cutting ties with the group in 2016, then led ŌĆŗa coalition of Islamist rebel factions in late 2024 to topple Assad.

item.title: Hungary's $82 million bank transit swoop deepens spat with Ukraine
item.description:
BUDAPEST/KYIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Hungary ŌĆŗsaid on Friday it had detained seven Ukrainians carrying around $82 million in cash and gold on suspicion of money laundering, ŌĆīas Kyiv accused Budapest of taking bank employees hostage amid a dispute over oil shipments.
The Hungarian Tax Authority's decision to have counter-terrorism forces swoop on two Ukrainian armoured vehicles transporting cash to Ukraine marks a dramatic escalation of tensions that have already resulted in Budapest blocking tens of billions of euros in European Union aid for Kyiv.
Ukrainian Foreign Minister ŌĆŗAndrii Sybiha said on X that the people detained were employees of Oschadbank and accused Hungary of "taking hostages and stealing money".
"This is ŌĆŗstate terrorism and racketeering," he added, demanding their immediate release and asking the EU to "provide a clear qualification of HungaryŌĆÖs ŌüĀunlawful actions".
Video posted on the Hungarian government's Facebook account showed armed counter-terrorism officers jumping out of a van as the Ukrainian vehicles pulled into ŌĆŗa service station, pointing their guns at the windscreen before handcuffing the people inside and forcing them to lie face down on the ground.
"The National Tax ŌĆŗand Customs Administration (NAV) is pursuing criminal proceedings on suspicion of money laundering," the authority said in a statement.
"On March 5, 2026, it detained seven Ukrainian citizens, including a former Ukrainian intelligence service general, and two armoured cash-in-transit vehicles, which were transporting a total of $40 million, 35 million euros and nine kilograms of gold from Austria to Ukraine."
The tax ŌĆŗauthority said it was working together with counter-terrorism forces.
Government spokesperson Zoltan Kovacs said the seven detainees would be expelled from Hungary. It was not ŌĆŗimmediately clear what would happen to the money they were transporting.
Ukraine's ambassador to Hungary, Sandor Fegyir, went to the Budapest headquarters of the Counter-Terrorism Centre to try and ŌĆīmeet with ŌüĀthe detained Ukrainians, two Reuters witnesses said.
The detainees' lawyer, Lorant Horvath, said he had been unable to contact them. Kyiv's ambassador to Budapest, Sandor Fegyir, was also unable to meet the detainees.
"This is an outrageous procedure... (T)he embassy, and indeed the consular service, must in all cases be allowed access to the accused," Horvath told Reuters. "Defence lawyers must also be allowed (access)."
Ukraine advised its citizens against travel to Hungary, saying it could not guarantee their safety amid what it called ŌĆŗthe "arbitrary actions" of Hungarian authorities.
Hungary and ŌĆŗSlovakia accuse Ukraine of deliberately delaying ŌüĀthe resumption of oil flows via the damaged Druzhba pipeline for political reasons. Kyiv denies the charge, saying it needs time to repair the damage caused to energy infrastructure by a Russian drone strike on January 27.
Hungarian Prime ŌĆŗMinister Viktor Orban, who faces a serious challenge to his 16-year rule in an election on April 12, has made ŌĆŗthe war in Ukraine ŌüĀa central plank of his campaign, saying the opposition would drag Hungary into the conflict.
He has vetoed new EU sanctions on Moscow as well as a huge loan for Ukraine over the oil dispute.
Speaking on state radio, Orban again accused Kyiv of blackmail and said Hungary would use all means at its disposal until oil flows resume. ŌüĀHe did ŌĆŗnot mention the detention of the Ukrainians.
Ukraine's Oschadbank said its employees had been engaged in a routine operation.
"Since the ŌĆŗstart of the full-scale invasion, foreign currency and bank metals have been transported exclusively by land," it said in a statement. "Similar trips are carried out by Oschadbank's cash collection vehicles on ŌĆŗa weekly basis."

item.title: Anglican conservative bloc calls for boycott of Canterbury leadership
item.description:
ABUJA, March 6 (Reuters) - A powerful ŌĆŗgroup of conservative Anglicans on Friday urged its members to boycott meetings convened by the ŌĆīArchbishop of Canterbury and halt any financial contributions to the current leadership, escalating a longŌĆærunning rift within the church.
The declaration by the Global Anglican Future Conference (GAFCON) - a grouping of conservative churches mainly from Africa and Asia that claims to represent a majority ŌĆŗof the world's Anglicans - follows a decision to establish a new council rivalling the current leadership.
Three days ŌĆŗof meetings of GAFCON members in Nigeria have underscored the deep rifts within the ŌüĀAnglican Communion over theological and social issues, including the ordination of women and the inclusion of LGBTQ+ members.
They ŌĆŗcome just weeks before the Communion is due to enthrone Sarah Mullally as its Archbishop of Canterbury, ŌĆŗuntil now spiritual leader of the world's 85 million Anglicans spread across 165 countries.
Laurent Mbanda, installed on Thursday as chairman of the new rival council, read a statement saying the Global Anglican Communion required "a principled disengagement" from structures associated with the Church ŌĆŗof England.
"Leaders who hold office in the Global Anglican Communion must not attend future Primates' meetings called by ŌĆŗthe Archbishop of Canterbury, nor attend the Lambeth Conference, nor attend ACC meetings or participate in Commissions of the ACC," the ŌĆīstatement ŌüĀsaid.
It added that leaders "should not personally approve financial contributions to the ACC. It is also expected that they will not receive financial assistance from compromised sources."
It was not immediately clear how much money was at stake.
The Church of England did not immediately comment.
Founded some 500 years ago when the Church of England broke ŌĆŗfrom Rome, the Anglican Communion ŌĆŗhas spread to many ŌüĀparts of the world, particularly former British colonies.
In recent decades, there have been some liberal shifts within part of the Church. GAFCON was set up in 2008, drawing ŌĆŗon the resistance to these changes, especially in Africa and Asia, where the ŌĆŗchurch is expanding ŌüĀfastest.
On Thursday, a spokesperson for the Anglican Communion Office in London said GAFCON was ignoring years of dialogue aimed at reforming the church.
On whether the church had now split, Diarmaid MacCulloch, emeritus professor of church history at the ŌüĀUniversity of ŌĆŗOxford, told Reuters: "Of course it's a schism."
But MacCulloch said the ŌĆŗrupture need not necessarily be permanent.
"Schisms do eventually get healed, when both sides see that the issues that caused the schism don't seem ŌĆŗthat important any longer," he said.

item.title: Germany's Merz warns against spread of war in Middle East
item.description:
MUNICH, March 6 (Reuters) - German Chancellor Friedrich Merz said on Friday Berlin was working with ŌĆīpartners to find an approach to end the fighting with Iran as he warned against allowing the country to fall into chaos as the war widens across the Middle East.
Merz said Germany shared the aims of the United States and ŌĆŗIsrael to stop Tehran's nuclear and missile programme , its threats to Israel and its support for ŌĆŗproxy groups and he said the Iranian people had the right to decide ŌüĀtheir own future.
"However, as the fighting continues and spreads, we also see growing risks," he said in ŌĆŗa statement. "An endless war is not in our interest."
He said Germany was working with partners to formulate ŌĆŗa common perspective on ending the conflict. "These considerations are ongoing," he said, without giving details.
Merz's comments reflect the unease felt in Europe at the war unleashed by the U.S. and Israeli operation that killed Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei a ŌĆŗweek ago and which has since spread into Lebanon with no clear sign of an end in ŌĆŗsight.
As American and Israeli jets have continued to pound targets across Iran and Iranian missiles and drones have hit ŌĆīcities ŌüĀin the Gulf and Israel, the economic risks have risen with global air transport in chaos and the Strait of Hormuz, one of the world's key oil shipping lanes, shut down.
Iran has not yet named a successor to Khamenei, who held power for 37 years, and Merz said the collapse of Iranian statehood ŌĆŗor proxy conflicts fought ŌĆŗon Iranian soil would ŌüĀnot be in anyone's interest.
"Such scenarios could have far-reaching consequences for Europe," he said, including uncontrolled migration by Iranians fleeing across the border if the country ŌĆŗfell into chaos.
"The Iranian state must remain functional, and public order and ŌĆŗbasic services must ŌüĀbe maintained. The Iranian economy must not collapse," Merz said.
Merz said a political process of dialogue and trust building in the region would have to begin once fighting ends. Iran's nuclear and ballistic missile programmes ŌüĀwould have ŌĆŗto end and be subject to verification. Iran would have ŌĆŗto stop supporting proxy groups and all nuclear material would have to be secured.
If that happened sanctions might be lifted and aid ŌĆŗcould be provided.

item.title: White House says US well on way toward controlling Iran airspace
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The United States is well on its way toward controlling Iranian airspace, ŌĆŗWhite House press secretary Karoline Leavitt said on Friday, adding that ŌĆīWashington expects the achievable U.S. objectives to be completed in four to six weeks.
Speaking to reporters at the White House, Leavitt also said ŌĆŗWashington was looking at potential candidates to lead Iran, ŌĆŗa day after President Donald Trump told Reuters in ŌüĀan interview that the United States must be involved ŌĆŗin choosing the next leader of Iran.
"I know there's a number ŌĆŗof people that our intelligence agencies and the United States government are looking at, but I won't get any further on that," Leavitt said.
ŌĆŗIn the interview on Thursday, Trump said that he thinks ŌĆŗthe next leader of Iran is unlikely to be the late Supreme ŌĆīLeader ŌüĀAyatollah Ali Khamenei's son, who has emerged as a frontrunner to succeed his father, who was killed in a military strike at the start of the war.
Earlier on Friday, Trump ŌĆŗsaid there would ŌĆŗbe no ŌüĀdeal struck with Iran except "unconditional surrender."
"What the President means is that when he, as Commander in Chief ŌĆŗof the US Armed Forces, determines that Iran ŌĆŗno ŌüĀlonger poses a threat to the United States of America, and the goals of Operation Epic Fury has been fully realized, ŌüĀthen Iran ŌĆŗwill essentially be in a place ŌĆŗof unconditional surrender, whether they say it themselves or not," Leavitt said.

item.title: Pentagon taps former DOGE official to lead its AI efforts
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - The Pentagon on Friday named as Chief Data Officer ŌĆŗa computer scientist who aided billionaire Elon Musk's efforts to ŌĆīoverhaul the government last year and who has boosted white supremacists and misogynists online.
In a social media post, the Pentagon said Gavin Kliger's new role "places him at the center of the DepartmentŌĆÖs most ŌĆŗambitious AI efforts," focusing on "day-to-day alignment and execution of the DepartmentŌĆÖs ŌĆŗAI projects, working directly with America's frontier AI labs to ŌüĀsupport the warfighter."
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for ŌĆŗmore comment. Kliger did not immediately return a message.
Kliger, in social media posts between ŌĆŗOctober 2024 and January 2025, has voiced controversial views and reposted content from white supremacist Nick Fuentes and self-described misogynist Andrew Tate.
The Pentagon's use of AI has taken center ŌĆŗstage after a heated weeks-long dispute with Anthropic over guardrails on how the ŌĆŗmilitary can use its AI tools led to last week's decision by the Trump administration to ŌĆīshun ŌüĀthe company and replace it with OpenAI.
On Thursday, the Pentagon slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic - an extraordinary rebuke by the United States against an American tech company that was earlier than its rivals to work ŌĆŗwith the Pentagon.
Anthropic was ŌĆŗthe most aggressive ŌüĀof its rivals in courting U.S. national-security officials. But the company and the Pentagon have been at odds for ŌĆŗmonths over how the military can use its technology on ŌĆŗthe battlefield. ŌüĀThis conflict erupted into public view earlier this year.
Anthropic has refused to back down on bans for its Claude AI to power autonomous weapons and mass ŌüĀU.S. surveillance. ŌĆŗThe Pentagon has pushed back, saying it ŌĆŗshould be able to use this technology as needed, so long as it complies with U.S. law.

item.title: Iranian attacks on civilians won't go unanswered, CENTCOM commander says
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - The United States ŌĆīwill respond to Iranian attacks on civilians ŌĆŗthroughout the Middle ŌĆŗEast, the leader of the ŌüĀU.S. Central Command, ŌĆŗBrad Cooper, said on ŌĆŗFriday.
Cooper said that Iran has attacked 12 different countries ŌĆŗsince the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israel launched strikes on ŌĆīthe ŌüĀIslamic Republic last week, including firing seven attack drones at ŌĆŗcivilian, residential ŌĆŗneighborhoods ŌüĀin Bahrain on Thursday night.
"This ŌĆŗis unacceptable and ŌĆŗwill ŌüĀnot go unanswered," Cooper said in a ŌüĀstatement.

item.title: Oil derivatives signal traders see Middle East shock as short-lived
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Oil options and futures are signalling that the latest Middle East conflict may be shortŌĆælived, as traders pile into structures that profit from a retreat in prices after the initial spike.
Options and futures markets often provide the earliest signal of whether traders see ŌĆŗa supply shock as fleeting or structural, creating opportunities to profit from sharp swings in prices.
The IsraelŌĆæU.S. attack on Iran has ŌĆīsent shockwaves through energy markets as warŌĆærisk insurance costs surge, freight rates hit record levels and disruptions at the Strait of Hormuz snarl oil flows and strand hundreds of vessels. Oil prices , on Friday were at multi-year highs.
In a sign traders see the price shock as temporary, 30-day at-the-money Brent implied volatility jumped 17.5 points to 68% over the past ŌĆŗweek through Tuesday, while 60- and 90-day tenors rose only 5.9 and 2.8 percentage points, LSEG data shows.
"What we're watching in real time is ŌĆŗthe difference between a logistics crisis and a structural one," Brian E. Kinsella, former Goldman Sachs energy specialist, told ŌüĀthe Reuters Global Markets Forum. , opens new tab
"The market is betting it's logistical and I think that is the right read," he added.
The Brent futures curve is sending a ŌĆŗsimilar signal.
The spread between the front-month Brent contract and the six-month contract widened to about $10, the steepest backwardation since the Russia-Ukraine war in 2022, pointing to ŌĆŗtight near-term supply while suggesting short-term disruption.
Meanwhile, the put-to-call ratio on West Texas Intermediate options roughly halved to 0.35 on Monday from Friday's close, CME data showed, pointing to heavy bullish call buying before rebounding to 0.56 on Tuesday as demand returned for downside protection.
A call option gives the holder the right, but not the obligation, to buy a ŌĆŗcrude futures contract at a set price, while a put option gives the right to sell one.
The ratio, meanwhile, compares bearish put options, which profit ŌĆŗfrom declines, with bullish call options that benefit from rising prices to gauge market sentiment.
"Dealers are already short a meaningful amount of these deep out-of-the-money calls, creating a more ŌĆīnegative gamma ŌüĀprofile in crude," said Rebecca Babin, senior energy trader at CIBC Private Wealth US. That contrasts with the more typical environment where dealers are long gamma and sell into rallies.
Gamma shows how much an option's sensitivity to the futures price (delta) will change if the market moves.
Much of the 2027 Brent strip still trades below $70 a barrel, a sign markets are not yet pricing a structural shift in long-term supply, Babin said.
Producers have also used the rally to hedge ŌĆŗforward output, creating natural selling pressure on ŌĆŗlonger-dated volatility, she added.
Risk premiums ŌüĀremain concentrated at the front of the futures curve, reinforcing the view that traders still see the disruption as temporary, said Darrell E. Fletcher, managing director of commodities at trading firm Bannockburn Capital Markets.
Brent options open interest plunged in late ŌĆŗFebruary before rebounding in early March, suggesting traders unwound positions before rebuilding hedges.
Front-month open interest fell from around ŌĆŗ388,000 contracts on Feb. ŌüĀ18 to roughly 73,000 by Feb. 27, before surging to more than 700,000 contracts on March 2 as fresh positions were established.
"The open interest data seems like a pretty clear tell that it was a sharp unwind in positioning and not a structural repricing. The front end is clearly closing a trade while the ŌüĀback end is ŌĆŗwhat to pay attention to,ŌĆØ Kinsella said.
Futures positioning shows a similar pattern, with more than ŌĆŗ40% of open interest concentrated in April through July expiries and thinner positioning further out the curve, according to CME data.
(Join GMF, a chat room hosted on LSEG Messenger for live interviews: https://lseg.group/3KFHrhe , opens new tab )

item.title: US drillers add oil, gas rigs for first time in four weeks, Baker Hughes says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. energy firms ŌĆīthis week added oil and natural gas rigs for the first time in four weeks, energy services firm Baker Hughes (BKR.O) , opens new tab said in its closely followed report on Friday.
The oil and gas rig count, an early indicator of future output, rose by one ŌĆŗto 551 in the week to March 6. , ,
Despite this week's rig increase, Baker Hughes said the ŌĆŗtotal count was still down 41 rigs, or 7% below this time last year.
Baker ŌüĀHughes said oil rigs rose by four to 411 this week, their highest since early February, while gas ŌĆŗrigs fell by two to 132, their lowest since early February.
In the Haynesville Shale in Louisiana and Texas, ŌĆŗthe rig count rose by one this week to 53, the highest since May 2023.
In the Marcellus Shale in Pennsylvania, Ohio and West Virginia, the rig count rose by one to 27, the highest since May 2024.
In the Williston Shale in North ŌĆŗDakota and Montana, the rig count fell by one to 27, the lowest since January 2022.
In North Dakota, ŌĆŗthe rig count fell by one to 25, the lowest since November 2021.
The oil and gas rig count declined by about ŌĆī7% ŌüĀin 2025, 5% in 2024, and 20% in 2023 as lower U.S. oil prices prompted energy firms to focus more on boosting shareholder returns and paying down debt rather than increasing output.
Financial services firm TD Cowen said 18 of the 21 exploration and production (E&P) companies it tracks planned to spend about 1% less in capital ŌĆŗexpenditures in 2026 than in ŌĆŗ2025.
That compares with a ŌüĀdecline of around 4% in 2025, roughly flat year-on-year spending in 2024, and increases of 27% in 2023, 40% in 2022, and 4% in 2021.
With U.S. spot crude ŌĆŗprices expected to fall for the fourth year in a row in 2026, ŌĆŗthe U.S. Energy ŌüĀInformation Administration (EIA) projected crude output would hold at 13.6 million barrels per day (bpd) in 2026, matching 2025's record high.
The EIA, however, made that oil price projection before the U.S.-Iran war. Since then, oil prices have spiked to their highest ŌüĀsince 2023.
While ŌĆŗon the gas side, EIA projected output would rise from a ŌĆŗrecord 107.6 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) in 2025 to 110.0 bcfd in 2026 with spot prices at the Henry Hub benchmark in ŌĆŗLouisiana forecast to jump by about 22% in 2026.

item.title: South Bow says US, Canada policy shift boosts prospects for Keystone XL revival
item.description:
CALGARY, March 6 (Reuters) - The company ŌĆŗbehind a plan to revive the Keystone XL pipeline system to increase Canadian oil exports to ŌĆīthe United States is hopeful a North American policy shift in favour of energy development will shelter its project from the political and regulatory hurdles that led to its cancellation in 2021.
CEO Bevin Wirzba of Canadian pipeline operator South Bow said Friday the world ŌĆŗhas changed since U.S. President Joe Biden canceled the permit for Keystone XL after years of Indigenous ŌĆŗand environmental opposition.
"The policy environment in North America has been far more constructive," Wirzba said. ŌüĀHe said the current war in Iran as well as the ongoing conflict in Ukraine emphasize the need ŌĆŗfor energy security.
"Those realities are a great backdrop for us to provide maybe a solution," Wirzba said on a ŌĆŗconference call.
South Bow, which was set up by former Keystone XL proponent TC Energy in 2024 to take over its oil pipeline business, is considering reviving some of the line that was already built in Alberta and already has all necessary Canadian permits.
The proposal is ŌĆŗnot identical to Keystone XL, as the U.S. segment of the pipeline would be built not by South Bow, ŌĆŗbut by U.S. partners. The full route and details of how the various segments would come together remain unknown, but the ŌĆīgoal is ŌüĀto ultimately transport up to 55,000 bpd of Canadian oil to the U.S. Gulf Coast.
The company's plan comes as U.S. President Donald Trump has sought to speed energy project permitting times and boost domestic oil and gas production. A presidential permit would be required for the proposed new pipeline to ŌĆŗcross the Canada-U.S. border.
In Canada, ŌĆŗPrime Minister Mark Carney ŌüĀis depending on the energy sector to help boost the country's economy, and has pledged to speed permitting times in addition to dropping some climate legislation that the oil industry said ŌĆŗwas impeding its growth.
Carney's moves thus far have materially moved the needle in favour ŌĆŗof considering a ŌüĀpipeline development, Wirzba said. He said South Bow has launched a formal open season process aimed at gauging commercial interest in new pipeline space, and more broadly, gauging the Canadian oil industry's confidence in its ability to grow production in the ŌüĀcoming ŌĆŗyears.
Wirzba declined to say what its proposed project ŌĆö which South Bow is ŌĆŗcalling the "Prairie Connector" pipeline ŌĆö would cost or when construction might start.
But he said many of Canada's oil sands companies have indicated they plan ŌĆŗto increase output materially over the next three to five years.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras raises possibility of extra dividends as oil prices surge
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 6 (Reuters) - Brazil's state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab could distribute extra dividends this year if ŌĆŗcash flow is high, as oil prices surge while its operations ŌĆīhave so far been unaffected by the Middle-East U.S.-Iran conflict, executives told analysts on Friday.
Petrobras will need to be ready for variations in oil prices, CEO Magda Chambriard said, adding ŌĆŗthat the company will not pass the sudden swings onto Brazilian consumers after attacks ŌĆŗby the U.S. and Israel in Iran disrupted global energy flows earlier this week.
"We're living in ŌüĀa time of high geopolitical instability, where our concern is to leave the ŌĆŗcompany prepared for any scenario that may occur regarding the price of oil," ŌĆŗChambriard said.
In 2025, Petrobras did not distribute extraordinary dividends, as lower Brent oil prices made it difficult for the firm to generate surplus cash flow. But Petrobras could do so this ŌĆŗyear, if the sharp upturn in prices remains, said CFO Fernando Melgarejo.
"I would ŌĆŗlove to submit an extraordinary dividend offering, provided we are certain that it will not impact ŌĆīthe ŌüĀfinancing of our projects," said Melgarejo, adding that Petrobras will remain cautious in its decision-making due to the unstable scenario.
In the short-term Petrobras has benefited from the conflict, said Petrobras' head of logistics, commercialization and markets, Claudio Schlosser, as it ŌĆŗships mainly to China, ŌĆŗIndia and Europe, ŌüĀregions far from the conflict zone.
"We've seen better margins," he said. "In a way, there is an increase in value and ŌĆŗan interesting positioning for the company."
Oil prices have hit highs ŌĆŗin the ŌüĀwake of the conflict, with Brent hitting $90 per barrel for the first time since April 2024 and on track for its strongest weekly gain since the volatility seen ŌüĀduring ŌĆŗthe COVID-19 pandemic in the spring of 2020.
On Thursday ŌĆŗPetrobras posted results for 2025 with record exports offsetting a drop in Brent oil prices for the year.

item.title: Hezbollah saw new war with Israel as inevitable and rearmed for months, sources say
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 5 (Reuters) - Lebanese armed group Hezbollah spent months restocking its arsenal of rockets and drones, using support from Iran and its own weapons factories to prepare for a new war with Israel, six sources familiar with the group's preparations said.
Down but not ŌĆŗout after its devastating 2024 conflict with Israel, Hezbollah had concluded that another round of fighting was inevitable - and that this time, it could face an existential threat, according to the sources.
Reuters spoke ŌĆīto three Lebanese sources briefed on Hezbollah's activities, two foreign officials in Lebanon and an Israeli military official, who all spoke on condition of anonymity as they were not authorised to speak to the press.
The details of Hezbollah's recent efforts to rearm have not been previously reported.
The head of Hezbollah's media office, Youssef al-Zein, told Reuters that Hezbollah would not comment on its military operations, though he said the group had decided to "fight to the last breath."
Founded by Iran's Revolutionary Guards in 1982, Hezbollah launched rockets and drones at Israel on Monday to ŌĆŗavenge the death of Iran's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, pulling Lebanon into the war raging across the Middle East.
Although the decision caught some of its own officials off guard , Hezbollah had been readying its military stockpiles and its command-and-control ŌĆŗstructure for an eventual rematch with Israel, the six sources said.
To do so, it had drawn on a monthly budget of $50 million, most of it from Iran and earmarked ŌüĀfor fighters' salaries, according to one of the Lebanese sources, who has been briefed on the group's finances and military activities. One of the foreign officials confirmed the $50 million budget.
It was not immediately clear how long the group had been ŌĆŗrelying on that monthly budget and how it compared to its previous financial resources.
The group has said funds from Iran helped finance rents for people displaced by the 2024 war. Around 60,000 Lebanese, most of them from the Shi'ite Muslim community from which ŌĆŗHezbollah draws its popular support, remained displaced over the last year, with their homes still in ruins.
Hezbollah had also worked to replenish its drone and rocket stashes through local manufacturing, the first Lebanese source, the foreign officials and the Israeli military official said. The Israeli military official said Hezbollah had used Iranian funding both to smuggle arms and make its own weapons, but added that its manufacturing capability had been diminished.
The second foreign official said the group had stationed new rockets and Iranian-made logistical materials in southern Lebanon before the latest war began.
Hezbollah's ŌĆŗmedia office did not immediately respond to questions on its rearmament and Iranian support for it.
Israeli military spokesperson Lieutenant Colonel Nadav Shoshani told Reuters that Hezbollah "had a lot of arms left" and was also seeking to rearm. "They were trying to ŌĆŗsmuggle and we were preventing that," Shoshani said.
In 2024, a punishing two-month war with Israel ended with a ceasefire brokered by the United States. Hezbollah halted its attacks on Israel, which continued strikes on what it said were Hezbollah's efforts ŌĆīto rebuild military ŌüĀcapabilities.
Israel also kept troops in five hilltop positions in southern Lebanon.
Last year, Lebanon also began confiscating Hezbollah weapons in the country's south - but Israel said the group was rearming faster than it was being disarmed.
Speaking to Reuters weeks before Hezbollah entered the regional war, the first Lebanese source confirmed that the group had been rebuilding its military capabilities "in parallel" with Israel's campaign to destroy them.
The pace of Hezbollah's attacks this week provides clues about its weapons stocks.
The group launched 60 drones and rockets on March 2, the first day it attacked Israel, and a similar number the following day, said the second foreign official, who tracks Hezbollah's activities closely.
But on March 4, Hezbollah launched more than double that number of projectiles, a sign it had been ŌĆŗable to draw from its larger caches, the official said.
ALMA, ŌĆŗan Israeli think tank that monitors security on Israel's ŌüĀnorthern border, said it assessed that Hezbollah's arsenal on the eve of its attack included approximately 25,000 rockets and missiles, most of them short- and medium-range.
A video published by Hezbollah on March 4 showed a fighter setting up a drone in a wooded area. Riad Kahwaji, a Dubai-based defence analyst and founder of the Institute for Near East and Gulf Military ŌĆŗAnalysis, identified the drone in the video as a Shahed-101, which he told Reuters could be produced locally.
Hezbollah has also dispatched fighters from its ŌĆŗelite Radwan force back to ŌüĀsouthern Lebanon, Reuters reported this week . They had been withdrawn from the area after the 2024 conflict.
Israeli strikes after the 2024 ceasefire included the targeting of what Israel said were Hezbollah training camps. In late February, the Israeli military said it struck eight military compounds used by the Radwan force to store weapons and prepare for a confrontation.
The Israeli official and the first foreign official said Hezbollah had been struggling to recruit new operatives as a result.
The group lost 5,000 fighters in the 2024 war, an unprecedented blow ŌüĀto its fighting ŌĆŗforce, though the second Lebanese source said it still had some 95,000 fighters left.
In the lead-up to its entry into the current regional war, ŌĆŗHezbollah had become convinced Israel would carry out a major strike on the group that would seek to "disable its ability to retaliate," the first Lebanese source said.
A third foreign official familiar with Hezbollah's thinking said that assessment had driven the group's decision to launch the first salvo, fearing Israel would eventually ŌĆŗturn its attention from Iran to Hezbollah.
"They knew they were next on the list," the official said.

item.title: US files complaints for $15.3 million in proceeds allegedly linked to Iranian oil network
item.description:

Hossein Shamkhani is the son of Ali Shamkhani, it said, a ŌĆŗtop adviser ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe former Supreme Leader of Iran Ayatollah ŌĆŗAli Khamenei. Both Ali ŌĆŗShamkhani ŌüĀand Ali Khamenei were killed in the U.S. and ŌüĀIsraeli strikes ŌĆŗagainst Iran ŌĆŗlast week.

item.title: Swedish coast guard boards suspected stateless ship in the Baltic Sea

"At 3:50 p.m. today, the Coast Guard took control of a vessel with unclear flag ŌĆŗstatus and thus suspected of being a stateless vessel," ŌĆŗit said in a statement, adding that the ŌüĀboarding had taken place off the coast of the ŌĆŗsouthern Swedish town Trelleborg.
The ship Caffa was sailing under a ŌĆŗGuinean flag, but the coast guard said it regarded the vessel to be stateless "based on national and international legislation". Swedish police said the ŌĆŗship was suspected of sailing under a false flag.

The coast ŌüĀguard said it had launched a preliminary investigation into suspected violation of maritime law regarding lack of seaworthiness.

item.title: Ghana peacekeeping battalion hit by missile attacks in Lebanon, Ghana army says

item.title: Germany pulls further troops out of Middle East

BERLIN, March 6 (Reuters) - Germany has pulled additional Bundeswehr troops out of the Middle East, ŌĆŗa military spokesman told Reuters on Friday, ŌĆīthe seventh day of a U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli war on Iran that has convulsed the region.

The RND newspaper network, which first reported the news, said that German troops stationed ŌĆŗin Bahrain had already returned home, and ŌĆŗpreparations for withdrawals from Kuwait were underway. The spokesperson ŌĆīdeclined ŌüĀto comment on this.
The RND report also said that soldiers and staff from the German embassy in Baghdad were being ŌĆŗrelocated to Jordan. ŌĆŗThe ŌüĀforeign ministry did not respond to a request for comment from ŌĆŗReuters on the move.

item.title: Barclays says Brent could test $120/bbl if Middle East tensions persist
item.description:
March 6 (Reuters) - Barclays said on Friday that Brent crude could potentially test $120 a barrel if the Middle ŌĆŗEast conflict persists for another couple of weeks.
"These numbers ŌĆīmight seem too high, especially given widespread pessimism about the oil market outlook heading into this year, but we reiterate that fundamentals are stronger ŌĆŗand risks are bigger than the Russia-Ukraine conflict , when we ŌĆŗsaw these levels materialize," Barclays added.
Oil prices have jumped ŌüĀsharply as the widening U.S.-Israeli conflict with Iran has effectively closed ŌĆŗthe Strait of Hormuz, constraining Middle East supplies.
Shipping through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, which carries about a fifth of global oil and oil and liquefied natural gas, has been disrupted after Iran threatened to fire on ŌĆŗpassing vessels.
Brent crude futures were trading around $93.60 per barrel and ŌĆŗWest Texas Intermediate were at $91.62 as of 1857 GMT.
Barclays said oil volumes stranded ŌĆīon ŌüĀtankers in the Middle East Gulf have risen by about 85 million barrels since the conflict began, adding that risks to oil prices remain skewed to the upside.
U.S. President Donald Trump demanded ŌĆŗIran's "unconditional surrender" on ŌĆŗFriday, a dramatic ŌüĀescalation of his demands a week into the war he launched alongside Israel, which could make ŌĆŗit more difficult to negotiate a swift end ŌĆŗto hostilities.
"Production shut-ins ŌüĀin Iraq and Kuwait are already happening and might spread to UAE and even Saudi Arabia over time," Barclays said.
Barclays said ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗfarŌĆæend 10% scenario now implies Brent could ŌĆŗhit $150 a barrel before the end of the month.

item.title: Trump to forge ahead with immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to forge ahead with his aggressive immigration crackdown driven by top aide Stephen Miller and a new homeland secretary nominee who shares Trump's ŌĆŗhardline view, current and former U.S. officials and lawmakers said.
The Republican president fired Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem on Thursday after months of controversy over heavy-handed immigration enforcement tactics ŌĆīand lawmaker questions about government contracts and turmoil within her department.
But Miller - the White House deputy chief of staff and the architect of TrumpŌĆÖs immigration agenda - retains control of the issue, three U.S. officials said. And TrumpŌĆÖs pick to become homeland secretary, U.S. Senator Markwayne Mullin of Oklahoma, is viewed as closely aligned with TrumpŌĆÖs approach.
ŌĆ£Stephen is a survivor,ŌĆØ one of the officials said, requesting anonymity to discuss internal dynamics. The official said NoemŌĆÖs firing was not a referendum on Trump's restrictive ŌĆŗagenda, but rather on the execution of it.
In response to a request for comment, a White House official said Miller helps coordinate a wide range of issues - from immigration to counter-cartel ŌĆŗoperations - and is "working to ensure the President's policy agenda is implemented."
The Department of Homeland Security and Mullin's office did not respond to requests for comment.
Trump won ŌüĀback the White House in 2024 with a campaign that heavily focused on stopping illegal immigration and ramping up deportations. Immigration was one of Trump's best-polling issues after returning to office - and a key ŌĆŗfocus for the Republican Party broadly. But amid backlash over his aggressive enforcement in U.S. cities, support for his immigration approach declined in recent months , Reuters/Ipsos polls show.
Republicans currently hold narrow majorities in both chambers of ŌĆŗthe U.S. Congress but are heading into midterm elections in November that threaten their control on Washington.
White House spokesperson Karoline Leavitt said on Thursday in an X post related to NoemŌĆÖs dismissal that TrumpŌĆÖs immigration goals remained unchanged.
ŌĆ£President TrumpŌĆÖs immigration agenda is keeping our border secure and deporting illegal alien criminals from our country, and it will continue without interruption,ŌĆØ she said.
Under NoemŌĆÖs leadership, DHS sent thousands of federal immigration agents to Los Angeles, Chicago, Minneapolis and other Democratic-led cities to seek ŌĆŗout immigration offenders, sweeping through residential neighborhoods and chasing day laborers in Home Depot parking lots.
But after federal agents shot and killed two U.S. citizens in Minneapolis - Renee Good and Alex Pretti - the administration said it ŌĆŗwould move to a less public-facing ŌĆ£targetedŌĆØ approach to immigration arrests.
Current and former federal immigration officials said there had been no immediate policy shift since NoemŌĆÖs departure was announced, but several said they expected the administration to continue to avoid ŌĆīhigh-profile surges into ŌüĀU.S. cities following the fallout in Minneapolis.
Mullin, 48, became a senator in 2023 after a decade in the U.S. House of Representatives. Like Noem, he is a rancher and owns a cattle ranch in Oklahoma that also serves as a wedding venue, among other businesses.
Even as a freshman, Mullin has stood out from the crowd of 100 senators, at times presiding over the Senate wearing a large, light-colored cowboy hat.
He had a short-lived career as a mixed martial arts fighter and garnered widespread media attention in 2023 when he rose from his chair during a Senate hearing to challenge Teamsters President ŌĆŗSean O'Brien to "stand your butt up" and fight.
Mullin voted ŌĆŗfor a Republican-backed funding package last year ŌüĀthat devoted a historic $170 billion to immigration enforcement through September 2029 and backs core elements of TrumpŌĆÖs immigration platform.
Republican lawmakers praised Mullin after the news that Trump would nominate him for DHS secretary, a position that requires majority confirmation in the Senate.
ŌĆ£He's strong on the border and that's what we need,ŌĆØ Republican Senator Eric ŌĆŗSchmitt of Missouri told reporters on Thursday. ŌĆ£The deportations will continue."
Noem was sharply criticized by both Democrats and Republicans for swiftly saying Good and Pretti were ŌĆŗengaged in ŌĆ£domestic terrorismŌĆØ after they ŌüĀwere killed and before a full investigation.
Mullin similarly portrayed Pretti as a threat despite video evidence that undercut that claim. He said Pretti was "a deranged individual" who had a loaded pistol and intended "to cause max damage," during an interview with Fox News on January 24, the day Pretti was killed.
Democrats on Capitol Hill welcomed NoemŌĆÖs removal but said the Trump administrationŌĆÖs approach to immigration enforcement still needed to change. Democrats have blocked funding for DHS ŌüĀsince mid-February in ŌĆŗa push to force the White House to moderate its tactics.
Senator Chris Murphy, a Democrat from Connecticut, said funding talks are ŌĆŗbeing run out of the White House and that he does not expect NoemŌĆÖs firing to break the stalemate.
ŌĆ£I think weŌĆÖre better off without her, but she wasnŌĆÖt running the department,ŌĆØ Murphy said on Thursday. ŌĆ£Stephen Miller runs that department and will continue to ŌĆŗrun the department so I donŌĆÖt really think much will change.ŌĆØ

item.title: Israeli military says unintentionally struck UN agency truck in Gaza
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 6 (Reuters) - Israel's military said on Friday that a "firing component" launched by its navy unintentionally struck a fuel ŌĆŗtruck belonging to a United Nations agency in Gaza ŌĆīthe previous day, an incident that prompted the agency to publicly call for a full investigation.
The United Nations Office for Project Services, ŌĆŗwhich oversees fuel distribution in Gaza, said that the ŌĆŗempty fuel truck was struck on Thursday around ŌüĀ5 a.m. from the direction of the sea, causing damage ŌĆŗto the vehicle. There were no injuries.
"Our teams are taking ŌĆŗextraordinary risks every day to keep humanitarian operations and life-sustaining services running," UNOPS Executive Director Jorge Moreira da Silva said in a statement, ŌĆŗcalling for an investigation into the incident.
"They should not ŌĆŗhave to do that under fire," he said.
In response to Reuters questions, ŌĆīthe ŌüĀIsraeli military said that the incident occurred during defensive naval activity, and that a firing component deviated from its intended trajectory.
The fuel truck sustained "minor damage", the military said in a ŌĆŗstatement. The military ŌĆŗdid not ŌüĀsay what type of munitions had been fired, or what had been the navy's intended ŌĆŗtarget.
"The incident was reviewed, and lessons were learned ŌĆŗaccordingly," ŌüĀit said, without providing further details.
The fuel truck had been on its way to the Kerem Shalom crossing when it was ŌüĀstruck, ŌĆŗand the truck's movements had been ŌĆŗcoordinated with Israeli authorities in advance, UNOPS said.

item.title: Private credit woes come for Larry FinkŌĆÖs legacy
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Rising disquiet in private credit is uncomfortable for a lot of Wall Street titans, but especially for Larry Fink. The BlackRock (BLK.N) , opens new tab boss moved aggressively to transform his firm, the kingpin of public-markets investing for ŌĆŗthe masses, into a private-markets rival to the likes of Blackstone (BX.N) , opens new tab or Apollo Global Management (APO.N) , opens new tab . The timing now looks awkward, as worries ŌĆīabout overheated lending mount and a key retail fund caps investor withdrawals. FinkŌĆÖs legacy project , and his companyŌĆÖs strengths, will be tested.
As part of a $28 billion string of deals to move into private markets, BlackRock agreed to acquire non-bank lender HPS Investment Partners in 2024. Included in that $12 billion acquisition was HPS Corporate Lending Partners, or HLEND, a nontraded business development ŌĆŗcompany (BDC). Such funds target affluent individual investors, allowing them to withdraw their cash up to a set cap, typically of 5% of shares per ŌĆŗquarter. According to a shareholder letter , opens new tab , HLEND received requests totaling 9.3%, but will only offer redemptions up to its limit, ŌüĀas it was designed to do.
This is far from the first hiccup in private credit. A Blue Owl (OWL.N) , opens new tab fund barred regular withdrawals, instead selling assets to raise ŌĆŗcash and return a huge chunk at once. To satisfy elevated redemptions at BCRED, the largest nontraded BDC around, Blackstone employees and the firm itself put in ŌĆŗa combined $400 million to ensure all requests were covered.
HLEND, though, is the first BDC of its kind to actually prorate redemptions, though others are expected to end up following suit, according to Kevin Gannon of investment bank RA Stanger. Turning theoretical fine-print restrictions into reality may well annoy, and possibly frighten, customers.
When BlackRock bought HPS, private credit was running red-hot. ŌĆŗAssets under management in direct lending funds alone had climbed to $889 billion, according to Preqin data. Apollo and its ilk saw an addressable market in ŌĆŗthe tens of trillions of dollars by expanding into IOUs secured by specific assets. These strategies are increasingly pitched as a fit for the kind of mom-and-pop savers that made ŌĆīBlackRock a ŌüĀcolossus, with $14 trillion of assets under management.
The company got there in part by inking perhaps the most successful deal in modern asset management history, snapping up BarclaysŌĆÖ BGI in 2009 for $13.5 billion and riding then-explosive growth in exchange traded funds. If anyone has a track record of cracking new markets and reaching the retail masses through M&A, itŌĆÖs Larry Fink.
His public-markets business is still immensely successful, and HLEND is only one small piece of a larger plan. Still, rivals ŌĆŗlike Blackstone are increasingly trying to eat ŌĆŗBlackRock's lunch by selling retail products. ŌüĀMeanwhile, from Apollo boss Marc Rowan to JPMorgan CEO Jamie Dimon, warnings of a coming private credit , opens new tab shakeout are rising. As HLENDŌĆÖs investor letter put it, disruptive periods breed new opportunities. BlackRockŌĆÖs challenge is to prove it can capitalize on ŌĆŗthem.
Follow Jonathan Guilford on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran's UN envoy says 1,332 Iranian civilians killed in war

item.title: EU countries joining US, Israel attacks would become Iran's "legitimate target", Deputy Minister says

item.title: UK's Starmer discusses military, intelligence support with Saudi crown prince

item.title: US, Ecuador bomb drug trafficker camp near Colombia border, militaries say
item.description:
WASHINGTON/QUITO, March 6 (Reuters) - The U.S. and Ecuador carried out a ŌĆŗjoint operation targeting drug trafficking operations in the South American ŌĆīcountry, authorities in both countries said on Friday, with the U.S. calling the move "lethal kinetic operations."
Neither the U.S. Southern Command, a branch of its military that oversees forces ŌĆŗin Latin America, nor Ecuador's defense ministry, said if anyone ŌĆŗwas killed or captured in the strike, which Ecuador dubbed ŌüĀoperation "Total Extermination."
The operations used helicopters, aircraft, river boats and drones to ŌĆŗlocate and bomb a drug traffickers' training camp in north-east Ecuador near ŌĆŗthe Colombian border, Ecuador's defense ministry said in a statement.
The camp belonged to the Comandos de la Frontera (CDF), a Colombian crime group made up of FARC dissidents, and ŌĆŗhad a capacity for 50 people, it added.
Ecuadorean President Daniel Noboa ŌĆŗhas made a military crackdown on organised crime a cornerstone of his administration, and ŌĆīhis ŌüĀgovernment imposed tariffs on its larger neighbor Colombia, accusing it of not doing enough to fight drug trafficking.
He is set to travel to Miami this weekend to take part in the Trump administration's "Shield of the Americas" ŌĆŗsummit, which brings ŌĆŗtogether many right-wing ŌüĀleaders across the region with a focus on regional security and organized crime .
"The United States is a key ally ŌĆŗin this fight," the defense ministry said.

The ŌüĀoperation followed ŌĆŗa similar U.S-Ecuadorean operation announced by the ŌĆŗU.S. Southern Command earlier this week .

item.title: US skips congressional review to approve munitions sale to Israel

item.title: US judge dismisses case by deported student who declined flight back
item.description:
BOSTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A U.S. judge who had ordered President Donald Trump 's administration ŌĆŗto facilitate the return of a college student it deported to Honduras said on Friday the "sad truth" is ŌĆīthat her decision not to board a plane it arranged back to the United States meant her case must be dismissed.
Any Lucia Lopez Belloza, a 20-year-old freshman at Babson College in Massachusetts, declined on February 27 to board a flight arranged by U.S. Immigration and Customs Enforcement to bring her back ŌĆŗto the United States after the administration said it would try to deport her again if she returned.
Lopez Belloza, ŌĆŗwho came to the United States from Honduras when she was 8, has said she was unaware ŌüĀshe was subject to a final order of removal that was entered when she was 11.
Her lawyers had urged Boston-based U.S. ŌĆŗDistrict Judge Richard Stearns to let Lopez Belloza continue the lawsuit she filed after being detained by immigration authorities at Boston's ŌĆŗLogan International Airport in November while traveling to spend the Thanksgiving holiday with her family in Texas.
But Stearns reaffirmed his earlier conclusion that he did not have jurisdiction to hear the case concerning her detention because by the time it was filed on November 21, she had been flown by ŌĆŗimmigration authorities to Texas.
His sole remaining basis for jurisdiction would have been to enforce an order issued by another judge just ŌĆŗminutes after Lopez Belloza's case was filed barring her from being deported or transferred out of Massachusetts for 72 hours. Lopez Belloza was flown ŌĆīfrom Texas ŌüĀto Honduras the next day despite that judicial order.
A U.S. government lawyer later apologized to Stearns for a "mistake" made by a ICE officer who failed to properly alert others in the agency about the existence of the judicial order.
Stearns on February 13 ordered the administration to rectify the error by facilitating her return to the United States. The administration last week said it would do so ŌĆŗby having Lopez Belloza board the ŌĆŗICE flight from Honduras to ŌüĀTexas.
But the administration also said ICE planned , opens new tab to move to deport her again upon arrival and had the authority to detain her. Lopez Belloza said the "nightmare" situation caused her to decline to board ŌĆŗthe flight and remain in Honduras.
"The sad truth is that when Any declined the flight she ŌĆŗalso waived this ŌüĀcourt's only remaining basis for jurisdiction," Stearns wrote.
Stearns said had she boarded the plane, the judicial order barring her swift deportation would have remained in effect, giving her "ample opportunity" to file a new case in Texas to challenge her detention.
Todd Pomerleau, Lopez Belloza's lawyer, said ŌüĀhe will ŌĆŗappeal.

item.title: Iran war enters second week as Trump demands 'unconditional surrender'
item.description:
BEIRUT/WASHINGTON/JERUSALEM, March 7 (Reuters) - The expanding war in Iran entered its second week on Saturday amid renewed uncertainty about how or when hostilities will end, as U.S. President Donald Trump declared he would only accept Tehran's "unconditional surrender" and Israel traded fresh attacks with Iran and Lebanon.
Trump's comments ŌĆŗon social media on Friday came hours after Iran's president announced that unnamed countries had begun mediation efforts, briefly raising the possibility, however faint, of a diplomatic resolution a week after ŌĆīthe U.S. and Israel launched strikes on Iran.
"There will be no deal with Iran except UNCONDITIONAL SURRENDER!" Trump wrote. "After that, and the selection of a GREAT & ACCEPTABLE Leader(s), we, and many of our wonderful and very brave allies and partners, will work tirelessly to bring Iran back from the brink of destruction, making it economically bigger, better, and stronger than ever before."
Trump has offered shifting explanations of his war aims, raising the possibility of an extended regional conflict that has already spilled well beyond Iran's borders, shaken global financial markets ŌĆŗand sent oil prices soaring.
In response to the attack, Iran has targeted Israel as well as multiple Gulf states that host U.S. military installations.
Inside Israel on Friday, explosions could be heard as Israeli defenses ŌĆŗactivated to shoot down incoming Iranian fire. The UAE, Kuwait, Qatar, Bahrain and Saudi Arabia all reported fresh drone and missile attacks.
Meanwhile, Israel pursued a major expansion of the ŌüĀwar in Lebanon, pounding the capital Beirut on Friday after ordering an unprecedented evacuation of the city's entire southern suburbs.
Israel also launched a new wave of attacks on Iran, saying 50 of its warplanes had hit a bunker ŌĆŗstill being used by Iran's leadership beneath slain Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei's destroyed Tehran compound.
Early on Saturday, Iran's semi-official Tasnim news agency reported that Mehrabad Airport in Tehran had been struck.
There was no immediate comment from Iran's Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuard or Hezbollah.
Israel has extended its bombing to Lebanon to root out Hezbollah , the Shi'ite militia allied to Iran that has been a dominant faction in Lebanese politics since the 1980s. Hezbollah fired on Israel this week to avenge the death of Khamenei.
"We're sleeping here in the streets - some in cars, some on the street, some on the beach," said Jamal Seifeddin, 43, who fled Beirut's southern suburbs and spent the night on the streets in the downtown district.
About 300,000 people have been displaced in Lebanon in the past four days, ŌĆŗaccording to the Norwegian Refugee Council.
The Israeli military says it has destroyed 80% of Iran's air-defense systems in the first week of the campaign and disabled more than 60% of its missile launchers.
Trump's demand for Iran's surrender, ŌĆŗand the likelihood that it would complicate any quick path to ending the conflict, sent European and U.S. stock indexes tumbling on Friday. Oil prices hit their highest prices in years with the critical shipping lane of the Strait of Hormuz effectively ŌĆīclosed down.
Trump ŌüĀtold Reuters in a telephone interview on Thursday that he must have a say in selecting Iran's new supreme leader to replace Khamenei, killed on the war's first day , a demand he repeated on Friday in a remarkable assertion of power over the country of more than 90 million people.
Iran's U.N. ambassador, Amir Saeid Iravani, told reporters that new leadership would be chosen "in accordance with our constitutional procedures and solely by the will of the Iranian people - without any foreign interference."
Israel has said openly that it aims to overthrow Iran's ruling system. It has been bombing parts of western Iran to support Iranian Kurdish militias who hope to exploit the war to seize towns near the frontier, according to three sources ŌĆŗfamiliar with Israel's talks with the factions.
Iran has cast the ŌĆŗwar as an unprovoked attack and describes the ŌüĀkilling of Khamenei as an assassination.
Earlier on Friday, Iran President Masoud Pezeshkian posted on social media: "Some countries have begun mediation efforts." He did not identify the countries or provide further details.
Russia is providing Iran with locations of U.S. warships and aircraft in the Middle East after Iran's ability to locate U.S. forces was degraded, the Washington ŌĆŗPost reported, citing three officials familiar with the intelligence.
Russian missions in the U.S. did not immediately respond to requests for comment on the report.
Trump met with executives from ŌĆŗseven defense contractors on Friday, who ŌüĀhe later said had agreed to accelerate weapons production. The administration has been pressuring contractors as Iran and other recent operations have drawn down supplies.
Karoline Leavitt, a White House spokeswoman, said the U.S. has enough weapons stockpiles to meet the needs of its Iran operations, which she said would take about four to six weeks to complete.
At least 1,332 people have been killed in Iran since the U.S. and Israel launched strikes on February 28, Iravani said, citing the Iranian Red Crescent ŌüĀSociety.
The Lebanese health ŌĆŗministry has reported 123 people killed and 683 wounded as a result of Israeli attacks. Iranian attacks have killed 11 people in ŌĆŗIsrael since the war started, and at least six U.S. service members have been killed.
Two U.S. officials told Reuters that military investigators believed it was likely that U.S. forces were responsible for an apparent strike on an Iranian girls' school that killed scores of children on the first day ŌĆŗof the war. The investigators have not yet reached a final conclusion.

item.title: Pakistani convicted of plotting to kill Trump over death of Iran commander
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 6 (Reuters) - A Pakistani man was convicted on Friday of planning to kill President Donald Trump and other prominent U.S. politicians two years ago at the behest ŌĆŗof Iran , the Department of Justice said.
Asif Merchant was accused of trying to recruit ŌĆīpeople in the U.S. in a plan targeting Trump and others in retaliation for Washington's killing of Iranian military commander Qassem Soleimani in 2020, when Trump was in his first term.
Targets in the ŌĆŗ2024 plot also included then-President Joe Biden and Nikki Haley, who ran ŌĆŗagainst Trump that year for the Republican presidential nomination, federal prosecutors said.
Merchant ŌüĀwas convicted of "murder for hire and attempting to commit an act of terrorism transcending ŌĆŗnational boundaries," directed by the Iranian authorities, the DOJ said , opens new tab in a statement.
The trial in ŌĆŗthe New York City borough of Brooklyn started last week, days before Trump ordered an assault on Iran, carried out with Israel, that has expanded into the region's biggest war in years.
Merchant admitted to ŌĆŗjoining the plot with Iran's elite Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps but testified he did ŌĆŗso unwillingly, to protect his family in Tehran.
Merchant said he was never ordered to kill a ŌĆīspecific person ŌüĀbut that his Iranian handler named three people in the course of conversations in the Iranian capital.
Law enforcement thwarted the plan before any attack occurred. A person Merchant contacted in April 2024 to help with the plot reported his activities and became a ŌĆŗconfidential informant, the DOJ ŌĆŗsaid. Merchant was arrested ŌüĀand pleaded not guilty that year.
The Revolutionary Guards have a central role in Iran, with a combination of military and economic power and ŌĆŗan intelligence network. Tehran has denied accusations that it targeted Trump ŌĆŗor other ŌüĀU.S. officials.
U.S. and Israeli attacks since Saturday have killed at least 1,332 Iranian civilians and wounded thousands, according to Iran's U.N. ambassador. Many top Iranian leaders, including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali ŌüĀKhamenei, ŌĆŗhave been killed.
The U.S. military has said six of its service ŌĆŗmembers were killed in a strike on a facility in Kuwait, while Israeli tallies show at least 10 ŌĆŗcivilians have been killed across Israel.

item.title: Tornadoes kill four, injure more than a dozen in rural┬ĀMichigan
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 6 (Reuters) - Tornadoes and strong storms killed at least four people and injured more than a dozen in southern Michigan on ŌĆŗFriday, destroying multiple homes and leaving hundreds of residents without power, ŌĆīlocal authorities said.
