item.title: German Greens lead Chancellor Merz's conservatives in state election
item.description:
BERLIN, March 8 (Reuters) - Germany's environmental Greens are set to win a state election in the southern region of Baden-Wuerttemberg, leaving them poised to continue a ŌĆŗcoalition with Chancellor Friedrich Merz's conservatives, initial forecasts showed after polls ŌĆīclosed on Sunday.
The far-right Alternative for Germany (AfD) party, which has made strong gains in recent years, came third, confirming its position as Germany's main opposition party, even outside its heartland in ŌĆŗthe former communist eastern states.
The first forecasts on public broadcaster ARD showed ŌĆŗthe Greens in first place with 32% of the vote, followed ŌüĀby Merz's Christian Democrats (CDU) on 29% and the AfD on 17.5%.
Merz's coalition partners ŌĆŗin Berlin, the centre-left Social Democrats were on course for just 5.5% of ŌĆŗthe vote, confirming the precipitous drop in support seen in recent years but just scraping past the minimum threshold to enter state parliament.
Baden-Wuerttemberg, home to Mercedes-Benz (MBGn.DE) , opens new tab , Porsche (P911_p.DE) , opens new tab , Bosch (ROBG.UL) and one of the ŌĆŗtraditional strongholds of the German car industry, is one of Germany's most prosperous ŌĆŗregions.
But its auto sector and wider economy have been hit hard by increasing competition from ŌĆīChina's surging ŌüĀelectric vehicles makers.
After a decade of Green-CDU coalition government in Baden-Wurttemberg, the moderate Green candidate for state premier, Cem Ozdemir is unlikely to make life difficult for Merz in the Bundesrat, the upper house of parliament that represents the states ŌĆŗin Germany's federal system.
But ŌĆŗthe result underlines ŌüĀMerz's struggles to cut through with voters, as promised reforms have stalled and a sluggish economy comes back slowly from ŌĆŗtwo years in recession.
Initially trailing in the race, the Greens ŌĆŗovertook the ŌüĀChristian Democrats as the ballot approached and the result is likely to fuel discontent among conservatives already alarmed at the record low approval levels for Merz's government in ŌüĀBerlin.
The Baden-Wuerttemberg ŌĆŗelection was the first of five state elections ŌĆŗthis year, with voters in neighbouring Rhineland-Palatinate due to go to the polls on March 22, followed ŌĆŗby Berlin, Saxony-Anhalt and Mecklenberg-Vorpommern in September.

item.title: Iraqi oil production collapses with Strait of Hormuz blocked by conflict, sources say
item.description:
BAGHDAD, March 8 (Reuters) - Iraqi oil ŌĆīproduction from its main southern oilfields has fallen by 70% to just 1.3 million barrels per day as the country is unable to export oil via the Strait of Hormuz due to ŌĆŗthe Iran war, three industry sources said on Sunday.
Production from the fields ŌĆŗstood at around 4.3 million bpd before the war.
"Crude storage has ŌüĀreached maximum capacity and the remaining output after the major cut will be ŌĆŗused to supply the countryŌĆÖs refineries," said an official with the stateŌĆærun Basra Oil ŌĆŗCompany (BOC), which manages production and export operations from the southern fields.
The Strait of Hormuz, a narrow, strategically vital waterway between the Persian Gulf and the Gulf of Oman, is one of the ŌĆŗworld's key oil transit chokepoints, carrying roughly a fifth of global oil and ŌĆŗliquefied natural gas flows.
The OPEC member's exports also fell sharply to an average of around 800,000 ŌĆībarrels ŌüĀper day on Sunday, with only two tankers loading because vessels cannot move freely through the strait to Iraq's southern terminals, the source said.
With no new tankers able to reach IraqŌĆÖs southern terminals, exports from there are expected to halt completely ŌĆŗat around 8 p.m. ŌĆŗlocal time (1700 GMT), ŌüĀsaid two oil officials with direct knowledge of terminal operations.
IraqŌĆÖs oil exports from the southern oilfields stood at 3.334 million ŌĆŗbarrels per day in February, an oil ministry document showed.
A ŌĆŗdrop in ŌüĀIraq's oil production and exports is set to strain the countryŌĆÖs already fragile finances, as the state relies on crude sales for nearly all public spending and more than ŌüĀ90% ŌĆŗof its income.
"This is the most serious operational threat ŌĆŗIraq has faced in more than 20 years," a senior Iraqi oil ministry official said.

item.title: Iranian army says at least 104 killed in US attack on Iranian warship last week
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - The Iranian army said on ŌĆŗSunday that at ŌĆīleast 104 people were killed and 32 ŌĆŗwere wounded ŌĆŗin an attack by ŌüĀthe U.S. on ŌĆŗan Iranian warship ŌĆŗoff Sri Lanka's coast last week.
A U.S. submarine sank ŌĆŗthe frigate Dena ŌĆŗin the Indian Ocean ŌĆīabout ŌüĀ19 nautical miles off Sri Lanka's southern port city ŌĆŗof Galle ŌĆŗon ŌüĀWednesday, killing dozens of ŌĆŗsailors and dramatically ŌĆŗwidening ŌüĀWashington's pursuit of the Iranian navy.

item.title: New York City police identify device outside Mamdani's home as explosive
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - New York City Police said on Sunday a device that was ignited and thrown during protests outside Mayor Zohran MamdaniŌĆÖs home over the weekend was an explosive that ŌĆŗcould have caused serious injury or death.
The device, a jar filled with nuts, ŌĆībolts and screws and wrapped in black tape with a fuse, was thrown by a counterprotester on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion, but it extinguished itself before any explosion, according to a statement from New York ŌĆŗCity Police Commissioner Jessica Tisch. Two people were in custody, Tisch said.
The device was ŌĆŗone of two that were thrown during the protests, which were led ŌüĀby two opposing groups, according to police. The second device was still being examined, Tisch ŌĆŗsaid.
Far-right activist Jake Lang led a protest on Saturday outside Gracie Mansion - where Mamdani lives ŌĆŗwith his wife - against a purported Islamic ŌĆ£takeoverŌĆØ of New York City and against public prayer by Muslims. Tisch said at a press conference on Saturday that she did not believe Mamdani and his wife were home ŌĆŗat the time.
In a statement on Sunday, Mamdani condemned LangŌĆÖs protest but said the violence ŌĆŗthat followed it was more disturbing.
ŌĆ£Violence at a protest is never acceptable,ŌĆØ Mamdani said. ŌĆ£The attempt to use ŌĆīan explosive ŌüĀdevice and hurt others is not only criminal, it is reprehensible and the antithesis of who we are.ŌĆØ
LangŌĆÖs protest, which consisted of about 20 people, was opposed by a far larger counterprotest of 125 people aimed at running ŌĆ£NazisŌĆØ out of New York, Tisch said at a ŌĆŗpress conference on Saturday. ŌĆŗPolice said Emir ŌüĀBalat, 18, was among the counterprotesters before he lit and threw the device.
The device rolled near police before it extinguished itself, Tisch said ŌĆŗon Saturday. Balat ran after throwing it and eventually lit and dropped ŌĆŗa second ŌüĀdevice in the street, according to Tisch.
Balat and another man, whom police on Sunday identified as Ibrahim Kayumi, were arrested at the scene, Tisch said. New York police are working with the ŌüĀFBI and ŌĆŗthe U.S. Department of Justice on the investigation.
Tisch said ŌĆŗon Saturday there was no immediate indication the incident was related to the U.S. attack on Iran , but that authorities ŌĆŗwere still investigating.

item.title: Iran names Khamenei's son, Mojtaba, as new supreme leader, state media report
item.description:
DUBAI, March 8 (Reuters) - Iran's Assembly ŌĆŗof Experts has named ŌĆīMojtaba Khamenei to replace his father, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as ŌĆŗthe country's new ŌĆŗsupreme leader, state media reported ŌüĀon Sunday.
Mojtaba , a mid-ranking ŌĆŗcleric with close ties ŌĆŗto the powerful Revolutionary Guards, had long been viewed by elements ŌĆŗof Iran's ruling ŌĆŗestablishment as a potential successor to ŌĆīhis ŌüĀfather, who was killed after the United States and Israel launched strikes on ŌĆŗIran.
Although ŌĆŗIran's ŌüĀruling ideology frowns on the principle of hereditary ŌĆŗsuccession, he has ŌĆŗa ŌüĀpowerful following within the Guards and his dead father's ŌüĀstill-influential ŌĆŗoffice.

item.title: Iran defies Trump, elevates Khamenei's son Mojtaba as successor
item.description:
DUBAI, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran's clerical leadership chose confrontation over compromise in appointing Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father, Ali Khamenei, a move regional officials say is a direct rebuke to U.S. President Donald Trump, who had declared the son "unacceptable".
Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was ŌĆŗkilled in a U.S.-Israeli strike at the start of the conflict, now in its second week .
The appointment of Mojtaba as his successor by the Assembly of Experts locks hardliners firmly ŌĆīin control in Tehran - a gamble that could reshape Iran's war with the U.S. and Israel and reverberate far beyond the Middle East.
"Having Mojtaba take over is the same playbook," said Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute.
"It's a big humiliation for the United States to carry out an operation of this scale, risk so much, and end up killing an 86-year-old man, only to have him replaced by his hardline son.ŌĆØ
Under Iran's complex, theocratic system, the supreme ŌĆŗleader is the ultimate authority, including over foreign policy and IranŌĆÖs nuclear programme, as well as guiding the elected president and parliament.
Analysts say the choice ŌĆŗof Mojtaba, a deeply hardline cleric whose wife, mother and other family members were also killed in U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli strikes - sends an unequivocal message: IranŌĆÖs leadership ŌüĀhas rejected any prospect of compromise to preserve the system and sees no path forward except confrontation, revenge and endurance.
According to insiders, Mojtaba will face immense internal and external strain from a disaffected population and ŌĆŗan escalating conflict, but is expected to move swiftly to consolidate power.
That will likely mean expanded authority for the Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps, harsher domestic controls and sweeping repression to crush dissent.
"The world will miss ŌĆŗthe era of his father," a regional official close to Tehran told Reuters. "Mojtaba will have no choice but to show an iron fist... even if the war ends, there will be severe internal repression."
That stance comes after months of deepening domestic unrest - the bloodiest since the 1979 Islamic Revolution - that had already weakened the Islamic Republic before the war began.
Iran was grappling with a battered economy, soaring inflation, currency collapse and widening poverty, alongside tightening repression that had fuelled public anger and ŌĆŗprotests - pressures now likely to intensify under wartime rule.
Difficult days lie ahead under Mojtaba, with far tighter internal controls, intensified pressure at home and an even more aggressive, hostile posture abroad, said ŌĆŗanother Iranian insider familiar with the situation on the ground.
Paul Salem, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute, said Mojtaba was not a figure positioned to strike a deal with the United States or pivot diplomatically.
ŌĆ£Nobody emerging now ŌĆīis going ŌüĀto be able to compromise,ŌĆØ Salem said. ŌĆ£This is a hardline choice, made in a hardline moment.ŌĆØ
In the eyes of IranŌĆÖs clerics, many of whom famously label America the "Great Satan", the assassination of Khamenei, the Islamic Republic's highest religious authority, has elevated him to "martyrdom".
Clerics have cast the slain leader as a heroic figure, likening him to Imam Hussein - the ShiŌĆÖite symbol of sacrifice and resistance against oppression.
"Mojtaba is even worse and more hardline than his father," said Alan Eyre, former U.S. diplomat and Iran specialist, adding that he was the preferred candidate of the Guards. "He's going to have a lot of revenge to exact."
That calculus carries risks. Israel ŌĆŗhas warned that any successor to Khamenei would also ŌĆŗbe a target, while Trump has said ŌüĀthe war may only end once IranŌĆÖs military leadership and ruling elite are eliminated .
A powerful midŌĆæranking cleric, Mojtaba, 56, has long opposed reformist groups advocating engagement with the West. His close ties to senior clerics and the IRGC - which dominates IranŌĆÖs security forces and its economy - give him leverage ŌĆŗacross the stateŌĆÖs political and coercive security institutions.
He amassed influence under his father as a key figure within the security apparatus and the vast business ŌĆŗempire it controls, operating for ŌüĀyears as Ali KhameneiŌĆÖs gatekeeper and, in practice, a ŌĆ£mini-supreme leaderŌĆØ, analysts say.
His elevation comes as the U.S.-Israeli campaign against Iran intensifies, with joint strikes hitting fuel depots and other targets inside Iran, while Iranian missiles and drones have struck Gulf states, widening the conflict.
Mojtaba studied under conservative clerics in the seminaries of Qom, the heart of ShiŌĆÖite theological learning, and holds the clerical rank of Hojjatoleslam.
The U.S. Treasury sanctioned Mojtaba in 2019, saying he represented the ŌüĀsupreme leader ŌĆŗin an official capacity despite never holding elected or formal government office.
A Gulf source familiar with regional government thinking said of ŌĆŗMojtabaŌĆÖs appointment: ŌĆ£This tells Trump and Washington that Iran will not back down, they will fight on until the finish."
Salem, of the Middle East Institute, likened IranŌĆÖs trajectory to Iraq under Saddam Hussein after 1991 or Syria under Bashar alŌĆæAssad after 2012 ŌĆö governments that ŌĆŗsurvived years of war and isolation but steadily lost control.
ŌĆ£TheyŌĆÖre doubling down on the hard line,ŌĆØ Salem said. ŌĆ£Internally, itŌĆÖs terrible ŌĆö and deeply destabilising.ŌĆØ

item.title: Jailed Istanbul mayor faces trial amid crackdown on Turkey's opposition
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 9 (Reuters) - Istanbul's jailed mayor Ekrem Imamoglu goes on ŌĆītrial on Monday in a sweeping corruption case that could extinguish the ambition of Tayyip Erdogan's main rival to become president at Turkey's next election.
Imamoglu, 55, is the chief suspect among more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul ŌĆŗmunicipality accused of corruption and bribery. He and his main opposition Republican People's Party (CHP) deny ŌĆŗthe charges, which carry prison sentences adding up to hundreds of years.
The case ŌüĀis being held at a courthouse in the Silivri prison complex west of Istanbul, where Imamoglu ŌĆŗhas been held for almost a year, and is the centrepiece of a judicial crackdown on the CHP ŌĆŗthat is overshadowing politics ahead of elections that are expected to be held next year.
In comments to Reuters , Imamoglu struck a defiant tone, saying Erdogan should call elections right away, but his prospects of challenging the veteran leader look ŌĆŗbleak amid a crackdown that rights groups and foreign observers say has undermined the democratic credentials of ŌĆŗNATO member Turkey.
"(I)t's hard to avoid the conclusion that prosecutors are trying to remove Imamoglu from politics and discredit ŌĆīhis ŌüĀparty in ways that undermine democracy," said Benjamin Ward, the deputy Europe and Central Asia director at Human Rights Watch, describing the trial as coming after more than a year of "weaponising" the criminal justice system against the CHP.
The government denies exerting influence over the judiciary, which it says is independent.
Newly appointed ŌĆŗJustice Minister Akin Gurlek , who ŌĆŗas chief Istanbul ŌüĀprosecutor led investigations into Imamoglu, said he carried out his duties in the case without bias or consideration for the mayor's position.
"I simply did my duty ŌĆŗas a public prosecutor. My conscience is clear," Gurlek told reporters in ŌĆŗcomments published ŌüĀon Friday.
Imamoglu has already suffered a severe blow to his presidential ambitions, with a court in January rejecting his lawsuit challenging the cancellation of his university degree ŌĆō a qualification required of any presidential candidate.
Erdogan, 72, has led ŌüĀTurkey ŌĆŗas prime minister or president since 2003. Presidential and parliamentary elections ŌĆŗare not scheduled until 2028 but if he wishes to seek a third term as president he is obliged to hold ŌĆŗthe vote early, unless the constitutional term limits are changed.

item.title: US energy chief defends waiver on Russian oil sanctions, blames fear for higher gas prices
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - Trump administration officials on Sunday defended a decision to temporarily lift some sanctions on Russian oil ŌĆīand predicted that a sharp increase in gasoline prices resulting from the Iran war would last only weeks.
Appearing on multiple TV talk shows, Energy Secretary Chris Wright and U.S. Ambassador to the United Nations Mike Waltz said a waiver issued last week to allow Indian ŌĆŗpurchases of Russian oil would alleviate pressure on the global market.
"It's a 30-day pause to allow, which ŌĆŗis just kind of common sense, to allow the millions and millions of barrels of ŌüĀoil that are sitting out on ships to go to Indian refineries," Waltz said on NBC's "Meet the Press."
Wright ŌĆŗtold CNN's "State of the Union" that the waiver can help "tamp this fear of shortage of oil, tamp the price ŌĆŗspikes and the concerns we see in the marketplace."
With the war now in its second week and no end in sight, Americans are grappling with higher prices at the pump, a new complicating factor for the U.S. economy, which unexpectedly lost 92,000 jobs in February.
As ŌĆŗof Friday, the national average price for regular gasoline stood at $3.32 a gallon, up 11% from the previous ŌĆŗweek and the highest since September 2024, according to data from the motorists group AAA. Diesel was at $4.33, up 15% from a ŌĆīweek ŌüĀago, surging to the highest level since November 2023.
"We believe this is a small price to pay to get to a world where energy prices are returned back to where they were," Wright said on the "Fox News Sunday" program.
There is no shortage of oil or natural gas, said Wright, who asserted that the price increases are based on "fear ŌĆŗand perception" that the Iran ŌĆŗoperation will be a ŌüĀdrawn-out affair.
"But it won't be," Wright said, echoing President Donald Trump 's prediction that the war will last weeks rather than months.
Trump, in a Reuters interview on Thursday, predicted that gasoline prices ŌĆŗwill "drop very rapidly" when the war is over.
Senator John Kennedy, a Louisiana Republican, ŌĆŗcriticized energy speculators.
"The ŌüĀoil prices have gone up because you've got a bunch of oil traders out there in their Gucci loafers, with their caramel Frappuccinos who are bidding up the price," Kennedy said on "Fox News Sunday."
Political analysts say a persistent rise in ŌüĀgasoline prices ŌĆŗcould hurt Republicans in the November midterm elections when control of ŌĆŗthe U.S. Congress will be at stake. A Reuters/Ipsos poll last month found that most respondents rejected Trump's characterization of the economy as "booming."

item.title: Europe now world's biggest arms importer, think tank says
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 9 (Reuters) - Europe became the world's biggest arms importer over the last five years as governments reacted to Russia's threat and waning confidence in ŌĆŗU.S. security commitments, data from the Stockholm International Peace Research Institute ŌĆīshowed on Monday.
European countries more than trebled their arms imports in 2021-25 versus 2016-2020 to supply Ukraine in its defence against Russian invasion and to rebuild their own militaries ŌĆŗafter decades of under-investment.
"The sharp increase in arms flows to European ŌĆŗstates pushed global arms transfers up almost 10 per cent," Mathew ŌüĀGeorge, Director of the SIPRI Arms Transfers Programme, said.
Europe accounted for 33% ŌĆŗof global arms imports, up from 12% in the previous five-year period, the ŌĆŗreport showed.
Despite boosting domestic production, European countries continued to increase purchases of U.S. weapons, especially combat aircraft and long-range air-defence systems.
Arms imports by Middle Eastern states fell 13%, though ŌĆŗSaudi Arabia and Qatar were among the top four individual buyers.
The decline ŌĆŗmainly reflected large Saudi orders during the previous period that still needed to be integrated, ŌĆīSIPRI ŌüĀsenior researcher Pieter Wezeman said.
Even before the latest U.S. and Israeli attacks on Iran , states in the region had placed significant new orders that have yet to show up in the figures.
The current conflict is likely to lift procurement ŌĆŗfurther, particularly of anti-missile ŌĆŗand air-defence ŌüĀsystems.
"They will first replace what they have used, but also that they are going to look at buying more equipment ŌĆŗto protect themselves even better than they can do ŌĆŗnow," Wezeman ŌüĀsaid.
In terms of suppliers, the U.S. increased its global share of the arms export market to 42% from 36%, cementing its dominance. France was the second-largest supplier ŌüĀwith ŌĆŗ9.8% of global exports, while Russia's share dropped ŌĆŗto 6.8% from 21% after its 2022 invasion of Ukraine.
Europe's combined export share was 28% - four ŌĆŗtimes Russia's and five times China's.

item.title: Oman's Muscat airport limits private jet flights, email says
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - Oman's Muscat International Airport has asked private jet operators to avoid using the site for "additional flights," giving priority to government ŌĆŗand commercial flights as fresh airspace closures hit the regionŌĆÖs ŌĆīattempts to increase travel, according to an email seen by Reuters.
"Due to the current crisis management measures at Muscat International Airport, flight movements are restricted ŌĆŗto approved seasonal scheduled services only," the airport authorities ŌĆŗtold charter plane operators on Friday.
The airport said this measure ŌüĀwas necessary to manage congestion and ensure that airport capacity ŌĆŗremains within acceptable limits.
The email, first reported by the Financial Times, ŌĆŗasked that all airlines and operators cancel any slots falling outside the approved seasonal schedule and to refrain from submitting non-approved flight requests until further notice.
"The ŌĆŗonly additional flights that may be considered during this period are ŌĆŗembassy-sponsored repatriation flights, subject to prior approval through the applicable diplomatic channels and ŌĆīon ŌüĀthe condition that no commercial sale of seats is undertaken in connection with such operations," the email added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against Iran has led to flight cancellations across the Middle East , leaving airlines and ŌĆŗgovernments scrambling to ŌĆŗsupport thousands of ŌüĀstranded passengers.
With most airspace in the region still closed over missile and drone concerns, some people ŌĆŗhave turned to private jets while charter flights and ŌĆŗlimited ŌüĀcommercial services struggle to evacuate tens of thousands of travelers.
Travelers have shelled out high prices to get out of the Middle East, rushing ŌüĀto airports ŌĆŗor heading over land to quieter ŌĆŗhubs, with fighter jets occasionally escorting passenger jets.

item.title: Australia tells families of diplomats to leave UAE as conflict escalates
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australia has asked the family members and dependants of diplomatic officials in the ŌĆŗUnited Arab Emirates to leave the country, ŌĆīfollowing an escalation in the conflict in the Middle East as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.
U.S. ŌĆŗPresident Donald Trump told reporters on Air Force ŌĆŗOne that he was not seeking negotiations ŌüĀto end the U.S.-Israeli military strikes against Iran, ŌĆŗwhich have driven up global energy prices, disrupted ŌĆŗbusiness and snarled air travel.

item.title: As Fukushima memories fade, Japan embraces a nuclear-powered future
item.description:
IWAKI, Japan, March 9 (Reuters) - Takuma Hashimoto was three years old when a massive earthquake and tsunami struck on March 11, 2011, triggering nuclear meltdowns at the Fukushima Daiichi plant just an hour's drive from his home.
As the worst nuclear disaster since Chornobyl unfolded, ŌĆŗhis terrified family was trapped, unable to flee like their neighbours because they couldn't find gas for their car.
Now 18, Hashimoto wants to become part of Japan's next generation of nuclear talent.
"I don't think nuclear power ŌĆīshould be treated as something that's automatically dangerous," said the engineering student at a technical college in Iwaki, where a monitoring station still keeps check on local radiation levels.
Reuters spoke with Hashimoto, as well as a former crisis management official and a nuclear industry veteran, ahead of the 15th anniversary of the disaster. Their stories illustrate how Japan is pivoting back to a power source it had all but shunned.
Resource-poor Japan was once one of the world's biggest proponents of nuclear power, which provided roughly 30% of the country's electricity from 54 reactors.
The Fukushima disaster saw public opinion swing dramatically against nuclear, ŌĆŗand all reactors were ordered to be shut down for safety inspections and upgrades. In 2012, the government even decided to phase out nuclear energy. That decision was reversed two years later, but reactor restarts have been slow and ŌĆŗmany have been shut permanently.
Now Tokyo's staunchly pro-nuclear leader Sanae Takaichi, buoyed by a thumping election win , is pushing to accelerate restarts and advance new nuclear technologies ŌüĀto wean the country off costly imported fossil fuels.
The restart of one of the seven reactors at the world's biggest nuclear plant, Kashiwazaki-Kariwa , in January was a milestone. That said, only 15 of 33 reactors in Japan that remain operable are back online.
Middle East ŌĆŗchaos unleashed by U.S. President Donald Trump's attack on Iran - a region that accounts for 95% of Japan's oil supplies - and an anticipated surge in energy needs of power-hungry AI data centres promise to bolster a shift in public opinion.
A slim majority of people - 51% - ŌĆŗare now in favour of the restarts, an Asahi newspaper survey last month found. That's up from 28% when it began polling on the issue in 2013. The most supportive are young people aged 18 to 29 - at 66%.
Hashimoto's school, which has sent him on visits to nuclear power plants, receives government funding to nurture workers in nuclear power, regulation and decommissioning.
But securing enough talent may be a bottleneck in Japan's nuclear renaissance.
There were just 177 students admitted to nuclear-related courses across Japan in 2024, versus 317 just before the Fukushima disaster and a peak of 673 in the early ŌĆŗ1990s.
Hashimoto knows not everyone agrees with him. Almost every week, he encounters a vocal band of anti-nuclear protesters at the train station on his commute to school.
But he says: "What matters is using nuclear power properly, having measures in place in case something happens, and ŌĆŗdeveloping technology to make sure accidents don't occur."
Seiji Inada, 49, was part of the government's crisis response team in 2011. Tasked with tallying the dead, he hunkered down for days with hundreds of officials in an underground bunker beneath the prime minister's office in ŌĆīTokyo.
Inada remembers being ŌüĀin the crisis room, watching aghast footage of a building that housed a reactor exploding.
Around 150,000 people living around the plant were evacuated, many of whom never returned, while the government assessed the risks that Tokyo, one of the world's biggest cities, might be blanketed in a radioactive plume.
"I remember during my short lunch break, I called my dad and I told him: 'Well, I can't tell you any details, but just prepare for the worst case scenario," recalled Inada, who now works for private consultancy FGS Global.
An inquiry published in 2012 blamed plant operator TEPCO (9501.T) , opens new tab , regulators and the government for failing to develop safety protocols that could have contained the damage, calling it a "man-made" disaster.
The prime minister at the time, Naoto Kan, quit after he was criticised for bungling the response.
"The lesson of 3/11 is ŌĆŗhumility: low-probability shocks happen. What matters is governance," said Inada.
Back then, ŌĆŗInada, like most other Japanese, found it hard to ŌüĀimagine that the country would ever return to nuclear power, but "time heals trauma", he added.
"The shock of 2011 was profound and it shaped attitudes for years. Over time, the debate shifted from emotion to trade-offs."
Nuclear industry veteran Keiji Matsunaga, 59, is putting the lessons learned from Fukushima into practice by developing safer reactors at his company Toshiba.
Joining the firm straight from ŌĆŗuniversity in 1991, five years after Chornobyl, he's always been conscious of the stigma attached to the industry.
Fukushima made that worse and some years after the disaster, his teenage daughter ended ŌĆŗup in an argument with a ŌüĀteacher at her school who said nuclear power was dangerous and Japan shouldn't be using it.
But even as Toshiba's nuclear business faltered in the wake of Fukushima and the bankruptcy of its U.S. unit Westinghouse, Matsunaga says he has never wavered from the view that Japan needs nuclear for energy security.
And the gradual increase in restarts is helping business.
Though Toshiba, now a private company, does not release its nuclear power division earnings targets, another big player, Mitsubishi Heavy Industries (7011.T) , opens new tab , told Reuters last week that sales for its nuclear power unit will hit a record 400 ŌüĀbillion yen ($2.5 billion) ŌĆŗnext year, a target it didn't expect to reach until 2030.
Any new plants that might be built have been designed to be much safer, says Matsunaga. ŌĆŗFor example, buildings housing reactors would have roofs fortified with steel plate and concrete that can resist the impact of falling aircraft.
The plants would also have more failproof natural circulation systems, in addition to other systems, to keep the reactor cool. That would help avoid a situation like Fukushima when the loss of air-conditioner ŌĆŗcooling functionality contributed to the meltdowns.
"By adopting these measures, we believe events like Fukushima can be prevented," he said.

item.title: Trump says ending Iran war will be 'mutual' decision with Netanyahu
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 8 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗsaid on Sunday ŌĆīthat when to end the war ŌĆŗwith Iran will ŌĆŗbe a "mutual" decision made ŌüĀwith Israeli ŌĆŗPrime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
Speaking ŌĆŗby phone with The Times of Israel, ŌĆŗTrump said Netanyahu ŌĆŗwill have input on ŌĆīresolving ŌüĀthe conflict.
"I think itŌĆÖs mutual ... a little bit. WeŌĆÖve ŌĆŗbeen ŌĆŗtalking. ŌüĀIŌĆÖll make a decision at ŌĆŗthe right ŌĆŗtime, ŌüĀbut everythingŌĆÖs going to be taken ŌüĀinto ŌĆŗaccount," said ŌĆŗTrump.

item.title: South Korea parliament due to finalise draft US investment bill under trade deal
item.description:
SEOUL, March 9 (Reuters) - A South Korean parliamentary committee is expected to finalise the wording of a special bill on Monday, ŌĆŗpaving the way for a plenary vote this ŌĆīweek to enable $350 billion of U.S. investments under a bilateral trade deal between the countries.
The bill is expected to be put to a ŌĆŗfull National Assembly vote on March 12, as ŌĆŗthe U.S. ally responds to pressure from Washington over ŌüĀperceived delays in enacting the deal.
The "Special Act on Investment ŌĆŗin the U.S." will set up an investment vehicle, as ŌĆŗwell as a risk management committee to implement last year's agreement for South Korea to invest in sectors such as shipbuilding and chips in ŌĆŗreturn for lower U.S. tariffs.
U.S. President Donald Trump threatened in January ŌĆŗto hike tariffs on imports from South Korea, accusing the country's parliament ŌĆīof ŌüĀdelays in enacting the trade deal.
Top South Korean officials have said the U.S.-South Korea trade deal remains valid despite a U.S. Supreme Court decision in February that struck down a large ŌĆŗswath of Trump's ŌĆŗtariffs.
Officials in ŌüĀSeoul have, however, voiced concerns about the impact of U.S. investments on an already weak ŌĆŗwon currency and said that projects would be ŌĆŗbased ŌüĀon consideration of commercial feasibility and foreign exchange market conditions.
The special committee, which includes lawmakers from the ruling Democratic Party and ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗopposition People Power Party, is expected to ŌĆŗreview the final bill at a subcommittee on Monday and approve it at a general ŌĆŗmeeting in the afternoon.

item.title: Brothers of Epstein accuser Virginia Giuffre visit New Mexico ranch, demand unredacted documents
item.description:
STANLEY, N.M., March 8 (Reuters) - Two brothers of one of Jeffrey Epstein's most prominent accusers visited the sex offender's former New Mexico ranch on Sunday for ŌĆŗthe first time to demand the Trump administration release unredacted documents to reveal ŌĆīthe identities of men their late sister alleged sexually abused her at the property.
With Epstein's hacienda-style mansion in the background, the brothers of Virginia Giuffre , who took her own life in April, ŌĆŗjoined hundreds of protesters at a roadside rally to mark international ŌĆŗwomen's day near the gate of the ranch located 30 miles (48 ŌüĀkm) south of state capital Santa Fe.
Giuffre's brother Sky Roberts, 37, called on ŌĆŗthe U.S. Department of Justice to release documents showing, among other things, names of ŌĆŗvisitors to Epstein's Zorro Ranch where he and his acquaintances are accused of sexually abusing women and girls.
"All those names are in the files and right now the government is covering ŌĆŗthose up," said Roberts, flanked by Giuffre's older brother, Daniel Wilson, 47, and ŌĆŗtheir families.
The release of millions of records on Epstein has exposed the financier's social connections with ŌĆīpoliticians, business ŌüĀpeople and scientists who he invited to the ranch.
The files have become a persistent political problem for U.S. President Donald Trump, who was named in FBI records released on Thursday in which an unidentified woman made accusations against him related to an ŌĆŗalleged sexual encounter.
New Mexico ŌĆŗin February became ŌüĀthe first U.S. state to launch a legislative "truth commission" into how Epstein was able to operate in secrecy at Zorro ŌĆŗRanch for 26 years.
ŌĆ£New Mexico is setting the example and we ŌĆŗexpect other ŌüĀstates to follow behind,ŌĆØ said Amanda Roberts, 37, Sky Roberts' wife, citing New York and Florida where Epstein had residences where similar probes needed to occur.
Americans generally view ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗEpstein case as an example of wealthy and ŌĆŗpowerful people rarely being held accountable and believe the U.S. government is still hiding information about Epstein's ŌĆŗclients, according to Reuters/Ipsos polling .

item.title: Compounding errors and narrow self-interest threaten global fuel crisis
item.description:
LAUNCESTON, Australia, March 9 (Reuters) - Mounting miscalculations and a retreat to narrow self-interest by major countries, including the United States and China, are threatening to turn the current conflict in Iran into a global crisis for the supply of refined oil products.
Much of the media focus tends to be on the price of crude oil, with benchmark Brent crude futures jumping as much as 20% to $111.04 a barrel, the highest since July 2022, in early Asian trade on Monday.
But while the ŌĆŗleap in crude is dramatic, of more concern is the even bigger surge in the cost of refined fuels such as gasoline, diesel and jet fuel, which are the products that consumers actually buy.
The explosive climb in refined ŌĆīproduct prices last week was led by jet fuel, with Singapore spot prices hitting a record high of $225.44 a barrel on March 4, before easing to end the week at $155.82.
However, that price is still 66.7% higher than the $93.45 a barrel that prevailed on February 27, the day prior to the United States and Israel launched an aerial campaign against Iran.
Singapore gasoil , the building block for diesel and jet fuel, reached $123.39 a barrel on March 4, the highest since September 2023 and up 33.5% from the close on February 27.
In effect, product markets in Asia are starting to price in a shortage of supply of the key fuels that keep economies running.
The effective ŌĆŗclosure of the Strait of Hormuz is cutting some 18 million barrels per day (bpd) of crude and products, roughly split into 14 million bpd of crude and 3 million bpd of products, according to three-month moving average data compiled by commodity analysts Kpler.
While ŌĆŗthe administration of U.S. President Donald Trump claims the Strait remains open and has offered to insure vessels transiting the narrow waterway between the gulfs of Persia and Oman, the market has yet to ŌüĀbe convinced that Iran won't attack any ship attempting passage.
It is the closure of the Strait of Hormuz that is the most important strategic error made by the Trump administration and Israel in their attack on Iran.
In common with the majority of analysts and presumably Gulf governments ŌĆŗthe assumption was that this conflict would follow the same template as previous flare-ups in the region.
The assumption was that everybody would behave rationally and not target oil production and transportation infrastructure, as it is in nobody's interest to halt the flow of crude and products.
But it turns out ŌĆŗif you tell a dictatorial religious government that your aim is regime change, then that government feels little compunction to play by the prior rules.
Iran's decision to target its Gulf neighbours that host U.S. military bases has upended all previous calculations about the conflict.
Gulf countries Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Kuwait and Iraq are highly dependent on their oil, fuel and liquefied natural gas exports, and right now their revenue is severely curtailed.
Dubai, one of the Emirates, is also increasingly dependent on its status as the region's financial and tourism hub, with both sectors now taking a massive hit from the ongoing conflict.
The compounding errors of ŌĆŗthe Trump administration are now becoming more apparent.
It's clear that they did not expect the Strait of Hormuz to be effectively closed, and their stop-gap solution hasn't won market backing.
It's one thing to offer insurance and perhaps naval escorts, it's entirely another to ensure safe passage can happen ŌĆŗfor hundreds of vessels each week.
All Iran has to do is hit one fully laden crude tanker with a ballistic missile and all bets will be off, with the situation then likely to be worse than it is currently.
The next U.S. step of offering a waiver to India to resume purchases ŌĆīof Russian crude is ŌüĀa bit like feeding cookies to an elephant, it's nice but largely ineffective.
What is already happening is that Asia's refiners are scrambling to source crude from other suppliers outside the Strait of Hormuz choke point.
What is the likely result of this?
Crude oil prices will rise, and the longer the close of the Strait continues the more short of crude, and thus refined products, the region will become.
The response to this looming shortage is that countries with refining capacity will focus on their domestic needs and cut exports of fuels, thereby exacerbating the shortage of refined products.
This can be seen by reports that China has ordered its major state-owned refiners to stop exports of refined products.
Some Indian refiners are also reported to be ending exports of fuels, while plants in some countries, such as South Korea, are cutting refinery processing.
The obvious question for the authorities in Beijing is ŌĆŗhow long before the decision not to supply refined fuels to ŌĆŗimporting nations blows back on the Chinese economy.
It is another example ŌüĀof a miscalculation that will prove more costly than the alternative of actually ramping up fuel exports to help meet demand.
China isn't a major exporter of refined products, with about 600,000 barrels per day.
But it is the only major producer with significant spare capacity and a massive crude oil stockpile, estimated at over 1 billion barrels.
This level of inventories means China could continue to refine oil at its current rate for about ŌĆŗthree years, even if its imports dropped to zero.
In effect, China could use its huge stockpile to boost refinery runs, lift exports and ease the looming supply crisis.
This would not only be highly ŌĆŗprofitable but would also win favour with ŌüĀimporting nations, who may otherwise run short of fuels.
Australia is Asia's biggest fuel importer, taking about 900,000 bpd, according to Kpler.
Imagine a situation when the country could no longer meet this demand, resulting in shortages.
The government would have to prioritise food production and delivery, and keeping as much of the economy going as possible.
But a brave Australian government could say to China that no more iron ore would be shipped because of diesel shortages unless Beijing supplied refined fuels.
China gets about two-thirds of its iron ore imports from Australia and without these flows its steel industry would be severely curtailed, which in ŌüĀturn would devastate manufacturing ŌĆŗand construction.
Getting to this point would only happen if the Strait of Hormuz remains largely closed and countries follow narrow and short-term self-interest.
The problem is that political leaders ŌĆŗso far seem unable to grasp the extent of the stress they are putting on the energy supply system, and their actions suggest they see only their own country's needs without realising that it is a global system that needs global solutions.

ROI delivers thought-provoking, ŌĆŗdata-driven analysis of everything from swap rates to soybeans. Markets are moving faster than ever. ROI can help you keep up. Follow ROI on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
The views expressed here are those of the author, a columnist for Reuters.

item.title: The Week in Breakingviews: WhatŌĆÖs next for Iran?
item.description:
LONDON, March 8 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Welcome back! A week ago I woke up to find out that the United States and Israel were attacking Iran . ItŌĆÖs the second time this year that President Donald Trump has targeted another countryŌĆÖs leader ŌĆō on a Saturday. If this newsletter was forwarded to you, sign up here to get it in your inbox every weekend.
ŌĆ£In SienaŌĆÖs brutal Palio horse race, a steed can ŌĆŗstill win after losing its rider. Banca Monte dei Paschi di Siena (BMPS.MI) , opens new tab , headquartered in the same picturesque Tuscan town, is applying that tradition to hostile bank M&A.ŌĆØ
Read more here: ItalyŌĆÖs MPS adds governance risk to dicey ŌĆīM&A .
ŌĆ£No operational plan can extend with any degree of certainty beyond the initial encounter with the main enemy force.ŌĆØ Field Marshal Helmuth von MoltkeŌĆÖs advice , opens new tab to military ŌĆŗstrategists is also a warning for anyone drawing lasting conclusions from the first days of a war. A week after the United States and Israel attacked Iran and killed its Supreme Leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, it would be ŌĆŗfoolish to predict the outcome of the conflict, let alone its long-term repercussions. Instead, here are the five most pressing questions:
This key question is the hardest ŌüĀto answer. Many strategists expect it to be short, in part because the U.S. bombing campaign means Iran will eventually run out of missiles and launching sites it needs to retaliate. With tepid public support at home and the price of petrol rising, ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump may also want to avoid a drawn-out battle. But on Friday he demanded ŌĆ£ unconditional surrender ŌĆØ. Financial markets are beginning to price in a longer conflict: the price of a barrel of oil ended the week above $90. Yet while the spot ŌĆŗprice of gas in Europe has jumped, contracts for delivery in a few years have barely budged.
Trump could declare ŌĆ£mission accomplishedŌĆØ and attempt to move on, as his predecessor George W. Bush did six weeks after the invasion of Iraq in 2003. But that historical precedent reminds us that an end to fighting depends on what happens on the ground. As George Hay argues , there are three broad scenarios for Iran: the current government clings on to power under a new leader; the country splits along factional or ethnic lines; or the population rises up ŌĆŗand takes power. Only the third scenario is consistent with more enduring calm. DonŌĆÖt forget that itŌĆÖs less than nine months since Trump claimed to have destroyed IranŌĆÖs nuclear program.
Blocking the Strait of Hormuz was long seen ŌĆŗas a move Iran would only make in desperation. About a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil and gas passes through the narrow waterway. Yet the outbreak of hostilities has produced two extraordinary outcomes. First, tanker traffic has stopped largely on the strength of Iranian threats : anxious shipping ŌĆīcompanies and jittery ŌüĀinsurers did the rest. Second, the blockage has not yet led to the $200-a-barrel oil price traders once predicted. Markets may expect a swift reopening, but as Yawen Chen points out , this is not entirely in TrumpŌĆÖs gift.
The United Arab Emirates, Qatar, and their neighbours have presented themselves as hyper-modern and impartial finance and technology hubs. They have attracted banks, tax exiles and tourists, while making splashy investments around the world. Iranian missiles and drones flying over gleaming towers shattered that glitzy image. Even though most of the projectiles have been shot down , George Hay and Afiq Fitri Alias make the case that the blow to the GulfŌĆÖs regional reputation is likely to last ŌĆō and may benefit Saudi Arabia. Meanwhile, extra spending on domestic defence ŌĆŗmeans less cash for overseas investments.
The president revels in his unpredictability. After abducting Venezuelan ŌüĀPresident Nicolas Maduro and killing Khamenei he may feel emboldened to settle other scores. On Thursday, he hinted at intervention in Cuba as ŌĆ£just a question of timeŌĆØ. Yet overseas military adventures clash with his domestic agenda. Meanwhile, increased military spending, higher interest rates and missing tariff revenue are increasing the risks of a sovereign debt spiral, Gabriel Rubin warns . Success in Iran could backfire ŌĆŗat home.
Every financial meltdown brings obscure acronyms to the fore. The Enron scandal taught us about special purpose entities (SPEs). In 2008 collateralised debt obligations (CDOs) were on everybodyŌĆÖs ŌĆŗlips. Today, BDCs are finding a ŌüĀwider audience. Business development companies are vehicles that allow asset managers to accumulate funds without a set expiration date. Now theyŌĆÖre under stress. Jonathan Guilford explains whatŌĆÖs going on .
If youŌĆÖre looking for something a bit more optimistic, how about a fresh perspective on Africa? ItŌĆÖs easy to be gloomy about its prospects. But Joe Studwell, author of ŌĆ£How Africa WorksŌĆØ, offers a glass-half-full perspective about the vast continent. He joined me on The Big View , opens new tab to discuss his research and conclusions.
Over on the Viewsroom , opens new tab , George Hay and Yawen Chen joined Aimee Donnellan ŌüĀand Jonathan Guilford ŌĆŗto provide a download on the fast-moving Iran conflict, and the fallout for energy markets.
For years, Goldman Sachs (GS.N) , opens new tab hid its inner workings behind a ŌĆŗthick veil of discretion. But the firm has changed, and this week Lloyd Blankfein published ŌĆ£Streetwise: Getting to and Through Goldman SachsŌĆØ, an amusing and at times brutally frank account of his career at the bank he led between 2006 and 2018. It makes for a highly readable book ŌĆō and also reminds us ŌĆŗthat Goldman is now a bit less special .
Want to receive The Week in Breakingviews in your inbox every Saturday? Sign up for the newsletter here .
Follow Peter Thal Larsen on Bluesky , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: Japan should use nuclear plants to offset Iran crisis, opposition party head says
item.description:
TOKYO, March 9 (Reuters) - Japan should operate all of its nuclear power plants available to offset the impact of the ŌĆŗIran war on electricity bills, Yuichiro Tamaki, the ŌĆīleader of a Japanese opposition party, said on Monday.
Japan relies on the Middle East for around 95% of its oil supplies ŌĆŗand 11% of its liquefied natural gas imports ŌĆŗwith around 70% and 6% respectively coming via ŌüĀthe Strait of Hormuz, which is effectively closed due ŌĆŗto the war.
"Unless we make full use of nuclear power ŌĆŗas a carbon-free power source with less dependence on overseas sources, electricity bills will inevitably rise," Tamaki, who heads the Democratic ŌĆŗParty for the People, said in a statement on ŌĆŗX.
Oil prices surged about 20% on Monday, hitting their highest since ŌĆīJuly ŌüĀ2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran fuelled fears of tighter supply.
Japan shut down all of its 54 nuclear power reactors - which provided roughly 30% of the ŌĆŗcountry's electricity - in ŌĆŗthe wake ŌüĀof the 2011 Fukushima Daiichi plant nuclear meltdowns. Thirty-three remain operable but only 15 ŌĆŗhave been restarted.
"A policy shift is needed ŌĆŗto protect ŌüĀthe lives of the people," said Tamaki, who has consistently supported restarting and expanding nuclear power in Japan.
New Prime ŌüĀMinister ŌĆŗSanae Takaichi is also staunchly ŌĆŗpro-nuclear and Japan's public is also gradually becoming more supportive of nuclear power.

item.title: VIEW Oil surges 20% as Iran war fuels supply fears
item.description:
SINGAPORE, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices surged about 20% in early trade on Monday, hitting their highest since July 2022, as the expanding U.S.-Israeli war with Iran led some major Middle East oil producers to cut supplies and on concerns of ŌĆŗprolonged disruption to shipping through the Strait of Hormuz.
COMMENTARY:
"I think prices have ŌĆīrallied this morning on the reports that Middle East producers are now reducing output due to storage facilities filling up fast."
"I certainly think the spectre now of Middle Eastern producers curtailing output is going to keep those prices elevated. The next flag will be whether it eventually reaches a ŌĆŗpoint where they have to start shutting in oil wells, which not only further impacts output but also delays a ŌĆŗresponse once the conflict eases. That would potentially sustain those prices for much longer."
"A sudden supply shock reverberates well beyond just who is a net energy exporter and ŌĆŗimporter. There are acute supply chain effects beyond how just price eats into margins. Even for a place like Indonesia (where) it is ŌĆŗnot unusual to see street protests if pump prices go up."
"Asia takes the brunt of the sharp escalation in oil prices and there are few places to run and hide. The dollar has to be the one outperforming, given Japan and Korea's exposures here and the sharp pain that ŌĆŗcan be expected from Brent at $107."
"The market had been complacent about the scope ŌĆŗand duration of the war and associated supply disruptions until last Friday.
"Its a case of the oil market who cried wolf. After three years ŌĆīof geopolitical ŌüĀrisk premia rising only to fail to translate to supply disruptions, the market became complacent about the current events.
"But this existential Iran war is the energy crisis scenario that has been wargamed for 50 years, finally coming to fore."
"Our baseline scenario is the conflict in Iran will be large but short-lived, though there is a clear risk of a prolonged ŌĆŗwar. Among emerging markets, the ŌĆŗeconomic impact will be felt ŌüĀmost clearly in the Gulf Cooperation Council, reflecting the shock's adverse impact on trade, logistics, tourism and investment."
"First, Pakistan and India are most vulnerable as they are energy importers with relatively high exposure ŌĆŗto the Strait of Hormuz. Second, outside of physical trade disruption, Egypt and Turkiye are ŌĆŗmost exposed. This ŌüĀis due to their high energy import bill, fragile external positions, large energy subsidies and unanchored inflation. Third, commodity producing economies in Sub-Saharan Africa and Latin America are least at risk, including Nigeria, Ghana and Peru."
"The threats to attack refineries ŌüĀare ŌĆŗvery concerning. Because it threatens the worst of all economic worlds. Cutting off ŌĆŗ15%-20% of the world's oil supply not only slows down every economy globally, but it introduces an inflation impulse. And inflation plus slowing growth is stagflation, and ŌĆŗthat's an economic disaster."

item.title: Australians reach for VPNs, find porn sites blocked as online age-restrictions take effect
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Australians ŌĆŗwere downloading virtual private networks (VPNs) in droves, while one of the world's largest pornography distributors said ŌĆīit was blocking users as the country rolled out sweeping online age restrictions on Monday.
Last December, Australia became the first country to impose a nationwide ban on teenagers using social media. A separate law now requires AI-powered chatbot services to keep certain content - including ŌĆŗpornography, extreme violence and self-harm and eating disorder material - from minors or face fines of up to ŌĆŗA$49.5 million ($34.5 million).
The country also joined Britain, France and dozens of U.S. states requiring ŌüĀwebsites which disseminate pornography to verify users are over 18. App stores must also run age checks before ŌĆŗallowing downloads of software labelled 18+.
The country's eSafety Commissioner Julie Inman Grant said the measures aimed to afford children ŌĆŗthe same protection online as the world expected offline.
"A child today can't walk into a bar and order a drink, they can't stroll into a strip club or browse an adult shop or sit down at a blackjack table in a casino," she told ŌĆŗthe Australian Broadcasting Corp.
"This just really brings ... those protections that we put for kids in place to the ŌĆŗdigital realm."
Three of the 15 most downloaded free smartphone apps on Monday were VPNs, a chart published by iPhone maker Apple (AAPL.O) , opens new tab ŌĆīshowed. The ŌüĀmost downloaded VPN, called VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy, ranked ahead of any social media platform, the chart showed.
VPN - Super Unlimited Proxy did not immediately respond to a Reuters request for comment. All internet-connected devices carry an individual code which discloses their location, and VPNs hide the user's location by assigning a new code to the device.
Canada-based Aylo, ŌĆŗowner of a large network ŌĆŗof pornography websites, meanwhile ŌüĀblocked Australians from accessing the platforms RedTube and YouPorn, while presenting a version of Pornhub without explicit content.
All the websites carried a banner saying it was "not currently accepting ŌĆŗnew account registrations in your region".
Aylo did not respond to a Reuters request for ŌĆŗcomment. The Sydney ŌüĀMorning Herald quoted an Aylo spokesperson saying the company's Australian users would be "presented with a safe-for-work experience when they view our platforms".
Tom Sulston, deputy chair of advocacy group Digital Rights Watch, said it was unsurprising that people were reaching ŌüĀfor VPNs.
"My ŌĆŗhope is that, not only will they discover that this works ŌĆŗfor looking at spicier internet sites, but that it's just generally a good idea to use VPNs when you're traversing the internet, because they ŌĆŗdo offer you some privacy protections."
($1 = 1.4351 Australian dollars)

item.title: Shots fired at Rihanna's house in Beverly Hills, no injuries reported, media say
item.description:
March 8 (Reuters) - A woman fired numerous shots into the Beverly ŌĆŗHills home of pop music ŌĆīstar Rihanna on Sunday, and a round went through a wall ŌĆŗof the house, local news ŌĆŗoutlets reported Sunday.
The Los Angeles Times ŌüĀand NBC4, citing a ŌĆŗLos Angeles Police spokesperson, reported ŌĆŗthat authorities responded to the shooting at 1:21 p.m. (2021 GMT) Sunday and detained ŌĆŗa 30-year-old female suspect.
Los ŌĆŗAngeles police and a representative for Rihanna did ŌĆīnot ŌüĀimmediately respond to Reuters requests for comment.
Rihanna was at home but no injuries were ŌĆŗreported, the ŌĆŗLos ŌüĀAngeles Times said, citing a source. The ŌĆŗnewspaper cited police radio ŌĆŗtraffic ŌüĀthat "approximately 10 shotsŌĆØ had been fired from a vehicle across ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗstreet from the ŌĆŗpropertyŌĆÖs gate.

item.title: Bangladesh shuts universities early to save power amid energy crisis
item.description:
DHAKA, March 9 (Reuters) - Bangladesh will close all universities from Monday, bringing forward the Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ŌĆŗelectricity and fuel amid a worsening energy crisis linked to ŌĆīthe conflict in the Middle East.
Authorities said the decision applies to all public and private universities across the country, a move that officials said will not only ŌĆŗreduce electricity consumption but also ease traffic congestion, which leads ŌĆŗto fuel wastage.
Officials said university campuses consume large amounts of ŌüĀelectricity for residential halls, classrooms, laboratories and air conditioning, and the ŌĆŗearly closure would help ease pressure on the countryŌĆÖs strained power ŌĆŗsystem.
Government and private schools in Bangladesh are already closed for the Islamic holy month of Ramadan, meaning most educational institutions across the country will now remain shut ŌĆŗduring the period.
The move comes as Bangladesh faces mounting uncertainty over ŌĆŗfuel and gas supplies following disruptions to global energy markets caused by the ongoing ŌĆīconflict ŌüĀin the Middle East.
The country, which relies on imports for 95% of its energy needs, imposed daily limits on Friday on fuel sales after panic buying and stockpiling.
As part of broader austerity measures, the government has also ŌĆŗasked all foreignŌĆæcurriculum schools and ŌĆŗprivate coaching ŌüĀcentres to suspend operations during this period to limit electricity use.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt ŌĆŗoperations at four of its five stateŌĆærun fertiliser ŌĆŗfactories, redirecting ŌüĀavailable gas to power plants to avoid widespread outages.
The country has also bought LNG from the spot market at sharply higher prices while seeking additional ŌüĀcargoes ŌĆŗto bridge supply gaps.
ŌĆ£We are doing everything ŌĆŗwe can to reduce consumption and ensure stability in power, fuel and import supplies,ŌĆØ a ŌĆŗsenior energy ministry official said.

item.title: Ukraine sent drone experts to protect US bases in Jordan, Zelenskiy tells NYT
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Ukraine sent interceptor drones and ŌĆŗa team of ŌĆīdrone experts to protect U.S. military ŌĆŗbases in ŌĆŗJordan, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy ŌüĀsaid in ŌĆŗan interview with The ŌĆŗNew York Times published on Monday.
The U.S. ŌĆŗmade the ŌĆŗrequest for help on Thursday, ŌĆīand ŌüĀthe Ukrainian team left the next day, Zelenskiy ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗadding ŌüĀthat it was expected to ŌĆŗarrive in ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀMiddle East soon, according to ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗreport.

item.title: Indonesia searches for missing after landfill collapse kills four
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Rescuers are still searching for five missing people after a large stack of ŌĆŗgarbage collapsed at Indonesia's biggest landfill ŌĆīsite over the weekend, killing at least four people, an official said on Monday.
The collapse took place ŌĆŗat the Bantargebang landfill on Sunday, located ŌĆŗin the region of Bekasi on the ŌüĀoutskirts of the capital Jakarta, said Desiana ŌĆŗKartika Bahari, head of the local rescue agency.
It ŌĆŗwas likely triggered by the heavy rain that hit the area from Saturday evening, she told Reuters.
"It was ŌĆŗraining all day even from (Saturday) evening and ŌĆŗthe mountain of garbage was unstable," Bahari said.
Some trucks were ŌĆīunloading ŌüĀgarbage when the collapse took place, and there was also a food stall near the site.
"The missing people are the truck drivers and ŌĆŗscavengers," she ŌĆŗsaid, adding ŌüĀthat there there could be more people buried in the landfill.
Four ŌĆŗpeople survived the accident.
More than 200 rescuers, ŌĆŗincluding ŌüĀpolice and military personnel and 17 excavators, have been deployed since Monday morning to search ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗthe missing.
Bantargebang covers around 110 ŌĆŗhectares and receives around 6,500 tons-7,000 tons of garbage per ŌĆŗday.

item.title: Lawyers for Bondi Beach shooting suspect seek gag order to protect family
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 9 (Reuters) - Lawyers for a man accused of carrying out a deadly shooting on Sydney's Bondi Beach sought a court order ŌĆŗon Monday to stop media naming his family, saying their ŌĆīsafety was at risk.
Police allege 24-year-old Naveed Akram opened fire on a Jewish Hanukkah celebration on December 14, killing 15, in Australia's worst mass shooting in decades.
His ŌĆŗfather, Sajid Akram, who police also accuse of carrying out ŌĆŗthe shooting, was shot dead by police at the scene.
Police ŌüĀsay the men were inspired by the Islamic State militant group ŌĆŗto carry out the attack using several high-powered weapons the elder man had legally ŌĆŗacquired.
Lawyers for Naveed Akram on Monday requested a court in Sydney prohibit the publication of the names of his mother, brother and sister, as well as their ŌĆŗhome address and places of work and schooling. Magistrate Greg Grogan ŌĆŗextended an interim suppression order until next month, citing the global media interest in ŌĆīthe ŌüĀcase.
Akram faces a total of 59 charges over the attack, including 15 counts of murder, 40 counts of wounding with intent to murder and a terror offence. He is yet to enter a plea.
The case ŌĆŗis expected to ŌĆŗbriefly return ŌüĀto court next week, before a longer hearing on April 8, where the prosecution will outline its brief ŌĆŗof evidence.
The Bondi attack shocked a country with strict ŌĆŗgun laws ŌüĀand fuelled calls for tougher controls and stronger action against antisemitism.
Australia last month launched a government-backed inquiry into antisemitism and social cohesion in the country ŌüĀwhich ŌĆŗis expected to report its findings by ŌĆŗDecember this year.
The government has already responded by tightening gun laws and introducing new legislation ŌĆŗagainst hate speech.

item.title: Governments' actions in response to oil price surge and the escalating Middle East conflict
item.description:
March 9 - Oil prices have soared while share markets have skidded on fears that the escalating U.S.-Israeli war on ŌĆŗIran will squeeze energy supplies and hamstring industries ŌĆīaround the world.
Following are actions that governments are taking or plan to take to reduce the impact of the war on ŌĆŗtheir economies.
South Korean President ŌĆŗLee Jae Myung said on Monday that authorities ŌüĀwould cap domestic fuel prices for the first time ŌĆŗin nearly 30 years. The country will also look ŌĆŗfor sources of energy beyond supplies shipped via the Strait of Hormuz, and a 100 trillion won ($67 billion) market-stabilisation programme should be ŌĆŗexpanded if needed, he added.
The Japanese government instructed a national oil ŌĆīreserve ŌüĀstorage site to prepare for a possible release of crude, Akira Nagatsuma, a member of the Centrist Reform Alliance opposition party, told Reuters on Sunday.
Details such as ŌĆŗthe timing of ŌĆŗthe release ŌüĀremain unclear, Nagatsuma said.
Vietnam is planning to remove import tariffs ŌĆŗon fuels to ensure supplies amid disruptions, ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀgovernment said, adding that the measure is expected to last until the end of April.
Bangladesh ŌüĀwill close ŌĆŗall universities from Monday, bringing forward ŌĆŗthe Eid al-Fitr holidays as part of emergency measures to conserve ŌĆŗelectricity and fuel.

item.title: How Europe can play a bad hand over Iran
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - How many wake-up calls does Europe need? Donald TrumpŌĆÖs first administration did not do the trick, despite the U.S. presidentŌĆÖs anti-European rhetoric. Nor did RussiaŌĆÖs full-scale invasion of Ukraine four years ago. Nor did TrumpŌĆÖs re-election in 2024. Now in quick succession this year, Washington has pummelled Venezuela, threatened Greenland and attacked Iran.
It is hard to see anything good for Europe from the ŌĆŗUnited States-Israeli attack on the Islamic Republic. It strengthens Russia and weakens Ukraine, threatening EuropeŌĆÖs own security. It undermines the global rule of law, which is valuable for the region given that it is ŌĆīnot a geopolitical power.
The latest Middle Eastern conflict is also threatening EuropeŌĆÖs economy. Much, of course, will depend on how long the conflict goes on ŌĆö in particular whether Iran can keep up a blockade of oil and gas through the Strait of Hormuz. As a hydrocarbon importer, Europe is vulnerable to high energy prices. Its mostly highly indebted governments are not well placed to cushion a blow to either businesses or consumers. Meanwhile, a descent into civil war in Iran, or a further crackdown on internal dissent, could unleash a wave of immigration, with some of its 93 million ŌĆŗcitizens heading towards Europe.
In the short run, there is little the region can do but suffer and squirm. A principled response would have been to condemn TrumpŌĆÖs attack as contrary to international law while at the same time ŌĆŗdenouncing IranŌĆÖs nuclear weapons programme. But Europe is too dependent on U.S. military support to do that, especially given the Russian threat. It is also increasingly dependent on American energy ŌüĀsince its gas purchases from Moscow are down dramatically and now Qatar is offline .
Spain has criticised the attack on Iran. But EuropeŌĆÖs big three ŌĆö Germany, France and Britain ŌĆö have provided half-supportive , opens new tab statements , opens new tab that sit uneasily with their values. Not that that has earned them U.S. ŌĆŗgratitude: Trump lambasted British Prime Minister Keir Starmer as ŌĆ£ not Winston Churchill ŌĆØ.
The lesson is clear. Europe will be helpless in a world of sharks until it gets stronger and more united. The EU itself will need greater integration. Meanwhile, the United Kingdom requires ŌĆŗa bolder partnership with the club it left as a result of Brexit, covering economic and energy security as well as defence.
EuropeŌĆÖs core vulnerability is that it needs U.S. protection because it cannot yet defend itself against Russia on its own. Its biggest worry therefore is that the Iran war is strengthening Moscow by relaxing the grip on the KremlinŌĆÖs financial windpipe.
True, Vladimir Putin has had to sit by idly as the United States has bombed an ally and killed its leader. This is bad for the Russian presidentŌĆÖs prestige. What is more, Tehran will ŌĆŗstruggle to supply Moscow with more attack drones, as it will need any it can produce for itself.
But what Russia gains from higher oil and gas prices probably more than compensates for this loss. At the start of the year Moscow ŌĆŗwas feeling the squeeze from tighter sanctions and lower crude prices. Trump was strong-arming India, previously RussiaŌĆÖs largest customer after China, to stop buying its oil. Moscow had to offer purchasers deep discounts on the global price while some cargoes were piling up on the high ŌĆīseas unsold. ŌüĀThe governmentŌĆÖs oil and gas revenue halved to $5.1 billion in January compared to the same month last year.
These dynamics are going into reverse. Brent crude prices are up 50% to around $90 a barrel since the start of the year, before the drumbeats of the Middle East war started in earnest. European gas prices have roughly doubled. With rival Gulf suppliers unable to export, Russia has bargaining power. Indian refiners have started buying its crude again, paying a $4-$5 a barrel premium over Brent on a delivered basis compared to a $13 discount for cargoes traded last month. The KremlinŌĆÖs war chest could soon fill up.
What is more, Ukraine will struggle to get more of the anti-missile defences it desperately needs from the United States, as Washington will keep these for itself, Israel ŌĆŗand its Gulf allies. This will make UkraineŌĆÖs infrastructure vulnerable ŌĆŗto Russian attack. Not that Kyiv is totally without ŌüĀcards. It has developed cheap anti-drone defences that could be of use to Washington and Gulf states ŌĆö and may be able to trade some of these for sophisticated weapons it does not produce itself.
Even so, the overall balance has swung in MoscowŌĆÖs favour. And that is bad for Europe.
To be fair, Europe increasingly realises that it will only be able to advance its ŌĆŗvalues and interests if it increases its autonomy. For example, France last week set out a plan to boost its nuclear arsenal and involve European allies in war games with ŌĆŗits atomic weapons. Meanwhile, the EU ŌüĀunveiled proposals to work with trusted partners to boost investment in strategic economic sectors to cut dependence on the likes of China.
The EU is also making a renewed attempt to boost its competitiveness by breaking down barriers in services industries and unifying its capital markets. It seems increasingly determined to move ahead even if some of its 27 members disagree. Great Britain is also getting closer to the bloc, albeit with insufficient ambition or urgency.
But it will take a long time for these initiatives to bear fruit. In the meantime, ŌüĀmany Europeans will be ŌĆŗin two minds about how they want TrumpŌĆÖs new war to pan out. If the U.S president triumphs he may feel emboldened to bully ŌĆŗEurope ŌĆö perhaps turning his attention back to Greenland, which is part of Denmark, an EU member. On the other hand, if the United States gets embroiled in a quagmire in Iran, the big winner may be China ŌĆö and Europe will not be happy if the PeopleŌĆÖs Republic throws its weight around more.
All ŌĆŗthe more reason to heed the wake-up call and make itself stronger and more unified.
Follow @Hugodixon , opens new tab on X

item.title: New Delhi has weak hand in bank deal frenzy
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - A dealmaking boom in India's banking sector has an unlikely loser: the government. Canadian insurance holding firm Fairfax Financial (FFH.TO) , opens new tab leads , opens new tab the race to buy a ŌĆŗ61% stake from Indian state entities in $13 billion IDBI Bank (IDBI.NS) , opens new tab , Bloomberg reported in February, citing sources. An $8 ŌĆībillion transaction would be the largest-ever foreign direct investment in a local bank. But crystallising a premium valuation looks challenging.
A deal would complete a full circle for the lender hardest hit by an asset quality crisis: in 2018, bad loans comprised nearly one-third of its portfolio. Provisions for that ŌĆŗsour pool eroded its capital base and prompted New Delhi, which then owned 86% of IDBI, to press state-backed ŌĆŗLife Insurance Corporation (LIFI.NS) , opens new tab to pump in 216 billion , opens new tab rupees, or $2.4 billion at current rates, to raise its 8% ŌüĀstake to 51% in 2019.
LIC now holds 49% of IDBI's shares and the government owns 45%. Selling a 30% stake ŌĆŗto Fairfax at the latest market price would fetch the insurer a 136% return on its 2019 investment. New Delhi would be ŌĆŗworse off, though: the lender's shares trade lower than they did 13 years ago.
Yet even current multiples may be difficult to fetch. IDBI's shares are trading at about 2 times forward book value, almost twice that of similar-sized rivals Yes Bank (YESB.NS) , opens new tab and IDFC First Bank (IDFB.NS) , opens new tab . Throwing in employee liabilities, restructuring ŌĆŗcosts and the likely absence of indemnity clauses gives the buyer a strong case for a discount.
An abundance of takeover targets ŌĆŗhas hurt New Delhi, too. Launched in 2022, the slow-moving sale process of IDBI prompted early potential bidders to look elsewhere: last year ŌĆīSumitomo Mitsui ŌüĀBanking Corporation (8316.T) , opens new tab bought a 24% stake in Yes Bank.
With Emirates NBD (ENBD.DU) , opens new tab still in the reckoning with Fairfax, it's a two-horse race to own IDBI. Both bidders already have a foothold in India's credit market: the Dubai-headquartered lender is set to take control of the $2 billion RBL Bank (RATB.NS) , opens new tab and Fairfax owns $675 million CSB Bank (CSBB.NS) , opens new tab .
That chips away at any shred of bargaining power left with the sellers, ŌĆŗwho can hardly demand a ŌĆŗcontrol premium. Regulations cap voting ŌüĀrights of private bank shareholders at 26%. That puts the new owner effectively at par on voting decisions with LIC and the government, which will hold a combined 34% after the sale. ŌĆŗTo maximise takings, officials could ask the central bank to relax the voting rule. The ŌĆŗother option is ŌüĀto reduce their total stake to well below 26%.
Otherwise, New Delhi risks catching the weak end of India's banking M&A wave.
Follow Shritama Bose on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: G7 to discuss joint release of emergency oil reserves, FT reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - The ŌĆŗGroup of Seven (G7) finance ministers will ŌĆīdiscuss on Monday a joint release of oil from emergency reserves coordinated by ŌĆŗthe International Energy Agency, the ŌĆŗFinancial Times reported.
Three G7 countries, including ŌüĀthe U.S., have so far ŌĆŗexpressed support for the idea, the ŌĆŗFT said citing sources, and added that the ministers and the IEA Executive Director ŌĆŗFatih Birol will hold a call ŌĆŗto discuss the impact of the Iran ŌĆīwar.
The ŌüĀreport comes as oil prices surged more than 25% on Monday to their highest levels since mid-2022 as some ŌĆŗmajor producers ŌĆŗcut ŌüĀsupplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the ŌĆŗmarket due to the expanding ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli ŌüĀwar with Iran.
The IEA and the G7 presidency did not respond to ŌüĀrequests ŌĆŗfor comment outside regular ŌĆŗbusiness hours.

item.title: Indian refiners tumble as crude surges on widening Iran war
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Shares of Indian refiners slumped on Monday, as the widening U.S.-Israeli war with Iran propelled ŌĆŗBrent crude prices toward $120 per barrel - a near four-year high - threatening their ŌĆīnear-term earnings.
State-run Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab dipped 6.6%, Hindustan Petroleum (HPCL.NS) , opens new tab slid 7.5% and Bharat Petroleum (BPCL.NS) , opens new tab dropped 7.1%, and were headed for their steepest falls in more than ŌĆŗa year.
Reliance Industries (RELI.NS) , opens new tab lost 2%.
Brent crude soared as ŌĆŗmuch as 26.4% to $117.16 a barrel and was ŌüĀlast up 23% at $114.08 by 9:15 a.m. IST.
UBS said ŌĆŗoil marketing companies are "negatively leveraged" to the crude spike as ŌĆŗthey sell more diesel and gasoline than they produce, estimating sales-to-production ratios at 1:2 for IOC and BPCL and 2:2 for ŌĆŗHPCL.
The brokerage cut IOC and BPCL to "neutral" and BPCL to "sell" from "buy".
The ŌĆŗrout dragged the Nifty oil and gas index (.NIFOILGAS) , opens new tab down 2.7% and the ŌĆīenergy ŌüĀindex (.NIFTYENR) , opens new tab 2.1% lower, while the benchmark Nifty 50 (.NSEI) , opens new tab slid 2.8%. The oil and gas index has fallen 6.6% since the U.S.-Israeli strike on Iran on February 27.
Citi warned that refiners' ŌĆŗearnings will hinge ŌĆŗon how long the ŌüĀgeopolitical shock lasts, flagging risks from a potential closure of the Strait of Hormuz and ŌĆŗany shutdown of Qatar's LNG output - each supplying ŌĆŗabout ŌüĀhalf of India's crude and LNG imports.
Any disruption beyond the one month currently priced in could sharply tighten LNG ŌüĀmarkets, ŌĆŗwith low European storage for October ŌĆŗ2026 raising the risk of "non-linear" price spikes, the brokerage added.

item.title: Azerbaijan reopens Iran border to cargo traffic after drone incident, TASS reports
item.description:
BAKU, March 9 (Reuters) - Azerbaijan said on Monday it ŌĆŗhad reopened its border ŌĆīcrossings with Iran for all cargo traffic, the ŌĆŗRussian state-run TASS ŌĆŗnews agency reported.
The border ŌüĀcrossings, one of the ŌĆŗshortest land routes ŌĆŗconnecting Iran to its ally Russia, were closed last week ŌĆŗafter what Baku ŌĆŗdescribed as an Iranian drone attack ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe Nakhchivan exclave.
Late on Sunday, Iranian President Masoud Pezeshkian phoned his ŌĆŗAzerbaijani ŌĆŗcounterpart ŌüĀIlham Aliyev, telling him Iran had ŌĆŗnot been involved ŌĆŗin ŌüĀthe Nakhchivan incident and that Tehran was ŌüĀinvestigating, Aliyev's ŌĆŗoffice said.

item.title: Khamenei's hardline son Mojtaba appointed as Iran's new leader
item.description:
DUBAI/JERUSALEM, March 9 (Reuters) - Iran on Monday named Mojtaba Khamenei to succeed his father Ali Khamenei as supreme leader , signalling that hardliners remain firmly in charge, while the week-old ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli war with Iran sent oil prices surging and Asian stock markets into a nosedive.
Iranian institutions and politicians, from the foreign ministry to lawmakers, issued statements expressing their allegiance to the country's new supreme leader ŌĆīas the war entered its 10th day and fresh missile and drone strikes reverberated across the Middle East.
"We will obey the commander-in-chief until the last drop of our blood," a statement from the defence council said.
Senior cleric Ayatollah Sadeq Amoli-Larijani said Mojtaba Khamenei's appointment was "a balm for the spiritual suffering of our people and an emphasis on the need to continue the luminous path of the late Imam (Khamenei senior)."
Mojtaba's father, Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei, was killed in one of the first strikes launched against Iran more than a week ago.
The show of solidarity for Mojtaba comes ŌĆŗafter U.S. President Donald Trump earlier rejected him as a candidate to be Iran's new supreme leader, and Israel saying it would target whoever leads Iran.
Mojtaba, a cleric with influence inside Iran's security forces and vast ŌĆŗbusiness networks under his father, had been viewed as a frontrunner in the lead-up to Sunday's vote by the Assembly of Experts, a body of 88 clerics tasked with choosing ŌüĀAli Khamenei's successor.
The position gives Mojtaba the final say in all matters of state in the Islamic Republic.
Trump said on Sunday that Washington should have a say in the selection. "If he doesn't get approval from us, he's not going to ŌĆŗlast long," he told ABC News, adding that ending the war would be a "mutual" decision with Israeli Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu.
In an interview with the Times of Israel after the new supreme leader was named, Trump declined to respond, saying only "We'll see ŌĆŗwhat happens," according to the newspaper.
Pope Leo on Sunday warned of "a tragedy of enormous proportions" due to the "widespread climate of hatred and fearŌĆØ in Iran and across the region.
"Let us raise our humble prayer to the Lord that the roar of bombs may cease, that weapons may fall silent, and that space may be opened for dialogue in which the voices of peoples can be heard", Leo said at the Angelus prayer in St. PeterŌĆÖs Square.
The Israeli military said on Monday it had begun a wave of attacks in central Iran ŌĆŗand had struck infrastructure belonging to Hezbollah militants in Beirut.
Iran and its proxies appeared to have launched attacks too, with rocket and drone strikes targeting a U.S. diplomatic facility near Baghdad's international airport that were intercepted by C-RAM defence system, ŌĆŗsaid police sources.
A drone strike targeted a U.S. military base near Erbil airport in Iraqi Kurdistan, security sources said, while Saudi authorities reported intercepting a drone east of its northern Jawf region.
