Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox ŌĆŗevery weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Kuwait mourns two officials killed as Iran war rages

March 9 (Reuters) - Kuwait held a military funeral on Monday for two interior ministry officials ŌĆŗit said were killed "while performing their ŌĆīduty".
The state - which hosts U.S. military installations and has come under Iranian drone and missile fire ŌĆŗduring Tehran's war with Israel and ŌĆŗthe U.S. - said the two men died ŌüĀearly on Sunday, but did not ŌĆŗgo into further details.
Kuwait's army separately said ŌĆŗareas near the international airport were hit on Sunday and its forces had intercepted missiles and ŌĆŗdrones.
The Interior Ministry said on Sunday ŌĆŗit mourned "the martyrs of duty, Lieutenant Colonel Staff Abdullah ŌĆīEmad ŌüĀAl-Sharrah and Major Fahad Abdulaziz Al-Mejmed, from the General Administration of Land Border Security, who were martyred at dawn".
The funeral ŌĆŗin Sulaibikhat ŌĆŗCemetery was ŌüĀled by First Deputy Prime Minister and Interior Minister Sheikh ŌĆŗFahad Al-Yousef Al-Sabah, along with other ŌĆŗsenior ŌüĀfigures.

item.title: Turkey says second Iranian ballistic missile shot down by NATO defences in airspace
item.description:
ANKARA, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Monday that NATO ŌĆīair defences shot down a second ballistic missile that was fired from Iran and had entered Turkish airspace, warning it will take any necessary steps against threats.
This marks the second Iranian ballistic missile in the last week that has targeted the south of Turkey, ŌĆŗwhich is a NATO member and Iran's neighbour. Ankara had warned Iran against attacking again on Saturday, but has ŌĆŗnot suggested it wants to formally call on alliance members for further protection.
"We once ŌüĀagain emphasise that all necessary measures will be taken decisively and without hesitation against any threat directed at ŌĆŗour country's territory and airspace," the Turkish Defence Ministry said, adding some ammunition parts had fallen in the ŌĆŗsoutheastern province of Gaziantep but there had been no casualties.
"We also reiterate that it is in everyone's interest to heed Turkey's warnings in this regard," it said.
It was unclear where the missile was headed before it was intercepted by NATO defences stationed in ŌĆŗthe eastern Mediterranean Sea.
U.S. air forces are stationed at the Incirlik base in southern Turkey, and there is ŌĆŗa NATO radar base in Malatya province to the northeast that provides vital protection for the alliance. Ankara said fragments ŌĆīfrom the ŌüĀmissile fell in empty fields in Gaziantep, which sits roughly between the two.
Burhanettin Duran, President Tayyip Erdogan's communications director, said Ankara was strongly reiterating its warning to all parties, namely Iran, to avoid steps that endanger regional stability and civilians.
Ankara says that Washington has not used Incirlik in its air assault, alongside Israel, on Iran, ŌĆŗwhich triggered Tehran's missile and ŌĆŗdrone attacks.
Iran did not ŌüĀimmediately comment on the incident, but it has said repeatedly that it is not at war with regional countries and is not explicitly targeting Turkey.
Turkey, which had sought to ŌĆŗmediate U.S.-Iran talks before the air war that began last week, has previously ŌĆŗsaid it had ŌüĀno intention currently of invoking NATO's Article 4 that would call allies to consult if a member is threatened.
That could lead to invoking the alliance's Article 5, which would call NATO to defend its attacked ally.
Turkey has the trans-Atlantic alliances' second-largest ŌüĀarmy ŌĆŗand has become a leading global actor in the defence industry ŌĆŗin recent years. However, it still lacks fulsome air defence of its own despite efforts to develop them. It has relied on NATO's ŌĆŗair defences in both incidents in the last week.

item.title: Serbia suspends energy exports to shield market as crude prices surge on Iran war
item.description:
BELGRADE, March 9 (Reuters) - Serbia has suspended crude oil and fuel ŌĆīproduct exports to safeguard its market from shortages and price spikes, the energy minister said on Monday, as global crude prices jump above $119 a barrel amid supply ŌĆŗuncertainty due to the war in Iran .
The suspension, effective until March 19, applies ŌĆŗto the export of diesel, gasoline and crude oil by ŌüĀall modes of transport, Energy Minister Dubravka Djedovic Handanovic said in ŌĆŗa statement.
Crude oil price on Monday reached its highest since mid-2022 of above $119 ŌĆŗa barrel, due to uncertainty over global supplies amid the war in Iran.
"The essence of this (temporary) ban is to protect the domestic market from shortages and price spikes ŌĆŗdue to global disruptions in the world market,ŌĆØ she said.
The government ŌĆŗin neighboring Croatia on Monday introduced fuel price caps until March 23 to counter effects ŌĆīof ŌüĀsurging crude oil prices.
Croatian Prime Minister Andrej Plenkovic said that without caps, the price of diesel could rise by 16 percentage points to 1.72 euros per litre. The government cap limited it at 1.55 euros/litre.
Croatia last July ŌĆŗabolished fuel price ŌĆŗcaps introduced in ŌüĀ2022 when the war in Ukraine threatened global energy supplies.
Serbia has kept a price cap on fuel since ŌĆŗFebruary 2022. The government controls the maximum retail prices ŌĆŗfor Euro ŌüĀdiesel and Euro Premium BMB 95 gasoline to mitigate the impact of global price surges on the local economy.
Serbia's fuel exports in 2024, mainly to ŌüĀneighboring countries, ŌĆŗaccounted for around 3.6% of all exports, ŌĆŗor $1.26 billion. In 2025, they sharply fell due to U.S. sanctions against the country's only ŌĆŗoil refiner, Russian-owned NIS .

item.title: Helsinki energy group plans large investment in small nuclear reactors
item.description:
HELSINKI, March 9 (Reuters) - The city of Helsinki's energy utility Helen plans to invest between 1 billion and 5 billion euros ($1.16 billion-$5.78 billion) in Small Modular Reactor ŌĆŗcapacity in the coming years, the head of its newly-established nuclear subsidiary ŌĆītold Reuters.
Helsinki is not alone in eyeing new nuclear generation, as countries ranging from the United States to South Korea , or companies such as Meta (META.O) , opens new tab turn to SMRs to cater for the rapid rise in ŌĆŗpower demand from artificial intelligence and electrification.
SMRs are advanced reactors that can ŌĆŗbe factory-assembled and transported for installation elsewhere. They have a power ŌüĀgenerating capacity of up to 300 megawatts, about a third that of traditional reactors.
Finland, ŌĆŗtoo, expects its power demand to increase. But it also needs more steady base ŌĆŗload following a rapid rise in wind power production, which has rewarded Finns with some of Europe's lowest power prices on average, but also resulted in high price volatility during consumption peaks.
For ŌĆŗHelen, which shut down Finland's last coal-fired power plant last year , nuclear is a way ŌĆŗto add capacity while further reducing CO2 emissions, Helen Nuclear Ltd's CEO Pekka Tolonen said.
"Nuclear power ŌĆīis ŌüĀnow the most promising and, among current options, the best way for us to achieve a future without combustion," he told Reuters. Helen is reviewing half a dozen potential Western SMR suppliers, he added.
"The value of the planned investment would be in ŌĆŗthe low single billions, ŌĆŗmeaning 1 to ŌüĀ5 billion euros," he said. The amount would be in the lower end if Helen went for nuclear heat production only, ŌĆŗand in the upper end of the range if it opted ŌĆŗfor combined ŌüĀheat and power production.
Helen, which has to meet heat consumption peaks during cold winter days, believes power consumption in the capital region will double in a few years' time, ŌüĀdue ŌĆŗto electrification, data centres and expansion of clean tech, ŌĆŗincluding green hydrogen.
Last month, the company initiated a land use planning process in Helsinki and expects to pick ŌĆŗa site by late 2027, Tolonen said.
($1 = 0.8649 euros)

item.title: Exclusive: Trump's Gaza plan on hold as Iran war pauses disarmament talks
item.description:
JERUSALEM/CAIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Talks to advance President Donald Trump's plan to end the Gaza war have been on hold since last week when the U.S. and ŌĆŗIsrael jointly attacked Iran, sparking a broader Middle East war , three sources with direct knowledge of the negotiations said.
The pause threatens to stall implementation of Trump's flagship Middle ŌĆīEast peace initiative, which he has cast as a major foreign policy objective. It comes less than a month after he secured billions of dollars in pledges for Gaza from Gulf Arab states - countries that are now facing Iranian attacks as the conflict widens.
Trump's Gaza plan has hinged in part on whether Hamas militants would lay down their arms in exchange for amnesty, a step intended to pave the way for reconstruction and further Israeli military withdrawals. ŌĆŗWhite House mediators have been backchanneling between Israel and Hamas on the disarmament question.
Negotiations on this and other issues were paused when the Iran war began on February 28, ŌĆŗthe three sources said, speaking on condition of anonymity to discuss sensitive talks. The pause in disarmament talks has not been previously reported.
Zaha Hassan, ŌüĀof the Washington-based Carnegie Endowment for International Peace, said countries including the United Arab Emirates and Qatar which pledged funds for Trump's Board of Peace mission may be questioning if this is "really ŌĆŗmoney well spent now that they are dodging rocket fire."
One of the sources, who has direct knowledge of work by Trump's Board of Peace mission, described ŌĆŗthe pause as a brief, minor delay caused by flight disruptions preventing mediators and representatives from travelling around the region. Talks have frequently been held in Cairo.
Over the longer term, the Board of Peace believes the war could accelerate a resolution of the disarmament issue by removing Iranian influence, which has long supported Hamas financially, the source said.
Another source ŌĆö a Palestinian official close to the mediation effort ŌĆö said Hamas had been expected to hold talks ŌĆŗwith Egyptian, Qatari and Turkish mediators on the day the war erupted, but the meeting was scrapped and no new date has been set.
A Hamas official confirmed that talks on Trump's Gaza ŌĆŗplan had been frozen for now, but declined to elaborate. Israel's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment. The White House did not immediately provide comment.
Israel's military has eased off strikes in Gaza ŌĆīsince the ŌüĀstart of the war but, citing Hamas threats, has not ceased attacks, as Israeli jets carry out bombing campaigns in Iran and Lebanon. At least 16 Palestinians have been killed in Gaza since February 28, health officials say.
"The moment the war on Iran is over, (Israel) will come back at us with the same frequency, with the same violence," said Talal Hamouda, 46, who lives in Jabalia in the northern Gaza Strip, with his wife and five children.
Hamas, for its part, has continued to reassert its grip in areas under its control in Gaza since the war began. Sources close to ŌĆŗthe group say Hamas fighters in recent days ŌĆŗambushed several Israeli-backed militia members in Gaza's north ŌüĀand south, killing at least two people.
Trump's plan for Gaza began with an October ceasefire that left Israel controlling more than half the territory, with Hamas controlling the rest. The initiative appeared to gain momentum in the month leading up to ŌĆŗthe war, including the reopening of Gaza's border crossing with Egypt and new pledges for reconstruction.
Much of Washington's multinational coordination on Gaza policy ŌĆŗhas been run out ŌüĀof a U.S.-led military compound in southern Israel. Foreign diplomats posted there said momentum behind the plan appeared to stall as the Iran war escalated.
Three diplomats said the Civil Military Coordination Centre scaled back to minimal operations when the war started, and that there were concerns it could be targeted by Iranian missiles.
The diplomats said senior U.S. officials now appear focused on the Iran war, leaving Gaza with limited top-level attention. ŌüĀStill, working-level ŌĆŗdiscussions among countries have continued in the hope that the plan could move forward once the war ends.
Natan Sachs, a ŌĆŗsenior fellow at the Washington-based Middle East Institute, said that "only sustained attention from the Trump administration can keep the plan on track ŌĆö and the war with Iran has the potential to undermine exactly that."
"Without it, the divergent aims of the ŌĆŗtwo warring parties could easily lead to very different outcomes, and potentially to a resumption of fighting."

item.title: Turkey's Halkbank, U.S. Justice Department in deferred prosecution agreement, judge says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Turkish state-run lender Halkbank (HALKB.IS) , opens new tab and the U.S. Justice Department have reached a deferred prosecution agreement, the judge overseeing the long-running U.S. criminal case against the bank said in a ŌĆŗcourt filing on Monday, without providing further details of the deal.
In the case, prosecutors had accused ŌĆīof fraud, money laundering and conspiracy for allegedly helping Iran evade American economic sanctions.
Halkbank's case has long been a thorn in U.S.-Turkey relations, with Turkish President Tayyip Erdogan calling it an "unlawful, ugly" step.
The agreement was announced after the U.S. Supreme Court in October 2025 let ŌĆŗstand a federal appeals court decision that let the prosecution proceed.
U.S. prosecutors originally charged Halkbank in ŌĆŗ2019, during President Donald Trump's first White House term.
Erdogan met with Trump in September, and expressed ŌüĀhope the following month that the meeting would resolve various issues between the countries.
The Turkish president said Trump ŌĆŗtold him at the White House and in a subsequent phone call that "the Halkbank problem is finished for us."
Halkbank was accused of using money servicers and front companies in Iran, Turkey and the United Arab Emirates in its sanctions-evasion scheme .
U.S. prosecutors said the bank secretly transferred $20 billion of restricted funds, converted oil revenue into gold and cash to benefit Iranian ŌĆŗinterests and documented fake food shipments to justify transfers of oil proceeds.
Halkbank pleaded not guilty to bank fraud, money ŌĆŗlaundering and conspiracy charges.
The case was an outgrowth of several related criminal cases, including against Turkish-Iranian gold trader Reza Zarrab and ŌĆīformer ŌüĀHalkbank executive Mehmet Hakan Atilla.
Zarrab pleaded guilty to bank fraud, money laundering and conspiracy charges in 2017, but has not been sentenced.
He testified against Atilla, who was convicted in 2018 of bank fraud and conspiracy charges. Atilla returned to Turkey in 2019 after serving most of a 32-month prison term. He has denied wrongdoing.
Halkbank's case ŌĆŗhas taken a circuitous path ŌĆŗthrough the U.S. courts.
Berman originally ŌüĀruled in 2020 that Halkbank did not deserve immunity from prosecution.
The following year, the 2nd U.S. Circuit Court of Appeals in Manhattan upheld that ruling but on different grounds. ŌĆŗIt said Halkbank could be prosecuted under the federal Foreign Sovereign Immunities Act of ŌĆŗ1976, because its ŌüĀalleged misconduct involved commercial activity not covered by that law.
In 2023, the U.S. Supreme Court temporarily voided the prosecution , despite agreeing that Congress' desire to shield foreign countries and their instrumentalities from civil liability did not cover criminal cases. Instead, ŌüĀit ordered ŌĆŗthe 2nd Circuit to more thoroughly review whether immunity under centuries-old ŌĆŗcommon law shielded Halkbank.
The appeals court found no such shield in October 2024, prompting Halkbank's second Supreme Court appeal. The Trump administration argued that the common ŌĆŗlaw does not shield foreign state-owned companies from criminal prosecution.

item.title: Israel military says it is unaware of clash with Hezbollah on Syria border
item.description:
TEL AVIV, March 9 (Reuters) - The Israeli military on Monday said that it ŌĆŗwas unaware of any clash with ŌĆīHezbollah fighters in eastern Lebanon, after the Iran-backed Lebanese group said it had fought Israeli soldiers ŌĆŗapproaching from Syria.
Hezbollah said in a ŌĆŗstatement that its fighters had observed 15 ŌüĀhelicopters flying over eastern Lebanon just ŌĆŗafter midnight, dropping Israeli troops who were observed ŌĆŗapproaching Lebanese territory from an area on the Syrian side of the border.
It said that its fighters "confronted ŌĆŗthe helicopters and the infiltrating forces ŌĆŗwith appropriate weapons". The Israeli military, however, said that ŌĆīit ŌüĀwas unaware of any such incident.
Reuters was unable to immediately verify Hezbollah's account.
The Israeli military carried out an airborne raid in ŌĆŗthe same area, ŌĆŗnear ŌüĀthe village of Nabi Chit, overnight Friday-Saturday in what it said ŌĆŗwas a mission to seek the ŌĆŗremains ŌüĀof a soldier missing since 1986. item.title: Standard Lithium-Equinor JV inks lithium offtake deal with Trafigura

March 9 (Reuters) - Standard Lithium (SLI.V) , opens new tab said on Monday its joint venture with Norway's Equinor (EQNR.OL) , opens new tab ŌĆŗhad signed its first binding offtake ŌĆīagreement to supply lithium carbonate from its s outhwest Arkansas project to commodities trader ŌĆŗTrafigura.
The joint venture, Smackover Lithium, will ŌĆŗsupply Trafigura with 8,000 metric tons ŌüĀper year of battery-grade lithium ŌĆŗcarbonate over a 10-year period starting when ŌĆŗcommercial production begins.
The agreement marks a key milestone for the southwest Arkansas project as the ŌĆŗpartners seek to secure long-term ŌĆŗcustomers and financing ahead of a final investment ŌĆīdecision ŌüĀand construction.
Smackover Lithium is targeting about 22,500 metric tons of annual lithium carbonate production capacity in the project's first ŌĆŗphase and ŌĆŗplans ŌüĀto secure offtake agreements for around 80% of that output.

item.title: Oil shock to worsen should US-Israel seize Iran's Kharg Island, JP Morgan says
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 - Iran's oil exports would stall and output halve if the U.S. and Israel were to seize its port on Kharg Island, triggering further attacks from ŌĆŗTehran on regional oil infrastructure, JP Morgan said in a note.
Axios reported ŌĆīon March 7 that the U.S. administration had discussed seizing the island, which sits some 30 km off Iran's coast in the Gulf and processes 90% of its crude exports.
"A direct strike ŌĆŗwould immediately halt the bulk of IranŌĆÖs crude exports, likely triggering severe ŌĆŗretaliation in the Strait of Hormuz or against regional energy infrastructure," ŌüĀJP Morgan said.
Iran , the third largest producer in the Organization of the Petroleum Exporting ŌĆŗCountries, pumps about 4.5% of global oil supplies, with output of about 3.3 million ŌĆŗbarrels per day of crude, plus 1.3 million bpd of condensate and other liquids.
During the 1979 Iran hostage crisis, U.S. President Jimmy Carter imposed sanctions on Iran but refrained from ordering strikes ŌĆŗon the island. His successor, Ronald Reagan, during the 1980s Iran-Iraq Tanker War, prioritised ŌĆŗprotecting shipping and targeting Iranian vessels and missile batteries, leaving Kharg untouched.
"Although Iraqi forces struck some ŌĆīterminals ŌüĀand tankers during the eight-year war, Kharg remained largely operational and damage was typically repaired quickly, demonstrating that disabling it would require sustained, large-scale attacks," JP Morgan said.
The island collects oil coming by pipeline from IranŌĆÖs largest producing fields, including Ahvaz, Marun ŌĆŗand Gachsaran.
In the days ŌĆŗleading up to ŌüĀthe U.S.-Israeli attack, Iran ramped up exports from Kharg to near record levels, loading over 3 million bpd over February 15-20, ŌĆŗnearly triple its normal export pace of around 1.3 million to ŌĆŗ1.6 million ŌüĀbpd, according to JP Morgan.
Storage capacity on Kharg is estimated at roughly 30 million barrels and, according to Kpler, approximately 18 million barrels of crude are currently stored on ŌüĀthe ŌĆŗisland, equivalent to roughly 10-12 days of exports under ŌĆŗnormal conditions.
Oil prices jumped to $119 per barrel on Monday as production cuts in the Middle East spread with Iraq , Kuwait , Saudi Arabia and ŌĆŗthe United Arab Emirates all affected.

item.title: Exclusive: US nears deal to resume intelligence operations in Mali
item.description:
DAKAR, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is nearing a deal with Mali that will allow Washington to resume flying aircraft and drones over the West African country's airspace to gather intelligence on jihadist groups linked to al Qaeda, according to one current ŌĆŗU.S. official and a former U.S. official.
Last month, Washington made the first move to implement the agreement by lifting sanctions targeting the defense minister and other senior officials who ŌĆīit said had ties with Russian mercenaries, a key request by Mali's government, the sources said. The U.S. hopes this will lead Mali to grant the U.S. permission to fly intelligence-gathering missions over the country's vast terrain where jihadists have been gaining ground, according to the current and former U.S. officials.
The Trump administration has sought to rebuild ties with Mali after they came under strain during the previous administration.
In Washington, the goal to resume intelligence gathering is partly driven by a ŌĆŗdesire to find an American pilot who was kidnapped by armed men while working for Christian missionaries in neighboring Niger. The pilot is currently believed to be held in Mali by ŌĆŗthe local al Qaeda affiliate, Jama'at Nusrat al-Islam wal-Muslimin (JNIM), the officials said.
The State Department declined to comment.
A Malian government spokesperson did not respond to a request ŌüĀfor comment.
But after the sanctions were lifted on February 27, Mali's government praised the move, describing it as a step that would help "improve relations between our two countries, while reiterating respect for national sovereignty."
Mali, ŌĆŗa gold-producing, landlocked nation in the Sahel region of Africa, is roughly twice the size of France. Like several of its neighbors in the Sahel, it is battling an increasingly violent insurgency led by JNIM.
JNIM ŌĆŗhas pursued widespread kidnapping campaigns targeting foreign nationals to finance its operations in West Africa, according to U.S. crisis-monitoring group Armed Conflict Location & Event Data.
The top U.S. envoy to Africa, Nick Checker, visited Mali last month to meet with Malian Foreign Minister Abdoulaye Diop. The goal was to convey Washington's "desire to chart a new course in the bilateral relationship and move past policy missteps," according to the State Department, an apparent reference to Biden ŌĆŗadministration policies.
Diop praised American efforts to relaunch the relationship with a new approach based on mutual respect for its sovereignty and non-interference.
The Trump administration has largely dropped efforts to promote elections in the Sahel ŌĆŗregion, where military officials have overthrown elected but unpopular, Western-backed governments in recent years and turned to Russia for security support.
That has been a welcome move in Bamako and other Sahelian capitals. In the Sahel, perceived lecturing by ŌĆīWashington is often ŌüĀviewed as paternalistic and even racist, according to Catherine Nzuki of the Center for Strategic and International Studies, a U.S.-based think tank, which published a note about the U.S. loss of a base in Niger.
The Biden administrationŌĆÖs efforts to promote democracy after the coups helped trigger a break with Niger in 2024, with Niamey ordering all U.S. troops to leave a newly built, sprawling drone base in Agadez after a meeting with Biden officials went poorly. The multimillion-dollar base was intended to underpin intelligence-gathering operations across the region.
But the Trump administration's effort to improve relations has also encountered setbacks. Mali, Niger and Burkina Faso in December took ŌĆŗsteps to implement a reciprocal travel ban on Americans after ŌĆŗthe three nations were added to the ŌüĀWhite House's travel ban list.
In Mali, militants allied with al Qaeda and other groups have gained influence, spreading across the region despite a nearly decade-long intervention backed by France and the West, and later the efforts of Russian mercenaries. Over the past year, suspected jihadists have attacked gold mining assets ŌĆŗand key highways , starving the capital of fuel for businesses and schools.
Reuters was unable to determine whether Mali was taking steps to give Washington permission ŌĆŗto conduct missions in its ŌüĀairspace or when it was expected to take action.
The former U.S. official said cooperation was likely to come eventually because it was in Mali's interest to allow the U.S. to resume intelligence-gathering operations. ŌĆ£They will derive direct benefit from U.S. ISR activities where their national capabilities are insufficient,ŌĆØ the former official said, referring to intelligence, surveillance and reconnaissance.
"This overflight would build on likely ongoing cooperation with other USG entities,ŌĆØ the former official added, referring to ŌüĀU.S. government agencies.
The U.S. ŌĆŗhas shared intelligence with Mali that helped lead to a strike on senior JNIM leadership last year.
It wasnŌĆÖt immediately clear where ŌĆŗthe U.S. might base aircraft if Mali proceeds with granting permission to resume overflight operations. Ivory Coast has previously been discussed as an option, as it shares its northern border with Mali. Ghana hosted U.S. contractors late last year when the Trump administration began surveillance ŌĆŗflights over Nigeria, ahead of its Christmas Day bombing of what the U.S. said were Islamic State positions.

item.title: Trump says Australia has taken care of some Iranian women soccer players

item.title: Israel will target anyone promoting radical ideas against it, says UN envoy
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Israel's U.N. ambassador Danny Danon said on Monday that the new supreme leader of Iran , Mojtaba Khamenei, ŌĆŗhas the same radical ideas as in the past ŌĆīand Israel will target anyone who promotes radical ideas against it.
"Changing the man at the top does not change the regime," Danon told reporters at the United ŌĆŗNations when asked about Iran's naming of Khamenei to replace ŌĆŗhis father Ali Khamenei, who was killed on the ŌüĀfirst day of the war the U.S. and Israel launched against Iran at ŌĆŗthe end of last month.
"The new leader, unfortunately, is more of the ŌĆŗsame ideology, the same radical ideas, and ... anyone who will promote those radical ideas against us, we will target them, we will find them," Danon said.
Danon said ŌĆŗthe people of Iran should rise up to choose their next ŌĆŗleader and added: "We will have to create the conditions for them, and that ŌĆīis ŌüĀwhat we are doing now."
Asked about the threat to crucial energy traffic in the Strait of Hormuz and Iran's attacks against neighboring countries, Danon said Israel and the U.S. were hunting Iran's missile launchers and ŌĆŗdegrading its capabilities.
"So ŌĆŗit's going to ŌüĀbe harder for them to attack civilians in Dubai and to attack vessels in the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz. It doesn't mean it will be 100% guaranteed, ŌĆŗbut ŌüĀit will be harder for them to do that.
"So I'm optimistic about that. Every day we see the numbers of attacks, going down," Danon ŌüĀsaid.
The ŌĆŗwar has effectively shut the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz, where a fifth of global oil and liquefied natural gas is shipped along ŌĆŗIran's coast.

item.title: Anthropic sues to block Pentagon blacklisting over AI use restrictions
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Anthropic on Monday filed a lawsuit to block ŌĆŗthe Pentagon from placing it on a national security blacklist , escalating the artificial intelligence labŌĆÖs high-stakes battle with the U.S. military over usage ŌĆīrestrictions on its technology.
Anthropic said in its lawsuit that the designation was unlawful and violated its free speech and due process rights. The filing in federal court in California asked a judge to undo the designation and block federal agencies from enforcing it.
ŌĆ£These actions are unprecedented and unlawful. The Constitution does not allow the government to wield its enormous power to punish a ŌĆŗcompany for its protected speech," Anthropic said.
The Pentagon on Thursday slapped a formal supply-chain risk designation on Anthropic, limiting use of a technology that a ŌĆŗsource said was being used for military operations in Iran . Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth designated Anthropic after the startup refused to remove ŌüĀguardrails against using its AI for autonomous weapons or domestic surveillance. The two sides had been in increasingly contentious talks over those limitations for months, Reuters first reported.
Anthropic ŌĆŗofficials said the lawsuit doesn't preclude re-opening negotiations with the U.S. government and reaching a settlement. The company has said it does not want to be fighting with ŌĆŗthe U.S. government. The Pentagon said it wouldn't comment on litigation. Last week, a Pentagon official said the two sides were no longer active talks.
The designation poses a big threat to AnthropicŌĆÖs business with the government, and the outcome could shape how other AI companies negotiate restrictions on military use of their technology, though the company's CEO Dario Amodei clarified on Thursday that the ŌĆŗdesignation had "a narrow scope" and businesses could still use its tools in projects unrelated to the Pentagon.
President Donald Trump has also directed the government to stop working ŌĆŗwith Anthropic, whose financial backers include Alphabet's Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon.com (AMZN.O) , opens new tab . Trump and Hegseth said there would be a six-month phase-out.
Reuters has reported that Anthropic's investors were racing to contain the damage ŌĆīcaused by ŌüĀthe fallout with the Pentagon.
Trump and HegsethŌĆÖs actions on February 27 came after months of talks with Anthropic over whether the companyŌĆÖs policies could constrain military action and shortly after Amodei met with Hegseth in hopes of reaching a deal.
The Pentagon said U.S. law, not a private company, would determine how to defend the country and insisted on having full flexibility in using AI for ŌĆ£any lawful use,ŌĆØ asserting that AnthropicŌĆÖs restrictions could endanger American lives.
Anthropic said even the best AI models were not ŌĆŗreliable enough for fully autonomous weapons ŌĆŗand that using them for that ŌüĀpurpose would be dangerous. The company also drew a red line on domestic surveillance of Americans, calling that a violation of fundamental rights.
After HegsethŌĆÖs announcement, Anthropic said in a statement that the designation would be legally unsound and set a dangerous ŌĆŗprecedent for companies that negotiate with the government. The company said it would not be swayed by ŌĆ£intimidation or punishment,ŌĆØ and ŌĆŗon Thursday Amodei reiterated ŌüĀthat Anthropic would challenge the designation in court.
He also apologized for an internal memo published on Wednesday by tech news site The Information. In the memo, which was written last Friday, Amodei said Pentagon officials did not like the company in part because ŌĆ£we havenŌĆÖt given dictator-style praise to Trump."
The Defense Department signed agreements worth up to $200 million ŌüĀeach with major ŌĆŗAI labs in the past year, including Anthropic, OpenAI and Google.
Microsoft-backed (MSFT.O) , opens new tab OpenAI announced a deal to ŌĆŗuse its technology in the Defense Department network shortly after Hegseth moved to blacklist Anthropic. CEO Sam Altman said the Pentagon shared OpenAI's principles of ensuring human oversight of weapon systems and opposing ŌĆŗmass U.S. surveillance.

item.title: Brazil's finance minister expected to step down next week, sources say
item.description:
BRASILIA, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian Finance Minister Fernando Haddad is expected to step down by the end of next week ŌĆŗto run for governor of Sao Paulo state as pushed ŌĆīfor by President Luiz Inacio Lula da Silva, four sources told Reuters on Monday.
Haddad has not yet had a final conversation with Lula about running ŌĆŗfor governor, one of the sources said, but the two ŌĆŗhave been speaking frequently about the matter.
The move was ŌüĀreported by newspaper O Globo earlier in the day. The finance ŌĆŗministry declined to comment.
Lula's push for Haddad to run in Sao ŌĆŗPaulo reflects the president's need for a strong presence in a state that often votes heavily for the right-wing opposition. A weak performance for the leftist ŌĆŗleader in Sao Paulo could undermine his presidential campaign, according ŌĆŗto his party.
Haddad had not planned to run for office this year and previously ŌĆīvoiced ŌüĀhis intention to serve as one of the coordinators of Lula's re-election campaign .
Sources told Reuters, however, that expectations of a tough election for the president have put pressure on Haddad to become a candidate ŌĆŗin Sao Paulo.
A Datafolha ŌĆŗpoll released ŌüĀover the weekend showed that a potential second-round runoff between Lula and Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, the eldest ŌĆŗson of former President Jair Bolsonaro, would result in ŌĆŗa ŌüĀtechnical tie.
Another Datafolha poll focused on Sao Paulo state showed Haddad would take 31% of the vote in a first round against 44% ŌüĀfor Governor ŌĆŗTarcisio de Freitas, backed by the ŌĆŗBolsonaro family, who defeated Haddad in 2022.

item.title: US indicts two alleged 'terrorists' accused of trying to bomb NYC protest
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. ŌĆŗDepartment of ŌĆīJustice has indicted ŌĆŗtwo ŌĆŗpeople accused of trying ŌüĀto ŌĆŗbomb a ŌĆŗprotest in New York City, ŌĆŗAttorney ŌĆŗGeneral Pam Bondi ŌĆīsaid ŌüĀin a post on X ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗMonday, ŌüĀcalling them "ISIS-inspired ŌĆŗterrorists."

item.title: Ukraine ready for new peace talks 'at any moment', Zelenskiy says

item.title: Two right-wing politicians lead in Peru's presidential race, but most are undecided, poll says
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Two right-wing candidates - the daughter ŌĆīof a former jailed president and the former mayor of Lima - are out in front of other contenders for Peru's April 12 presidential election, according to the latest poll, though many voters are either undecided or do not plan ŌĆŗon voting.
Keiko Fujimori and Rafael Lopez Aliaga are the only candidates out of a record ŌĆŗroster of 36 presidential hopefuls polling in the double digits. Fujimori polled at ŌüĀ10.7% versus her rival's 10%, according to a Datum Internacional poll released Sunday night on ŌĆŗlocal TV, though roughly two-fifths of those polled either declined to choose a candidate or do not ŌĆŗplan on voting in the first place.
The scant support for the leaders suggests that a June 7 runoff is likely, which would be typical for the Andean nation, as no candidate has won in the first round since ŌĆŗthe end of the last century.
The job of Peru's head of state has become a revolving ŌĆŗdoor, with eight presidents since 2018. Four former leaders are currently behind bars. Its unpopular right-wing-controlled Congress removed its ŌĆīlatest ŌüĀpresident last month after he held secret meetings with a Chinese businessman.
Over 38% of voters polled between February 27 and March 4 were undecided or had decided not to vote, Datum found. Many of those who do vote will not decide until a week before the election, CEO Urpi Torrado ŌĆŗsaid.
Fujimori, daughter of late ŌĆŗformer president Alberto Fujimori, ŌüĀwho was imprisoned for 16 years for crimes against humanity - including the use of death squads during his decade-long tenure - is on her fourth run ŌĆŗfor the presidency.
She has been trading places in polling with Lopez Aliaga, ŌĆŗan ultra-conservative ŌüĀformer mayor who has embraced his nickname "Porky" for his resemblance to a U.S. cartoon pig, as part of his campaign. A fortnight ago, another Datum poll showed Lopez Aliaga in the lead with 13.4% and ŌüĀFujimori at ŌĆŗ9.7%.
Other candidates polling around 5% include leftist Alfonso Lopez-Chau, ŌĆŗa former central bank director; millionaire businessman Cesar Acuna; Carlos Alvarez, a popular comedian and impersonator of former presidents; and retired ŌĆŗgeneral and former intelligence chief Wolfgang Grozo.

item.title: Trump tells NY Post he is 'nowhere near' deciding to send troops to Iran to secure nuclear stockpile
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump said on Monday that he ŌĆŗwas "nowhere near" deciding whether to send ŌĆīU.S. troops into Iran to secure the stockpile of highly enriched uranium there.
"We havenŌĆÖt made any ŌĆŗdecision on that. WeŌĆÖre nowhere near ŌĆŗit,ŌĆØ Trump told the New York Post ŌüĀwhen asked about reported discussions between ŌĆŗIsrael and the United States on ŌĆŗpossibly deploying special forces to Iran to seize and secure the material.
A longstanding dispute over Iran's ŌĆŗnuclear program escalated into military conflict ten ŌĆŗdays ago, with the U.S. and Israel launching strikes ŌĆīagainst ŌüĀIran and Iran responding with attacks across the Middle East.
Without providing evidence, Trump said last month that Tehran was ŌĆŗbeginning to ŌĆŗrebuild the ŌüĀnuclear program that he claimed had been ŌĆ£obliteratedŌĆØ by U.S. strikes ŌĆŗin June last year.
Iran denies ŌĆŗseeking ŌüĀa nuclear arsenal, saying its enrichment of uranium ŌĆō a process that produces fuel for ŌüĀpower ŌĆŗplants and nuclear warheads ŌĆŗdepending on its duration ŌĆō is strictly for civilian use.

item.title: Rubio could designate Afghanistan as sponsor of wrongful detention, source says
item.description: WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ŌĆŗSecretary of ŌĆīState Marco Rubio ŌĆŗis ŌĆŗconsidering whether to ŌüĀdesignate ŌĆŗAfghanistan as ŌĆŗa state sponsor of ŌĆŗwrongful ŌĆŗdetention and could ŌĆīact ŌüĀas soon as Monday, ŌĆŗaccording ŌĆŗto ŌüĀa source ŌĆŗfamiliar ŌĆŗwith ŌüĀthe matter.

item.title: Islamist militants kill 15 in coordinated raids across northeast Nigeria
item.description:
MAIDUGURI, Nigeria, March 9 (Reuters) - Islamist militants killed at least 12 soldiers and three civilians in coordinated overnight attacks in northeast Nigeria, military ŌĆŗsources and residents said on Monday, as attacks on armed forces intensify.
A ŌĆī17-year Islamist insurgency in the region has killed thousands of people and displaced 2 million, according to aid groups, despite major military campaigns.
The assaults in Kukawa, Dalwa and Goniri came days after a similar ŌĆŗattack on an army post in Ngoshe and showed the militant groups' ŌĆŗability to hit multiple fronts at once.
Boko Haram and Islamic State West ŌüĀAfrica Province (ISWAP) fighters swept into Kukawa district in Borno state before dawn on Monday, ŌĆŗpushing towards the nearby military camp in a three-hour battle, soldiers involved told Reuters.
Troops ŌĆŗlater retook the camp but not before the commanding officer and five other soldiers were killed, a military source said. Karta Maina Ma'aji Lawan, the lawmaker representing Kukawa, confirmed the attack and the officer's ŌĆŗdeath.
In Dalwa, militants killed two soldiers and three residents before torching more than 250 ŌĆŗhomes, said resident and traditional leader Shetima Isa.
In neighbouring Yobe state, insurgents overran the Goniri military ŌĆībase, killing ŌüĀfour soldiers and setting vehicles and buildings ablaze, another soldier said.
Military spokesperson Sani Uba said troops had repelled several coordinated attacks on military positions in the northeast, and that all locations remained firmly under control.
"Regrettably, the engagements claimed the lives of some brave ŌĆŗand gallant soldiers, including ŌĆŗan outstanding officer ŌüĀin Kukawa, who paid the ultimate price," Uba said, without providing further casualty details.
Ground forces, backed by air support, were conducting follow-up ŌĆŗoperations in the affected areas, while cordon-and-search missions continued in nearby ŌĆŗvillages where ŌüĀwounded militants were reported to be hiding, he said.
Nigeria's military has ramped up raids on insurgent hideouts this year as part of a renewed offensive, but ISWAP and Boko Haram continue ŌüĀto ŌĆŗexploit the tough terrain, porous borders and a thin ŌĆŗstate presence across the northeast's arid belt.
The latest attacks came just hours after military intelligence had warned of ŌĆŗa likely ISWAP offensive.

item.title: China's foreign minister calls for ceasefire in Gulf region
item.description:
BEIJING, March 9 (Reuters) - Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi on Monday ŌĆŗcalled for a ceasefire in ŌĆīthe Gulf region as soon as possible, stressing that the ŌĆŗsovereignty, security and territorial ŌĆŗintegrity of Gulf countries should be ŌüĀfully respected, according to ŌĆŗstatements from his ministry.
Wang made ŌĆŗthe remarks in a phone call with his Kuwaiti counterpart Jarrah Jaber ŌĆŗAl-Ahmad Al-Sabah on Monday ŌĆŗas Iran answered a U.S.-Israeli bombing ŌĆīcampaign ŌüĀwith strikes around the Gulf region.
In a separate call, Wang told Bahraini Foreign Minister ŌĆŗAbdullatif bin ŌĆŗRashid ŌüĀAl Zayani that China was deeply concerned about ŌĆŗthe rapidly escalating situation ŌĆŗand ŌüĀwould play a constructive role in maintaining peace and ŌüĀstability ŌĆŗin the region.

item.title: Exclusive: Trump reviews options to curb energy prices as Iran strikes roil markets
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump is expected to review as early as Monday a set of options to tame oil prices, ŌĆŗwhich have spiked to more than $100 a barrel due to the Iran war, according to two people familiar ŌĆīwith the matter.
The effort reflects White House worries that the surge in oil prices will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm elections when Trump's fellow Republicans are hoping to retain control of Congress.
U.S. officials in Washington have been discussing with counterparts from the Group of Seven major ŌĆŗeconomies a possible joint release of crude oil from strategic reserves as one of several measures currently under discussion, the ŌĆŗsources said.
Other options include restricting U.S. exports, intervening in oil futures markets, waiving some federal taxes and ŌüĀlifting requirements under a U.S. law called the Jones Act that domestic fuel move only on U.S.-flagged ships, among others, the ŌĆŗsources said, speaking on condition of anonymity.
Analysts have said that U.S. policy options will have little sway over global oil markets as ŌĆŗlong as the fighting blocks Middle East oil exports accounting for a fifth of global supply through the Strait of Hormuz.
"The White House is in constant coordination with the relevant agencies on this important issue, as it is a top priority to the president. President Trump and his entire energy ŌĆŗteam have had a strong game plan to keep the energy markets stable well before Operation Epic Fury began, and they will ŌĆŗcontinue to review all credible options," White House spokesperson Taylor Rogers said in a statement, using the Trump administration's name for the U.S.-Israel military ŌĆīoperations targeting ŌüĀIran.
Global crude oil prices have hit levels not seen since mid-2022, briefly touching $119 a barrel, with gasoline and other fuel costs surging as a result since the U.S. and Israeli strikes began on February 28.
The White House last week asked federal agencies to assemble proposals that could help ease pressure on crude and gasoline prices, Reuters previously reported. The deliberations involve top White House officials, including White ŌĆŗHouse Chief of Staff Susie Wiles ŌĆŗand top adviser Stephen Miller, ŌüĀthe sources said.
Analysts and industry officials have said the White House has few meaningful tools to quickly curb rising oil prices unless authorities can restore the flow of tankers through the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz, the narrow waterway between Iran and Oman that carries roughly a fifth of the ŌĆŗworld's oil supply.
"The ŌüĀproblem is options range from marginal through symbolic to deeply unwise," said one of the sources, who is engaged with the White House on the effort.
The turmoil in energy markets comes at a sensitive moment for the president, who has sought to keep fuel prices low ŌüĀas a ŌĆŗcornerstone of his economic message to voters. A prolonged spike in oil and ŌĆŗgasoline costs could ripple through the broader economy, raising transportation and consumer prices.
A White House plan to provide naval escorts and backstop insurance for tankers traveling the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz so far has failed to significantly boost shipping traffic through the vital waterway.

item.title: Back to the 1970s? Investors brace for a return of stagflation
item.description:
LONDON, March 9 (Reuters) - Investors are now seriously considering the possibility that war in the Middle East could create a stagflationary shock, just as it did 50 years ago, when disruption to global ŌĆŗenergy supplies sent inflation surging, and battered growth
ŌĆ£The risk of a 1970s scenario is rising,ŌĆØ said Kaspar Hense, portfolio manager at RBC BlueBay Asset Management.
ŌĆ£If there is another extended war, ŌĆīwith oil prices going up significantly further, then the safe-haven status of government bonds are at risk, and with that, all assets.ŌĆØ
Here's how stagflation is a risk and could play out in markets in five charts.
The epicentre of stagflation fears is the surge in oil prices, and the biggest question for world markets now is how long will those prices remain elevated.
Brent crude vaulted above $100 a barrel on Monday and was on track for its biggest daily jump since the 2020 COVID ŌĆŗcrisis .
It is up 70% since the start of the year, while European wholesale gas prices are at their highest level in over three years .
That's bad news for inflation.
"A useful rule of thumb is ŌĆŗthat a 5% rise in oil prices adds around 0.1 percentage points to developed market inflation," Capital Economics said.
And high oil prices also could dampen economic growth.
The ŌüĀInternational Monetary Fund estimates for every persistent 10% rise in oil prices, a 0.1ŌĆō0.2 percent fall in global economic output follows.
Oil price surges contributed to U.S. recessions in 1973, 1980, 1990 and 2008.
This ŌĆŗputs central banks in a bind, as rate hikes to restrain inflation could further undermine economic growth.
Chicago Fed President Austan Goolsbee told the Wall Street Journal on Friday a "stagflationary environment that's as uncomfortable as any" could be looming.
Markets now ŌĆŗsee at least one European Central Bank hike this year, compared to a 40% chance of a cut before the war.
They also see a chance of a rate hike this year from the Bank of England, having earlier priced at least two cuts.
ŌĆ£It seems only retreating oil prices could reverse rate hike fears, even with dovish minds at the ECB also stressing downside growth risks,ŌĆØ said Commerzbank rates strategist Rainer Guntermann.
That has battered global bond markets as investors ditch fixed-income assets, ŌĆŗwhere inflation erodes future returns.
Short-dated bonds are the most sensitive. In Britain, two-year gilt yields have shot up by nearly 50 basis points in the last week, their worst sell-off since the budget crisis of 2022, in ŌĆŗlight of the UK's sticky inflation and flat-lining growth .
German and Australian two-year yields have risen more than 30 bps in that time, while U.S. two-year yields are up a comparatively modest 13 bps. ,
That's got investors looking at inflation-linked ŌĆīdebt, where both ŌüĀthe principal and the interest are linked to the rate of inflation.
This has pushed British five-year breakeven inflation rates - the difference between inflation-linked and nominal yields - up 28 basis points since the end of February. On Monday, they hit almost 3.5%, their highest since last April .
Meanwhile, five-year inflation-linked Treasury yields have risen by 4.2 bps in the last week, compared with a 15-bp rise in nominal yields .
Those wondering whether the market and economic hit will cause U.S. President Donald Trump to change course have to remember the stagflationary hit from the war will likely hurt the U.S. less than Europe or Asia.
"The U.S., with the Americas, ŌĆŗis self-sufficient in many (of the) commodities being choked off ŌĆŗdirectly or indirectly via (Strait of) Hormuz," said Rabobank ŌüĀsenior global strategist Michael Every in a note.
As well as oil he flags fertiliser and helium, important for semiconductor manufacturing.
Not surprisingly, U.S. markets have held up better in relative terms. The S&P 500 dropped 2% last week compared to a 5.5% fall in Europe and 6.3% drop for MSCI's Asia Pacific ex-Japan index. (.SPX) , opens new tab , (.STOXX) , opens new tab , (.MIAPJ0000PUS) , opens new tab
U.S. bonds also ŌĆŗoutperformed German last week. ,
However, the U.S. is by no means immune to stagflation and was looking a bit vulnerable even before the energy price spike. ŌĆŗThe economy unexpectedly shed jobs in ŌüĀFebruary, and data this week is expected to show a tick higher in U.S. inflation.
Investors don't like stagflation because it hurts stocks, bonds - that aren't inflation linked - and even potentially gold, given it does not have a yield.
The precious metal dropped 2% last week, and was down again on Monday, though analysts said this was in part selling to make up for losses elsewhere.
The only safe haven that has really held up since the ŌüĀwar begun has ŌĆŗbeen the dollar, which has gained on nearly every other developed market currency.
"The U.S. is a major oil producer and ŌĆŗcan withstand an oil shock - though there will be political fallout," said Kit Juckes, head of FX strategy at Societe Generale.
"The same simply isnŌĆÖt true of Europe, and the UK in particular."

item.title: Swiss parliament approves compensation for Crans-Montana bar fire victims
item.description:
GENEVA, March 9 (Reuters) - The Swiss lower house of parliament on Monday approved plans to make a ŌĆŗone-off payment of 50,000 Swiss francs ($64,000) ŌĆīto survivors and bereaved families of a New Year bar fire at a ski resort that killed 41 ŌĆŗpeople and injured 115.
The so-called solidarity contribution, ŌĆŗwhich aims to provide swift financial support ŌüĀto victims of the blaze at the ŌĆŗLe Constellation bar in the town of Crans-Montana, ŌĆŗhad already been voted through by the upper house of parliament last week.
The payment is meant to apply ŌĆŗto each individual who lost their life ŌĆŗin the fire and everyone who was hospitalised. Most ŌĆīof ŌüĀthose who died were teenagers and many of the victims were foreigners, including several from France and Italy.
According to witnesses and prosecutors, the ŌĆŗfire appeared ŌĆŗto have ŌüĀbeen started by the use of sparkling candles that ignited foam ŌĆŗsoundproofing on the bar's basement ceiling.
Earlier, a document ŌĆŗshowed ŌüĀthat Swiss prosecutors have widened their investigation into the fire, adding the mayor of Crans-Montana ŌüĀand ŌĆŗfour other current and former ŌĆŗlocal officials to the list of suspects.
($1 = 0.7785 Swiss francs)

item.title: Venture Global pledges on-time LNG deliveries amid Middle East conflict
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. liquefied natural gas developer Venture ŌĆīGlobal LNG (VG.N) , opens new tab has told customers of its Plaquemines export plant under development in Louisiana that it will start delivering contracted cargoes on schedule and at the already-agreed-upon prices, according to a letter seen by Reuters.
The Virginia-based company wrote on Friday to buyers of Plaquemines Phase 1 - ŌĆŗwhich include British major Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab and PolandŌĆÖs Orlen (PKN.WA) , opens new tab - and said it remains committed to beginning longŌĆæterm deliveries from ŌĆŗOctober 31 despite a surge in global gas prices because of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran .
ŌĆ£As global ŌüĀenergy markets react to the critical developments surrounding Iran and the Middle East, we wanted to assure you that, ŌĆŗas of today, Phase 1 remains on schedule,ŌĆØ the letter said.
Companies including BP (BP.L) , opens new tab , Shell, Unipec, Edison (EDNn.MI) , opens new tab , Galp (GALP.LS) , opens new tab , Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab and Orlen took ŌĆŗVenture Global to arbitration in one of the biggest disputes in the history of the LNG industry. The companies accused Venture Global of withholding contracted cargoes during commissioning of its nearby Calcasieu Pass plant to take advantage of higher prices on the spot market during the ŌĆŗ2021ŌĆō2023 energy crisis.
While Venture Global - which blamed technical issues for the delays in formal commissioning at Calcasieu - prevailed over Shell, ŌĆŗBP is seeking at least $3.7 billion after winning its case, a Venture Global filing showed. OrlenŌĆÖs case remains pending.
ŌĆ£To be clear, fluctuating market ŌĆīconditions have ŌüĀno impact on our previously communicated schedule,ŌĆØ a Venture Global spokesperson said. ŌĆ£As we reaffirmed on our earnings call last week, nearly 70% of the 2026 cargoes at Plaquemines are already contracted for and we reaffirm that Phase One remains on target to declare COD in Q4 2026.ŌĆØ
The U.S.-Israeli war on Iran and Tehran's attacks on Gulf neighbors have disrupted oil and ŌĆŗnatural gas exports from the ŌĆŗMiddle East and forced production ŌüĀstoppages, including in Qatar, which stopped operations at its LNG facilities, affecting some of the world's largest plants and a source that supplies about 20% of global LNG.
Benchmark gas prices ŌĆŗhave jumped, with Dutch Title Transfer Facility futures trading at a three-year high of ŌĆŗaround $21 per million ŌüĀBritish thermal units, and the Japan-Korea Marker near a two-year high around $16.
Venture Global is the second-largest U.S. LNG exporter. Plaquemines is its second export terminal and it shipped 2 million metric tons of LNG last month from Plaquemines, according to LSEG data. ŌüĀThe plant ŌĆŗis sending out early commission cargoes even though it is still under ŌĆŗdevelopment.
The company told customers it will begin developing its annual delivery program for the first contract year in early May, followed by the second contract ŌĆŗyear in July.

item.title: Oil and gas industry urge EU to pause methane emissions law
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil and gas companies urged the European Union on Monday to pause its methane emissions law, warning it could disrupt Europe's fuel ŌĆŗimports when tougher provisions kick in next year.
The call from industry ŌĆīcomes after the U.S. government demanded the EU exempt U.S. oil and gas from the rules. Brussels has since offered companies more flexible compliance options but declined to roll back the policy, a central ŌĆŗpillar of its climate strategy.
An industry-backed study by Wood Mackenzie, published on ŌĆŗMonday, said up to 43% of EU gas imports and 87% ŌüĀof EU crude oil imports could struggle to meet the EU rules ŌĆŗfrom 2027, exposing importers to heavy fines and risking supply gaps if producers are ŌĆŗdeterred from shipping fuel to Europe.
IOGP and fuel manufacturing group FuelsEurope called on Brussels to pause and amend the law.
"The EU cannot afford a self-made regulatory supply shock, even more so ŌĆŗin the current geopolitical context," said Francois-Regis Mouton de Lostalot, managing director of ŌĆŗIOGP Europe, whose members include ExxonMobil, Chevron, BP and TotalEnergies.
Oil and gas prices have surged ŌĆīthis ŌüĀmonth in response to the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , adding short-term pressure on governments already navigating Europe's phase-out of Russian energy imports. Most EU oil and gas now comes from Norway and the U.S.
The EU methane law requires that, from January ŌĆŗ2027, imported gas ŌĆŗmust comply with ŌüĀmonitoring and verification rules equivalent to Europe's, or meet a voluntary industry standard known as "Oil and Gas Methane Partnership 2.0 ŌĆŗlevel 5". The aforementioned companies are all part of the ŌĆŗOGMP initiative.
Climate ŌüĀgroups disputed the study's findings. The Environmental Defense Fund said its own analysis indicated that, following methane-reduction commitments by many oil and gas majors, the global volume of ŌüĀOGMP ŌĆŗ2.0 level 5-compliant gas in 2027 would be ŌĆŗmore than double EU demand, including supplies from Norway and the U.S.
A European Commission spokesperson did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to a request for comment.

item.title: Much of Iran's near-bomb-grade uranium likely to be in Isfahan, IAEA's Grossi says
item.description:
PARIS, March 9 (Reuters) - Almost half of ŌĆīIran's uranium enriched to up to 60% purity, a short step from weapons-grade, was stored in a tunnel complex at Isfahan and is probably still there, U.N. nuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi said on Monday.
The tunnel complex is the only ŌĆŗtarget that appears not to have been badly damaged in attacks last June by Israel ŌĆŗand the U.S. on Iran's nuclear facilities.
Diplomats have long said Isfahan has been ŌüĀused to store 60% uranium, which the International Atomic Energy Agency confirmed in a report to member ŌĆŗstates last month, without saying how much was there.
The IAEA estimates ŌĆŗthat when Israel launched its first attacks in June, Iran had 440.9 kg of 60% uranium. If enriched further, that would provide the explosive needed for 10 nuclear weapons, according to an IAEA yardstick.
"What we believe is that Isfahan had ŌĆŗuntil our last inspection a bit more than 200 kg, maybe a little bit more than that, ŌĆŗof 60% uranium," IAEA chief Rafael Grossi told reporters in Paris.
He said the stock was "mainly" at Isfahan, and some held elsewhere ŌĆīmay ŌüĀhave been destroyed.
"The widespread assumption is that the material is still there. So we haven't seen - and not only us, I think in general all those observing the facility through satellite imagery and other means to see what's going on there - movement indicating that the material could have been transferred," Grossi ŌĆŗsaid.
Iran has not informed the ŌĆŗIAEA of the ŌüĀstatus or whereabouts of its highly enriched uranium since the June attacks, nor has it let IAEA inspectors return to its bombed facilities.
Iran's nuclear programme is ŌĆŗone reason Israel and the U.S. have given for their current attacks on ŌĆŗIran , arguing ŌüĀthat it was getting too close to being able to produce a bomb, despite Trump saying in June that U.S. strikes had obliterated the programme. The IAEA has said it has no credible indication of a coordinated nuclear ŌüĀweapons ŌĆŗprogramme.
All three Iranian uranium-enrichment plants known to have been operating - two at ŌĆŗNatanz and one at Fordow - were destroyed or badly damaged in June.
"There is an amount (of 60% uranium) in Natanz also, which ŌĆŗwe believe is still there," Grossi said.

item.title: Airline shares battered, airfares surge as Iran war pushes oil above $100
item.description:
HONG KONG/FRANKFURT, March 9 (Reuters) - Airline stocks were hammered on ŌĆŗMonday, while airfares soared as the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran sent oil prices surging, sparking fears of a deep travel slump and the potential for the widespread grounding ŌĆīof planes.
Oil prices jumped 15% to above $105 a barrel, hitting levels not seen since 2022 as some major producers cut supplies and fears of prolonged shipping disruptions gripped the market. At one point, Brent crude futures jumped as much as 29%.
Some jet fuel prices have doubled since the start of the conflict, piling pressure on carriers already navigating tight airspace as pilots reroute to avoid the Middle East conflict and thousands of stranded passengers try to leave the ŌĆŗregion.
"Absent near-term relief, airlines around the world could be forced to ground thousands of aircraft while some of the industry's financially weakest carriers could halt operations," Deutsche analysts said ŌĆŗin a note to clients.
In Asia, airline shares tumbled, with the worst-hit, including Korean Air Lines (003490.KS) , opens new tab , which slid 8.6%, Air ŌüĀNew Zealand (AIR.NZ) , opens new tab down 7.8% and Hong Kong's Cathay Pacific (0293.HK) , opens new tab which dropped 5%.
In Europe, Air France KLM (AIRF.PA) , opens new tab , British Airways-owner IAG (ICAG.L) , opens new tab , Wizz Air (WIZZ.L) , opens new tab and Lufthansa (LHAG.DE) , opens new tab fell between 2.5% and 6% in morning trade.
Major U.S. ŌĆŗairlines shares were down about 1% to 5% in afternoon trading with JetBlue Airways (JBLU.O) , opens new tab down 5.35% followed by American Airlines (AAL.O) , opens new tab down 3.44%.
Underscoring that pain on the consumer side were jumps in ticket prices. Direct ŌĆŗflights from Seoul to London on March 11 with Korean Air Lines, for example, leapt to $4,359, from $564 seven days earlier, according to Google Flights data. Flights from Los Angeles to Lima on LATAM Airlines (LTM.SN) , opens new tab rose to $2,125 compared to $499 in the same period.
"The issue for the airlines now is that travel demand may be curtailed as costs become prohibitive for leisure travellers and as some companies start to limit business travel due to the uncertain outlook," said ŌĆŗLorraine Tan, director of equity research, Asia at Morningstar.
The impact of high airfares could limit travel demand for much of 2026, Tan added.
Fuel is the second-largest expense for air carriers after labour, typically ŌĆŗaccounting for a fifth to a quarter of operating expenses. Some major Asian and European airlines have oil hedging in place, but U.S. airlines largely stopped the practice over the last two decades.
"We assume the airlines are ŌĆīable to recapture ŌüĀa portion of the spike in fuel prices, but it's hard to envision margin expansion this year barring a rapid decline in energy prices," said Tom Fitzgerald, TD Cowen vice president of equity research on six major U.S. airlines and Air Canada.
A one-way trip to Quebec City from Newark on March 11 aboard Air Canada nearly tripled to $1,499 compared to a week ago, according to data from Google Flights.
High prices could have severe implications for the industry.
"If crude is rising 20%, jet fuel is rising several times more as it is even more scarce, adding significant cost ŌĆŗto operations together with crew resources, which are ŌĆŗstretched due to longer flying times when ŌüĀairspace is closed," said Subhas Menon, head of the Association of Asia Pacific Airlines.
The Deutsche analysts noted that a sharp spike in jet fuel costs in 2005 in the aftermath of hurricanes Katrina and Rita resulted in widespread and significant damage to the industry, including major airlines Delta and Northwest ŌĆŗfiling for Chapter 11 bankruptcy that year.
Since February 28, when the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran started, through March 9, more than 40,000 flights to ŌĆŗand from the Middle ŌüĀEast have been cancelled, according to data from Cirium.
With airspace severely constrained, airlines have been forced to reroute flights, carry extra fuel or make additional refuelling stops to guard against sudden diversions or longer flight paths through safer corridors.
Combined, Emirates, Qatar Airways and Etihad normally fly about one-third of the passengers from Europe to Asia and more than half of all passengers from Europe to Australia, New Zealand and nearby Pacific ŌüĀIslands, according to ŌĆŗCirium.
Flights to Iraq, Syria, Lebanon and Jordan by Turkish Airlines (THYAO.IS) , opens new tab , AJet, Pegasus (PGSUS.IS) , opens new tab and SunExpress have been cancelled until March ŌĆŗ13, Turkish Transport Minister Abdulkadir Uraloglu said on Sunday.

item.title: Exclusive: Canadian oil tycoon backs South Bow pipeline to boost US exports
item.description:
CALGARY, March 9 (Reuters) - The Canadian tycoon who heads one of North America's fastest growing oil companies told Reuters he supports ŌĆŗa proposal to potentially revive parts of the former Keystone XL pipeline and wants to see Canada use the project as ŌĆīleverage in upcoming trade negotiations with the United States.
Still, Adam Waterous, CEO of Strathcona Resources (SCR.TO) , opens new tab , said he would prefer a new West Coast pipeline to increase access to Asian markets.
The comments by Waterous, one of Canada's most aggressive oil industry dealmakers, are the first public statements by a Canadian oil shipper in favour of a new pipeline proposal led by Canadian company ŌĆŗSouth Bow (SOBO.TO) , opens new tab that could increase the country's crude exports to the U.S. by more than 12%.
They are an early indication that the project ŌĆö which would require both ŌĆŗa green light from U.S. President Donald Trump and the construction of additional links to U.S. refining hubs ŌĆö has at ŌüĀleast some level of commercial support from Canadian oil sands producers at a time when the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has disrupted international supply and sent global ŌĆŗcrude prices soaring.
The plan involves a different route through the U.S. than the previous Keystone XL pipeline, which was canceled by former U.S. President Joe Biden ŌĆŗin 2021 after years of Indigenous and environmental opposition, but would use some of that project's already built pipe in Alberta.
South Bow launched a competitive bidding process last week offering 450,000 barrels per day of contracted pipeline capacity from Hardisty, Alberta to multiple U.S. delivery points including Cushing, Oklahoma, and destinations on the U.S. Gulf Coast, to gauge potential commercial use.
Waterous ŌĆŗdeclined to say if Strathcona will commit barrels to the South Bow project, citing ongoing commercial negotiations. But he pointed to his company's stated goal to expand ŌĆŗits own oil production from 125,000 barrels per day in 2026 to up to 300,000 bpd by 2035.
"I've been a very early advocate of this project," Waterous said.
"And we ŌĆīare looking ŌüĀfor egress now," he said, referring to oil shippers' ability to transport their product to market.
But Waterous said the South Bow proposal is not the Canadian oil industry's first choice for bolstering crude exports. Currently more than 90% of Canada's oil exports go to the United States, and Waterous said Canadian oil shippers have long wanted greater market diversification.
Canadian oil companies would prefer another pipeline to the Pacific, to supplement the existing Trans Mountain pipeline which runs from Alberta to the British Columbia coast ŌĆŗand offers an outlet to Asian ŌĆŗmarkets, Waterous said.
Four other large Canadian ŌüĀoil sands producers did not respond to a Reuters request for comment on whether they support the South Bow proposal.
The Alberta government has said it will submit an application for a new crude oil pipeline to the west ŌĆŗcoast for fast-track approval by the federal government, but no private company has said it will build the project.
The ŌĆŗmain benefit to ŌüĀthe South Bow proposal, Waterous said, is that it can likely be built faster and more cheaply than a new West Coast pipeline, while also carrying potential political benefits for Canada.
He said Canada should try to leverage Trump's previously stated comments that he would like to see the Keystone XL project built.
Prime Minister Mark Carney has also raised with ŌüĀTrump the prospect of ŌĆŗreviving the Keystone XL oil pipeline from Alberta to the United States as part of ŌĆŗhis efforts to ease trade tensions between the two countries.
"The utility of a pipeline to the south is that it is a tool to achieve a tariff agreement to protect principally Canada's steel, ŌĆŗauto and aluminum sectors, while also allowing the Canadian oil sector to grow," Waterous said.

item.title: Peru's pipeline disruption tests industrial resilience; businesses blanch at remote work order
item.description:
LIMA, March 9 (Reuters) - Peru's business groups are clashing with the new interim government over sweeping emergency measures imposed after a rupture in the country's ŌĆŗmain naturalŌĆægas pipeline triggered the worst energy crisis in two decades, an early test of President Jose BalcazarŌĆÖs ŌĆīinterim mandate weeks before national elections.
The breakdown of Transportadora de Gas del Peru's pipeline, the backbone of the system that supplies nearly half of PeruŌĆÖs electricity and most of its LPG, has forced widespread gas rationing, pushing up energy prices and exposing longŌĆæstanding vulnerabilities in the Andean nation's energy ŌĆŗsystem.
Balcazar, appointed on February 18 after Congress ousted his predecessor, now faces a highŌĆæstakes leadership test. More than ŌĆŗa thousand firms have struggled with halted gas production, and business associations have called for the rollback ŌüĀof emergency directives.
Authorities say gas flows may not resume until midŌĆæMarch.
The outage could stoke inflation, which has remained among the ŌĆŗlowest in the world despite years of political turmoil.
"It can hit inflation, but in a heterogeneous way depending on how energy ŌĆŗaffects each activity,ŌĆØ said former Economy Minister Alonso Segura.
Inflation reached 0.69% in February and could exceed the 0.81% recorded in March last year, Segura said.
Natural gas is used to produce 70% of the LPG consumed domestically. Some 2.1 million households rely on piped gas and about one million vehicles ŌĆŗrun on natural gas or LPG.
Peru is the worldŌĆÖs third-largest copper producer.
Former Energy and Mines Minister Carlos Herrera said the rupture ŌĆŗunderscored PeruŌĆÖs vulnerability after an alternative pipeline project was canceled in 2017 when the Brazilian construction company Odebrecht was unable to secure financing ŌĆīwhile being ŌüĀinvestigated in a case involving bribes paid to secure public works contracts in Peru.
"This crisis highlights the need to diversify the countryŌĆÖs energy matrix," Herrera said. "The serious problem we have is that we havenŌĆÖt made exploratory efforts for natural gas over time either."
Industry groups said more than 1,000 companies have been affected, with some halting production. Felipe James, head of the National Society of Industry (SNI), ŌĆŗsaid emergency rules allowing firms ŌĆŗto switch temporarily to alternative ŌüĀfuels will be costly and not all companies are equipped to make the change.
"Not all can switch... they are not prepared," James said.
On Friday, the government ordered public employees in Lima to ŌĆŗwork remotely for a week and encouraged private firms to do the same. Schools and ŌĆŗuniversities will shift ŌüĀto virtual classes during the contingency.
Business associations criticized the measures, saying they amount to a partial shutdown of the economy.
"Stopping the country can never be the solution. We demand this decision be reversed," said COMEX, the foreign trade guild.
Peru's mining and energy chamber said it ŌüĀwas working "intensely" ŌĆŗto mitigate the crisis and seeking to import LPG in "record time."
Spokespeople for ŌĆŗthe presidency and the Ministry of Energy and Mines did not respond to a request for comment.
The TGP gas pipeline began operating in 2004 and also ŌĆŗenables the export of natural gas to Japan, South Korea and China.

item.title: Two men charged with terrorism after homemade bomb thrown at anti-Islam protesters in New York
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Two men accused of igniting homemade bombs at a chaotic anti-Islam protest outside the New York City mayor's mansion told police they were inspired by Islamic State, the Islamist militant group, and were charged on Monday with ŌĆŗfederal terrorism-related crimes, according to court records.
Emir Balat, 18, and Ibrahim Kayumi, 19, are accused of traveling from Pennsylvania with ŌĆīdangerous improvised explosive devices to Manhattan on Saturday, where a small group of far-right activists had planned the anti-Muslim rally outside Gracie Mansion against Zohran Mamdani, New York City's first Muslim mayor.
Balat was captured in photographs and video lighting a small IED and throwing it towards the anti-Muslim protesters before lighting a second device ŌĆŗhanded to him by Kayumi and dropping it, according to the criminal complaint. Police were able to intercept the smoking devices, ŌĆŗduct-taped jars containing screws and bolts and the explosive substance TATP, and no one was hurt.
After police arrested ŌüĀthem, they said they were inspired by Islamic State, and Balat told police that he wanted to carry out an attack "even bigger" than ŌĆŗthe Boston Marathon bombing in 2013 that killed three people, according to the criminal complaint filed in the U.S. district court in Manhattan on ŌĆŗMonday.
Mehdi Essmidi, who represents Balat, did not immediately reply to a request for comment. Kayumi's lawyer Michael Arthus declined to comment.
Speaking alongside police outside Gracie Mansion on Monday, Mamdani condemned the violence. He and his wife were not home at the time, because they had known about the "Stop the Muslim takeover" rally organized by ŌĆŗfar-right activist Jake Lang more than a week in advance. More than 100 counter-protesters gathered nearby, the vast majority peaceful, saying they were there ŌĆŗto "run Nazis out" of New York.
"New York City will never tolerate violence whether from protests or counter-protests," Mamdani told reporters. He said while he thought the ŌĆīfar-right anti-Muslim ŌüĀrally was "vile", he defended the "sacred right" of protest, even for objectionable speech.
Police said it was the first time an improvised bomb was deployed in New York City in nearly a decade. In 2017, Akayed Ullah, who also cited Islamic State as inspiration, ignited a homemade bomb strapped to his body in a passage near the Times Square subway station, wounding only himself.
Balat and Kayumi are each charged with five federal crimes, ŌĆŗaccording to a complaint filed by ŌĆŗthe U.S. attorney's office in Manhattan, ŌüĀincluding use of a weapon of mass destruction, attempting to provide material support to a designated foreign terrorist organization, and three charges related to the illegal possession or transportation of explosives.
During their separate police interviews, ŌĆŗBalat asked for a piece of paper and wrote on it he had pledged allegiance to Islamic ŌĆŗState, while Kayumi ŌüĀtold police he watched Islamic State videos on his phone, according to the criminal complaint.
A third man, Ian McGinnis, was arrested and appeared in a New York state court on Sunday accused of squirting pepper spray or a similar chemical irritant into the faces of two of the counter-protesters opposing ŌüĀthe anti-Islam ŌĆŗrally, according to the Manhattan district attorney's office and police. McGinnis faces six charges, including ŌĆŗthird-degree assault.
Police in New York have been on alert since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran, but did not say there was a sign of Saturday's attack being linked ŌĆŗto the war.

item.title: Trump says war against Iran is 'very complete,' CBS News reports
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗthinks the war against Iran "is ŌĆīvery complete" and that Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four ŌĆŗto five week estimated ŌĆŗtime frame, according to a CBS News ŌüĀreporter on X, who cited an ŌĆŗinterview with him.
"I think the ŌĆŗwar is very complete, pretty much. They have no navy, no communications, theyŌĆÖve ŌĆŗgot no Air Force," Trump ŌĆŗwas quoted as saying by CBS News' ŌĆīWhite ŌüĀHouse correspondent Weijia Jiang.
Trump also said the U.S. is "very far" ahead of his initial 4-5 ŌĆŗweek estimated ŌĆŗtime frame ŌüĀfor the war.
On Iran's new Supreme leader Mojtaba ŌĆŗKhamenei, Trump told CBS ŌĆŗNews: "I ŌüĀhave no message for him." Trump said he has someone in ŌüĀmind ŌĆŗto replace Khamenei, ŌĆŗbut did not elaborate.

item.title: First Brands row hints at banksŌĆÖ shadow exposure
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters Breakingviews) - Cockroach troubles tend to extend further than they first appear. JPMorgan boss Jamie Dimon likened high-profile credit ŌĆŗblowups to the sneaky insects, implying that deeper problems lurk. And now, six months after heavily indebted auto-parts retailer ŌĆīFirst Brands toppled, regional lender Western Alliance (WAL.N) , opens new tab has disclosed , opens new tab a $126 million hit from the mess. It never lent directly to First Brands, but instead to a fund run by Wall Street financiers at Jefferies (JEF.N) , opens new tab . ItŌĆÖs just one of many smaller banks piling into the murky world of shadow finance.
First BrandsŌĆÖ surprise bankruptcy last ŌĆŗyear sent a chill through credit markets. Federal prosecutors alleged , opens new tab that it pledged assets multiple times over to secure billions of ŌĆŗborrowings, largely from nonŌĆæbank lenders. It was not an isolated case, with the government also indicting , opens new tab the ŌüĀfounder of sub-prime auto lender Tricolor on similar claims. Jefferies, meanwhile, also lent to now-teetering real-estate lender Market Financial Solutions.
Western Alliance claims a ŌĆŗdeliberate breach of contract, saying that Jefferies has ceased to pay $126 million outstanding on loans to special-purpose vehicles that held First BrandsŌĆÖ ŌĆŗaccounts receivable. Jefferies said , opens new tab on Monday that these are non-recourse loans, with no guarantee of payment from the SPVsŌĆÖ parents, and that the suit lacks merit.
Whatever the case, shares of Western Alliance and Jefferies have tanked 11% and 14%, respectively, since Thursday. Morgan Stanley analysts downgraded their recommendation on JefferiesŌĆÖ shares, citing ŌĆŗthe litigation.
Western Alliance is a relative pipsqueak, at roughly $8 billion in market value. Yet many similarly sized institutions have been swept up ŌĆŗin the flood of lending to lightly regulated firms that the Federal Reserve labels ŌĆ£non-depository financial institutions.ŌĆØ Lending to these shadow banks has climbed to $1.9 trillion, ŌĆīup 180% ŌüĀfrom $680 billion in 2021, according to Fed data. This growth tracks the vertiginous rise of private credit and asset-backed lending , which have increasingly displaced traditional banks since the 2008 financial crisis. Providing leverage is a way to get a cut of the action back.
The question is how much risk this poses. BanksŌĆÖ equity is a loss-absorbing buffer, taking the hit if loans go awry en masse. Yet 40 ŌĆŗU.S. lenders with a combined $4.7 ŌĆŗtrillion in assets now have ŌüĀlent the equivalent of more than 100% of their total equity capital to shadow banks, up from 29 banks holding $1.1 trillion two years ago, according to data from KBRA Financial Intelligence. Nearly all borrowers ŌĆŗare current on their payments, and much of the activity relates to the mortgage market, which ŌĆŗenjoys effective government ŌüĀsupport. Yet the territory in which cockroaches can breed is growing.
Follow Stephen Gandel on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: Iran war boosts oil price, but oil major shares are stuck on the sidelines
item.description:
LONDON/HOUSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Oil prices on Monday climbed to the highest level since 2022, but shares of major producers like Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab ŌĆŗand Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab have so far seen limited gains since the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran broke out late last month, ŌĆīsuggesting that traders think the pain in the market may be short term.
