While crude oil futures have risen more than 40% since Israel and the U.S. launched joint airstrikes on February 28, the iShares Global Energy ETF - which tracks global energy companies - is up around 2%. That suggests that any immediate gains from increased prices will be temporary, ŌĆŗor offset by reduced production.
"The market is anticipating a swift end to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and a subsequent ŌĆŗcollapse in oil prices back to normalized levels," said James West, head of energy and power research at Melius ŌüĀResearch. "The rally in oil prices is primarily contained to near-term spot prices rather than longer-dated crude oil futures."
Brent crude futures and U.S. West Texas Intermediate ŌĆŗcrude futures rose by around 30% to start the week, as the war on Iran has disrupted the passage of traffic in the Strait of Hormuz, ŌĆŗoil and natural gas exports from the Middle East and has forced production stoppages.
At the same time, Brent's front-month contract hit an all-time high at around $36 per barrel over its 6-month delivery contract, indicating near-term supply tightness.
The rally conjured oil futures' 2008 all-time highs at around $147 per barrel, when tension between the West and Iran over its nuclear program, ŌĆŗa weak U.S. dollar and inflationary factors pushed prices higher. Continued growth in China and other emerging economies was also expected to offset the ŌĆŗimpact of any fall in consumption in developed countries.
However, just months after the all-time high, the 2008 financial crisis and concerns around an overbought market sunk oil to ŌĆībelow $40 per ŌüĀbarrel, at the time the lowest since 2004.
"Go back to 2008," said David Hewitt, senior consultant at Hewitt Energy Perspectives. "Oil stocks followed crude's rally until about $100 then almost completely disconnected as it went to $147."
"The market was right then - $147 per barrel rapidly became $30," he added. "It's a bit of the same now."
Shell shares rose as much as 4.9% on Monday from their close on Feb. 27, while Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab gained 2.6% and Exxon gained 0.9% over the same period. ŌĆŗBP (BP.L) , opens new tab gained 7.8%.
Deferred oil contracts are ŌĆŗnot as high as the ŌüĀspot price, signaling that investors do not expect the supply disruption to last, said Simon Lack, portfolio manager of the Catalyst Energy Infrastructure Fund.
U.S. shale producers, which have seen a bigger boost in share prices than the global ŌĆŗoil majors, may be benefitting because U.S. energy supply and infrastructure are safe and insulated from the risk, ŌĆŗprompting investors to place ŌüĀa higher value on those companies, he added.
Diamondback (FANG.O) , opens new tab , the largest independent producer in the Permian basin, has seen its shares gain as much as 7% on Monday from their close on February 27.
Simon Wong, a portfolio manager at Gabelli Funds, said share prices of Diamondback, APA Corp (APA.O) , opens new tab and Occidental (OXY.N) , opens new tab had risen less ŌüĀyear-to-date compared with ŌĆŗExxon and Chevron and are now beginning to catch up.
He also believes that the ŌĆŗhigher crude prices are temporary and will likely moderate when the disruptions are over.

item.title: Surging oil drives worries for US stock investors
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - A stunning surge in oil prices above $100 a barrel is rattling U.S. stock investors, as they brace for an even sharper rise in energy prices that could stagger the economy and further damage equities.
The 50% jump in U.S. crude to its highest ŌĆŗlevel in more than three years is among the most significant financial consequences of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, as investors continue to calibrate economic and market fallout following the ŌĆīattacks launched just over a week ago.
"It's a shock," said Michael Reynolds, vice president of investment strategy at Glenmede. "It's out of left field, and people and investors are having to figure out what it means in real time as it's unfolding."
Higher oil and gas prices stand to drive up costs for energy-intensive companies, erode the discretionary spending budgets of consumers and lead to worries about inflation that could stop the Federal Reserve from lowering interest rates.
Reynolds and other investors were scrambling to ŌĆŗgame out scenarios should oil reach heights that were not on the horizon just days earlier.
U.S. and Brent crude on Monday both broke above $100 a barrel, which investors have pointed to as a ŌĆŗlevel that could create more stock turbulence. The commodities at one point on Monday neared $120. U.S. crude settled at $67.02 on February 27, the last session before the ŌüĀU.S.-Israeli strikes.
Meanwhile, stock volatility has spiked. The Cboe Volatility Index (.VIX) , opens new tab on Monday topped 30 for the first time in nearly a year, up from below 20 in late February.
While the declines for U.S. stocks have been moderate ŌĆŗin comparison to other global regions, the benchmark S&P 500 (.SPX) , opens new tab was last down nearly 4% from its late January all-time closing high, after paring steeper declines earlier in Monday's session.
Strategists at Yardeni Research said last Tuesday they ŌĆŗexpected a 10% stock market correction.
"Now we can't rule out a bear market and even a recession," the firm said in a note on Sunday.
OIL AND STOCKS: MORE TIGHTLY LINKED
As oil prices have surged, the moves have become more tightly linked to the stock market.
The 20-day correlation between the S&P 500 and U.S. crude stood at -0.813 as of Monday morning, according to LSEG data, a strong inverse relationship that shows they have been tending to move in opposite directions.
Deutsche Bank strategists, watching whether the ŌĆŗIran situation could prompt a larger risk-off move, said in a note on Monday that the oil price shock ranks "among the more serious of history," but that investors are pricing in "a short rather than protracted conflict."
While stocks ŌĆŗand oil are typically thought of as separate markets, stock investors have watched oil's trajectory quite closely at times historically, especially after extreme price moves.
In early 2022, a jump in oil over $120 a barrel following the start of the conflict ŌĆīin Ukraine coincided ŌüĀwith declines in stock prices. In 2015-2016, stock investors worried that low oil prices, with U.S. crude dropping below $30 a barrel, represented a sign of broad economic weakness.
Focus is again on the economic ramifications of rising oil prices.
Each 10% increase in the oil price stands to translate to roughly a 15 to 20 basis-point drag on GDP growth, according to JPMorgan economists.
"Effects also could be non-linear, with larger oil price spikes producing an even larger hit to growth," the JPMorgan economists said in a note.
The JPMorgan economists and other analysts cautioned the economic fallout likely hinges on how long crude prices stay elevated.
For now, the jump in ŌĆŗcrude is driving up prices at the gas pump. ŌĆŗThe national average for gasoline rose to $3.478 a ŌüĀgallon on Monday, up from $2.902 a month ago, according to data from the motorists' group AAA. That is the highest level since the summer of 2024, the group said.
"From an average consumer perspective, oil prices are about ... as visceral as it gets just because of filling up your gas tank," said Kevin Gordon, head of macro ŌĆŗresearch and strategy at Charles Schwab.
Indeed, shares of companies most reliant on discretionary spending are among the potentially most vulnerable to higher oil prices.
Airlines, for which ŌĆŗfuel accounts for 20% to ŌüĀ25% of unit costs, according to Morningstar, have seen their shares hammered with the S&P 1500 passenger airlines index (.SPCOMAIR) , opens new tab down 15% since the conflict began.
Of course, higher oil prices could benefit some corners of the market. The S&P 500 energy sector (.SPNY) , opens new tab has gained 1% since late February, while the broader S&P 500 has fallen over 2% in that time.
And some investors are wary that the situation could change at any moment.
In a note ŌüĀon Monday, Raymond ŌĆŗJames Chief Investment Officer Larry Adam said the wealth management firm expects the conflict to be "relatively short-lived." The firm maintained a year-end ŌĆŗprice target for U.S. crude at $55-$60 a barrel.
Investors are also mindful of how U.S. President Donald Trump has changed course on market-sensitive policies during his term. In particular, there was his softening of his blanket "Liberation Day" tariff policies last April that caused a sharp rebound in ŌĆŗinitially battered asset prices.
"We're one ceasefire agreement headline away from all of this just reversing in a very aggressive fashion," Gordon said.

item.title: Australia to grant visa to five Iranian women soccer players after Trump appeal
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide ŌĆŗvisas to five Iranian ŌĆīwomen's soccer players to stay in the country, after ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗurged Canberra not to ŌüĀsend them back to ŌĆŗTehran due to concerns ŌĆŗover their safety.
Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told reporters the other ŌĆŗmembers of the ŌĆŗteam were welcome to stay back ŌĆīin ŌüĀAustralia. The team is in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Trump said ŌĆŗon Monday ŌĆŗhe ŌüĀhad spoken to Australian Prime Minister ŌĆŗAnthony Albanese about the ŌĆŗIran ŌüĀwomen's soccer team after reports that five players ŌüĀhad ŌĆŗsought asylum ŌĆŗin Australia.

item.title: US weighs easing Russian oil sanctions to cool global price surge, sources say
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump 's administration is considering reducing oil sanctions on Russia to help cool ŌĆŗa surge in global energy prices triggered by ŌĆīthe U.S. and Israeli war on Iran , with an announcement possible as soon as Monday, according to three sources familiar with the planning.
The ŌĆŗmove would be intended to boost world supplies ŌĆŗof oil amid massive disruptions to Middle East shipments ŌüĀfrom the expanding conflict, but could also complicate U.S. ŌĆŗefforts to deprive Russia of revenue for its war in ŌĆŗUkraine.
The discussions could include broad sanctions relief as well as more targeted options that would allow certain countries, such as India, to ŌĆŗbuy Russian oil without fear of U.S. penalties, including ŌĆŗtariffs, the sources told Reuters, speaking on condition of anonymity.
The United States ŌĆīlast ŌüĀweek allowed India to temporarily purchase Russian crude oil already on tankers at sea to help it cope with the cuts to Middle East supply.
The new moves could be ŌĆŗannounced as soon ŌĆŗas Monday, ŌüĀthe sources said.
"President Trump and his entire energy team have had a strong game ŌĆŗplan to keep the energy markets stable well ŌĆŗbefore ŌüĀOperation Epic Fury began, and they will continue to review all credible options," said Taylor Rogers, a White House spokesperson, ŌüĀusing ŌĆŗthe administration's term for the war.
"Any ŌĆŗpolicy announcement will come directly from the President or his team," Rogers said.

item.title: Kremlin: Putin, in a phone call with Trump, shares┬Āproposals to end Iran war quickly
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 9 (Reuters) - Russian President Vladimir Putin, in a telephone conversation on Monday with ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump , put forward proposals for a quick ŌĆīsettlement of the conflict around Iran , Kremlin foreign policy aide Yuri Ushakov said.
Ushakov, giving an account of the conversation to reporters, said the two ŌĆŗleaders also discussed the conflict in Ukraine and noted that ŌĆŗRussian gains should prompt Kyiv's negotiators to move ŌüĀtowards a settlement.
He described the entire discussion as "very substantial" and ŌĆŗsaid it would "likely have practical significance for further work between ŌĆŗthe two countries".
Ushakov said Putin "expressed several thoughts aimed at a quick political and diplomatic end to the Iranian conflict, including contacts that have taken ŌĆŗplace with leaders of Gulf states, the president of ŌĆŗIran and leaders of other countries".
He said Trump "offered his assessment of developments ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe U.S.-Israeli operation. Let me say that a very substantial and, without doubt, useful exchange of ideas took place."
Ushakov said Trump believed it was in the U.S. interest to see a "rapid end ŌĆŗto the ŌĆŗconflict in Ukraine ŌüĀwith a ceasefire and a long-term settlement".
Developments in Ukraine, he said, were characterised by "Russian troops ŌĆŗadvancing quite successfully. This, as was noted, is ŌĆŗa ŌüĀfactor that should prompt the regime in Kyiv to finally go down the path of negotiating a settlement to the conflict".
Ushakov ŌüĀalso ŌĆŗsaid the two presidents also discussed ŌĆŗthe situation in Venezuela in the context of global oil markets.

item.title: Iranian women's soccer players given asylum after Trump speaks with Australian PM
item.description:
WASHINGTON/SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia on Tuesday agreed to provide visas to five of Iran's women's soccer team players to ŌĆŗstay in the country after they sought asylum fearing persecution in their home nation.
The announcement came after U.S. President Donald Trump said he had ŌĆīspoken to Australian Prime Minister Anthony Albanese about the Iran women's soccer team in Australia following reports that the players had requested asylum.
The remaining players are in a hotel on the Gold Coast, Home Affairs Minister Tony Burke told a news conference, adding that he had also offered the other team members the chance to stay in Australia.
"I was able to tell ŌĆŗfive members of the Iranian women's soccer team that they are welcome to stay in Australia, that they are safe here, and they should ŌĆŗfeel at home here," Burke told reporters.
The Iranian women's soccer team are in Australia for the Asian Cup tournament.
Iran's semi-official ŌüĀFars news agency said five players in Australia for the Asian Cup secretly left the team hotel with Australian police, and media reports said they were ŌĆŗseeking Australian government assistance after "breaking free".
Having initially posted on social media that Australia was "making a terrible humanitarian mistake" by allowing the team to be sent back home, Trump said in a ŌĆŗlater post that he had spoken to Albanese and that the Australian leader was "doing a very good job having to do with this rather delicate situation".
Trump said five members of the Iran squad "have already been taken care of, and the rest are on their way.
"Some, however, feel they must go back because they are worried about the safety of their families, ŌĆŗincluding threats to those family members if they donŌĆÖt return," he said.
The Iranian team's campaign in the Australian-hosted Asian Cup tournament started just as the U.S. and ŌĆŗIsrael launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday after losing 2-0 to the Philippines .
In his earlier post Trump said members of ŌĆīthe team ŌüĀwould "likely be killed" if forced to return to Iran. "The U.S. will take them if you wonŌĆÖt, " he added.
Soccer's global players' union FIFPRO said on Monday there were serious concerns for the welfare of the Iran team after they were labelled for refusing to sing their national anthem before a game.
The players' decision to stand in silence during Iran's anthem before their first match against South Korea was labelled by a commentator on Islamic Republic of Iran Broadcasting as the "pinnacle of dishonour".
Iranian ŌĆŗmedia quoted Farideh Shojaei, vice president for ŌĆŗwomen's affairs at the Iranian Football ŌüĀFederation, as saying the team had left the hotel through the back door with the police.
"We have contacted the embassy, the football federation, the foreign ministry and anywhere possible to see what will happen," she said. "We have even spoken with ŌĆŗthe families of these five players.ŌĆØ
Iranian media named the players as Zahra Sarbali, Mona Hamoudi, Zahra Ghanbari, Fatemeh Pasandideh ŌĆŗand Atefeh Ramazani-Zadeh.
It said ŌüĀother team members were still in Australia and quoted Shojaei as saying the squad had planned to return to Iran via Dubai, but the United Arab Emirates had not allowed them to do so.
It said efforts were now expected to be made for the team to return via Malaysia and Turkey.
The Iranian team sang their national anthem and saluted ŌüĀbefore their second ŌĆŗmatch against Australia , sparking fears among human rights campaigners that the women had been coerced by ŌĆŗgovernment minders.
Australia granted emergency humanitarian visas to over 20 members of the Afghanistan women's cricket team after the Taliban returned to power in 2021.

item.title: Cuban university students stage rare protest amid energy, internet shortages
item.description:
HAVANA, March 9 (Reuters) - A group of just over 20 Cuban university students staged a rare, hours-long protest at ŌĆŗthe University of Havana on Monday, angry over class disruptions amid a U.S. oil blockade that has contributed to ŌĆīa near collapse of Cuba's electrical grid.
Students sat under a hot mid-morning sun, shading themselves with umbrellas while trading barbs with several of the university's professors and administration staff, who arrived shortly after the "sit-in" began.
"None of us wanted to get up early today in the dark, make a thermos of coffee, and sit ŌĆŗhere in the sun," said Anabel Oliva, a 20-year-old university student who spoke before the group. "But there was no other ŌĆŗway."
Students said officials had failed to take adequate measures to prevent major disruptions to classes as the ŌüĀUnited States has ratcheted up sanctions and pressure on the Caribbean island nation.
The United States in January cut off oil shipments from ŌĆŗVenezuela, which further decimated an already failing electrical grid, starving it of fuel and forcing ripple effects on transportation, food supply and internet ŌĆŗcommunications.
Cuba announced a series of measures in January, including distance learning as during the COVID-19 pandemic, that officials said would assure continuity for students of the island's once-robust higher-education system.
Students said those measures were not enough, and opted to protest to ensure their voices were heard.
Jose Julian Diaz, a university professor and ŌĆŗformer student representative, told Reuters the extraordinary circumstances required dialogue.
"These kinds of scenarios are really rare," he said in an interview. "I think we ŌĆŗhave to learn to have these discussions with students."
University security initially prohibited the protesting students from sitting on the stone steps at the main entrance ŌĆīof the ŌüĀUniversity of Havana, blocking the entrance with a long rope and a sign that read: "Do not pass, do not sit."
The students eventually walked up the steps and past the rope, however, prompting a rush of security guards who were eventually called off by Higher Education Vice Minister Modesto Ricardo Gomez.
The vice minister told reporters the students had raised legitimate concerns, and blamed the ŌĆŗTrump administration's tightening sanctions for ŌĆŗthe problems. He said there ŌüĀwould be no repercussions for those who protested.
"There are no consequences whatsoever. They have a concern, and we are addressing it. It will always be like this," Gomez said.
While the minister spoke to reporters, ŌĆŗthe students left the steps and entered the university with administration officials and teachers, declining requests for ŌĆŗinterviews.
The minister said ŌüĀthe dialogue would continue inside the university.
One student, who did not give his name to a reporter but spoke before cameras, said there would be more protests if the issues were not resolved.
"We are willing to engage in dialogue if it is to represent our demands, but if ŌüĀdialogue, as ŌĆŗhas already happened, does not work, we will sit down here again.ŌĆØ
Protests of any ŌĆŗkind in Communist-run Cuba are exceedingly rare. Cuba's 2019 constitution grants citizens the right to protest, but a law more specifically defining that right is stalled in the ŌĆŗlegislature, leaving those who take to the street in legal limbo.

item.title: Trading Day: Wall Street roars back on war resolution hopes
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - Stocks in Asia and Europe tumbled on Monday as the Iran war entered its second week and oil prices surged as much as 30% above $100 a barrel. But Wall Street rallied and oil later sank after President Donald Trump indicated the war may soon be over.
In my column today I put ŌĆŗWall Street's resilience under the microscope. As selling snowballs across other equity markets, why has the global avalanche not yet engulfed U.S. ŌĆīstocks? Are there reasonable explanations, or is complacency setting in?
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Central bank paralysis
There can be no doubt now - the eye-watering surge in oil prices since the U.S. and Israel launched their joint attack on Iran on February 28 has put central banks in an unenviable bind. Price pressures are clearly intensifying, but the impact of $100 a barrel oil on economic activity will be ŌĆŗharsh.
Do they hike rates to nip inflation in the bud, or adopt a more dovish stance in the face of rising unemployment and potential recession? In ŌĆŗthe U.S., the labor market was already creaking, household savings are run down, and now gas prices are soaring. Higher borrowing costs will hurt the average consumer. But so will ŌĆīhigher inflation.
* ŌüĀPre-war price pressures
Even before the Middle East crisis erupted, price pressures around the world were bubbling up. Figures on Monday showed that consumer inflation in China jumped in February to the highest in three years, Mexico 's inflation rose above the central bank's target, and real wages in Japan rose for the first time in 13 months.
With oil smashing through $100 a barrel - and now up considerably on the same period last year - inflation signals are only pointing one way. U.S. PCE inflation figures for February due ŌĆŗout later this week are expected to ŌĆŗfollow a similar path, rising further ŌüĀabove 3%.
* To tap or not to tap?
Countries around the world are suddenly scrambling for ways to cushion the economic blow of $100 oil. Releasing emergency oil reserves is an obvious option, but it seems like it's not going to be exercised ŌĆŗjust yet. G7 countries discussed it on Monday but say there's no immediate supply shortfall. Not yet, anyway.
Elsewhere, China has capped ŌĆŗfuel prices, South Korea is ŌüĀconsidering a similar move for the first time in 30 years, and Japan is preparing for a possible release of crude and could draw down cash reserves set aside for emergency spending. Authorities will be reluctant to raise interest rates, so they may need to get inventive.
What could move markets tomorrow?
Want to receive Trading Day in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for my newsletter here .
Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, ŌĆŗunder the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: US considering selling oil from strategic reserve, US energy chief says
item.description:
PLATTEVILLE, Colorado, March 9 (Reuters) - The U.S. is considering coordinating ŌĆŗsales of oil from the U.S. Strategic Petroleum Reserve with ŌĆīreleases from other countries as prices soar during the war on Iran , Energy Secretary Chris Wright said on Monday.
Wright also said the U.S. has "some other options" on allowing ŌĆŗmore sales of Russian oil held in tankers on the ŌĆŗwater in Asia. Late last week, for instance, Washington issued ŌüĀa 30-day waiver allowing the sale of Russian crude currently ŌĆŗstranded at sea to continue to India.
"We are talking about coordinated releases ŌĆŗfrom the SPR," Wright told reporters gathered at a natural gas plant in Colorado. The U.S. SPR , located on the coasts of Texas and Louisiana, holds 415 million ŌĆŗbarrels of oil, more than what the entire world uses in ŌĆŗfour days.
Global and domestic oil futures settled on Monday at the highest level ŌĆīsince ŌüĀAugust 2022 as the war led by the U.S. and Israel stranded tankers and shut in oil production in the Middle East.
The International Energy Agency, the West's energy watchdog, on Monday called for a coordinated ŌĆŗrelease of oil, ŌĆŗand G7 countries ŌüĀagreed to closely monitor developments in the energy markets. No release has yet been announced.
Fatih Birol, the ŌĆŗIEA head, told G7 finance ministers on Monday that ŌĆŗhis ŌüĀgroup's members hold more than 1.2 billion barrels of public emergency oil stocks and a further 600 million barrels of industry stocks are under ŌüĀgovernment ŌĆŗobligation.
The U.S. was not considering imposing restrictions ŌĆŗon exports of U.S. energy as a way to control prices, Wright said.

item.title: Canadian PM, Qatari emir discuss diplomacy to prevent wider war in Middle East
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney spoke with Qatar's Emir Sheikh ŌĆŗTamim bin Hamad al-Thani on Monday about avoiding a wider ŌĆīconflict in the Middle East , Carney's office said.
Here are some details:
ŌĆó They "underscored the importance of intensifying diplomatic engagement to avoid a wider conflict with ŌĆŗglobal consequences," Carney's office said in a statement.
ŌĆó The statement ŌĆŗsaid the leaders condemned Iranian missile and drone attacks ŌüĀon Qatar and Carney expressed Canada's solidarity with Qatar.
ŌĆó The U.S. ŌĆŗand Israel began attacking Iran on February 28. Iran responded with its ŌĆŗown strikes against Israel and Gulf states with U.S. bases. U.S. President Donald Trump has thus far offered shifting objectives and timelines for the war that ŌĆŗhas killed scores in Iran , including Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei .
ŌĆó "In accordance with ŌĆŗinternational law, the leaders called for an immediate end to attacks on civilians ŌĆīand ŌüĀcivilian infrastructure that only serve to destabilise the region and worsen the global economic and energy situation," Carney's office said, adding he and the Qatari emir discussed efforts to de-escalate the Iran war .
ŌĆó ŌĆŗAt least 1,230 ŌĆŗpeople have been ŌüĀkilled in Iran, including 175 schoolgirls and staff killed in a missile strike on a primary ŌĆŗschool in southern Iran, according to non-profit humanitarian ŌĆŗgroup Iranian ŌüĀRed Crescent Society.
ŌĆó The Iranian army additionally says over 100 were killed after a U.S. submarine sank an Iranian warship off Sri ŌüĀLanka's ŌĆŗcoast last week.
ŌĆó Eleven civilians have been ŌĆŗkilled in Israel, according to Israeli tallies. The U.S. military has said seven of ŌĆŗits service members have been killed.

item.title: Alexander brothers, top NY real estate brokers, convicted at sex trafficking trial
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 9 (Reuters) - Three ŌĆŗwealthy brothers, including two of New York's top real estate brokers, were found ŌĆīguilty on Monday at a closely watched sex trafficking trial over allegations they lured women and girls to exclusive parties where they drugged and raped them.
Oren Alexander, Tal Alexander and Alon Alexander had each pleaded not ŌĆŗguilty to all charges they respectively faced in an indictment alleging trafficking and other criminal ŌĆŗconduct related to seven victims.
Oren and Tal Alexander co-founded the luxury real ŌüĀestate brokerage Official, while Alon Alexander was an executive at a private security company.
Defense lawyers ŌĆŗhad contended that the women were willing participants who were not paid to have sex.
Tal Alexander ŌĆŗfaced seven charges in total, and his twin brothers Oren and Alon faced six.
Over the course of a six-week trial in Manhattan federal court, eleven women testified that one or more of the brothers sexually ŌĆŗabused them.
In his closing argument on March 3, prosecutor Andrew Jones said the brothers masqueraded ŌĆŗas party boys but really were predators.
"They used a consistent playbook to lure, isolate and rape their victims," ŌĆīJones ŌüĀsaid. "They did it with callousness and a perverse sense of pride."
Defense lawyers acknowledged that the brothers had discussed sex amongst themselves in crass terms and behaved in ways that angered women with whom they had relationships. But they argued the sexual encounters at issue were consensual.
"He should ŌĆŗbe and is embarrassed ŌĆŗby how he behaved ŌüĀover these years," Howard Srebnick, a lawyer for Alon Alexander, said in his closing argument on March 3. "That doesn't make the conduct a ŌĆŗcrime."
The three brothers were arrested in December 2024 and have been ŌĆŗjailed since ŌüĀthen at Brooklyn's Metropolitan Detention Center.
Each brother had his own legal defense team. Oren Alexander's lawyers include Marc Agnifilo and Teny Geragos, who last year defended rapper and music mogul Sean "Diddy" Combs at his ŌüĀsex ŌĆŗtrafficking trial.
Combs is serving a four-year prison sentence after being ŌĆŗconvicted on prostitution charges, but acquitted of the more serious sex trafficking charges he faced that could have landed him ŌĆŗin prison for life.

item.title: FBI examines Arizona election records in widening probe of Trump vote fraud claims
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - The FBI has obtained records related ŌĆŗto the 2020 election in Arizona , a top state lawmaker said on Monday, as federal ŌĆīlaw enforcement continues to pursue President Donald Trump's false claims that his defeat in that campaign was the result of voting fraud.
The FBI subpoenaed the Arizona Senate last week for material connected to a Republican-led audit of the 2020 election ŌĆŗresults in Maricopa County, the most populous county in the state, Arizona Senate President ŌĆŗWarren Petersen said in a social media post.
"The FBI has the records," Petersen, ŌüĀa Republican, wrote in the post.
The demand for information from Arizona is the latest instance of ŌĆŗthe Trump administration deploying U.S. law enforcement and intelligence agencies to gather evidence of alleged irregularities in the 2020 ŌĆŗelection, despite courts and audits repeatedly rejecting claims of widespread voter fraud.
It was not immediately clear what crimes the FBI may be investigating. An FBI spokesperson did not immediately respond to a request for comment. A spokesperson for ŌĆŗPetersen declined to elaborate on his social media post.
The FBI in January seized 2020 ballots from a Georgia ŌĆŗcounty elections center as part of a court-approved search of the facility. Fulton County has sued for the ŌĆīreturn of ŌüĀthe material, alleging the search violated the U.S. Constitution and relied on discredited allegations.
The 2021 Maricopa County audit, which was run by Trump's Republican allies in Arizona, largely affirmed the initial 2020 election results, finding slightly more votes for Trump's Democratic opponent Joe Biden . Trump's loss in Maricopa County was key ŌĆŗto his narrow defeat to ŌĆŗBiden statewide.
The audit ŌüĀmade several claims of election irregularities, which county officials vigorously disputed .
"What the Trump administration appears to be pursuing now is not a legitimate law enforcement inquiry," ŌĆŗArizona State Attorney General Kris Mayes, a Democrat, said in a statement. "It ŌĆŗis the weaponization ŌüĀof federal law enforcement in service of crackpots and lies."
The investigations into Georgia and Arizona, two states that have been hotly contested in recent presidential elections, come as Trump has spoken openly about the ŌüĀfederal government taking ŌĆŗcontrol of elections in certain unspecified areas of the ŌĆŗcountry.
Trump has put a lawyer, Kurt Olsen, who backed the "Stop the Steal" movement to overturn the 2020 election results, i n charge ŌĆŗof an effort to reexamine the 2020 vote.

item.title: Colombia's ruling party to be top force in Senate, but without majority
item.description:
BOGOTA, March 9 (Reuters) - Colombia's ruling party, the left-leaning Historic Pact, emerged from Sunday's elections as the strongest political force in the incoming Senate, ŌĆŗanalysts said on Monday, although its failure to secure an absolute ŌĆīmajority means it will need to form coalitions and may struggle if Colombia's divided right wing wins the presidency.
The party, which brought President Gustavo Petro to power in 2022, ŌĆŗwon 25 out of 102 Senate seats in Sunday's legislative elections, according ŌĆŗto official results. Finishing second was the right-wing Democratic Center, led by ŌüĀformer President Alvaro Uribe, with 17 seats.
The two forces will likely jostle ŌĆŗto make deals with other parties in the fragmented Senate, which include Colombia's ŌĆŗLiberal Party with 13 seats, the Green Alliance with 10, the Conservative Party with 10, La U with nine, and Radical Change with seven. Eleven other seats were distributed among ŌĆŗvarious smaller parties.
In the House of Representatives, results from the electoral authority ŌĆŗshow the opposition Democratic Center leading with 32 out of 182 seats, followed by the ŌĆīLiberal ŌüĀParty with 31, and the Historic Pact with 29. The Conservative Party obtained 18 seats, Radical Change 13, and the Green Alliance 7.
Ivan Cepeda, Historic Pact's presidential candidate in May's presidential election, described the legislative election results as a "categorical ŌĆŗvictory," saying his ŌĆŗparty had become ŌüĀthe primary political force in Congress.
However, analysts suggest that if Cepeda loses the presidential election to right-wing candidates Paloma ŌĆŗValencia or Abelardo De La Espriella, his party will become ŌĆŗthe main ŌüĀopposition force.
Ariel Avila, a re-elected senator from the Green Alliance, believes that Colombia's next president, whether from the right or the left, will have a tough ŌüĀtime getting ŌĆŗthings done, predicting a "vetocracy" where lawmakers block parties ŌĆŗsimply because they come from the opposing side.
"It is a very fragmented Congress and it is ŌĆŗdifficult to mend," Avila said.

item.title: Alexander Butterfield, White House aide who exposed Nixon's taping system, dead at 99
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Alexander Butterfield, the White House aide who disclosed Richard Nixon's secret audio taping system, providing the "smoking gun" of the Watergate scandal that brought down the president, has died at age 99.
The death of Butterfield, whose revelation about the listening devices and recording system set off a ŌĆŗroaring legal battle over the president's right of executive privilege, was confirmed by his wife Kim to The Washington Post and the New York Times.
Both newspapers said he died a ŌĆīmonth shy of his 100th birthday at his home in the seaside La Jolla area of San Diego, but no cause of death was cited.
Butterfield once told journalist Alicia Shepard he disliked being known as the man who exposed the existence of the tapes because it made it seem as if he had "eagerly and breathlessly" told the Watergate congressional committee about them.
A native of Pensacola, Florida, who grew up in California, Butterfield attended UCLA before joining the U.S. Air Force in 1948 and went on ŌĆŗto serve as a combat pilot during the Vietnam War, commanding a squadron of tactical reconnaissance aircraft. He later served as a military assistant to a high-ranking Pentagon aide, gaining White House exposure ŌĆŗin that job.
Butterfield finally left the Air Force to join the White House staff as a deputy to Nixon's chief of staff, H.R. Haldeman, an old friend ŌüĀfrom UCLA. Among Butterfield's White House duties was keeping a historical record of the presidency, which included overseeing installation of the voice-activated taping system.
Butterfield had left the White House for the top job at the Federal Aviation ŌĆŗAdministration by the time the investigation into the June 17, 1972, break-in at Democratic headquarters in the Watergate building was heating up.
He was one of the few in the White House who knew about the recording system, ŌĆŗand upon learning that he would be questioned by the Senate Watergate Committee, known officially as the Select Committee on Presidential Campaign Activities, Butterfield resolved neither to lie nor volunteer information.
A Republican staff attorney for the committee was questioning him in a private preliminary session when he asked if the White House had a recording system. Butterfield reluctantly acknowledged there was.
On July 16, 1973, three days after his initial closed-door disclosure, Butterfield appeared before the Senate committee at a televised meeting, and Fred ŌĆŗThompson, then counsel for the Republicans on the committee and a future actor and senator, asked the same question.
After a long pause, Butterfield said, "I was aware of listening devices, yes, sir."
It was stunning news for the nation ŌĆŗbecause it meant there was an actual record of what Nixon said, when he said it and to whom he said it.
Butterfield said the recording system had been secretly taping conversations and meetings in the Oval Office, Nixon's office in ŌĆīthe Executive Office ŌüĀBuilding and the Cabinet room, as well as four White House telephones. The purpose of the recordings, he said, was historical.
In a 1975 interview with People magazine, Butterfield said Nixon often forgot about the recorders and had disregarded advice to destroy the tapes because he never thought the Watergate affair would reach a point where he would have to surrender them.
"I'm sure that he hates me as much as anyone can," Butterfield said of his former boss, who died in 1994.
He said he thought Nixon should have resigned earlier.
"I don't feel awful about the president's resignation," he said. "Not at all."
A recording made six days after the Watergate break-in proved to ŌĆŗbe Nixon's undoing - the "smoking gun" that showed he knew ŌĆŗof the cover-up. He was heard agreeing to ŌüĀa plan to have the break-in investigation halted for national security reasons.
The U.S. Supreme Court ultimately rejected White House claims of executive privilege and ordered Nixon to turn over the subpoenaed tapes as his public and political support withered. Rather than face impeachment and a Senate trial, he resigned on August 9, 1974.
Since he was not ŌĆŗinvolved with the break-in or cover-up, Butterfield was never indicted, but his old friend Haldeman would be among several Nixon insiders who went to prison in ŌĆŗthe scandal.
Butterfield was the focus ŌüĀof the 2015 book "The Last of the President's Men" by Bob Woodward, the Washington Post reporter who helped break the Watergate story, and he provided Woodward with thousands of documents he had secretly removed from Nixon administration offices.
The documents and interviews with Butterfield portrayed what the former White House aide described as a "cesspool" within the administration and characterized Nixon as odd, isolated and resentful.
Butterfield said he frequently was the target of hostility from Nixon loyalists and told Time ŌüĀmagazine that long-time ŌĆŗNixon secretary Rose Mary Woods, who said she accidentally erased 18 1/2 minutes of those White House tapes, had disparaged him as ŌĆŗa "son of a bitch" who had "destroyed the greatest leader this country ever had."
Butterfield worked as an adviser to Oliver Stone on his 1995 film "Nixon" and had a cameo role in it as a White House staffer.
Butterfield's first marriage, to Charlotte Maguire, ended in divorce in 1985. ŌĆŗHe also had previously dated Audrey Geisel, widow of the children's author-illustrator Theodor Seuss Geisel, better known as Dr. Seuss.

item.title: Trump on Cuba: May or may not be a "friendly takeover"
item.description:
DORAL, Fla., March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump said on Monday Cuba is in "deep ŌĆŗtrouble" on a humanitarian basis and ŌĆīthat U.S. Secretary of State Marco Rubio was dealing with the issue which may or may ŌĆŗnot be a "friendly takeover."
"He's dealing (with it) ŌĆŗand it may be a friendly takover, ŌüĀit may not be a friendly takeover. ŌĆŗWouldn't really matter because they're really down ŌĆŗto...as they say, fumes. They have no energy, they have no money," Trump, speaking at a news ŌĆŗconference in Doral, Florida told reporters.
The Cuban ŌĆŗgovernment has said it is not in any high-level ŌĆītalks ŌüĀwith the United States but has not outright denied press reports that U.S. officials may be in informal talks with Raul ŌĆŗGuillermo Rodriguez ŌĆŗCastro, the ŌüĀgrandson of former Cuban President Raul Castro.
Cuban exiles who are largely ŌĆŗconcentrated in Miami have long dreamed ŌĆŗof ŌüĀoverthrowing the Cuban government or seeing it fall and have in the past plotted ŌüĀagainst ŌĆŗthe government that was established ŌĆŗby the late revolutionary leader Fidel Castro.

item.title: Trump says he discussed Ukraine and Iran conflicts with Putin
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday told reporters he had a "very good call" with ŌĆŗRussian President Vladimir Putin about Ukraine ŌĆŗand the conflict in the Middle East.
"There's ŌüĀtremendous hatred between President Putin and (Ukraine's) ŌĆŗPresident Zelenskiy. They can't seem to ŌĆŗget it together, but I think it was a positive call on that subject," Trump said.
Trump ŌĆŗadded that Putin "wants to be helpful" ŌĆŗwith the Iran conflict.
"I said, 'You could be more helpful ŌĆīby ŌüĀgetting the Ukraine-Russia war over with. That will be more helpful,'" Trump told reporters in a press conference at ŌĆŗhis Florida ŌĆŗgolf club.
A ŌüĀforeign policy aide for Putin said on Monday that Putin proposed ŌĆŗan idea for a "quick political ŌĆŗand ŌüĀdiplomatic end to the Iranian conflict."
Trump recently has expressed dismay about the ongoing conflict ŌüĀbetween ŌĆŗRussia and Ukraine, which ŌĆŗstarted more than four years ago.

item.title: Russian drone injures six by apartment block in Ukraine's Kharkiv
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - A Russian drone ŌĆŗstruck an area near a ŌĆīhigh-rise apartment building on Monday in Ukraine's second-largest city, Kharkiv, ŌĆŗinjuring six people, smashing ŌĆŗwindows and setting cars ablaze, ŌüĀthe city's mayor said.
Ihor ŌĆŗTerekhov said a small child ŌĆŗwas among the injured.
Kharkiv, located 30 km (18 miles) from the Russian ŌĆŗborder, withstood early advances ŌĆŗby Russian forces after their full-scale invasion ŌĆīin ŌüĀFebruary 2022 and has since been a frequent target of Russian air attacks.
Separately, ŌĆŗRussian shelling ŌĆŗof ŌüĀthe southeastern city of Dnipro injured seven ŌĆŗpeople, regional Governor Oleksandr ŌĆŗGanzha ŌüĀsaid on Telegram. Ganzha posted photos online showing rubble ŌüĀin ŌĆŗstreets and damaged ŌĆŗbuilding facades.

item.title: Americans expect gasoline prices to keep rising after Iran strikes, Reuters/Ipsos poll finds
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Most Americans think gasoline prices are ŌĆŗgoing to rise in coming months following President Donald Trump 's decision to launch military strikes on Iran , and ŌĆīmany expect a protracted conflict, according to a Reuters/Ipsos poll that closed on Monday.
Some 67% of respondents in the four-day poll - including 44% of Republicans and 85% of Democrats - said they expect gas prices in the U.S. to get worse over the next year. ŌĆŗSixty percent of Americans expect U.S. military involvement in Iran will "go on for an extended period of ŌĆŗtime," according to the poll.
U.S. and Israeli forces launched coordinated strikes on Iran on February ŌüĀ28, killing the nation's leader in an initial surprise attack.
The latest Reuters/Ipsos poll found just 29% of Americans ŌĆŗapprove of the strikes, little changed from a 27% approval rate in a Reuters/Ipsos poll conducted in the hours immediately ŌĆŗfollowing the start of the military campaign. Both polls had margins of error of about 3 percentage points.
The latest poll underscores the political risks Trump brings to his Republican Party ahead of the November midterm elections when Democrats hope to seize control from Trump's party ŌĆŗof at least one chamber of Congress.
Some 64% of poll respondents - including one in four Republicans and nine ŌĆŗin 10 Democrats - said Trump has not clearly explained the goals of U.S. military involvement.
Trump returned to the White House ŌĆīlast ŌüĀyear after promising to tame inflation and prevent the military from getting stuck in a foreign conflict. Since he launched strikes on Iran, U.S. gasoline prices have surged by roughly 50 cents a gallon and at least seven U.S. soldiers have been killed.
Gasoline prices are highly sensitive in the U.S., as one of the most immediate and visceral ŌĆŗindications Americans have of increases ŌĆŗand decreases in their ŌüĀcost of living.
Energy prices were rising daily last week across the country and internationally, including during the weekend as the Reuters/Ipsos poll was conducted. Analysts expect weeks or months of ŌĆŗhigher fuel prices worldwide even if the week-old conflict ends quickly.
Trump is considering ways to ŌĆŗcombat surging ŌüĀprices, but U.S. policy options could have limited sway over global oil markets. On Monday, he told reporters the military operation in Iran is ŌĆ£ahead of our initial timeline by a lot.ŌĆØ
Forty-nine percent of Americans - including a third of Republicans and two-thirds ŌüĀof Democrats - ŌĆŗthink the war in Iran will have a mostly negative impact on ŌĆŗtheir personal finances. About one in three Republicans said they were not sure how the war would affect their finances.
The Reuters/Ipsos poll, which ŌĆŗwas conducted online, surveyed 1,021 U.S. adults nationwide.

item.title: Trump says he is 'disappointed' that Mojtaba Khamenei became Iran's supreme leader
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. ŌĆīPresident Donald Trump said on Monday he was "disappointed" that ŌĆŗIran named Mojtaba ŌĆŗKhamenei to succeed his ŌüĀslain father Ayatollah ŌĆŗAli Khamenei as the ŌĆŗsupreme leader of the country.
"We think it's going to ŌĆŗlead to just ŌĆŗmore of the same problem ŌĆīfor ŌüĀthe country," Trump told reporters at a press conference in ŌĆŗFlorida.
When ŌĆŗasked ŌüĀwhether the new leader had a ŌĆŗtarget on his ŌĆŗback, ŌüĀTrump said it would be "inappropriate" to say ŌüĀwhether ŌĆŗor not ŌĆŗhe does.

item.title: National Bureau of Economic Research cuts ties with Larry Summers, WSJ reports
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Former U.S. Treasury Secretary Larry Summers has lost his affiliation with The ŌĆŗNational Bureau of Economic Research, the ŌĆīWall Street Journal reported on Monday, citing an email from the research organization's president.
Losing the ŌĆŗNBER affiliation would be the latest ŌĆŗfallout for Summers from his ties with ŌüĀlate convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein. ŌĆŗSummers said last month he would resign from ŌĆŗteaching at Harvard University at the end of the academic year, months after receiving a lifetime ban ŌĆŗfrom The American Economic Association.
NBER said ŌĆŗan ad hoc committee reviewed Summers' conduct and recommended ŌĆīthe ŌüĀtermination of his appointment as a research associate with the agency, the report said. "He is no longer an NBER affiliate," ŌĆŗwrote NBER ŌĆŗpresident James ŌüĀPoterba in an email to the Journal.
Documents released by the ŌĆŗU.S. House Oversight Committee showed a ŌĆŗclose ŌüĀrelationship between Summers and Epstein that continued well after the disgraced financier's 2008 ŌüĀconviction. ŌĆŗThere is no evidence Summers ŌĆŗwas involved in Epstein's sex trafficking activities.

item.title: Australia to send missiles to UAE, deploy military surveillance aircraft
item.description:
SYDNEY, March 10 (Reuters) - Australia will deploy a military surveillance ŌĆīaircraft to the Middle East and send missiles to the United Arab Emirates but will not put troops on the ground in ŌĆŗIran, Prime Minister Anthony Albanese said on Tuesday.
Australia's ŌĆŗmilitary support would help the Gulf countries defend ŌüĀthemselves against unprovoked attacks from Iran, Albanese said, stressing Australia ŌĆŗwas "not a protagonist".
"Our involvement is purely defensive," Albanese told ŌĆŗreporters. "And it's in defence of Australians who are in the region as well as in defence of our friends in the United ŌĆŗArab Emirates."
Albanese said Australia would deploy one of its ŌĆŗBoeing-manufactured E-7A Wedgetail airborne early warning and control system aircraft for ŌĆīan ŌüĀinitial four weeks to protect the airspace above the Gulf countries.
Advanced medium-range air-to-air missiles would be provided to the United Arab Emirates, following a phone call with President ŌĆŗMohammed bin ŌĆŗZayed Al Nahyan, ŌüĀhe added.
The war has effectively shut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for a ŌĆŗfifth of global oil and liquefied natural ŌĆŗgas, ŌüĀas the conflict escalated.
Around 115,000 Australians were in the Middle East when the conflict began 11 days ago and ŌüĀ2,600 Australians ŌĆŗhave now returned home by ŌĆŗcommercial flights as several Gulf cities came under Iranian bombardment.

item.title: Trump says US is waiving certain oil-related sanctions to ensure supply
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - President Donald Trump ŌĆŗsaid on Monday that the ŌĆīUnited States is waiving certain oil-related sanctions as a way to ensure ŌĆŗadequate oil supply and lower ŌĆŗprices.
At a news conference at his ŌüĀDoral golf club, Trump ŌĆŗsaid oil prices have not spiked ŌĆŗas much as he had feared.
Trump said the United States is waiving certain ŌĆŗoil-related sanctions to cut ŌĆŗprices.
"We have sanctions on some countries. We're ŌĆīgoing ŌüĀto take those sanctions off till the Strait (of Hormuz) is up," Trump said.
He was not specific, ŌĆŗbut the ŌĆŗUnited ŌüĀStates last week issued a temporary, 30-day waiver ŌĆŗto allow for the ŌĆŗsale ŌüĀof Russian oil currently stranded at sea to India to alleviate ŌüĀpressure ŌĆŗon the global ŌĆŗoil market.

item.title: Petrobras rejects extra diesel orders as prices in Brazil lag global market
item.description:
RIO DE JANEIRO, March 9 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company Petrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab is refusing requests from fuel distributors for additional diesel volumes as domestic prices fall to a record discount versus ŌĆŗglobal levels, four sources familiar with the matter told Reuters.
Industry data shows that diesel ŌĆīsold by Petrobras, the main supplier of the fuel in Brazil , is currently 85% cheaper than imports, as global prices rise due to the U.S.-Iran conflict in the Middle East .
The imbalance between the two prices is stalling domestic diesel sales, impacting the country's ŌĆŗbooming agricultural sector during harvest season, said sources.
Petrobras is selling only the contractually obligated diesel amount ŌĆŗto its distributors, said a company source, adding that the firm won't sell extra ŌüĀso distributors can store it and make a profit when the state-run oil firm raises prices.
"We ŌĆŗcannot hand out additional diesel now so distributors can stock up cheaply and then profit later," said the ŌĆŗsource.
Petrobras CEO Magda Chambriard said last week the company does not pass short-term global volatility through to consumers and is still assessing the new oil price level before considering adjustments.
A spike in diesel prices would be the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's ŌĆŗfarm sector from the Middle East conflict, raising costs for producers harvesting a record soybean crop and ŌĆŗplanting corn they cannot afford to delay.
In Brazil, Petrobras is responsible for around 55% of diesel production, giving it outsized ŌĆīinfluence ŌüĀover the price of fuel, said Sergio Araujo, head of Brazilian fuel importers association Abicom.
The steep discount is drawing buyers away from imported cargoes and private refiners, reshaping fuel flows and creating logistical strains that are leading to supply imbalances, Araujo said.
The uncertainty is already rippling into the ŌĆŗagricultural hub of Rio Grande ŌĆŗdo Sul, a ŌüĀstate with two refineries and normally ample diesel supply, where farmers have reported difficulty sourcing fuel.
Oil regulator ANP said on Sunday it would investigate complaints from rural ŌĆŗproducers about supply constraints during the peak harvest period.
ANP said it contacted major ŌĆŗsuppliers over ŌüĀthe weekend and concluded that Rio Grande do Sul "has sufficient stocks to ensure regular diesel supply."
Araujo said the issue is not with supply, but with buyers in the region looking to purchase at current Petrobras prices, while sellers ask ŌüĀfor higher ŌĆŗprices to hedge against potential costs if the state-run firm ŌĆŗadjusts them in the coming weeks.
"Sellers think they'll have to pay more to restock," said Araujo.
Petrobras did not immediately reply to a ŌĆŗrequest for comment.

item.title: Democratic US senators demand immediate hearings on Iran war
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Six Democrats in the U.S. Senate on Monday said they are embarking on an intensified campaign for public hearings on the Iran war, with top Trump administration officials testifying under ŌĆŗoath and to take steps to interrupt regular Senate business if Republicans resist.
Senator Cory Booker ŌĆīof New Jersey told reporters that the senior Democrats on the Foreign Relations and Armed Services committees have made the requests to Republican chairmen James Risch and Roger Wicker, respectively.
The senators said their goal is to bring a quick end ŌĆŗto the Iran war and protect American troops, who already have suffered some casualties.
Aides to the ŌĆŗcommittees did not immediately comment.
Senator Chris Murphy of Connecticut said such hearings should be ŌüĀheld next week, with Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth and Secretary of State and Acting National Security Adviser ŌĆŗMarco Rubio testifying.
Administration officials have held closed-door, classified briefings for Congress. Democrats argue that the American public needs ŌĆŗto hear from them on the possible duration of the war, Trump's specific goals and related matters.
Republicans currently hold a narrow, 53-47 Senate majority, which gives them the power to control what legislation comes to the floor for debate.
But Booker noted ŌĆŗthat individual senators have "a tremendous amount of power to disrupt the normal functioning of the Senate, as ŌĆŗwell as certain privileges that we can exercise."
He said he would not detail the steps this group of senators is ŌĆīwilling to ŌüĀtake.
Calling the Iran war "the biggest military engagement since the war in Afghanistan," Booker said, "We are not going to let the Senate continue as business as usual."
Both the House of Representatives, which also is run by Republicans, and the Senate have rejected Democrats' calls for debate and votes on the War Powers Act to authorize ŌĆŗU.S. military attacks on Iran.
Trump ŌĆŗhas provided a series ŌüĀof rationales, often changing, for unilaterally launching the war.
The results of a Reuters/Ipsos poll released on Monday showed deep public discontent over the war.
Sixty percent of Americans expect ŌĆŗthe U.S. military engagement to continue "for an extended period of time," causing gasoline ŌĆŗprices to ŌüĀrise and only 29% of adults approve of the attacks, the poll found.
While Republicans mainly have stood by as President Donald Trump has taken actions that have bypassed Congress' powers to appropriate funding and to levy tariffs, ŌüĀDemocrats have ŌĆŗhad some successes in getting the Senate to stand up to ŌĆŗTrump on those issues.
Joining Booker and Murphy in this effort are Senators Tammy Duckworth of Illinois, an Iraq war veteran, Adam Schiff ŌĆŗof California, Tammy Baldwin of Wisconsin and Tim Kaine of Virginia.

item.title: US says UN aid to Afghanistan needs evaluation
item.description:
March 9 (Reuters) - Despite what it called a humanitarian "disaster" in Afghanistan, the U.S. said on Monday international assistance to the country should be evaluated, given Taliban "intransigence" and ŌĆŗits exclusion of the female population from basic rights.
Speaking to a ŌĆīU.N. Security Council meeting on Afghanistan, the U.S. ambassador to the United Nations , Mike Waltz, noted that the budget for the United Nations Assistance Mission in Afghanistan (UNAMA), the mandate of which is ŌĆŗup for renewal next week, is the largest of any special U.N. ŌĆŗmission in the world.
"In light of the Taliban's intransigence, we must ŌüĀcarefully evaluate the utility of international assistance and engagement in Afghanistan," Waltz said, ŌĆŗeven as he highlighted an ongoing "humanitarian disaster" there.
"This council must consider carefully the ŌĆŗfunds we collectively provide for this mission's budget, when the mission's female national staff are not even able to go into the office to work," he added.
Afghanistan under the Taliban faces one ŌĆŗof the world's most pressing humanitarian crises.
According to the U.N. World Food Programme, ŌĆŗmore than 17 million Afghans - or one-third of the population - are facing acute food shortages, ŌĆīincluding 4.7 ŌüĀmillion facing emergency levels of hunger.
The temporary head of UNAMA, Georgette Gagnon, told the meeting Afghanistan had "urgent" humanitarian needs and the humanitarian crisis there had worsened due to funding cuts. She said humanitarian agencies aimed to assist 17.5 million Afghans ŌĆŗin 2026 through an ŌĆŗappeal for $1.71 billion, ŌüĀbut this was currently only 10% funded.
Gagnon said Afghanistan's nearly two-week conflict with Pakistan had had "punishing human and economic costs" ŌĆŗand the Iran war on its other border was causing ŌĆŗprices of ŌüĀbasic commodities to rise.
She said some positive developments showed the value of international engagement, including the Taliban ban on opium poppy cultivation. She warned that if rights and ŌüĀhumanitarian issues ŌĆŗwere not dealt with, Afghanistan could "once again become ŌĆŗa driver of regional and global instability in the form of outmigration, terrorism, narcotics and more."

item.title: Indonesia says 7 killed in landfill collapse as rescue operation ends
item.description:
JAKARTA, March 10 (Reuters) - Indonesian authorities have ended rescue operations at a landfill collapse that killed ŌĆŗseven people as all those who ŌĆīwere at the site have been accounted for, an official said on Tuesday.
A mountain of garbage collapsed ŌĆŗon Sunday at the Bantargebang landfill, ŌĆŗthe largest in the country, triggered by ŌüĀheavy rains.
Rescuers wrapped up the search ŌĆŗoperation at the site on the outskirts of ŌĆŗJakarta, after finding the last of seven victims buried in the landfill, said Desiana Kartika Bahari, head ŌĆŗof the local rescue agency. Six people ŌĆŗsurvived the incident.
On Monday, the agency had said that four ŌĆīpeople ŌüĀwere killed, four had survived and five were still missing.
"We received information from police that two among those missing were safe ŌĆŗand had returned ŌĆŗto ŌüĀtheir homes," Bahari said.
The casualties were truck drivers and owners of ŌĆŗfood stalls at the landfill site, ŌĆŗshe ŌüĀsaid.
More than 200 rescuers used excavators and thermal drones to find the victims during the ŌüĀsearch ŌĆŗoperation.
Bantargebang spans around 110 hectares ŌĆŗand receives 6,500 to 7,000 tons of waste every ŌĆŗday.

item.title: Taiwan says US has not approached it about weapons transfers to Middle East
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 10 (Reuters) - The United States has not approached Taiwan about transfers of weapons to the Middle East, ŌĆŗDefence Minister Wellington Koo said on Tuesday, after ŌĆīSouth Korea said it was in talks about the possible redeployment of some U.S. Patriot missile systems.
South Korean Foreign Minister ŌĆŗCho Hyun said on Friday the U.S. and ŌĆŗSouth Korean militaries are discussing the possible redeployment of ŌüĀsome U.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in ŌĆŗSouth Korea to be used in the war against ŌĆŗIran.

item.title: South Korea president says can't stop US forces from redeploying weapons
item.description:
SEOUL, March 10 (Reuters) - South Korea President Lee Jae Myung said ŌĆŗon Tuesday that his country ŌĆīcould not stop U.S. forces in Korea from shipping some weapons ŌĆŗout of the Asian ŌĆŗcountry, but that it would ŌüĀnot impact deterrence against ŌĆŗNorth Korea.
"It appears that there ŌĆŗis a controversy recently over U.S. Forces in Korea shipping some weapons ŌĆŗout of the country," ŌĆŗLee said, noting while Seoul had expressed ŌĆīopposition ŌüĀit was not in a position to make demands.
South Korean Foreign Minister Cho Hyun ŌĆŗsaid on ŌĆŗFriday ŌüĀthe U.S. and South Korean militaries were ŌĆŗdiscussing the possible redeployment ŌĆŗof ŌüĀsome U.S. Patriot missile defence systems based in South Korea ŌüĀto ŌĆŗbe used in ŌĆŗthe conflict in the Middle East.

item.title: Lawsuit challenges Trump administration's ending of protections for Somalis
item.description:
BOSTON, March 9 (Reuters) - Immigrant rights advocates filed a lawsuit on Monday seeking to stop U.S. President Donald ŌĆŗTrump's administration from next week ending legal protections that allow nearly 1,100 Somalis to live and ŌĆīwork in the United States.
The lawsuit , opens new tab , brought by four Somalis and two advocacy groups, challenges the U.S. Department of Homeland Security's decision to end Temporary Protected Status for Somali immigrants, whom Trump has derided in public remarks.
Outgoing Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem in January announced that TPS ŌĆŗfor Somalis would end on March 17, arguing that Somalia's conditions had improved, despite fighting continuing between Somali forces ŌĆŗand al-Shabaab militants.
The plaintiffs, who include the groups African Communities Together and Partnership for the ŌüĀAdvancement of New Americans, in the lawsuit filed in Boston federal court argue the move was procedurally flawed and ŌĆŗdriven by a discriminatory, predetermined agenda.
The lawsuit cites a series of statements Trump has made describing Somalis as "garbage" and "low ŌĆŗIQ people" who "contribute nothing."
The plaintiffs said the administration is ending TPS for Somalia and other countries due to unconstitutional bias against non-white immigrants, not based on objective assessments of country conditions.
"The termination of TPS for Somalia is racism masking as immigration policy," Omar Farah, executive ŌĆŗdirector at the legal group Muslim Advocates, said in a statement.
DHS did not respond to a request for comment. It has ŌĆŗpreviously said TPS was "never intended to be a de facto amnesty program."
TPS is a form of humanitarian immigration protection that shields eligible ŌĆīmigrants ŌüĀfrom deportation and allows them to work. Under Noem, DHS has moved to end TPS for a dozen countries, sparking legal challenges.
The administration on Saturday announced plans to pursue an appeal at the U.S. Supreme Court in order to end TPS for over 350,000 Haitians. It also wants the high court to allow it to end TPS for about 6,000 Syrians.
Somalia was first designated for ŌĆŗTPS in 1991, with ŌüĀits latest extension in 2024. About 1,082 Somalis currently hold TPS, and 1,383 more have pending applications, according to DHS.
Somalis in Minnesota in recent months had become a target of ŌĆŗTrump's immigration crackdown, with officials pointing to a fraud scandal in which many people ŌĆŗcharged come from ŌüĀthe state's large Somali community.
The Trump administration cited those fraud allegations as a basis for a months-long immigration enforcement surge in Democratic-led Minnesota, during which about 3,000 immigration agents were deployed, spurring protests and leading to the killing of two U.S. citizens by ŌüĀfederal agents.
In ŌĆŗNovember, Trump announced he would end TPS for Somalis in Minnesota, ŌĆŗand a month later said he wanted them sent "back to where they came from."
The U.S. Department of State advises against traveling to Somalia, citing crime ŌĆŗand civil unrest among numerous factors.

item.title: North Korea's Kim Yo Jong says joint US-South Korea drills to harm regional stability
item.description:
SEOUL, March 10 (Reuters) - North Korea's Kim Yo Jong, ŌĆŗthe sister of leader Kim Jong Un, ŌĆīsaid U.S.-South Korea military drills that began this week were a "provocative and aggressive war rehearsal" that would harm regional stability, state media ŌĆŗKCNA reported on Tuesday.
The annual Freedom Shield military ŌĆŗexercises revealed the allies' "habitual hostile policy" toward North ŌüĀKorea and "will further destroy regional stability," Kim said in a ŌĆŗstatement.
Kim described the drills in South Korea as involving ŌĆŗmore than 18,000 South Korean and U.S. forces and being staged "day and night across the territorial ground, sea, air, ŌĆŗouter space and cyberspace" of North Korea.
South Korea ŌĆŗand the United States have said the drills, which run from ŌĆīMarch ŌüĀ9 to 19, were "defensive in nature" and would incorporate deterrence scenarios related to North Korea's nuclear weapons.
The exercise will also serve as an opportunity to support ongoing ŌĆŗpreparations for ŌĆŗthe transfer ŌüĀof U.S. wartime operational control to South Korea, officials from both countries have ŌĆŗsaid.
South Korea aimed to complete the ŌĆŗhandover ŌüĀof military command from the U.S. before President Lee Jae Myung's term ends in 2030.
The drills come ŌüĀa ŌĆŗmonth after Kim Jong Un said ŌĆŗat North Korea's ruling Party Congress that he would focus on expanding his ŌĆŗcountry's nuclear arsenal.

item.title: Iran bets on endurance, energy disruption to outlast US, Israel
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters)- - Iran is wagering it can outlast the United States and Israel--not militarily, but by grinding the war into a brutal contest of endurance. Its strategy is stark: Unleash drones and missiles, cut vital energy routes and jolt global markets hard enough ŌĆŗto force Washington to blink first.
Despite the shock of the U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli strikes and the loss of key figures, the Islamic Revolutionary Guards Corps (IRGC)--long the ultimate guardian of the Islamic Republic -- is firmly ŌĆīin control, directing the battlefield, executing pre-planned contingencies and dictating strategy and targets in the war.
The IRGC also played the decisive role in elevating Mojtaba Khamenei as supreme leader after Ayatollah Ali Khamenei was killed in the opening U.S.ŌĆōIsraeli strikes.
ŌĆ£For them, they are waging an existential fight. This is an all-out war,ŌĆØ said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics. ŌĆ£They believe their very survival is at stake. They're willing to bring the temple down on everyoneŌĆÖs heads.ŌĆØAlex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute and expert on Iranian politics, added: ŌĆ£They're ŌĆŗlike a bleeding animal-- wounded, but therefore more dangerous than ever."
That all-out war mindset is behind IranŌĆÖs escalating strikes across the Gulf, targeting energy hubs from Qatar to Saudi Arabia to maximise economic disruption in ŌĆŗa calculated attempt to drive up costs for its neighbours, Europe and the United States and test WashingtonŌĆÖs political will.
U.S. President Donald Trump told Republican lawmakers on Monday the ŌüĀwar would continue until Iran is "totally and decisively defeated" but predicted it would be over soon.
He added that once the United States is done with the military operation against Iran, Tehran will not have any weapons against the ŌĆŗUnited States, Israel and U.S. allies for a long time.
Iranian insiders say this escalation was anticipated long before the war began 11 days ago. Iranian planners assumed confrontation with Washington and Israel was inevitable, and prepared a layered strategy coordinated ŌĆŗacross the GuardsŌĆÖ sprawling military networks and proxy forces.
Now, with little left to lose, Iran is executing that plan and turning the conflict into a grinding war of attrition aimed at exhausting its adversaries politically and economically.
The consequences are already visible at home.
Mojtaba's selection as supreme leader, insiders say, proves the GuardsŌĆÖ dominance as kingmakers. They say the balance of power has shifted. The supreme leader holds the title, but the future of the Islamic Republic, and the authority of the clerical establishment itself, now depends on whether the Guards can weather ŌĆŗthe storm unleashed by the U.S.-Israeli campaign.
But a critical unknown in the war, says Mohannad Hage Ali, a senior fellow at the Carnegie Middle East Center, is how long the Guards can sustain its missile campaign, the backbone ŌĆŗof its strategy against its adversaries.
U.S. officials say a large share of IranŌĆÖs arsenal has already been destroyed, but regional sources say Tehran may still retain more than half its pre-war stockpile. If that estimate holds, Iran could keep launching missiles for several ŌĆīmore weeks, ŌüĀa timeframe that could prove significant for Washington as economic pressure mounts at home and abroad.
The GuardsŌĆÖ reach also extends far beyond the battlefield as it reshapes daily life. An Iranian observer said goods that once sat for weeks at ports are now cleared immediately. Paperwork comes later.
Officials described that as preparation for a war economy, ensuring supply lines keep moving under pressure, while also consolidating the IRGCŌĆÖs control over the state and asserting continuity of governance.
Equally critical is internal stability. So far, there are no signs of protests, elite defections or fractures within the establishment, according to observers and contacts inside Iran.
An insider in Tehran described a city under bombardment but still functioning. ŌĆ£The windows shake day and night,ŌĆØ the person said. ŌĆ£But ŌĆŗlife goes on.ŌĆØ Shops and banks remain open, supplies are ŌĆŗavailable and most residents have not fled the ŌüĀcapital.
The attacks, however, may be producing an effect opposite to what Washington and Israel intended, he noted. Despite long-standing grievances with the government, a surge of national solidarity is taking hold as strikes hit infrastructure and the possibility of internal insurgencies is openly discussed.
ŌĆ£People are not prepared for Iran to disintegrate,ŌĆØ the source said.
For now, that sentiment may be buying ŌĆŗthe leadership time. ŌĆ£I donŌĆÖt know if the regime will survive in the long term,ŌĆØ he added. ŌĆ£But for the next couple of weeks, it will not collapse.ŌĆØ
For strategists on both sides, ŌüĀthe war is increasingly defined by two parallel tests of endurance: whether Iran can keep firing missiles and whether the United States and Israel can sustain the economic, military and political costs of stopping them.
ŌĆ£The big question is who blinks first in this allŌĆæout warŌĆöDonald Trump or IranŌĆÖs leaders?ŌĆØ Gerges said.
By driving up energy prices and spreading financial pain across Western economies, Tehran hopes the pressure will force a U.S. retreat.
Early signs are that the effects are already biting. Oil prices are spiking, gas ŌüĀcosts are rising ŌĆŗand political unease is growing in Washington as the economic fallout collides with November midterm elections.
Under that pressure, Trump, Gerges said, could eventually ŌĆŗseek an exit by declaring victory, citing the killing of IranŌĆÖs supreme leader, the destruction of Iran's nuclear and missile capabilities and key military infrastructure.
For Tehran, however, survival alone would be enough.
Even if much of its strategic infrastructure is destroyed, Iran's leadership can claim triumph and survival against one ŌĆŗof the greatest military armadas in history.
What emerges may be a wounded Iran, but a bleeding Iran could prove just as dangerous -- and perhaps more unpredictable -- than the establishment that entered this conflict.

item.title: Trump presses Republicans for voting restrictions ahead of midterm elections
item.description:
DORAL, Florida, March 9 (Reuters) - U.S. President Donald Trump, addressing Republican lawmakers directly on Monday, ŌĆŗpressed them to enact sweeping new voting restrictions as a way to "guarantee" victory in November's midterm elections, saying he would refuse ŌĆīto sign new bills into law until they do.
The SAVE America Act would impose new voter restrictions including a requirement for proof of citizenship for people registering to vote. It first emerged two years ago in response to Trump's false claims that people in the country illegally were voting in U.S. elections.
"It'll guarantee the midterms. If you don't ŌĆŗget it, big trouble, my opinion," Trump told House Republicans gathered at his Doral golf club in Miami to discuss the president's ŌĆŗlegislative agenda.
Trump's Republican Party is heading into the campaign season facing political headwinds, and analysts say it could struggle ŌüĀto keep its slim majority in the House of Representatives as Democrats seize on voter unhappiness over the economy.
"I'm not going to sign anything ŌĆŗuntil this is approved," Trump said. "If it takes you six months, I'm for not approving anything."
The legislation would require Americans to prove their citizenship while ŌĆŗregistering to vote and show a photo ID before casting ballots. It would also restrict the use of mail-in ballots.
It is already illegal for non-citizens to vote in federal elections. Independent groups on the left and the right, as well as state election officials, have found such voting to be extremely rare .
Democrats say the legislation is intended to ŌĆŗsuppress the vote and undermine their chances of taking control of the House in November. Democracy advocates say the legislation could perpetuate doubts about ŌĆŗU.S. election integrity and the outcome of the midterms.
The House passed the SAVE America Act last month but Trump wants Speaker Mike Johnson ŌĆīto revise ŌüĀit to add bans on transgender women in women's sports and transgender surgery for children, arguing that it will make it easier to pass Congress.
The latest version is still in the Senate. Under the current Senate filibuster rule, it would need 60 votes to pass. But Republicans have only a 53-47 seat majority.
Trump's speech ignored soaring energy costs from the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran , which has unsettled Republican lawmakers at a time when voters are already ŌĆŗfrustrated with Trump's handling of the cost ŌĆŗof living.
Instead, he urged Republicans ŌüĀto address affordability concerns by moving legislation that would codify his policies to lower drug prices and limit the participation of institutional investors in the housing industry.
Republicans are keenly aware that affordability, especially on healthcare and housing, ŌĆŗis the overriding issue for voters in November. But Democrats hold a slight edge over Republicans on the ŌĆŗcost-of-living issue, according to Reuters/Ipsos ŌüĀpolling.
With the two parties locked in a stalemate over immigration reforms and funding for the Department of Homeland Security, House Republicans hope to find consensus in Florida on legislation that would use a parliamentary tool called budget reconciliation to circumvent Democratic opposition in the Senate.
Republicans used the same technique last year to pass ŌüĀTrump's " One Big ŌĆŗBeautiful Bill ," legislation that proved unpopular with the public and is expected to add $4.7 trillion ŌĆŗto the U.S. deficit over 10 years. U.S. debt currently stands at $38.5 trillion.
House Republicans, who are promoting the law's tax cuts on tip and overtime income as affordability measures for American ŌĆŗfamilies, believe another reconciliation bill would help win over voters.

item.title: China's property reset comes with a heavy price
item.description:
HONG KONG, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - There is no doubt that ChinaŌĆÖs real-estate bubble needed popping. Beijing has spent nearly a decade letting air out of the country's historically speculative property market, which at times powered a quarter of the worldŌĆÖs second-largest economy. However, the structural distortions that fed the bubble still exist, while the cleanup is exerting a lasting drag on growth.
For years, real estate soaked up Chinese ŌĆŗsavings, drove urbanisation and bankrolled local governments, who partly relied on land sales for income. Easy credit, perceived implicit state backing and a lack of attractive investment alternatives all pushed households and developers to ŌĆībet on ever-rising prices. So entrenched was the mania that few people took Xi Jinping seriously in 2016 when the Chinese president declared that houses are for living in, not for speculation.
The real estate market only started buckling in 2020 when Beijing unleashed its ŌĆ£three red linesŌĆØ policy, which limited developersŌĆÖ debt-fuelled growth by testing their borrowings against assets, equity and cash. By that point, the problem was acute. Floor space under construction amounted to over five times annual sales, implying a giant backlog of developments that would take years to shift, if they could even be sold at all.
Xi's ensuing crackdown ŌĆŗupended the decades-old property order by deflating prices. In economic terms, the seismic shift faintly echoes the Communist PartyŌĆÖs early efforts, beginning in the 1940s, to reshape the distribution of wealth by uprooting the landlord class.
China Evergrande, ŌĆŗa colossus of the boom years, went into liquidation in 2024 under the weight of $300 billion in liabilities. Its founding chairman Xu Jiayin, once listed as ChinaŌĆÖs wealthiest , opens new tab man, is under police control. ŌüĀVirtually all other major developers in the private sector have defaulted , opens new tab .
After much upheaval, ChinaŌĆÖs property debt reduction drive now seems to be over. Local media reported earlier this year that the "three red lines" policy has effectively been shelved.
The fiveŌĆæyear deleveraging campaign has proved ŌĆŗtransformative. Property investment has declined from about 12% of GDP in 2021 to roughly half that share today. The financial systemŌĆÖs exposure to a sudden, uncontrolled realŌĆæestate blowup has therefore reduced.
The combined debt of the 46 largest developers listed in Hong Kong ŌĆŗand mainland China stood at 5.19 trillion yuan ($753 billion) in 2025, down 17% since 2020, according to data from investment platform FSMOne. Much of the leverage reduction, FSMOne data shows, reflects up to $50ŌĆ»billion in offshore restructurings, referring to debts that were issued outside of mainland China. Creditors were forced to accept steep haircuts or swap their claims into equity.
Yet the real-estate bubble, despite being largely deflated, is having lasting consequences. Homebuyers' confidence has crashed, while a collapse in revenue has left developers in a worse position to service their debts. Contracted sales among the top 100 ŌĆŗdevelopers last year plunged to 3.36 trillion yuan, down from 13ŌĆ»trillion yuan in 2020, based on figures from UBS. The combined net profit margin of 26 major listed developers fell from 10.7% in 2020 to -1.1% in 2024, according to UBS.
Worse, five years ŌĆŗafter the start of the crackdown, the weakening property market continues to drag on growth. According to Andrew Batson of Gavekal Dragonomics, the sharp fall in construction and property investment shaved about 0.1 percentage points off ChinaŌĆÖs 5% GDP expansion last year.
After slipping into contraction ŌĆīin early 2022, ŌüĀproperty investment has now posted four straight years of declines, according to the National Bureau of Statistics. It culminated in a record 17.2% yearŌĆæonŌĆæyear drop in December 2025. That compares with a more sedate 2.2% decline in infrastructure investment last year.
