item.title: Aramco sees 'catastrophic consequences' for oil if shipping doesn't resume in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's ŌĆītop oil exporter, said on Tuesday that there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the Iran war continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait ŌĆŗof Hormuz.
The disruption has not only upended the shipping and ŌĆŗinsurance sectors but also promises to have drastic domino effects ŌüĀon aviation, agriculture, automotive and other industries, Aramco CEO Amin Nasser told ŌĆŗreporters on an earnings call.
Nasser noted global inventories of oil were at ŌĆŗa five-year low and said the crisis will lead to drawdowns at a faster rate, adding that it was critical that shipping in the strait resumed.
"There would ŌĆŗbe catastrophic consequences for the worldŌĆÖs oil markets and the longer the ŌĆŗdisruption goes on, and the more drastic the consequences for the global economy," he ŌĆīsaid.
Nasser ŌüĀalso said a small fire from an attack last week on Aramco's Ras Tanura refinery, its largest domestically, was quickly extinguished and brought under control, adding that the refinery was in the process of being restarted.
Iran's ŌĆŗRevolutionary Guards said on ŌĆŗTuesday they would ŌüĀnot allow "one litre of oil" to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. and Israeli attacks continue, ŌĆŗprompting a warning from President Donald Trump that the ŌĆŗU.S. ŌüĀwould hit Iran much harder if it blocked exports from the vital energy-producing region.
His comments come after Aramco reported a 12% drop in annual profit mainly ŌüĀdue ŌĆŗto lower crude prices. It also announced ŌĆŗit would repurchase up to $3 billion worth of shares in its first-ever buyback.

item.title: Spain's Repsol hikes payout targets, reduces investments
item.description:
MADRID, March 10 (Reuters) - Spanish energy ŌĆŗgroup Repsol (REP.MC) , opens new tab pledged on Tuesday ŌĆīto give shareholders between 30% and 40% of the cash ŌĆŗflow it generates from ŌĆŗits operations through 2028 via dividends ŌüĀand buybacks while slowing down ŌĆŗinvestments.
The payout move its in ŌĆŗline with the company's strategic plans in the past years, which had ŌĆŗshareholder returns as a ŌĆŗkey priority.
In its previous plan, the ŌĆīcompany ŌüĀhad set a 25%-35% target for remuneration.
In coming years, Repsol will slow down investments after ŌĆŗyears of ŌĆŗsignificant ŌüĀcapital expenditures.
The company sees net investments through 2028 ŌĆŗat between 7.5 and ŌĆŗ9 ŌüĀbillion euros. This is down from a 16 to ŌüĀ19 ŌĆŗbillion euros range ŌĆŗin its previous multi-year plan.

item.title: 'Maybe we die together': Voices at the Iran-Turkey mountain crossing
item.description:
KAPIKOY, Turkey, March 10 (Reuters) - At a remote mountain pass in eastern Turkey, travellers from Iran step across the threshold with a mix of fear, exhaustion and relief - arriving after a week marked by war, long journeys by train or car, communications blackouts and borrowed phones.
Snow-covered hills surround the Iranian side of the frontier at the Kapikoy border gate in Turkey's Van province, where families and lone travellers emerge, many after days on the road. Hundreds have ŌĆŗcrossed in recent days and there is now a steady flow in both directions as the U.S.-Israeli war with Iran expands across the region.
Some say they fled because bombs were falling in their cities. Others decided ŌĆīto leave after losing contact with loved ones, travelling by land when flights were cancelled. Most carry only small suitcases, a phone without a local SIM card, and a determination to finish an already punishing journey.
New arrivals ask how to reach Van, the nearest city two hours away, and how to reconnect to the outside world. Some journalists hand over their phones so travellers can reassure family that they are safe. Each person has a different reason for leaving ŌĆö work drying up, a sick relative to visit, or a life interrupted by conflict.
Ebrahim Eidi, 61, had recently been ŌĆŗin Tehran and said many Iranians were waiting to see whether the government was weakened enough for protests to erupt. He said some believed opposition figure Reza Pahlavi ŌĆö the son of Iran's last shah ŌĆö could become a rallying point ŌĆŗif large demonstrations begin.
"People are waiting for something to happen, for some change. They want the government to change completely, and many people say they are waiting for Reza Pahlavi."
Eidi left ŌüĀIran 34 years ago for the Netherlands, where he works with asylum seekers and refugees in camps. He returns to Iran roughly once a year. This time, he said, the situation felt different.
He said many people chose to stay in Iran because ŌĆŗthey still had hope for the country. But they feared their own power was not enough to change the government, and that the situation could spiral.
"People are afraid to go to the streets. They are not afraid of America. Unfortunately, they are afraid of ŌĆŗtheir own government."
Leila, 45, was travelling in the opposite direction, heading back into Iran. After losing contact with her family in Shiraz, she decided to return from Istanbul, where she occasionally assists academics working with a German historical research institution.
"How can I be safe when I feel my family maybe they are in danger?"
One of her brothers is seriously ill and in a coma, increasing her worry. For her, being physically with her family - even in danger - felt more bearable than waiting abroad.
She plans to remain in Iran until the war is over.
"I cannot guard them against bombs. ŌĆŗBut when I feel I can be with them together, maybe we die together, or I can help them as long as we are alive."
Hamid Shirmohammadzadeh, 35, had travelled back to Iran shortly before the war and fled with his ŌĆŗwife and children when Tehran came under attack. He had been building a life in Tokyo, working for an import-export firm, but returned to Iran after his visa lapsed. His wife and two children had remained there.
"The day before the war started I was in TehranŌĆ” ŌĆīThen the bombs ŌüĀstarted falling. We saw the war had begun, so we came to Turkey."
His wife, son and daughter ŌĆö aged 9 and 10 ŌĆö are waiting near the border as he seeks help from Japanese authorities to secure visas for them.
ŌĆ£I worked in Japan, paid my taxes and followed the rules. I donŌĆÖt understand why they are not helping me now.ŌĆØ
ŌĆ£In times like this we should help each other. I need help now because my country is at war.ŌĆØ
Mohammad Soltanzadeh, who lives in Hamburg and is originally from Afghanistan, had been visiting relatives in Iran's Mashhad when flights were cancelled, forcing him onto the road.
"The journey was very exhausting. We were on the train for about 24 hours, then we travelled four hours by car and finally came by taxi."
He described Mashhad as calm despite ŌĆŗthe wider turmoil.
"People were a bit saddened and mourning but ŌĆŗthey were not stressed. The shops were open, the ŌüĀmarkets and shopping centres were open and people were continuing their activities. Life was going on."
Egyptian factory worker Mohammad Fauzi, 46, crossed from Iran with no Turkish SIM card, no local currency and no knowledge of the language. He only had the phone numbers of two Egyptian friends in Ankara and Izmir - and a plan to reach Cairo.
He had watched work grind to a halt ŌĆŗin Iran during his three months there working in the marble and granite sector, with many factories closing.
"The situation is very difficult and working has stopped. I can't work, I ŌĆŗcan't stay because the situation is ŌüĀdangerous now, so I want to go to my home, my country."
Jalileh Jabari, 63, said she fled Tehran because "bombs are falling" and the situation had become unbearable. The highways to the border were calm, but uncertainty in the capital pushed her to leave. She was travelling to Istanbul, where her daughter studies.
"If things become good there, if Iran becomes good, I will come back. If there is peace, I will return."
Two sisters, Shaylin, 9, and Celine Azizour, 11, crossed into Turkey with their mother, travelling from ŌüĀTehran toward Istanbul ŌĆŗwith hopes of eventually reaching London.
"We from Tehran," Shaylin said. Asked about conditions there, she replied: "ItŌĆÖs not so good." Despite the arduous journey, she smiled: "IŌĆÖm so ŌĆŗhappy."
Yasna, 63, crossed over from Iran with her husband and one of her daughters, travelling to Antalya on TurkeyŌĆÖs southern coast to visit another daughter who lives there with her family.
"I came to see my daughter ŌĆö I haven't seen her for six years. She has children there and I have two grandchildren."
She ŌĆŗsaid she preferred not to comment on Iran's political situation.
"I donŌĆÖt know what to say about the situation in Iran because we will be back to Iran."

item.title: Congo Republic's Sassou set to extend long rule, focus on succession
item.description:
DAKAR, March 10 (Reuters) - Congo Republic President Denis Sassou Nguesso looks set to extend his decades-long rule in elections on Sunday, even as his advanced age and a term limit fuel speculation about who ŌĆŗwill eventually succeed him.
The 82-year-old former paratrooper first took power in the oil-rich Central African nation in a coup in 1979. He lost ŌĆīCongo Republic's first multi-party elections in 1992 but seized power again in 1997 after a civil war.
He has now ruled for a combined total of almost 42 years, making him Africa's third longest-serving leader, after Equatorial Guinea's Teodoro Obiang and Cameroon's Paul Biya.
Sassou will face six candidates in an election with an organising commission dominated by figures appointed by the ruling Congolese Labour Party. Two of ŌĆŗthe main opposition parties are boycotting the vote, saying the process lacks transparency, and several potential challengers are in prison or in exile.
"This election is a ŌĆŗmere formality. The real stakes lie in what comes next," Remadji Hoinathy of the Pretoria-based Institute for Security Studies think tank ŌüĀsaid.
A 2015 constitutional reform, adopted despite opposition protests, reset the presidential term limit and allowed Sassou to stay in power. But it also capped presidents at three five-year ŌĆŗmandates, meaning that, barring another reform, this is his last election.
The president himself has begun speaking more openly about eventual succession, telling young supporters at his ŌĆŗcampaign opening rally on February 28 that his generation was "laying the groundwork" for them to take over.
One potential successor is his son, Denis-Christel Sassou Nguesso, who entered government as Minister of International Cooperation and Public-Private Partnerships in 2021 and has since taken on a higher public profile.
Some analysts say he may not be ready.
"Denis-Christel does not command the same authority within the ruling party as ŌĆŗhis father and is widely unpopular. His potential accession to power threatens to unleash a violent succession struggle," Maja Bovcon, an independent consultant focused on West and Central ŌĆŗAfrica, said.
Other figures within the ruling system who could take over are Jean-Dominique Okemba, head of CongoŌĆÖs National Security Council and a nephew of the president, and Jean-Jacques Bouya, the Minister of ŌĆīSpatial Planning ŌüĀand Major Works and a cousin of Sassou's.
"Sassou wonŌĆÖt leave power unless he can hand it to someone trusted in his close circle, his son or a trusted ally who guarantees stability," Hoinathy said.
A government spokesperson did not respond to a request for comment on the president's succession plans.
Sassou's campaign has focused on continuity. Five years ago, his slogan was "Let us continue the march towards development". This year, it is "Let us accelerate the march".
For all the turbulence of Sassou's early years in power, his ŌĆŗcountry is currently one of Central AfricaŌĆÖs ŌĆŗmore stable states after a series ŌüĀof upheavals, including the 2023 coup in Gabon and last year's post-electoral violence in Cameroon.
The economy returned to modest per-capita growth in 2024 after a decade-long recession triggered by a slump in global oil prices. Oil still accounts for about half of Congo's ŌĆŗGDP and 80% of its exports.
The debt-to-GDP ratio, which peaked at 103.6% in 2020, fell to about 93.6% in 2024, and ŌĆŗa three-year IMF programme ŌüĀcompleted last year helped stabilise public finances. Liquidity pressures remain, with debt servicing on the regional market absorbing half of the country's tax revenues.
Sassou has promised new infrastructure upgrades, increased investment in agriculture and economic diversification, though most ordinary Congolese have seen little improvement to date.
More than half - 52% - of Congo's 6.1 million people live in poverty, a rate unchanged ŌüĀsince 2021, according ŌĆŗto the World Bank. Youth unemployment is around 42% in a country where nearly half the population ŌĆŗis under 18.
Meanwhile, French and U.S. prosecutors have launched investigations into assets held abroad by Sassou's relatives. The family has regularly denied any wrongdoing.
"We need better health care and education," Fr├®d├®ric Nkou, a jobless voter ŌĆŗin Brazzaville, told Reuters. "But with this new term, we will experience more of the same."
(This story has been refiled to fix the formatting)

item.title: US warns citizens in Nigeria of possible 'terrorist threat'
item.description: LAGOS, March 10 - The U.S. embassy in Nigeria warned American citizens of ŌĆŗa possible "terrorist threat" against U.S. ŌĆīfacilities and affiliated schools in the West African country in a security ŌĆŗalert late on Monday.

item.title: UAE condemns drone attack on its consulate in Iraq's Erbil
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - UAE's foreign ministry condemned ŌĆŗa drone attack ŌĆīon its consulate in Iraqi ŌĆŗKurdistan's Erbil, ŌĆŗit said in a ŌüĀstatement early ŌĆŗon Tuesday.
Iraqi ŌĆŗKurdistan's counter-terrorism service said late on Monday ŌĆŗthat it ŌĆŗhad downed three drones ŌĆīin ŌüĀErbil, with debris from one of the ŌĆŗdrones ŌĆŗfalling ŌüĀnear the UAE consulate. ŌĆŗIt said ŌĆŗno ŌüĀcasualties were reported.

item.title: Turkey says U.S. Patriot system deployed to boost air defence amid Iran war
item.description:
ANKARA, March 10 (Reuters) - Turkey said on Tuesday that a U.S. Patriot air defence system was deployed ŌĆŗto its southeast, near a NATO radar base, as part ŌĆīof steps by the alliance to boost air defences in the face of missile threats from the Iran war.
It is being deployed to Malatya province, the ŌĆŗlocation of the Kurecik NATO radar base, which provides vital ŌĆŗdata for the alliance and helped identify two Iranian ballistic ŌüĀmissiles heading toward Turkey over the last week.
Iran has said it ŌĆŗis not at war with regional countries and denies explicitly targeting its neighbour ŌĆŗTurkey. Ankara has warned Tehran against firing any more missiles towards it and the two countries' presidents discussed the issue on Monday.
"In addition to the measures we ŌĆŗtake on a national level, air and missile defence measures by ŌĆŗNATO have been increased. In that framework, one Patriot System is being deployed to ŌĆīMalatya ŌüĀto contribute to defending our air space," the defence ministry said.
It added that Turkey would continue to evaluate regional developments and cooperate with NATO allies.
The deployment comes amid reports that Washington is looking into redeploying its military assets, ŌĆŗincluding Patriot systems, ŌĆŗcurrently stationed in ŌüĀSouth Korea.
It was not immediately clear where the Patriot system or its batteries were being redeployed from.
Turkey, ŌĆŗan emerging leader in the global defence industry that ŌĆŗhas the ŌüĀalliance's second-largest army, lacks its own fully fledged air defences despite development efforts, and has relied on NATO air defences stationed in the ŌüĀeastern Mediterranean ŌĆŗSea in both missile incidents in ŌĆŗthe last week.
There is currently one Patriot system, from Spain, deployed in Turkey as part ŌĆŗof NATO defences.

item.title: Indian restaurants warn of shutdowns as Iran war makes cooking gas scarce
item.description:
BENGALURU/CHENNAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Restaurants and hotels across India warned of disruptions ŌĆŗand possible shutdowns on Tuesday, as the Iran war constricts supplies of cooking gas, prompting authorities to set up ŌĆīa panel to review industry requests.
The fuel shortage comes as the U.S.-Israel war on Iran has halted ship traffic in the Gulf and the Strait of Hormuz, driving up energy prices and transport costs, hitting exports and output from Gulf producers, such as Qatar and Saudi Arabia.
India, the ŌĆŗworld's second-biggest importer of liquefied petroleum gas (LPG), invoked emergency powers last week to order refiners to crank up production ŌĆŗfor domestic use, leaving the hospitality industry struggling to get sufficient supply.
"We have LPG stock for ŌüĀtwo days. We are working on contingencies," said Bert Mueller, founder of Mexican food chain California Burrito, with more than ŌĆŗ100 stores spanning Bengaluru and Chennai in India's south to Delhi and Noida in the north.
"We are conserving gas and installing ŌĆŗinduction stoves at certain stores."
India's oil ministry said it had set up , opens new tab a panel to review requests for LPG supply to restaurants and other industries, following appeals from two industry bodies.
"The restaurant industry is predominantly dependent on commercial LPG for its operations," the National Restaurant Association of India, which ŌĆŗrepresents more than half a million restaurants, told the food processing ministry on Monday.
"Any disruption therein will lead to a ŌĆŗcatastrophic closure," the NRAI said in a letter, while another body, the Federation of Hotel & Restaurant Associations of India, also sought government help.
Indian ŌĆīcompanies ŌüĀhave raised LPG prices for the first time in about a year, as the war boosts prices of the imports that fill two-thirds of annual LPG consumption.
India's largest supplier of LNG, Qatar, halted production last week after Iran's strikes on Gulf countries in retaliation for Israeli and U.S. strikes against it.
In the southern tech hub of Bengaluru dubbed India's Silicon Valley, several ŌĆŗrestaurants said deliveries had dropped ŌĆŗsharply, fanning that kitchens will ŌüĀcome to a halt if the situation is not resolved.
"One of our restaurants did not receive gas cylinders today," Manish V Shetty, who runs the Udupi Food Hub chain of restaurants ŌĆŗin Bengaluru told Reuters.
"Thankfully, one of our older vendors helped us," he added, because the ŌĆŗchain offers immediate ŌüĀpayment, rather than credit terms of a week or month. "We're also seeing a spike in the price of the sunflower oil that we use for cooking."
Few restaurants stockpile LPG cylinders, because of safety concerns, relying instead on frequent replacement, said Veerendra Kamat, secretary ŌüĀof the ŌĆŗBengaluru Hotels Association.
"It's a very grave situation. Most of the (gas) companies have ŌĆŗstopped supplying," said Ananth Narayan of the NRAI's Bengaluru branch, adding that even those restaurants that do store gas could run out within a week or ŌĆŗtwo.

item.title: Middle East countries cut daily oil output, Bloomberg News reports
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Saudi Arabia has lowered oil ŌĆŗoutput by between ŌĆī2 million and 2.5 million ŌĆŗbarrels a day, ŌĆŗand the United ŌüĀArab Emirates has ŌĆŗcut its output ŌĆŗby 500,00-800,000 barrels a day, Bloomberg News ŌĆŗreported on ŌĆŗTuesday.
Kuwait has also cut output ŌĆīby ŌüĀhalf a million barrels a day, and Iraq ŌĆŗby ŌĆŗabout ŌüĀ2.9 million, the report ŌĆŗadded, citing ŌĆŗpeople ŌüĀwith knowledge of the matter.
Reuters could ŌüĀnot ŌĆŗimmediately ŌĆŗverify the report.

item.title: India invokes force majeure, gives priority to gas sales to key sectors
item.description:
NEW DELHI, March 10 - India has invoked emergency measures to divert gas supplies from non-priority ŌĆŗsectors to key users after the ŌĆīdisruption of liquefied natural gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz, a government notification said.
India meets ŌĆŗhalf of its 195 million standard ŌĆŗcubic metres per day (mmscmd) of gas consumption ŌüĀthrough imports. The country was getting ŌĆŗabout 60 mmscmd gas from the Middle East ŌĆŗprior to the closure of the Strait of Hormuz and force majeure by Qatar .
Depending on the availability of ŌĆŗgas, India will be supplying the ŌĆŗfuel in proportion to the average use over the ŌĆīlast ŌüĀsix months by priority sectors, the notification, issued late on Monday, said.

item.title: German start-up plans 30-megawatt AI data centre in boost to sovereign control
item.description:
FRANKFURT, March 10 (Reuters) - German start-up Polarise plans to build a new 30-megawatt artificial intelligence ŌĆŗdata centre that would double Germany's domestically-run computing capacity as European nations push ŌĆīto gain more control over critical tech infrastructure.
The facility, set to come online in the Bavarian town of Amberg in mid-2027, could eventually expand to 120 MW, the company told Reuters.
AI data centres in ŌĆŗGermany boasted total capacity of 530 MW at the end of last year, according ŌĆŗto figures from German lobby group Bitkom. Much of that, however, was ŌüĀoperated by non-German providers.
European countries are pushing for more sovereign control over data and ŌĆŗthe infrastructure required for AI due to global tensions - from tariffs to armed conflict - as well ŌĆŗas sharply diverging rules around online content.
The plans for what would be one of Germany's largest data centres have not been previously reported. Large technology companies like Google (GOOGL.O) , opens new tab and Amazon's (AMZN.O) , opens new tab ŌĆŗAWS typically run data centres of around 100 MW or more.
Polarise, which operates 13 ŌĆŗdata centres in Germany and abroad, declined to give details on the size of the required investment.
"The final ŌĆīinvestment ŌüĀvolume depends heavily on how many customers install their own servers or rent computing power," said Marc Gazivoda, Polarise's marketing director, adding that it did not receive state subsidies and investments could vary.
A source close to the company said the first stage of the project would ŌĆŗcost in the "triple-digit million ŌĆŗeuro range", which ŌüĀincludes the main infrastructure but not the chips themselves. The final cost would depend on the number of chips required and their specific ŌĆŗtypes, the source said.
A 12-MW data centre opened by Polarise in ŌĆŗMunich last ŌüĀmonth cost 1 billion euros ($1.16 billion), according to an estimate by Deutsche Telekom. That facility already doubled Germany's existing locally-operated capacity.
With oil prices shooting above $100 per barrel, the cost of energy for ŌüĀpower-hungry ŌĆŗdata centres is increasingly in focus.
Polarise said its partner ŌĆŗWV Energie will install a wind and solar power plant for the centre as well as batteries for temporary ŌĆŗstorage of electricity.
($1 = 0.8640 euros)

item.title: Horse racing-Leading British horses line up for stellar first day as Cheltenham Festival begins
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - British trainers Dan Skelton and Nicky Henderson will take on the might of the Irish at jump racing's showcase event with the likes of The New Lion ŌĆŗand Lulamba vying for a share of the five-million-pound ($6.70 million) prize pot as the Cheltenham Festival opens on Tuesday.
Attracting some 200,000 racegoers across four ŌĆīdays, the festival is worth an estimated 274 million pounds to the local economy boosting local restaurants, hotels and pubs.
It is also horse racing's biggest betting week of the year with bookmaker William Hill predicting 450 million pounds will be wagered across Britain. Guinness, reduced in price by 30 pence, will be drunk by the gallon.
But for those at the heart of the sport, it is all about the horses ŌĆŗand whether this year's crop of jumps stars can rival the Irish.
It is almost two decades since the golden years of British jump racing when Somerset-based Paul ŌĆŗNicholls and his crop of horses like dual Gold Cup-winner Kauto Star and 18-times consecutive hurdle winner Big Buck's outclassed the rest, before ŌüĀthe pendulum swung in favour of the Irish.
Since then, Willie Mullins' sizeable team has continued to dominate the festival, racking up 113 winners to snatch the leading trainer title ŌĆŗ12 times. Backed by big-spending owners like former Barclays executive Rich Ricci and businessman J.P. McManus, this year's squad looks no less dangerous.
Mullins' pick for jump racing's most prestigious chase is ŌĆŗGaelic Warrior who looks set to go off favourite in Friday's feature, the Gold Cup, a race that has so far evaded owner Susannah Ricci. Before that, Majborough is favourite for the Queen Mother Champion Chase on Wednesday.
In with a strong chance of ending Irish dominance is a fresh crop of talented trainers that includes Skelton and Ben Pauling who will take on Mullins in the 625,000-pound Gold Cup ŌĆŗwith the fancied Grey Dawning and the Harry Redknapp-owned King George VI Chase winner The Jukebox Man.
Battle lines will first be drawn in Tuesday's opening race, the Grade 1 ŌĆŗSupreme Novices' Hurdle, when Mullins' Mighty Park takes on British hope and three-time winning 2/1 favourite Old Park Star, trained by Henderson.
Henderson has made no secret of the fact a win on Tuesday would ŌĆībe a ŌüĀbig boost for Seven Barrows, setting a strong tone for the week, after Constitution Hill and Sir Gino were sidelined. He also sends out highly fancied Lulamba in the Arkle Novices' Chase and Jango Baie in the Gold Cup.
Were Britain to also clinch Tuesday's feature race, the 450,000-pound Champion Hurdle in which Mullins' prolific mare Lossiemouth will battle with Skelton's The New Lion, nerves may start jangling in the Irish camp.
Skelton sends his strongest ever team to Cheltenham, lining up some 30-35 horses. But while he's bullish about some big British winners, ŌĆŗhe described his chances of snatching the ŌĆŗtop trainer prize at the festival as "a ŌüĀpossibility... not a probability."
"I've thought about it because I'm a competitive bugger but the reality is Willie will be leading trainer," he told the Racing Post on Sunday. "With a bit of luck, we might be pitching behind him along with a few others."
Ireland has won ŌĆŗCheltenham's Prestbury Cup title, awarded to the country fielding the most winners, outright in nine of the last 10 years.
Cheltenham's opening ŌĆŗday also marks an important ŌüĀone for British racing, which is in the spotlight for governance issues at its regulator and recent moves by gaming companies to reduce sponsorship in light of higher taxes .
Cheltenham is also looking to recover from lower ticket sales in recent years, something CEO Guy Lavender is trying to address by lowering the cost of Guinness to 2022 rates, reducing restrictions on drinking areas and reintroducing ŌüĀLadies Day ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
"There is no doubt that everyone operating in the leisure sector is having to work harder to ŌĆŗmake their offerings stand out to the customer," said Lavender.
"However, British racing continues to provide an exceptional day out and we saw total attendance across all 60 racecourses top 5 million last year for the first time ŌĆŗsince 2019."
Lavender added he was "very positive" about ticket sales with growth from last year. "We are definitely moving in the right direction," he said.
($1 = 0.7460 pounds)

item.title: Russia is the only winner of Middle East war, EU's Costa says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 10 (Reuters) - Russia has so far been the only winner from the war in the ŌĆŗMiddle East as energy prices soar and ŌĆīattention for its war against Ukraine has faded, EU Council President Antonio Costa said on Tuesday.
"So ŌĆŗfar, there is only one winner ŌĆŗin this war ŌĆō Russia," Costa said in ŌüĀa speech to EU ambassadors in Brussels.
"It ŌĆŗgains new resources to finance its war ŌĆŗagainst Ukraine as energy prices rise. It profits from the diversion of military capabilities that could otherwise have ŌĆŗbeen sent to support Ukraine. And ŌĆŗit benefits from reduced attention to the Ukrainian front ŌĆīas ŌüĀthe conflict in the Middle East takes centre stage."
Costa stressed the need for the EU to protect the international rules-based order, which ŌĆŗhe said ŌĆŗwas now ŌüĀbeing challenged by the United States, and for all parties in ŌĆŗthe Middle East to return to ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀnegotiating table.
"Freedom and human rights cannot be achieved through bombs. Only international law upholds them," ŌüĀhe ŌĆŗsaid.
"We must avoid further escalation. ŌĆŗSuch a path threatens the Middle East, Europe, and ŌĆŗbeyond."

item.title: How many people have been killed in the US-Israel war on Iran?
item.description:
March 10 - Scores of people have been killed across the Middle East since the U.S. and Israel attacked Iran on February 28, with Gulf states that host U.S. military bases and personnel ŌĆŗas well as Lebanon quickly drawn into the conflict.
Here are the death tolls from the war ŌĆīas reported by countries as of March 9, more than a week after it began. Reuters has not independently verified these deaths.

item.title: Israel ahead of schedule on war goals in Iran, ambassador to France says
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Israel is ahead of schedule in achieving its war goals in Iran, its ambassador to France ŌĆŗsaid on Tuesday, adding that the operation aims ŌĆīto weaken IranŌĆÖs authorities to curb attacks beyond its borders and allow its people to shape their own future.
"When we were asked at ŌĆŗthe start of this war about its duration, ŌĆŗwe said it would last a few weeks. That ŌüĀhasnŌĆÖt changed," Joshua Zarka told BFM TV. "We are ahead ŌĆŗof schedule to achieve our war objectives."
Zarka, formerly IsraelŌĆÖs lead ŌĆŗdiplomat on Iran, said the goals extend beyond ending IranŌĆÖs nuclear programme. They include weakening the government to the point that its population ŌĆŗcan "take its fate into its own hands," and ensuring ŌĆŗTehran can no longer mount attacks beyond its borders.
Asked about the appointment ŌĆīof ŌüĀMojtaba Khamenei as the new Supreme Leader, Zarka said that if he follows his father Ali Khamenei's positions, he "would also be on a list of those who should be eliminated."
Israel ŌĆŗhas simultaneously ŌĆŗlaunched major operations ŌüĀagainst IranŌĆæbacked Hezbollah after it struck Israeli territory. The Lebanese government has said it wants ŌĆŗdirect talks with Israel to halt the ŌĆŗfighting, but ŌüĀZarka argued that Beirut is not disarming Hezbollah.
"At this stage, IŌĆÖm not aware of any decision to enter negotiations to ŌüĀend ŌĆŗthis war," he said. "What would end ŌĆŗit is the disarmament of Hezbollah ŌĆö and that is a choice for the ŌĆŗLebanese government."

item.title: Over 10,000 Chinese citizens returned from Middle East amid war
item.description: BEIJING, March 10 (Reuters) - Chinese authorities and airlines have ŌĆŗassisted over 10,000 Chinese citizens in returning from ŌĆīthe Middle East, the foreign ministry said on Tuesday, after the war in the region forced airspace closures and grounded ŌĆŗcommercial flights.

item.title: Bangladesh secures diesel supplies amid major energy disruptions, sources say
item.description:
DHAKA, March 10 (Reuters) - Bangladesh has begun receiving diesel from suppliers including China and India, with officials ŌĆŗsaying the country now has enough fuel to cover about one month of demand, with arrangements under ŌĆīway for another month, after the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran disrupted shipments, including to the massive garments industry.
The South Asian nation of 175 million people, which relies on imports for roughly 95% of its energy needs, has imposed fuel rationing for vehicles, restrictions on diesel sales and closed universities as the war ŌĆŗon Iran causes severe disruption to Middle East oil exports.
Some garment makers in Bangladesh, the worldŌĆÖs secondŌĆælargest clothing exporter ŌĆŗafter China, have already complained they do not have enough diesel to run their back-up generators when ŌüĀpublic utilities cut power supplies.
Mahmud Hasan Khan, president of the Bangladesh Garment Manufacturers and Exporters Association, said power cuts had ŌĆŗdoubled to up to five hours a day since the war began on February 28.
"When power goes out, we have to ŌĆŗrun our standby generators on diesel, but we canŌĆÖt buy enough diesel anymore, and itŌĆÖs causing problems for us," he said.
State-run Bangladesh Petroleum Corporation (BPC) is receiving around 60,000 metric tons of diesel from three traders, with a further 90,000 metric tons scheduled to arrive later this month, two energy ŌĆŗofficials said, requesting anonymity because they were not authorised to speak to the media.
A 27,000-metric-ton diesel cargo from PetroChina arrived ŌĆŗat Chittagong port on Monday, while a cargo of 28,000 metric tons from Vitol is waiting at the portŌĆÖs outer anchorage, a BPC official ŌĆīsaid.
In addition, ŌüĀaround 5,000 metric tons of diesel is being supplied through a cross-border pipeline from IndiaŌĆÖs Numaligarh Refinery. Officials said talks are ongoing to secure some 30,000 metric tons from Indian Oil (IOC.NS) , opens new tab .
"We have supplies for one month, and another month is being arranged,ŌĆØ a BPC official said.
The BPC did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
Bangladesh normally needs about 380,000 metric tons of diesel ŌĆŗeach month, but officials said ŌĆŗthat since rationing began, one ŌüĀestimate put current monthly requirements at roughly 270,000 metric tons.
Officials said securing refined fuel cargoes had been relatively easy because most of the traders did not rely on the disrupted Gulf shipping ŌĆŗroutes used for crude oil shipments.
However, prices have increased as a result of the intense market ŌĆŗvolatility triggered by ŌüĀthe Middle East crisis, they added.
Bangladesh also imports around 1.4 million metric tons of crude oil a year for its refineries under long-term contracts with Saudi Aramco and Abu Dhabi National Oil Company (ADNOC).
However, supplies from Saudi Aramco and ADNOC could be affected because their ŌüĀcrude shipments ŌĆŗto Bangladesh must pass through the Strait of Hormuz. One Aramco cargo ŌĆŗof about 100,000 tons has already been delayed in the Gulf due to the ongoing crisis, officials said.
Severe gas shortages have already forced Bangladesh to halt operations ŌĆŗat four of its five stateŌĆærun fertiliser factories, redirecting available gas to power plants.

item.title: Greenpeace activists storm stage at France's nuclear summit, confront Macron
item.description:
PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - Two Greenpeace activists broke onto the stage at the start of a global nuclear summit in ŌĆŗFrance on Tuesday, interrupting President Emmanuel Macron and U.N. ŌĆīnuclear watchdog chief Rafael Grossi as they were greeting heads of state.
The protesters, dressed sharply in black suits and ties, held banners bearing the Greenpeace logo ŌĆŗand reading "Nuclear Power = Energy Insecurity" and "Nuclear power fuels RussiaŌĆÖs war".
One ŌĆŗof them shouted at Macron, "Why are we still buying ŌüĀuranium from Russia?" to which the president replied, "We produce nuclear power ŌĆŗourselves."
France has its own uranium enrichment capacity, but also imports enriched ŌĆŗuranium for its power plants, including from Russia, according to the latest customs data published by the French government.
RussiaŌĆÖs state nuclear company Rosatom accounted for about ŌĆŗ44% of the global uranium enrichment capacity in 2025, according to ŌĆŗthe World Nuclear Association, and European nuclear power producers have struggled to wean ŌĆīthemselves ŌüĀoff these supplies four years after Russia invaded Ukraine.
Around 15 Greenpeace activists blocked arriving convoys outside the venue in Boulogne-Billancourt on the outskirts of Paris on Tuesday, the environmental campaigning group said in ŌĆŗa statement.
France is ŌĆŗhosting the ŌüĀsecond world nuclear energy summit on Tuesday, where world leaders will meet to discuss and promote nuclear ŌĆŗpower.
"For Greenpeace France, the holding of such a ŌĆŗsummit is ŌüĀan anachronism, an event completely out of touch with reality and with the lessons to be learned from the tragic situations of the ŌüĀRussian ŌĆŗaggression in Ukraine, the strikes on ŌĆŗIran, and the impacts of the worsening climate disruption," the group said.

item.title: 'My friends are still in jail': Cubans take to the streets, but fear crossing line
item.description:
SAN ANTONIO DE LOS BA├æOS, Cuba, March 10 (Reuters) - Without warning, the sleepy Cuban farm town of San Antonio de los Ba├▒os burst into protest one morning nearly five years ago, triggering a series of copycat demonstrations across the country that shook the Communist government unlike any event in decades.
Frustrated by chronic shortages of food and medicine and ŌĆŗlengthy power outages as the coronavirus pandemic lingered, protesters marched in the streets chanting ŌĆ£Patria y Vida,ŌĆØ or ŌĆ£Homeland and Life,ŌĆØ the title of a popular song that inverts the Communist slogan "Patria o Muerte," or "Homeland or Death."
The phrase became a rallying cry for a movement that ŌĆīwas quickly crushed by state security forces.
Now, with Cuba under a U.S.-imposed oil blockade and sanctions that have thrown the country into a deeper crisis, Reuters visited the same neighborhoods where police and protesters clashed most fiercely on July 11, 2021: San Antonio de los Ba├▒os and the marginalized Havana neighborhood of La G├╝inera.
In interviews with 12 people on the record, and several others who declined to be identified, Reuters found that people remember the brutality of the last crackdown, a testament to the enduring power of CubaŌĆÖs security apparatus. Some, however, were eager to place blame on Washington and the punishing U.S. economic sanctions against Cuba.
While Cubans have expressed frustration over lengthy power blackouts, banging on kitchen pots in a plea to turn the ŌĆŗlights back on, for now, at least, a repeat of July 11 seems unlikely. Dissident leaders have largely been jailed or exiled since then, and the government has shut off the internet at signs of protests, as it did that day. Fear, though, is also a factor.
ŌĆ£I assure ŌĆŗyou, people won't protest in the streets because they're afraid,ŌĆØ said Brian Jimenez, 26, a baker from San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, who said he was beaten by police that day and detained for several days afterward.
Police showed restraint ŌüĀat first, he said, but then came the feared "black berets," a brigade of the Interior Ministry, and "they roughed up the people," setting an example that was still reverberating.
"A lot of my friends are still in jail," Jimenez said.
Reuters could not verify his account of police beatings of detainees, though other demonstrators and human ŌĆŗrights groups reported similar accounts of abuse.
The Cuban government did not reply to a request for comment.
Cubans expressed a range of opinions, from sympathy toward the Cuban government in the face of U.S. antagonism to openly criticizing the Communist government for failing to produce prosperity. Others called for ŌĆŗdialogue and understanding.
But all agreed there was little prospect of another massive popular uprising any time soon, citing the government's violent repression of 2021, resulting in long prison terms for many protesters, while also observing that demonstrations failed to produce any meaningful change.
Though only 40 km (25 miles) outside Havana, San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, population 50,000, is a world apart, typical of many towns across the eyebrow-shaped island. Tractors and carts drawn by scraggly horses, their ribs showing through weather-beaten hides, are almost as common as cars on the streets, where vendors sell locally grown potatoes, onions and cucumbers.
At the town square where many Cubans gathered in front of the church that day, university student Robert Perez said the government has retaliated against ŌĆŗJuly 11 protesters and their families, effectively causing self-censorship.
"When you protest, you can't find work. They take action against you or your family," said Perez, 27, a sociology student at Artemisa University. "In Cuba, everyone knows that one way to make a living is to open a small business and sell goods. Those who ŌĆŗrose up on July 11th can't do that."
Rights groups, the European Union and the United States estimate Cuba has jailed between 1,000 and 1,500 people since those widespread protests, many of whom continue to serve extended sentences.
The Cuban government did not respond to a query on how many prisoners it currently holds on convictions related to July 11 ŌĆīprotests.
Cuban authorities say those ŌüĀjailed were found guilty of crimes including public disorder, resisting arrest, robbery and vandalism. Cuba blames the United States for funding the unrest and encouraging its spread, part of a broader plot to overthrow the island's government.
Frustration over blackouts sometimes spills into the streets. Reuters witnessed several demonstrations in poor neighborhoods of Havana on Saturday night, as people banged on pots. In one instance, the lights came back on mid-protest. The protest promptly ended.
"We're tired of so many days of blackouts. Three days in a row of the same," said a man who identified himself as Edward Rafael, 18.
On Monday, about 20 students sat on the steps of the University of Havana in protest, airing grievances over blackouts and internet disruptions that have made remote learning almost impossible, as classes have been suspended during the crisis.
Long before July 11, the United States imposed the most comprehensive and longestŌĆærunning unilateral sanctions regime in the world on Cuba, largely with the intent to pressure and destabilize ŌĆŗthe Communist government in the name of promoting democracy and human rights.
The ŌĆŗUnited States has further tightened the screws this year, capturing Venezuelan President ŌüĀNicolas Maduro, Cuba's most important patron. It then imposed a near-total oil blockade on Cuba and joined Israel in attacking another longtime Washington adversary, Iran. Trump has said his government is negotiating with Cuban leaders to reach some kind of deal, though Cuba has yet to acknowledge official talks.
While tempers may flare should the shortages grow more acute, for now some Cubans are turning their ire toward Washington, not Havana.
Nieves Fuentes Osoria, an 82-year-old woman from San Antonio de los Ba├▒os, ŌĆŗcalled the U.S. embargo of Cuba a "genocide." She said Cuba would show the same type of fierce resistance as it did in the Bay of Pigs invasion of 1961, when the armed forces defeated an invasion of ŌĆŗexiles financed and trained by the United States.
"When ŌüĀwe're in danger, this is what we do," she said, holding up two fists. "Because I saw it on different occasions. When they tried to come through the Bay of Pigs, we stuck together."
Others took a neutral political stand but still concluded the protests went for naught.
"What did they accomplish? Nothing," said Deisy Garcia, 73. "They made a fuss, stirred up all this, and life goes on as usual."
Back in Havana, the streets of La G├╝inera bustled under the midday heat, despite the fuel blockade and lengthy blackouts. Pedestrians and electric motorbikes navigated the potholed streets, while barbers clipped hair with rechargeable cordless razors and food kiosks sold whatever produce was available. ŌüĀOn this day, tomatoes ŌĆŗwere abundant at several points of sale.
Yuniel Romero, 37, doubted there would be another uprising, as he cut hair from the front porch of a high-street business.
"The people are not going to ŌĆŗget involved because in real life nobody wants to be imprisoned again. The people have no way to defend themselves," Romero said.
Ariel Anillo, 52, sold sacks of charcoal, suddenly in demand due to a lack of cooking gas, beneath a poster of Fidel Castro.
He said most Cubans were persevering - "the lights go out for three days and nobody dies" - but the repression was also a factor.
"You know ŌĆŗthat if you go to a protest they can put you in prison for 15 to 20 years," Anillo said. "Then the situation is worse, and your family suffers."

item.title: Suspected Hamas member detained in Cyprus over weapons procurement

The Lebanese-born ŌĆŗsuspect, identified only as Kamel M. ŌĆŗin line with German privacy rules, ŌüĀwas detained at Cyprus' Larnaca airport on ŌĆŗMarch 6, arriving from Lebanon, they added ŌĆŗin a statement.
The suspect is wanted in relation to the transport of 300 rounds of live ammunition, ŌĆŗaccording to prosecutors. It wasn't clear ŌĆŗfrom the statement where the rounds had come from, ŌĆīor ŌüĀwhere they were thought to be heading.
"The operation served as preparation for deadly Hamas attacks on Israeli or Jewish institutions in ŌĆŗGermany and ŌĆŗEurope," they ŌüĀsaid.
Police also searched the suspect's apartment in Berlin.

Attacks against Jews and Jewish targets have risen worldwide ŌüĀsince ŌĆŗIsrael's war against Hamas in ŌĆŗGaza, triggered by the militant group's 2023 attacks on ŌĆŗIsrael.

item.title: Kremlin says Moscow mobile internet outages are done for sake of security

MOSCOW, March 10 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Tuesday said that recent instances of ŌĆŗmobile internet being turned off in ŌĆīMoscow and other major cities were aimed at ensuring security and were in accordance ŌĆŗwith the law.
In a call with ŌĆŗreporters, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said: "All ŌüĀdisconnections and communication restrictions are carried ŌĆŗout in strict compliance with current legislation."
"This ŌĆŗis all related to the need to ensure security," he added.
Peskov said that the impact ŌĆŗof the move on businesses needed ŌĆŗadditional analysis, and that solutions to their problems would ŌĆībe ŌüĀproposed.
On a call with reporters on Monday after Russian President Vladimir Putin spoke to U.S. President Donald Trump, Kremlin ŌĆŗforeign policy ŌĆŗaide Yury ŌüĀUshakov's connection cut out at least three times, Reuters reporters ŌĆŗon the call said.

item.title: Exxon looks to ditch┬ĀNew Jersey incorporation for Texas homecoming
item.description:
HOUSTON/BOSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Exxon Mobil (XOM.N) , opens new tab is seeking to abandon its corporate registration in New Jersey and reincorporate ŌĆīin Texas where its headquarters is located, the U.S. oil major said on Tuesday, in a move that could help bolster its defenses against activist shareholders and climate advocates.
Exxon announced the proposal in a proxy filing. If the move is approved by ŌĆŗshareholders, Exxon would become the latest of several high-profile companies including SpaceX, Tesla (TSLA.O) , opens new tab and Coinbase (COIN.O) , opens new tab to register in ŌĆŗthe business-friendly Lone Star State.
Last year, a new Texas law enhanced legal protections for ŌüĀbusinesses through several mechanisms, including reducing the threat of shareholder litigation by allowing companies to set stock ownership ŌĆŗthresholds for lawsuits.
"The Board believes Texas legislators, judges, and juries who might make decisions that impact Exxon Mobil are ŌĆŗgenerally more familiar with our business and operations," the company said in the filing.
The top U.S. oil producer, physically headquartered in Spring, Texas, has long been the target of environment-related lawsuits.
New Jersey officials sued Exxon, Chevron (CVX.N) , opens new tab and other fossil-fuel companies in 2022, ŌĆŗalleging that they contributed to climate change, forcing the state to spend billions of dollars to clean ŌĆŗup after major natural disasters such as Superstorm Sandy and Hurricane Ida. The suit was dismissed last year.
Incorporating in the same ŌĆīstate ŌüĀas a company's headquarters can help executives get the attention of state politicians whose help they might need on taxes or other policy questions, said Jill Fisch, a law professor at the University of Pennsylvania.
ŌĆ£One way you might show your loyalty and get the legislators to pay attention to you is to incorporate in your ŌĆŗhome state,ŌĆØ she said.
Exxon's ŌĆŗroots in New Jersey ŌüĀtrace back to its formation after the breakup of Standard Oil in the early 1900s, though its headquarters has been in Texas since 1989.
In September, Exxon took a novel ŌĆŗstep to help fend off activist shareholder resolutions during annual meetings, introducing a program ŌĆŗto allow ŌüĀretail investors to automatically cast their ballots in step with the board's recommendations.
Nearly 40% of the company's shares are held by individuals but just a quarter of them vote during proxy season, though they mostly support the board, ŌüĀthe company ŌĆŗsaid at the time.
Texas has sought to reinforce its reputation as ŌĆŗa safe haven for business and has been a major beneficiary as some companies have departed states such as Delaware, a top destination ŌĆŗfor incorporation.

item.title: India gains favour with global investors on returns, ESG momentum
item.description:
MUMBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - India is maturing as a market in which foreign investors who take into account ŌĆŗenvironmental, social and governance (ESG) factors are confident in dedicating more ŌĆīof their portfolio to, according to fund managers at the Indian Venture and Alternate Capital Association conclave in Mumbai, who cite the economy's scale, improving return outlook and advancing ŌĆŗESG standards as key differentiators.
India's market regulator SEBI has been ŌĆŗexpanding the scope of ESG-related regulations for over a decade, ŌüĀmaking detailed Business and Sustainability Report disclosures mandatory for the ŌĆŗcountry's top 1000 listed companies since 2023.
"India is clearly earning its space ŌĆŗin terms of allocations from global investors," said Ralph Keitel, head of Fund Investments at Zurich-based responsAbility Investment, speaking to Reuters at the conclave.
"Fifteen to 20 years ŌĆŗago, India was a great space, but returns took longer," Keitel ŌĆŗsaid. Now he sees a stronger near-term case as well.
Investors also said the ŌĆīcountry's ŌüĀappeal rests on its relative resilience at a time when global allocations are being reassessed across China, the United States and other emerging markets.
India's economy is projected to grow 6.5% in both fiscal years 2024-25 and 2025-26, ŌĆŗaccording to the ŌĆŗInternational Monetary Fund.
"Besides ŌüĀrisk mitigation, ESG impact ŌĆö having (a) good impact on communities adds value," Neha Grover, South Asia Lead for ŌĆŗPrivate Equity at IFC told Reuters. Grover added that India has become ŌĆŗone ŌüĀof the most consistent emerging markets for profitable exits, supported by a strengthening initial public offering (IPO) pipeline.
ResponsAbility Investment's Keitel also pointed to climateŌĆærelated opportunities as ŌüĀa ŌĆŗcore area of interest, citing sectors such ŌĆŗas mobility and solar panels. As incomes rise, environmental pressures tend to intensify, he added.

item.title: Russian strike on Ukraine's Sloviansk kills four, local official says

item.title: China ready to work with countries on inclusive nuclear governance, Vice Premier says

item.title: Court bars Kosovo president from announcing snap election date
item.description:
PRISTINA, March 10 (Reuters) - Kosovo's highest court told the president late on Monday not to announce the date for a snap election before March 31, ŌĆŗa move that further prolongs a political crisis that has ŌĆīengulfed the tiny Balkan nation.
Last Friday, President Vjosa Osmani dissolved the parliament and called a snap election - without setting a date - after lawmakers failed to pick a ŌĆŗnew head of state within the constitutional deadline that expired at ŌĆŗmidnight on Thursday.
Her move extended a political deadlock in ŌüĀKosovo, which is Europe's youngest nation and has aspirations to join the ŌĆŗEuropean Union. Kosovo had no functioning government for most of last year ŌĆŗas the fractured parliament failed to elect a speaker for months.
In a verdict published late on Monday, the Constitutional Court temporarily barred the president from setting an ŌĆŗelection date and also froze the parliament's efforts to elect a ŌĆŗnew head of state. The court decision stays in effect until March 31.
Prime Minister ŌĆīAlbin ŌüĀKurti had asked the Constitutional Court to rule on whether Osmani had acted in line with the constitution. Kurti is a former ally of Osmani but decided not to back her for a second term in ŌĆŗthe ceremonial role ŌĆŗof president.
Kurti's government ŌüĀwelcomed the court verdict but added: "For three weeks we will be in a state of waiting, while ŌĆŗa number of agreements and decisions of importance for ŌĆŗcitizens continue ŌüĀto remain without approval by the parliament."
The parliamentary election, if it goes ahead, would be the third in just over a year.
Choosing a president - ŌüĀthough the ŌĆŗrole brings no significant political powers - has ŌĆŗalways proven politically challenging because two-thirds of lawmakers must attend the voting session in parliament, ŌĆŗmaking it easy to disrupt.

item.title: US designation of Afghanistan 'regrettable,' Afghan Taliban says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The Afghan Taliban on Tuesday ŌĆŗcalled the U.S decision ŌĆīto designate Afghanistan a "state sponsor of ŌĆŗwrongful detention" regrettable, ŌĆŗand said it wanted ŌüĀto resolve the ŌĆŗmatter through dialogue ŌĆŗafter the U.S. demanded the release of U.S. ŌĆŗcitizens held.

item.title: Morning Bid: Oil's combustible calm
item.description:
March 10 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
The fog of war remains thick. Crude oil prices have become wildly volatile this week, with intraday swings exceeding $30 per barrel - some of the biggest one-day moves ŌĆŗon record.
After soaring as high as $120 early Monday - the highest in four years - oil prices then slumped back below $100 later in the day after ŌĆīPresident Trump once again teased the prospect of a short-lived conflict with Iran, saying the war was ŌĆ£very completeŌĆØ.
IŌĆÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why inflation isn't the only risk keeping central bankers up at night as the conflict in Iran continues.
And listen to the latest episode of the Morning Bid daily podcast where I discuss how headlineŌĆædriven oil swings are ŌĆŗrewiring stocks, bonds and rate expectations.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news in markets and finance seven days a week.
TrumpŌĆÖs apparent ŌĆŗabout-face on Monday not only calmed oil prices but also the complex of world stocks and bonds that had shivered after oil's ŌüĀtriple-digit price spike. Wall Street stocks closed up on Monday, while on Tuesday South KoreaŌĆÖs KOSPI index regained nearly 6% and JapanŌĆÖs Nikkei climbed nearly 3%.
Meantime, U.S. ŌĆŗTreasury yields tumbled and the dollar took a breather on Tuesday, steadying against major currencies, helping gold edge up in turn. U.S. stock futures were up ahead of the bell, having ŌĆŗremained remarkably calm amid yesterday's tumult.
Many will say this is the TACO (ŌĆ£Trump always chickens outŌĆØ) trade par excellence, but there are few signs that TrumpŌĆÖs optimistic turn is playing out on the ground, with IranŌĆÖs Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps still asserting that no oil will be exported as long as U.S.-Israeli attacks continued.
Responding to that over social media, Trump threatened further retaliation against Iran if ŌĆŗit continued to disrupt oil flows through the Strait of Hormuz. So, for now, the tit-for-tat looks set to continue.
Through all of this, oil prices held above $90 per ŌĆŗbarrel - a level that would have seemed frightening only last month. And pass-through to fuel costs is already being felt in the U.S., where a comfortable majority of Americans now believe prices ŌĆīwill worsen ŌüĀover the next year.
In the background, G7 finance ministers on Monday mulled a possible joint release of their oil reserves to calm the horses, though they stopped short of doing so now , with a G7 official telling Reuters that the decision was ŌĆ£just about timingŌĆØ.
Elsewhere, China reported a spectacular surge in its trade surplus for the first two months of the year, with exports up more than 20% year-on-year. While that certainly supports its new growth target of just under 5% and comes despite falling bilateral trade with the U.S., ŌĆŗit also predates this monthŌĆÖs spike in ŌĆŗoil prices.
Later in the day, after ŌüĀthe bell, Oracle is set to report earnings, with traders likely to be on the lookout for signs of a payoff from spiralling capex as it ploughs billions into AI data center expansion.
Chart of the day
China roared into 2026 with exports far outstripping ŌĆŗforecasts, fuelled by red-hot electronics demand, putting the economy on track to top last year's record $1.2 trillion trade surplus, despite ŌĆŗthe persistent drop in ŌüĀbilateral trade with the United States.
Although the data preceded this month's energy and shipping shock from the war in Iran, outbound shipments from the world's second-largest economy grew 21.8% in U.S. dollar terms in the January-February period - up sharply from the 6.6% increase recorded in December and blowing past the median forecast of 7.1% in a Reuters poll.
Today's events to ŌüĀwatch
* U.S. ŌĆŗFebruary existing home sales (10:00 AM EST)
* U.S. 3-year note auction
* U.S. corporate earnings: Oracle, Domino's Pizza
Want to ŌĆŗreceive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of the ŌĆŗauthor. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Al Qaeda-linked group killed at least 12 truck drivers in Mali, HRW says

item.title: Qatar wants to bolster security partnership with US after Iran's strikes
item.description:
DOHA, March 10 (Reuters) - Qatar wants to strengthen its defence partnership with the United States in ŌĆŗthe wake of Iranian air strikes on Qatari territory, ŌĆīthe foreign ministry spokesperson said on Tuesday, even as it sees the existing deal as an important deterrent.
Iran launched missile and drone attacks ŌĆŗon its oil-producing Gulf neighbours after U.S.-Israeli strikes that ŌĆŗkilled its top leaders. The conflict disrupted output in the ŌüĀregion and sent oil prices surging.
Qatar hosts the biggest U.S. ŌĆŗbase in the Middle East, Al Udeid Air Base, which ŌĆŗwas attacked by Iran during the war that erupted on February 28.
The spokesperson, Majed Al Ansari, told a media briefing on Tuesday the security partnership ŌĆŗwith the United States was not in question, but added ŌĆŗthat its deals with the U.S. and European countries need to be strengthened.
"The ŌĆīpartnerships ŌüĀare the main stop-gap and deterrent against any attack on our country," he added.
"When things go out of control and spiral, the result is deterrents do not work... But that doesn't ŌĆŗmean you shouldn't ŌĆŗhave deterrents. ŌüĀYou should strengthen these deterrents and that is exactly what are we trying to do right," ŌĆŗAl Ansari said.
U.S. President Donald Trump pledged in ŌĆŗOctober ŌüĀto treat any armed attack on Qatar as a threat to the United States' own security.
Iran has vowed to retaliate ŌüĀagainst U.S. ŌĆŗtargets in the region in case ŌĆŗof any attack.

item.title: Human Rights Watch says that Israel has been illegally using white phosphorus in Lebanon
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 10 (Reuters) - The Israeli military illegally used white phosphorus munitions over homes in the southern Lebanese town Yohmor on ŌĆŗMarch 3, posing a threat to civilians, Human Rights Watch said in a ŌĆīreport on Monday.
"The incendiary effects of white phosphorus can cause death or cruel injuries that result in lifelong suffering," Ramzi Kaiss, Lebanon researcher at Human Rights Watch, said in the statement.
The rights group said it ŌĆŗhad verified eight images showing white phosphorus used over a residential part of Yohmor, ŌĆŗand civil defense workers responding to fires in the area.
Reuters could not ŌüĀindependently verify the group's findings.
The Israeli military told Reuters it was unaware and could not ŌĆŗconfirm the use of shells that contain white phosphorus in Lebanon. It added that it ŌĆŗhad not reviewed the same videos as HRW and could not comment on the claims.
Lebanese authorities have not commented.
The Israeli military told residents of Yohmor and 50 other villages and towns to evacuate in a ŌĆŗstatement early on March 3.
Reuters previously reported on Lebanese farmers testing their soil to determine whether ŌĆŗthey could resume planting in the aftermath of Israel's reported use of white phosphorus in 2023.
As of July 2024, the Lebanese ŌĆīNational ŌüĀCouncil for Scientific Research said there had been 175 Israeli attacks on south Lebanon using white phosphorus from October 2023, many of them sparking fires that have affected over 600 hectares (1,480 acres) of farmland.
White phosphorus munitions can legally be used on battlefields to make smoke screens, ŌĆŗgenerate illumination, mark targets ŌĆŗor burn bunkers and ŌüĀbuildings.
HRW said under international humanitarian law the use of airburst white phosphorus over populated areas is unlawful.
White phosphorus is considered an incendiary weapon ŌĆŗunder Protocol III of the Convention on the Prohibition of Use ŌĆŗof Certain ŌüĀConventional Weapons. The protocol prohibits using incendiary weapons against military targets located among civilians, although Israel has not signed it and is not bound by it.
Nearly 400 people have been killed and hundreds ŌüĀof thousands ŌĆŗhave been forced to leave their homes as Israeli ŌĆŗstrikes have pounded Lebanon for over a week.
HRW previously accused Israel of using white phosphorus during Israel's 2023 operations against ŌĆŗLebanon, a charge Israel's military denied at the time.
(This story has been refiled to correct the spelling of 'phosphorus' in the headline, and to add the missing word 'of' in paragraph 8)

item.title: Iran not more formidable than thought, top U.S. general says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The top U.S. general said on Tuesday that while Iran was fighting, ŌĆŗit was not more formidable than Washington had thought, as ŌĆīthe United States geared up for the most intense day of strikes against Iran in the war so far.
General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint ŌĆŗChiefs of Staff, told reporters that the United States was ŌĆŗcarrying out strikes against Iranian mine-laying vessels and the ŌüĀPentagon would look at a range of options if it was ŌĆŗtasked with escorting ships through the Strait of Hormuz.
The war has effectively ŌĆŗshut the Strait of Hormuz, a chokepoint for global oil and liquefied natural gas transport, leaving tankers unable to sail for more than a week and ŌĆŗforcing producers to halt pumping as storage fills.
"I think they're fighting, ŌĆŗand I respect that, but I don't think they're more formidable than what we ŌĆīthought," ŌüĀCaine said.
U.S. President Donald Trump on Monday threatened to escalate the war with Iran if it blocked oil shipments from the Middle East, even as he predicted a quick end to the conflict.
ŌĆŗDuring the press ŌĆŗconference at the ŌüĀPentagon, U.S. Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth said the United States would carry out the most intense day ŌĆŗof strikes against Iran on Tuesday.
Hegseth reiterated that ŌĆŗthis would ŌüĀnot be an endless war and said Trump would decide when the U.S. campaign would end.
The United States has carried out strikes ŌüĀagainst more ŌĆŗthan 5,000 targets in the first ŌĆŗ10 days of the campaign, including against more than 50 naval ships, Caine said.

item.title: Shots fired at US consulate in Toronto, no injuries reported
item.description:
TORONTO/WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Shots were ŌĆŗfired at the U.S. consulate in Toronto on Tuesday, ŌĆīpolice said, adding that evidence was found of a discharged firearm and that no injuries were ŌĆŗreported.

Representatives for ŌüĀthe U.S. consulate in Toronto and ŌĆŗthe U.S. Department of State did not ŌĆŗimmediately respond to requests for comment.
In another post, police said they had no information on a potential ŌĆŗsuspect and were on scene investigating the ŌĆŗincident. They did not provide details of any motive.
Separately, ŌĆīon ŌüĀSunday, an improvised device exploded in Norway at the U.S. embassy in Oslo , and police were still searching for a suspect, with ŌĆŗa possible ŌĆŗlink to ŌüĀthe Iran war among the lines of inquiry.
In New York ŌĆŗCity, two men have been charged with ŌĆŗterrorism ŌüĀafter throwing a homemade bomb at anti-Islam protesters over the weekend.

item.title: Britain working with allies to support shipping through Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Britain is working with allies on a range ŌĆŗof options to support commercial ŌĆīshipping through the Strait of Hormuz in the face of Iranian ŌĆŗthreats, Prime Minister Keir ŌĆŗStarmer's spokesperson said on Tuesday ŌüĀafter calls with the leaders ŌĆŗof Italy and Germany.
After speaking ŌĆŗto Germany's Friedrich Merz and Italy's Giorgia Meloni late on Monday, Downing ŌĆŗStreet said in a readout ŌĆŗthat they agreed on the "vital importance of ŌĆīfreedom ŌüĀof navigation" through the Strait and "agreed to work closely together in the coming days ŌĆŗin the ŌĆŗface ŌüĀof Iranian threats."

item.title: Russia's deportations of Ukrainian children amount to crimes against humanity, UN inquiry finds
item.description:
GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - A U.N. investigation found on Tuesday that the deportation and transfer of ŌĆŗUkrainian children by Russian authorities since Moscow's full-scale invasion of ŌĆīthe country in 2022 amounted to crimes against humanity.
"In this report, the Commission concluded that crimes against humanity and war crimes ŌĆŗby Russian authorities have targeted children, who are ŌĆŗamong the most vulnerable victims. These crimes have ŌüĀirreversible consequences on their lives and their future," according ŌĆŗto the report to be presented to the U.N. Human ŌĆŗRights Council in Geneva on Thursday.
The report by the Independent International Commission of Inquiry on Ukraine studied the cases of 1,205 children ŌĆŗfrom five oblasts, or regions, in Ukraine and said ŌĆŗthat 80% of them have yet to return to Ukraine.
Ukrainian authorities say ŌĆīRussia ŌüĀhas illegally deported or forcibly displaced more than 19,500 children to Russia and Belarus in violation of the Geneva Conventions.
U.S.-funded research last year showed Russia expanded its forced ŌĆŗre-education programmes of ŌĆŗdeported children.
"The ŌüĀdeportations and transfers have originated from various locations across a wide geographic area in ŌĆŗRussian-occupied areas in Ukraine, following a well-established ŌĆŗpattern ŌüĀof conduct, indicating that these acts have been widespread and systematic," the U.N. report said.
Russia denies it is taking ŌüĀchildren against ŌĆŗtheir will and says it ŌĆŗhas been evacuating people voluntarily to remove them from the war zone.

item.title: Pope Leo fires San Diego bishop accused of stealing $250,000

Bishop Emanuel Hana Shaleta had ŌĆŗled the small Chaldean Catholic community ŌĆŗin the California city since 2017. He ŌüĀpleaded not guilty to 16 counts ŌĆŗof embezzlement and money laundering at a hearing ŌĆŗon Monday, according to local media reports.
San Diego Deputy District Attorney Joel Madero said the alleged crimes ŌĆŗtook place in 2024 and were reported ŌĆŗby a church employee who noticed the missing funds, ŌĆīaccording ŌüĀto KGTV, a local ABC affiliate.
Hana Shaleta was arrested on March 5 at San Diego's international airport while attempting to leave ŌĆŗthe U.S., according ŌĆŗto ŌüĀa sheriff's office statement.
The bishop's lawyer said at Monday's hearing that ŌĆŗHana Shaleta was taking a planned ŌĆŗtrip ŌüĀto Germany.
Chaldean Catholics recognise the authority of the pope but worship according to an eastern ŌüĀChristian ŌĆŗliturgical rite. There are about ŌĆŗ71,000 Chaldean Catholics in San Diego, according to Vatican ŌĆŗstatistics.

item.title: Ex-Syrian intelligence officer appears in UK court charged with crimes against humanity
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - A former member of Syria's Air Force Intelligence attended a British court hearing via videolink on Tuesday charged with crimes against humanity and torture relating to the ŌĆŗsuppression of pro-democracy demonstrations in Damascus in 2011.
Salem Michel Al-Salem, 58, who now ŌĆīlives in Britain, appeared virtually at the hearing at London's Westminster Magistrates' Court from his home. He was wearing a breathing apparatus mask and the court was told he suffered from degenerative motor neurone disease.
Al-Salem is charged ŌĆŗwith three counts of murder as a crime against humanity relating to deaths in ŌĆŗApril and July 2011 "as part of a widespread or systematic attack against a ŌüĀcivilian population with knowledge of the attack".
He is also accused of three charges of torture ŌĆŗrelating to incidents in 2011 and 2012, and one of conduct ancillary to murder as a crime ŌĆŗagainst humanity. He did not speak during the hearing and there was no indication as to how he would plead.
His lawyer Sean Caulfield told the court that Al-Salem was too unwell to confirm his name.
The seven charges were ŌĆŗbrought under a British law that allows the prosecution of serious international crimes committed abroad. ŌĆŗThe Crown Prosecution Service said it was the first time it had brought charges of murder as crimes against ŌĆīhumanity.
In ŌüĀ2005, Afghan warlord Faryadi Zardad was convicted by a British court of torture that had taken place in Afghanistan.
Al-Salem, who has sought indefinite leave to remain in Britain, was a colonel in the Syrian Air Force Intelligence department with oversight of the Information Branch in the district of ŌĆŗJobar, to the east of ŌĆŗcentral Damascus, British ŌüĀprosecutors say.
He is accused of leading a group tasked with quelling the demonstrations, which mostly occurred during Friday afternoon prayers. Prosecutors say he gave ŌĆŗhis men orders to open fire on protesters, which led to the ŌĆŗdeaths of ŌüĀsome individuals.
Prosecutors say he was also present at, or took part in, the torture of men at the Information Branch building.
Al-Salem was first arrested in central England in December 2021. His lawyer had sought ŌüĀan order ŌĆŗto withhold his name, arguing it could pose a ŌĆŗrisk to his safety. England's Chief Magistrate Paul Goldspring rejected the application but ordered that his address not be made ŌĆŗpublic.
He will next appear on Friday at London's Old Bailey court.

item.title: Iran shock creates problematic fiscal loop
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - ItŌĆÖs never a convenient time for war-induced spikes in oil and gas prices, as RussiaŌĆÖs invasion of Ukraine in 2022 can attest. But todayŌĆÖs flare up in the Middle East hits Western economies at a ŌĆŗmore fragile moment. Labour markets are looser, corporate margins thinner, and interest rates much higher than they were four years ago. Whether ŌĆīgovernments shield households and firms from costlier energy or leave them exposed, the result is likely the same: borrowing costs will climb.
Bond markets are already tense. Even after Donald Trump on Tuesday predicted rapid de escalation with Iran , 10 year yields in Germany, France, the United Kingdom and Japan remain well above their pre crisis levels. Unlike the United States, all are large ŌĆŗnet importers of energy and particularly natural gas, which plays a key role in setting electricity prices.
Yet, if disruption at the Strait of Hormuz ŌĆŗpersists, governments may be forced to brave hostile debt markets and once again fund energy price caps, subsidies and tax ŌüĀcuts. South Korea has already moved in that direction, and UK Prime Minister Keir Starmer signalled on Monday that Britain may follow. BruegelŌĆÖs data on EuropeŌĆÖs last ŌĆŗenergy shock shows how expensive such measures can be: between September 2021 and January 2023, they cost European countries -- including the UK -- 3.7% of GDP. This would ŌĆŗsit atop the 3.4% deficit that official projections , opens new tab had already pinned on the European Union for this year.
But inaction would lead to a similar place. The post-pandemic experience showed that companies react to energy shocks by coordinating price hikes to protect their margins , opens new tab , which is why even ŌĆ£coreŌĆØ inflation in the U.S., the eurozone and Britain ŌĆō which excludes the price of fuel and food ŌĆō peaked ŌĆŗat range of between 5.7% and 7.1% in 2023. In theory, central banks should look through one off energy shocks; in practice, this so-called pass through ŌĆŗeffect forces them to respond. Markets have already shifted from expecting year end rate cuts at the Bank of England and the European Central Bank to pricing in a hike ŌĆīfrom the ŌüĀformer and no change from the latter, according to derivative prices collected by LSEG.
The inflation hit should still be smaller this time. European natural gas trades around 50 euros per megawatt hour, far below the 300-euro peak of 2022. Deutsche BankŌĆÖs Sanjay Raja estimates that even a generous UK support package would total only 14 billion pounds, or 0.4% of GDP, compared with 90 billion pounds previously. Still, that extra fiscal hit comes at a time when government tax revenues are already ŌĆŗstrained by low growth. The danger is ŌĆŗthat more, potentially open-ended financial commitments ŌüĀpush up bond yields, making the fiscal math even trickier.
Central banks could help break the feedback loop with a clear pledge to backstop sovereign bond markets, as the ECB did during the pandemic. At the margin, energy subsidies might help ease ŌĆŗfears of a price spiral. But the current level of inflation, which is at or above target in the ŌĆŗEU and UK, means ŌüĀratesetters probably wonŌĆÖt act unless bond markets are under extreme duress. If the Iran war continues, government coffers could become a pain point.
Follow Jon Sindreu on X , opens new tab and LinkedIn , opens new tab .

item.title: WHO warns of health risks from 'black rain' in Iran

GENEVA, March 10 (Reuters) - The World Health Organization warned on Tuesday that the "black rain" falling in Iran ŌĆŗafter strikes on oil facilities could cause respiratory ŌĆīproblems, and it backed IranŌĆÖs advisory urging people to remain indoors.
The U.N. health agency, which has an office in ŌĆŗIran and works with authorities on health emergencies, ŌĆŗsaid it has received multiple reports of oil-laden ŌüĀrain this week. Asked whether the WHO backed that advice, he said: "Given what is at risk right now, the ŌĆŗoil storage facilities, ŌĆŗthe refineries ŌüĀthat have been struck, triggering fires, bringing serious air quality concerns, that is ŌĆŗdefinitely a good idea."
One video sent to ŌĆŗReuters by ŌüĀa WHO staff member showed what they said was a cleaner mopping up black liquid at its office ŌüĀentrance ŌĆŗin Tehran on March 8. ŌĆŗReuters was not able to independently verify the footage.

item.title: Decision to turn back on nuclear was a strategic mistake, EU's Von der Leyen says

PARIS, March 10 (Reuters) - The decision to reduce the share of nuclear energy in the electricity production mix in the European Union ŌĆŗwas a strategic mistake given that the bloc is ŌĆīneither an oil or gas producer, EU Commission President Ursula Von der Leyen said on Tuesday.
"This reduction in the share of nuclear was ŌĆŗa choice, I believe that it was a strategic ŌĆŗmistake for Europe to turn its back on a ŌüĀreliable, affordable source of low-emissions power," she said in ŌĆŗa speech at an event on nuclear energy held in Paris.
The ŌĆŗEU relied on nuclear for one third of its electricity in 1990 and now the share is only close to 15%, she said.
The European ŌĆŗUnion aims to stimulate the development of small nuclear reactors, ŌĆŗto make Europe less dependent on imported fossil fuels, she added.
