item.title: Ukraine sends drone experts to three countries in Middle East, Zelenskiy says
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - Ukraine has sent air defence teams to Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and ŌĆīSaudi Arabia to help them in their fight against Iran's aerial attacks, President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said on Tuesday.
Gulf states have expended large quantities of precious air-defence missiles to counter IranŌĆÖs attack drones and have sought Ukraine's expertise in downing ŌĆŗthem. Kyiv downs Russian drones every night using an array of weaponry including cheaper, ŌĆŗsmaller drones or jamming equipment.
"Regarding the situation in the Middle East, we have ŌüĀsent our teams: three professional, equipped teams," Zelenskiy told reporters. He did not give details ŌĆŗon the teams but said they should all be in place this week.
His communications adviser also said ŌĆŗUkrainian specialists had already been deployed to a U.S. military base in Jordan, without providing details.
Zelenskiy said the presence of Ukrainian pilots was key to the effectiveness of interceptor drone operations.
"Even those countries that were quietly buying ŌĆŗinterceptors have, I think, realised that without our military, our operators, our software... without all of ŌĆŗthat, the interceptors simply don't work."
Since the U.S. and Israel began airstrikes on Iran on February 28, ŌĆīUkraine has ŌüĀsought to leverage its long experience of countering Russia's Iranian-designed Shahed attack drones.
Kyiv says Russia - which now manufactures them itself - has launched more than 57,000 Shahed-type drones at Ukraine so far.
Reuters reported last week that the U.S. and Qatar were looking to buy interceptor drones from Ukraine as a ŌĆŗmuch cheaper alternative to missiles ŌĆŗfor downing Shaheds.
Asked ŌüĀwhat Kyiv would get in return for the deployment of the specialist teams, Zelenskiy said Ukraine was seeking air defence missiles above all else.
Kyiv has ŌĆŗcomplained for months of critical shortages of those munitions, particularly for the ŌĆŗU.S.-made Patriot ŌüĀsystems which are the Ukraine's only effective tool to intercept ballistic missiles.
The Iran war has delayed a fresh round of peace talks brokered by Washington between Russia and Ukraine.
Zelenskiy told reporters on Tuesday the ŌüĀnext ŌĆŗtrilateral meeting had been postponed to next week, adding that ŌĆŗhe wanted more swaps of prisoners of war and discussion of a possible meeting between himself, U.S. President Donald Trump ŌĆŗand Russia's Vladimir Putin.

item.title: Exclusive: As many as 150 US troops wounded so far in Iran war, sources say
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - As many as 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the 10-day-old ŌĆŗwar with Iran, two people familiar with the matter told ŌĆīReuters on Tuesday.
The casualty figure has not been previously reported. Prior to Reuters' publication of the figure, the Pentagon had only disclosed eight U.S. personnel seriously injured.
In a statement after ŌĆŗReuters published its report, the Pentagon estimated the figure to be approximately ŌĆŗ140 wounded and said the vast majority of them were ŌüĀminor.
"Since the start of Operation Epic Fury, approximately 140 U.S. service members ŌĆŗhave been wounded over 10 days of sustained attacks," said chief Pentagon ŌĆŗspokesperson Sean Parnell.
He said 108 of the wounded service members had already returned to duty.
Parnell said the eight seriously wounded service members were receiving the highest level of medical ŌĆŗcare.
Reuters could not determine the types of injuries and whether they include traumatic brain ŌĆŗinjuries, which are common after exposure to blasts.
Iran has launched retaliatory strikes against U.S. military bases ŌĆīsince ŌüĀthe start of the conflict on Feb. 28. It has also struck diplomatic missions in Arab Gulf states as well as hotels and airports and damaged oil infrastructure.
The Pentagon says the number of Iranian strikes has fallen sharply ŌĆŗsince the start of ŌĆŗthe war, as ŌüĀthe U.S. military bombs Iran's weapons inventories and targets Iran's more limited number of missile launchers.
Asked if ŌĆŗIran was a stronger adversary than he expected when the ŌĆŗU.S. military ŌüĀdrew up its war plans, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, told reporters earlier on Tuesday that the fight was not harder than ŌüĀexpected.
"I think ŌĆŗthey're fighting, and I respect that, but ŌĆŗI don't think they are more formidable than what we thought," Caine told a Pentagon briefing.

item.title: US Senate confirms National Security Agency director, ending long vacancy
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Senate confirmed General Joshua Rudd on Tuesday to lead the National Security Agency and the ŌĆŗnation's Cyber Command, filling a position that had been vacant ŌĆīsince President Donald Trump abruptly fired his predecessor in a national security purge.
The Senate voted 71-29 to confirm Rudd, making him a four-star general.
Trump nominated Rudd, then ŌĆŗa lieutenant general and No. 2 at the military's ŌĆŗIndo-Pacific Command, for the so-called dual-hat position in December.
The NSA ŌüĀmonitors, collects and processes information and data for global intelligence ŌĆŗand counterintelligence purposes. Cyber Command conducts offensive and defensive cyber operations, ŌĆŗan increasingly important part of military campaigns including the current Iran war .
The agency was shaken up in April, when Trump fired General Timothy Haugh along with Wendy ŌĆŗNoble, his deputy.
No reason was given for the firings, which took ŌĆŗplace after the Republican president had an Oval Office meeting with Laura Loomer, ŌĆīa ŌüĀfar-right political activist known as a conspiracy theorist, who said on X that she had given Trump a list of officials she considered disloyal to him.
In a statement, Republican U.S. Senator Tom Cotton ŌĆŗof Arkansas, chairman ŌĆŗof the Senate ŌüĀIntelligence Committee, praised the confirmation.
"General Rudd is a war hero with a lifetime of service to ŌĆŗour nation. He is the right choice to lead ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀprotection of our nation from cyberattacks by Iran , Russia, and China," said Cotton, who criticized Democrats for not acting more quickly.
Senator Ron Wyden of ŌüĀOregon, a ŌĆŗsenior member of the intelligence panel, ŌĆŗsaid in a letter to Trump , opens new tab that he did not feel Rudd was qualified and ŌĆŗblocked efforts to fast-track his confirmation.

item.title: U.S. Navy has not yet escorted ships through Strait of Hormuz, White House says
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz, the White House said on Tuesday, just after U.S. Secretary of ŌĆīEnergy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which had said the U.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the key water way.
The U.S.-Israel war against Iran has already effectively halted ŌĆŗshipments through the Strait along Iran's coast, where a fifth of global ŌĆŗoil and liquefied natural gas normally passes, and Middle East oil ŌüĀproducers have run out of storage and stopped pumping.
U.S. President Donald Trump said ŌĆŗon March 3 that the U.S. would provide protection through the Strait for oil ŌĆŗtankers. The Pentagon on Tuesday renewed threats to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying vessels and mine storage facilities.
Wright then posted on X ŌĆŗthat the U.S. Navy had escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of ŌĆŗHormuz "to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets."
Soon after, he deleted the post for reasons that ŌĆīwere ŌüĀunclear.
The United States has not yet escorted any oil tankers or vessels through the Strait of Hormuz, White House Press Secretary Karoline Leavitt told reporters when asked about the issue at a press conference later on Tuesday.
Commenting on Wright's remarks, a ŌĆŗspokesperson for Iran's Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuards denied an ŌüĀoil ship had been escorted.
"Any movement of the U.S. fleet and its allies will be stopped by our missiles and ŌĆŗdrones," Ali Mohammad Naini said in comments carried by Iranian ŌĆŗstate media.
The ŌüĀtop U.S. general earlier on Tuesday said the U.S. military has started looking at ways to potentially escort ships through the Strait of Hormuz, should it be ordered to ŌüĀdo ŌĆŗso.
"We're looking at a range of options there," General ŌĆŗDan Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.

item.title: White House to Americans: Rise in energy prices over Iran is temporary
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - The American public will see oil and gas ŌĆŗprices drop rapidly, White House press secretary ŌĆīKaroline Leavitt said on Tuesday, once the objectives of the joint Israeli-U.S. air war against Iran are fully achieved.
"Rest ŌĆŗassured, to the American people, the recent ŌĆŗincrease in oil and gas prices is temporary, and ŌüĀthis operation will result in lower gas ŌĆŗprices in the long term," Leavitt told reporters.
Oil ŌĆŗprices on Monday surged to more than $119 a barrel - their highest since June 2022 - as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia ŌĆŗand other producers stoked fears of major ŌĆŗdisruptions to global supplies.
Leavitt added that Trump and his energy team ŌĆīwere ŌüĀclosely watching the markets, speaking with industry leaders, and that the U.S. military was drawing up additional options in line with the president's directive ŌĆŗto keep the ŌĆŗStrait of ŌüĀHormuz open.
The deliberations reflect White House worries that the surge in ŌĆŗoil prices - following more than a week ŌĆŗof ŌüĀU.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran - will hurt U.S. businesses and consumers ahead of the November midterm ŌüĀelections, ŌĆŗwhen Trump's fellow Republicans hope ŌĆŗto retain control of Congress.

item.title: Nigeria prioritises local gasoline supply, suspends import licences
item.description:
LAGOS, March 10 (Reuters) - Nigeria has suspended the issuance of gasoline import licences for a second straight month as regulators begin enforcing provisions of ŌĆŗthe Petroleum Industry Act (PIA) that only allow imports when the African ŌĆīcountry's domestic supply falls short.
Data from the Nigerian Midstream and Downstream Petroleum Regulatory Authority (NMDPRA) show no import licences were issued in February, while the Crude Oil Refineries Association of ŌĆŗNigeria (CORAN) confirmed that none has been issued so far in March, signalling ŌĆŗa shift towards prioritising local output.
This shift highlights a stronger ŌüĀintent by Nigerian authorities to protect domestic refining and marks a win ŌĆŗfor the Dangote Refinery, which last year sued the regulator and the state ŌĆŗoil company to force a halt to imports.
Under the PIA, the regulator may grant import permits only when domestic production is not enough to meet national demand.
The previous regulator, who quit ŌĆŗlast year, argued that issuing licences was necessary to maintain competition and ŌĆŗprevent market dominance.
Fuel pump prices have surged by more than 54% since the U.S. and ŌĆīIsrael ŌüĀbegan strikes on Iran last week, pushing global oil markets higher. NMDPRA spokesperson George EneŌĆæIta blamed the sharp rise in prices on escalating conflict in the Middle East.
Nigeria's average daily petrol consumption fell to 56.9 million litres per day ŌĆŗin February 2026, ŌĆŗdown from 60.2 ŌüĀmillion litres in January.
In February, the Dangote Refinery supplied 36.5 million litres of petrol and 8 million litres of ŌĆŗdiesel to the local market. The regulator deemed these volumes ŌĆŗwere sufficient, ŌüĀleading to its decision to withhold import licences.
Eche Idoko, spokesperson for the Crude Oil Refiners Association of Nigeria (CORAN), which has long urged the government to stop ŌüĀissuing import ŌĆŗlicences that undermine local refiners' margins, welcomed the ŌĆŗregulator's stance.
"For us, anything that protects local production is a good move. The challenge now is ŌĆŗto sustain the momentum," Idoko said.

item.title: Rising gas prices from Iran war imperil Trump's Republican majority in Congress
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Minutes into Donald Trump 's State of the Union address laying out his argument for why voters should keep Republicans in power in November's midterm elections, the president boasted that falling gasoline prices had ŌĆŗsolved a "disaster" left behind by his predecessor.
Two weeks later, gas prices have risen by nearly 60 cents a gallon, part of a burgeoning energy crisis that began when ŌĆīTrump and Israel launched air strikes against Iran on February 28 and set off a regional conflict that has expanded well beyond Iran's borders.
The pain at the pump is quickly turning into a political headache for Trump and his fellow Republicans, whose narrow congressional majority looks increasingly fragile ahead of the midterms.
Crude oil prices on Tuesday were easing off Monday's dramatic gains, and it remains to be seen where gasoline prices will head in the nearly nine months ŌĆŗleading up to the November 3 election. But even before the Iran war, U.S. voters were already angry about the high cost of living and frustrated that Trump has not done ŌĆŗmore to address it, Reuters/Ipsos polls show .
"You can't hide gas prices," said Jacob Perry, a Republican strategist. "You can lie about all this other stuff and claim ŌüĀeverything is fake news. But there's a giant sign on every street corner saying how bad things are. Literally every commute to work is a reminder."
Democrats have promised to put ŌĆŗaffordability center stage in their campaigns. The party needs to flip only three Republican-held House of Representatives seats to win a majority, though they face a tougher path in the Senate.
"Trump promised a Golden ŌĆŗAge in America," House Democratic leader Hakeem Jeffries said on X. "Meanwhile, Republicans are crashing the economy, gas prices are out of control and the extremists are spending billions dropping bombs in the Middle East."
Some House Republicans, who are huddling in Florida this week at a policy retreat to discuss their legislative agenda, acknowledged that surging gas prices are a worry to voters, but cited Trump's assertion that the increase would be short-lived and said the administration's efforts ŌĆŗto increase domestic energy production would lessen the sting.
"Every day that people pull into the gas station and pay what they're paying for fuel, I mean, it hurts, and we know it hurts," ŌĆŗsaid Representative Austin Scott, of Georgia. "We're going to do what we can to fix it. They understand it's a short-term issue."
The average gas price nationwide on Tuesday was $3.54 a gallon, up about 19% ŌĆīsince the ŌüĀwar began, according to AAA.
In a sign of the White HouseŌĆÖs concern, Trump planned to review several options aimed at curbing oil prices, Reuters reported on Monday, including a possible release of crude oil from strategic reserves, restricting U.S. exports and waiving some federal taxes. Trump said on Monday evening that the U.S. would waive some oil-related sanctions, though he did not offer details.
Trump plans to discuss the economy when he travels to Ohio and Kentucky on Wednesday, the White House said.
Higher gas prices may be particularly damaging for a president who won the White House in 2024 largely on the promise that ŌĆŗhe would curb inflation, lower energy rates and ŌĆŗbring down the cost of living.
Representative Warren Davidson, ŌüĀan Ohio Republican, said the rise in gas prices is a "concern" for the midterms.
"The economy is always going to be one of the things on the ballot," he said. "The other things the president's done on the economy have been tremendous, so we'll see how it turns out."
Trump's own response to the spiking prices seems unlikely to assuage Americans' anxiety. In an interview with Reuters last week, Trump said he had no ŌĆŗconcern about gas prices and ŌüĀpredicted they would drop once the Iran conflict ends, adding, ŌĆ£If they rise, they rise.ŌĆØ
He repeated his contention on social media over the weekend, saying that ŌĆ£short term oil pricesŌĆØ were a ŌĆ£very small price to payŌĆØ for global security.
TrumpŌĆÖs shifting rationales for the Iran war, however, have made it more difficult for him to argue that the disruption to the U.S. economy is worthwhile. Administration officials have not presented evidence that Iran posed an ŌüĀimmediate threat, and ŌĆŗTrump himself had insisted that U.S. strikes last year ŌĆ£obliteratedŌĆØ the countryŌĆÖs nuclear weapons program.
"I donŌĆÖt think that people care that ŌĆŗmuch about Iran," said John Feehery, a Republican strategist. "I think they care about their gas prices. Any president that spends the majority of his time talking about international issues and not talking about dinner table issues, they run a risk of ŌĆŗalienating voters.
"This better be pretty quick, thatŌĆÖs all I can say," he added of the war.

item.title: US power use to beat record highs in 2026 and 2027 as AI use surges, EIA says
item.description:
NEW YORK, March 10 (Reuters) - U.S. ŌĆīpower consumption, which hit its second straight record high in 2025, will rise further in 2026 and 2027, the Energy Information Administration (EIA) said in its Short-Term Energy Outlook (STEO) on Tuesday.
The EIA projected power demand ŌĆŗwill rise from a record 4,195 billion kilowatt-hours (kWh) in 2025 to 4,260 billion ŌĆŗkWh in 2026 and 4,388 billion kWh in 2027.
Demand is surging due ŌüĀin large part to data centers dedicated to artificial intelligence and cryptocurrency, and as ŌĆŗhomes and businesses use more electricity and less fossil fuel for heat and transportation.
The EIA ŌĆŗforecast power sales in 2026 will rise to 1,535 billion kWh for residential consumers, 1,525 billion kWh for commercial customers and 1,056 billion kWh for industrial customers.
The forecasts compare with all-time highs of 1,517 ŌĆŗbillion kWh for residential consumers in 2025, 1,486 billion kWh in 2025 for commercial ŌĆŗcustomers and 1,064 billion kWh for industrial customers in 2000.
As renewable output rises, the EIA said the ŌĆīshare ŌüĀof power generation from natural gas would hold at 40% in 2026, the same as 2025, before sliding to 39% in 2027. Coal's share will slide from 17% in 2025 to 16% in 2026 and 15% in 2027.
The percentage of renewable generation will rise ŌĆŗfrom around 24% in ŌĆŗ2025 to 25% ŌüĀin 2026 and 27% in 2027, while nuclear power's share will rise from 18% in 2025 to 19% in 2026 before easing ŌĆŗback to 18% in 2027, according to the outlook.
The EIA projected ŌĆŗgas sales ŌüĀin 2026 would slide to 13.0 billion cubic feet per day (bcfd) for residential consumers, 9.6 bcfd for commercial customers and 23.3 bcfd for industrial customers, but rise to 36.2 bcfd for ŌüĀpower generation.
Those ŌĆŗfigures compare with all-time highs of 14.3 bcfd ŌĆŗin 1996 for residential consumers, 9.9 bcfd in 2025 for commercial customers, 23.8 bcfd in 1973 for industrial customers, ŌĆŗand 36.8 bcfd in 2024 for power generation.

item.title: Russian oil prices soar though tanker costs eat into gains
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - The price of Russian Urals oil, shunned by many buyers ŌĆīamid Ukraine-related sanctions, has surged on the war in Iran, though costs are also rising, traders said on Tuesday.
With tankers from top exporter Saudi Arabia effectively blocked by Iranian ŌĆŗthreats to Gulf shipping, attention has returned to Russia, the world's ŌĆŗsecond-largest seller.
Urals crude from Russian ports was being offered at around $76 ŌüĀper barrel on Monday on a free on board (FOB) basis, Reuters data ŌĆŗshowed, up from $45 just two weeks ago.
The price of a Urals cargo loading ŌĆŗfrom the Baltic Sea port of Primorsk has jumped to around $54 million this week from about $35 million in February, according to Reuters calculations.
Russian sellers faced near negative margins on exports earlier this ŌĆŗyear, traders said.
Despite G7 and other efforts to cap Russian seaborne oil ŌĆŗprices, Urals for delivery to Indian ports is now selling at a premium to benchmark Brent ŌĆīcrude ŌüĀfor the first time ever.
The United States last week granted Indian refiners a temporary waiver to resume imports of Russian crude already aboard tankers.
Yet traders said Russian sellers also face sharply higher costs. They said several vessels were fixed ŌĆŗat around $22-23 million from ŌĆŗRussia's Baltic ŌüĀSea ports to India amid a scarcity of the vessels.
That's nearly double rates seen in early February and up about $5-8 ŌĆŗmillion from late last week.
From Russia's Black Sea port ŌĆŗof Novorossiysk, ŌüĀwhich resumed loadings on Friday after recent drone damage, freight costs also rose above $20 million to India, traders said.
Brent oil surged to more than $119 a barrel on ŌüĀMonday ŌĆŗto its highest since mid-2022 amid supply cuts by ŌĆŗSaudi Arabia and other Gulf producers.
Prices pared gains after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted the war in the ŌĆŗMiddle East could end soon .

item.title: US Navy tells shipping industry Hormuz escorts not possible for now
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - The U.S. Navy has refused near-daily requests from the shipping industry for military escorts through the Strait of Hormuz since ŌĆŗthe start of the war on Iran, saying the risk of attacks is too high for now, according to sources familiar with the matter.
The ŌĆīNavy's assessments spell continued disruption to Middle East oil exports and reflect a divergence from President Donald TrumpŌĆÖs statements that the U.S. is prepared to provide naval escorts whenever needed to restart regular shipments along the key waterway.
Shipping along the narrow strait has all but halted since the start of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran more than a week ago, preventing exports of around a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs oil supply ŌĆŗand sending global oil prices surging to highs not seen since 2022.
A senior official with Iran's Revolutionary Guards has said the strait is closed and Iran will ŌĆŗfire on any ship trying to pass, Iranian media reported last week . Several ships have already been hit.
The U.S. Navy has held regular ŌüĀbriefings with shipping and oil industry counterparts and has said during those briefings it is unable to provide escorts for the time being, three shipping industry sources familiar with ŌĆŗthe matter said.
The sources, who declined to be identified due to the sensitivity of the matter, said the shipping industry has been making requests almost daily during the calls for naval ŌĆŗescorts through the strait.
One of the sources said the NavyŌĆÖs assessment during TuesdayŌĆÖs briefing had not changed and that escorts would only be possible once the risk of attack was reduced.
The Pentagon did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Trump has said repeatedly in recent days that the United States is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary.
"When the time comes, ŌĆŗthe U.S. Navy and its partners will escort tankers through the strait, if needed. I hope it's not going to be needed, but if it's needed, we'll escort them ŌĆŗright through," he said on Monday during a press conference at his Mar-a-Lago resort in Florida.
The U.S. military has started looking at options to potentially escort ships through the strait, should it be ŌĆīordered to do ŌüĀso, General Dan Caine, chairman of the Joint Chiefs of Staff, said on Tuesday.
"We're looking at a range of options there," Caine told reporters at the Pentagon.
A U.S. official told Reuters the U.S. military has not yet escorted any commercial ships through the strait. Earlier in the day, U.S. Secretary of Energy Chris Wright deleted a post on X in which he said the Navy had successfully escorted one through.
While there have been some voyages through the waterway in recent days , the majority of shipping traffic remains on hold with hundreds of ships ŌĆŗanchored.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco, the ŌĆŗworld's top oil exporter, said on ŌüĀTuesday there would be "catastrophic consequences" for the world's oil markets if the war on Iran continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Maritime security specialists and analysts said securing the strait will be difficult, even if the effort involves an international coalition, because of ŌĆŗIranŌĆÖs ability to deploy mines or cheaply made attack drones.
"Neither France, the United States, an international coalition or anybody is in a ŌĆŗposition to secure the ŌüĀStrait of Hormuz," said Adel Bakawan, director of the European Institute for Studies on the Middle East and North Africa.
Last week, Iran used a remote-controlled boat laden with explosives to damage a crude oil tanker anchored in Iraqi waters, according to initial assessments from two Iraqi port security sources.
A maritime security source said securing the strait could require the U.S. to take control of Iran's ŌüĀvast coast.
"There ŌĆŗare not enough naval vessels to do that and the risks remain high even with an escort. One ŌĆŗor two vessels can be overwhelmed by a swarm (of fast boats or drones)," the source said.
The Pentagon renewed threats on Tuesday to hit Iran harder unless shipments can flow through and said it was striking Iranian mine-laying ŌĆŗvessels and mine-storage facilities.

item.title: Zelenskiy says Ukraine's military hit key Russian missile plant in Bryansk region
item.description:
KYIV, March 10 (Reuters) - President Volodymyr Zelenskiy said Ukraine's military struck a key ŌĆīplant in Russia's Bryansk region bordering Ukraine on Tuesday that produces components for missiles .
Ukraine's military said British Storm Shadow missiles were deployed against the Kremniy bfactory ŌĆŗand posted a video which it said showed explosions ŌĆŗand a fire at the plant. It said the facility ŌüĀproduced critical missile components.
"Our fighters struck one of the important ŌĆŗRussian military factories in Bryansk," Zelenskiy said in his nightly video ŌĆŗaddress, referring to a report from top Ukrainian commander Oleksandr Syrskyi.
"The plant produced electronics and components for Russian missiles. The very missiles that strike our ŌĆŗcities, our villages and civilians," he said.
The governor of Bryansk region, ŌĆŗAlexander Bogomaz, said on Telegram that six civilians were killed and 37 injured ŌĆīand ŌüĀposted a video showing him at the scene.
Bogomaz made no mention of the plant in what he described as a "terrorist missile attack". The injured were being treated in hospital.
The Ukrainian military's general ŌĆŗstaff, posting on ŌĆŗTelegram, described the ŌüĀplant as "a critically important link in the chain of production of Russian high precision weapons" producing ŌĆŗsemiconductor devices and integrated micro chips.
"The target was hit ŌĆŗand ŌüĀsignificant damage to production facilities was recorded," the General Staff said. "The extent of the damage is being clarified."
The video posted on Telegram shows ŌüĀaerial ŌĆŗimages of a series of explosions and ŌĆŗlarge fires over a large area near woods.

item.title: Israeli president tells Bild: War with Iran needs 'end result', not exact timetable
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Israel's President Isaac Herzog on Tuesday did not offer a timetable on when the war ŌĆŗwith Iran could end, telling Germany's Bild newspaper: "We ŌĆīneed to take a deep breath and get to the end result."
Herzog said the U.S. and Israeli attacks on ŌĆŗIran were changing the whole configuration of the ŌĆŗMiddle East. He defended strikes on Iranian ŌüĀoil sites as a way of taking away ŌĆŗmoney from Tehran's "war machine".
The interview was published as the ŌĆŗU.S. and Israel pounded Iran with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the most intense airstrikes of ŌĆŗthe war, despite global markets betting that President ŌĆŗDonald Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Israel's foreign minister, ŌĆīGideon ŌüĀSaar, had earlier said his country was not planning for an endless war and was consulting with Washington about when to stop it.
ŌĆ£The Iranians are ŌĆŗthe ones spreading ŌĆŗchaos and ŌüĀterror throughout the region and the world. So I think if we measure ŌĆŗeverything by a speedometer, we wonŌĆÖt get ŌĆŗanywhere. ŌüĀWe need to take a deep breath and get to the end result,ŌĆØ Herzog told Bild.
Eliminating the ŌüĀIranian threat ŌĆŗwould "enable the entire system in ŌĆŗthe region to suddenly breathe again and develop further. ThatŌĆÖs fantastic,ŌĆØ ŌĆŗhe added.

item.title: Anthropic gives lesson in AI revenue hallucination
item.description:
LONDON, March 10 (Reuters Breakingviews) - AnthropicŌĆÖs battle ŌĆŗwith the Pentagon carries immensely high stakes for the future of artificial intelligence. Along the way, though, it ŌĆīhas revealed something just as interesting about the prosaic realities of accounting.
In a court filing , opens new tab , Anthropic Chief Financial Officer Krishna Rao said revenue has exceeded ŌĆ£$5 billion to date.ŌĆØ This figure sits awkwardly beside the Claude developerŌĆÖs ŌĆ£run-rateŌĆØ claims: $14 billion , opens new tab as of February 12, rising to $19 billion by monthŌĆÖs end. The gap reflects ŌĆŗSilicon ValleyŌĆÖs habit of touting metrics that assume a lot about the future.
The $5 billion figure refers to GAAP revenue generated ŌĆŗfrom 2023 through to December 2025, a person familiar with the matter told Breakingviews; the $19 billion ŌüĀis an extrapolation. Anthropic defines ŌĆ£run-rate revenueŌĆØ in two parts. Use the last 28 days of sales from customers charged on a ŌĆŗconsumption basis and multiply it by 13. Then, multiply the monthly subscription take by 12, and add the two together.
These snapshots are ŌĆŗinexact, capturing or missing sudden consumption spikes or dips. It helps explain how $14 billion can become $19 billion within weeks.
Big businesses account for 80% of AnthropicŌĆÖs revenue, and they tend to be billed for consumption, making the headline run-rate highly sensitive. Pricing changes, promotional credits or attempts to optimize usage ŌĆŗall have an impact. This makes calculating trailing revenue from reported numbers error-prone. Still, working backwards from Anthropic's run-rate at various ŌĆŗtimes, it's clear most sales have been generated in recent months.
How metrics are defined also complicates comparisons. OpenAIŌĆÖs annual recurring revenue, which it said , opens new tab reached $20 ŌĆībillion as ŌüĀof the end of December, is meant to specifically capture subscriptions rather than metered sales. Further twists include revenue-sharing agreements with partners such as Microsoft (MSFT.O) , opens new tab .
Run-rate figures can be useful when companies start small and are growing quickly. But it also makes it easier to tout brief, rapid momentum in support of projections showing astronomical growth far out into the future.
Because the Pentagon has moved to ŌĆŗblacklist Anthropic, these dangers are ŌĆŗparamount. Anthropic Chief Commercial Officer ŌüĀPaul Smith said , opens new tab one customer paused discussions on a $15 million contract after the company was labeled a supply-chain risk, while two financial-services companies refused to finalize agreements worth a combined $80 million unless they secured ŌĆŗbroad cancellation rights. Anthropic has sued to challenge the designation.
No one is being misled. Until ŌĆŗAI companies standardize ŌüĀhow they report revenue and are upfront about potential volatility, however, their metrics risk looking like a plausible hallucination.
Follow Karen Kwok on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .

item.title: LNG Canada ramps up output as Iran war threatens global gas supplies
item.description:
HOUSTON, March 10 (Reuters) - LNG Canada, a Shell-led (SHEL.L) , opens new tab venture, has ramped up production and exports to Asia this ŌĆīmonth, LSEG data show, as the Iran war threatens Asian natural gas supplies which are particularly vulnerable to global disruptions.
The LNG project in Kitimat, British Columbia, which began operations in June 2025, has exported five cargoes in the first 11 days of March, already exceeding half its total February ŌĆŗvolume, the data shows. A sixth shipment is due to depart on Tuesday.
All cargoes have been sent to ŌĆŗAsia, with two heading to Japan, two to South Korea and one to the Philippines. ŌüĀThe plant appears to be operating close to its full capacity of 14 million metric tons per year, according to ŌĆŗthe LSEG data.
An LNG Canada spokesperson did not comment on the facility's current production volumes but said the company continues to ŌĆŗadvance early operations at the site safely and responsibly.
"A 58th cargo is scheduled to depart in the coming days," the spokesperson said in an email.
The venture can export just under 1.2 million metric tons per month. In the first one-third of this month it has loaded ŌĆŗmore than 400,000 tons, the data showed.
Global markets have rushed to adapt after Qatar, which supplies about 20% of globally traded ŌĆŗLNG, was forced to halt production and declare force majeure when the conflict blocked tankers from transiting the Strait of Hormuz.
"They are ŌüĀfurther ramping up activity to push toward full capacity, as well as trying to make a quick surge in LNG output to get more LNG on the water to Asia and take advantage of higher prices in the region," said Martin King, an analyst with RBN Energy.
LNG Canada is the first large-scale Canadian LNG facility to start production and the first major ŌĆŗNorth American plant with direct ŌĆŗaccess to the Pacific, shortening ŌüĀsailing time to Asian buyers compared with U.S. Gulf Coast exporters.
The plant has faced operational challenges since startup but has gradually increased output since January, LSEG data shows.
Canadian natural gas producers ŌĆŗhad stepped up production significantly in anticipation of LNG Canada's startup last summer, but then ŌĆŗfaced a slump ŌüĀin domestic prices when the project did not draw down supplies as quickly as the markets had expected.
"It seems like they are pretty close, for the last two weeks, to capacity," said Mike Belenkie, CEO of Advantage Energy (AAV.TO) , opens new tab , which was one of several ŌüĀcompanies that ŌĆŗtemporarily curtailed production in September when Western Canadian natural gas prices temporarily ŌĆŗwent negative.
Daily spot prices at the Alberta Energy Company (AECO) storage hub hovered close to $2 per million British thermal units on Tuesday, a $1.25 discount to the ŌĆŗU.S. Henry Hub benchmark.

item.title: US lawmakers worry Trump may put 'boots on the ground' in Iran
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - Democratic U.S. senators expressed alarm on Tuesday about the Iran war , saying they were worried that President Donald Trump could deploy U.S. ground forces and noting the high risks ŌĆŗgiven Russian support for Tehran's military.
"We know that Russia is already helping with ŌĆīintel, providing that to Iran , and that there's an axis with Russia and China , Iran and North Korea that puts at greater risk the United States and our national security," Democratic Senator Jeanne Shaheen of New Hampshire said on ŌĆŗCNN.
Speaking after a classified briefing from administration officials to the Senate Armed Services ŌĆŗCommittee, Shaheen called on the administration to send officials to testify publicly in ŌüĀCongress.
Two sources familiar with U.S. intelligence reports have said that Russia is providing Iran ŌĆŗwith targeting intelligence on U.S. bases and ships.
Shaheen and other senators said a series of closed-door ŌĆŗbriefings from Trump aides have left them with more questions than answers, especially about the cost and expected duration of the war, and whether U.S. forces would be sent into Tehran.
"We seem to be on a ŌĆŗpath toward deploying American troops on the ground in Iran to accomplish any of the ŌĆŗpotential objectives here," Senator Richard Blumenthal of Connecticut told reporters after Tuesday's classified briefing.
Trump has not ruled ŌĆīout sending ŌüĀU.S. ground troops into Iran. His Republicans, who have slim majorities in both houses of Congress, have almost unanimously backed his strategy on Iran, with only a handful expressing doubt about the war.
Blumenthal also cited the risk of Russian involvement putting American lives at risk. "Literally, Russia ŌĆŗseems to be aiding ŌĆŗour enemy actively and ŌüĀintensively with intelligence and perhaps with other means and China also may be assisting Iran," Blumenthal said.
"So the American people deserve to know ŌĆŗmuch more than this administration has told them about the cost ŌĆŗof the war, ŌüĀthe danger to our sons and daughters in uniform and the potential for further escalation and widening of this war," he said.
The lawmakers' comments came as they awaited a request the White ŌüĀHouse ŌĆŗis expected to make for more funding for the war. ŌĆŗSeveral congressional aides have said they expect Trump to ask for $50 billion, although others have said that estimate seemed ŌĆŗlow.

item.title: Boeing signs $289 million Israel contract for 5,000 smart bombs, source says
item.description:
March 10 (Reuters) - Boeing (BA.N) , opens new tab has signed a new $289 million contract ŌĆŗwith Israel to deliver ŌĆŗas many as 5,000 new ŌüĀair-launched smart bombs, a ŌĆŗsource told Reuters on Tuesday.
The ŌĆŗnew contract is not related to the ongoing U.S.-Israeli air strikes on ŌĆŗIran, with deliveries not ŌĆŗscheduled to start for 36 months, Bloomberg ŌĆīNews ŌüĀreported earlier, citing a person familiar with the matter.
Boeing declined to comment when contacted ŌĆŗby Reuters.
The ŌĆŗcompany's ŌüĀSmall Diameter Bomb is a guided munition ŌĆŗthat can be launched ŌĆŗby ŌüĀIsraeli jets at targets more than 40 miles (64 kilometers) away.

item.title: Trading Day: Role reversal, as Wall Street lags
item.description:
ORLANDO, Florida, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil slumped more than 10% on Tuesday, the biggest one-day fall since 2022, on hopes that conflict in the Middle East will de-escalate. Asian and European stocks rose sharply, but in a rare instance since the war broke out, Wall Street lagged and U.S. stocks posted mild losses.
In my ŌĆŗcolumn today I look at parallels between the stresses emerging in private credit markets today and U.S. subprime mortgages in 2007. Of course, ŌĆīthat doesn't mean a global crash will follow, but investors should be aware of the potential risks under the hood.
If you have more time to read, here are a few articles I recommend to help you make sense of what happened in markets today.
Today's Key Market Moves
Today's Talking Points
* Timing is everything
Like many things in life, timing is everything in investing. In trading, it's even more crucial. In that light, the ŌĆŗlast 24 hours have been particularly challenging for oil traders, to put it mildly, with crude prices notching their biggest intraday swings on record.
With oil trading in ŌĆŗa $36 intraday range, as it did on Monday, fortunes and careers can be made - or lost - in minutes. Leveraged positions will be particularly exposed, and it wouldn't be a total ŌĆīshock if ŌüĀit soon emerges that some hedge funds suffered some big losses.
* Fake news?
Underlining how sensitive markets are right now to headlines, oil extended heavy losses on Tuesday after U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright posted on X that the U.S. navy escorted a tanker through Strait of Hormuz, indicating that supply constraints may be easing.
But the post was deleted minutes later, and oil rebounded around $10, a bounce boosted by a CBS report that U.S. intelligence detected signs Iran may be considering steps to deploy mines ŌĆŗin the Strait of Hormuz. Headlines can ŌĆŗalways move markets. But these are ŌüĀextraordinary times.
* Trading places
China's powerful export machine is racing up through the gears. Exports in the first two months of this year soared 22%, more than three times the pace of growth in December and the Reuters poll forecast. The January-February ŌĆŗtrade surplus was $213 billion.
With tariffs crimping shipments to the U.S., trade with the rest of the world is booming. Last ŌĆŗyear's record $1.2 trillion trade ŌüĀsurplus, which revived complaints about China's FX regime, could be broken this year. Meanwhile, figures from Berlin on Tuesday showed that German exports in January shrank at their fastest rate since May 2024.
What could move markets tomorrow?

Opinions expressed are those of the author. They do not reflect the views ŌĆŗof Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Brazil's Petrobras to auction 20 million liters of diesel amid shortage reports, sources say

RIO DE JANEIRO, March 10 (Reuters) - Brazilian state-run oil company ŌĆŗPetrobras (PETR3.SA) , opens new tab plans to ŌĆīhold an auction to sell 20 million liters of ŌĆŗdiesel in the ŌĆŗsouthern Rio Grande do Sul ŌüĀstate amid reports ŌĆŗof shortages, sources familiar ŌĆŗwith the issue told Reuters on Tuesday.
The auction will take ŌĆŗplace on Wednesday, ŌĆŗthey said.
A spike in diesel prices is ŌĆīemerging ŌüĀas the first and most immediate threat to Brazil's farm sector ŌĆŗfrom the ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli ŌüĀattacks on Iran, raising costs for ŌĆŗproducers harvesting a ŌĆŗrecord ŌüĀsoybean crop and planting corn they cannot afford ŌüĀto ŌĆŗdelay.

item.title: Oil dives, settles down 11% after Trump predicts Middle East de-escalation
item.description:
NEW YORK/LONDON, March 10 (Reuters) - Oil prices plunged by more than 11% on Tuesday, the steepest percentage drop of any session since ŌĆŗ2022, a day after U.S. President Donald Trump predicted a quick end to the war with Iran that has disrupted global crude flows .
Brent futures fell $11.16, or 11%, to settle at $87.80 a barrel. U.S. West Texas ŌĆīIntermediate (WTI) crude settled at $83.45 a barrel, down $11.32, or 11.9%. Both benchmarks logged the biggest single-day percentage loss since March 2022, after rocketing to four-year highs a day earlier.
Prices temporarily sank lower in midday trade, after U.S. Energy Secretary Chris Wright wrote on X that the American military had facilitated a shipment of oil out of the Strait of Hormuz.
"President Trump is maintaining stability of global energy during the military operations against Iran," Wright posted at 1:02 p.m. local time before the post appeared to be removed.
"The ŌĆŗU.S. Navy successfully escorted an oil tanker through the Strait of Hormuz to ensure oil remains flowing to global markets," Wright said.
Trump's Republicans will soon be campaigning to retain control of U.S. Congress ŌĆŗin November midterm elections, with many voters worried about rising energy prices.
ŌĆ£This is the market reacting to the possibility that the Strait of Hormuz could reopen," said ŌüĀAndrew Lipow, founder of Lipow Oil Associates. "From the administrationŌĆÖs perspective, the move also carries clear optics: lower oil and gasoline prices help ease consumer pain."
On Monday, both crude benchmarks surged to a session high above $119 a barrel, ŌĆŗtheir highest since June 2022, as supply cuts by Saudi Arabia and other producers stoked fears of major disruptions to global supplies.
Prices settled with more modest gains on Monday, then retreated in late trade and into Tuesday after Trump ŌĆŗand Russian President Vladimir Putin had a call and shared proposals aimed at a quick settlement to the war, according to a Kremlin aide.
In addition, Trump said on Monday in a CBS News interview that he thought the war against Iran was "very complete" and Washington was "very far ahead" of his initial four- to five-week estimated time frame.
Israel's foreign minister said Israel is not seeking an endless war with Iran and will coordinate with the U.S. on when to end the fighting.
Brent oil prices are set to trade above $95 a ŌĆŗbarrel over the next two months as the Iran war disrupts supplies, before falling to around $70 by the end of the year, the Energy Information Administration said in a monthly report.
Even ŌĆŗif the war ends, oil supplies will not immediately rebound, said Simon Flowers, chairman and chief analyst at Wood Mackenzie.
"When the conflict ends, cranking up the supply chain won't be swift," Flowers said. "Product barrels in storage at refineries or in port ŌĆīmight be ŌüĀmoved on vessels quite quickly.
But if wells are shut-in for a prolonged period, restarting production to full output could take weeks or even longer."
In response to Trump, Iran's Islamic Revolutionary Guard Corps said Tehran would not allow "one litre of oil" to be exported from the region if U.S. and Israeli attacks continued, state media reported on Tuesday.
Meanwhile, Trump was considering easing oil sanctions on Russia related to Moscow's war in Ukraine, and releasing emergency crude stockpiles to help curb spiking prices, according to multiple sources.
"Discussions around easing sanctions on Russian oil, comments from Donald Trump hinting that the conflict could eventually de-escalate, and the possibility of G7 (Group of Seven) countries tapping strategic oil reserves all pointed ŌĆŗto the same message - that oil barrels will somehow ŌĆŗcontinue to reach the market," Priyanka Sachdeva, a ŌüĀPhillip Nova analyst, said in a note.
G7 energy ministers stopped short of agreeing on a release of strategic oil reserves on Tuesday and instead asked the International Energy Agency to assess the situation before acting.
The U.S. and Israel pounded Iran on Tuesday with what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground said were the ŌĆŗmost intense airstrikes of the war, even as global markets bet that Trump will seek to end the conflict soon.
Saudi Arabia's Aramco (2222.SE) , opens new tab , the world's top oil ŌĆŗexporter, said there would be "catastrophic consequences" ŌüĀfor global oil markets if the Iran war continues to disrupt shipping in the Strait of Hormuz.
Nearly 1.9 million barrels per day of crude refining capacity in the Gulf has been shut in due to the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, consultancy IIR said.
"Policy measures may have limited impact on oil prices unless safe passage through the Strait of Hormuz is assured, given the potential losses of up to 12 million bpd over the next two weeks," JPMorgan said in a note.
In the ŌüĀlatest disruption to ŌĆŗglobal supplies, Abu Dhabi state oil giant ADNOC has shut its Ruwais refinery, a source said on Tuesday, after a fire broke ŌĆŗout at a facility within the complex following a drone strike.
Goldman Sachs said that because the situation remains fluid, it was not changing its oil price forecast for Brent at $66 per barrel in the fourth quarter and WTI at $62 per barrel.

item.title: Iran's new leader, still silent, was elevated by the Revolutionary Guards
item.description:
DUBAI, March 10 (Reuters) - Iran's Revolutionary Guards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, seeing him as a more pliant version of his father who would back their hardline policies, bludgeoning aside the concerns of pragmatists, senior Iranian sources said.
Already very powerful, the Guards have gained ŌĆŗyet greater sway since the war began and quickly overcame the misgivings of senior political and clerical figures whose opposition to the choice delayed the announcement by hours, the sources said.
Adding ŌĆīto the concerns of those who opposed Khamenei's installation as supreme leader, he had still issued no statement by Tuesday evening, nearly 48 hours after his selection during a war that has killed more than a thousand Iranians.
Khamenei's selection, engineered by the Guards, may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal repression, said the three senior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and another insider.
Two of them said they feared the Guards' domination of the system would further transform the Islamic Republic into a military state ŌĆŗwith only a thin veneer of religious legitimacy,
undermining an already shrinking support base and allowing less room to address complex threats.
Though an influential backroom operator for decades spent ŌĆŗrunning his father's office, Mojtaba Khamenei remains an obscure figure to many Iranians and may have been wounded in the U.S.-Israeli strikes that killed his father.
A state television anchor ŌüĀappeared to confirm widespread rumours Khamenei was hurt, describing him as a "janbaz", or "wounded veteran" of the Ramadan War, as Iran calls the current conflict. Reuters has not been able to confirm his condition.
That - and security fears after ŌĆŗhis father's assassination on February 28 - may explain his silence since the 88-member Assembly of Experts announced late on Sunday that they had elected him as the country's supreme leader.
Authority is most visibly held by the Guards and the ŌĆŗsupreme leader's office, known as the beyt, which operates a parallel system of influence across the bureaucracy.
Any doubts over who was really in charge evaporated on Saturday when President Masoud Pezeshkian, part of a triumvirate mandated to rule during the gap between leaders, was forced into a climbdown after apologising to Gulf states for attacks. Senior Guards were furious at his apology, sources told Reuters.
One of the three senior sources, who said the Guards were now running Iran, said the late Ayatollah Ali Khamenei had been able to rein in ŌĆŗthe corps, balancing its views against those of political and clerical elites in the system.
But even assuming the new leader is well enough to take the helm, the Guards may now get the final say in major decisions ŌĆŗin future, the source added.
Alex Vatanka, a senior fellow at the Middle East Institute in Washington, D.C. said: "Mojtaba owes his position to the Revolutionary Guards and as such he is not going to be as supreme as his father was."
The choice of leader constitutionally belongs to the Assembly of Experts, but in both elections of a new leader since the Islamic Revolution of 1979, it has been swayed by the advice of other power brokers.
When Ayatollah Ruhollah Khomeini died in 1989, the kingmaker was influential politician Ali Akbar Hashemi Rafsanjani, who told the assembly that Khomeini had whispered Khamenei's name to him on his deathbed.
This time, the kingmakers were the Guards and they were a lot blunter in their messages, all five of the sources said. The Guards used the argument that the war required a fast process and selecting a candidate who defied the United States.
Because their hall in the seminary city of Qom was bombed, ŌĆŗthe Assembly of Experts had to gather in a different - ŌĆŗso far undisclosed location - and some of the ŌüĀmembers could not be present or even informed of the vote, said one member, Ayatollah Mohsen Heydari, on state television.
The body reached its quorum of two-thirds, he said, without specifying how many had in fact taken part, with 85-90% of those present backing Mojtaba Khamenei.
It was not clear how many of those not present might have backed or ŌĆŗopposed him but the figures showed less than the unanimous decision the Guards may have hoped for.
A group of ayatollahs had disliked the apparent ŌĆŗhereditary succession and feared that the ŌüĀchoice would alienate even many supporters of the ruling system, said two of the sources.
Behind the scenes, some clerics and members of the political establishment were trying to push for an alternative in numerous discussions over the past week, one of the sources said.
However, the reformist former official said the Guards had threatened critics of Khamenei's accession. The Islamic Republic insider said the Guards had contacted members of the assembly, prompting objections, but in the end they felt compelled to support him.
Khamenei's ŌüĀappointment was originally ŌĆŗintended to be announced on Sunday morning, but only came late in the evening as a result of the lingering opposition to ŌĆŗhis choice, all five sources said.
As head of the beyt for many years under his father, Mojtaba Khamenei had built very close ties with the Guards, particularly the second-tier commanders who have replaced the top generals killed in the war, one of the officials said.
The upshot, said the ŌĆŗreformist former official, will be a foreign and domestic policy moving in a more radical direction with the Guards finally having what they sought for years: full control.

item.title: North Korea supports Iran's choice of new supreme leader, KCNA says

item.title: Two more Iranian women's soccer players seek asylum in Australia, media reports

The Iranian team's campaign in the Australia-hosted tournament started just as the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israel launched air strikes on Iran , killing the Islamic Republic's Supreme Leader ŌĆŗAyatollah Ali Khamenei. They were eliminated from the tournament on Sunday.

item.title: At least six people die in coach fire in Switzerland, local police say

item.title: Macron to host G7 leaders call on Iran crisis, energy prices

item.title: El Salvador likely committed crimes against humanity during state of emergency, jurists say

item.title: Russian consulate in Iran's Isfahan damaged in strikes, spokeswoman says

An attack on a diplomatic representation ŌĆŗwas a "blatant violation" of international ŌĆŗconventions and all sides should observe ŌüĀthe "inviolability of diplomatic sites", she ŌĆŗsaid.

"Windows were shattered in the office building and residential apartments, ŌĆŗand several ŌĆŗemployees were ŌüĀthrown by the blast wave. Fortunately, there were ŌĆŗno casualties or serious injuries."

item.title: Satellite firm extends Middle East image delay to prevent use by US adversaries
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - California-based Planet Labs has expanded restrictions on accessing ŌĆīits imagery of the Middle East to prevent adversaries from using it to attack the U.S. and its allies, a sign of how the expansion of commercial space business can impact conflicts.
Planet, which operates a large fleet of Earth-imaging satellites and ŌĆŗsells frequently updated images to governments, companies and media, told customers on Monday that it ŌĆŗwas extending restrictions to a period of 14 days from a delay of ŌüĀfour days imposed last week.
The move was temporary and in ŌĆ£an effort to limit any uncontrolled distribution ŌĆŗof the images that might result in their unintentional access and use as tactical leverage by adversarial ŌĆŗactors,ŌĆØ a Planet spokesperson told Reuters in a statement.
ŌĆ£This conflict is dynamic and in many ways unique to others, and thus Planet is taking robust steps to help ensure our images do not contribute in any way to attacks ŌĆŗon allied and NATO personnel and civilians," the spokesperson said.
Some space specialists say that Iran could ŌĆŗbe accessing commercial imagery, including via other U.S. adversaries.
Militaries rely on space for everything from identifying targets, ŌĆīguiding weapons ŌüĀand tracking missiles to communications. In a sign of spaceŌĆÖs central role in modern warfare, U.S. officials last week said their space forces were among ŌĆ£the first moversŌĆØ in the operation against Iran.
A U.S. Space Command spokesperson declined to detail the capabilities it used. Space Command is responsible for helping with missile ŌĆŗtracking, secure communications and ŌĆŗusing Pentagon satellites as ŌüĀoverwatch for U.S. and joint forces on the ground.
While high-quality satellite imagery used to be the preserve of advanced space powers, access to commercial satellite ŌĆŗimagery has leveled the playing field, as Ukraine has experienced during its war ŌĆŗwith Russia . Now, satellite ŌüĀoperators are deploying AI to help speed up the ability to analyze imagery and identify areas of interest.
ŌĆ£This expert analysis used to be the preserve of high-end military analysts, not anymore,ŌĆØ said Chris Moore, a ŌüĀdefense industry ŌĆŗconsultant and retired air vice-marshal in the British military.
ŌĆ£Ultimately it ŌĆŗwill create an all-seeing eye from space that will make the concealment of military forces and deception operations difficult to achieve.ŌĆØ

item.title: US oil prices up nearly $3 as Middle East crisis constrains supply
item.description:
TOKYO, March 11 (Reuters) - U.S. West Texas Intermediate crude oil prices rose by $2.90 per barrel, or 3.5%, to $86.33 in early trade on Wednesday as supplies from the Gulf remain constrained amid the U.S. and Israeli war on Iran.
Brent futures start trading ŌĆŗat 0100 GMT. WTI rebounded after both contracts plunged by more than 11% on ŌĆŗTuesday, the steepest percentage drop since 2022, a day after U.S. President ŌüĀDonald Trump predicted a quick end to the war.
The U.S. and Israel pounded Iran on Tuesday ŌĆŗwith what the Pentagon and Iranians on the ground called the most intense airstrikes of the ŌĆŗwar.
The U.S. military also "eliminated" 16 Iranian mine-laying vessels near the Strait of Hormuz on Tuesday, the U.S. Central Command said , as Trump warned any mines laid in the Strait by Iran must be removed immediately.
Trump has repeatedly said ŌĆŗthe U.S. is prepared to escort tankers through the Strait of Hormuz when necessary. However, ŌĆŗsources told Reuters the U.S. Navy has refused requests from the shipping industry for military escorts as the risk of ŌĆīattacks is ŌüĀtoo high for now.
"We continue to expect crude oil to remain highly volatile, driven by headlines while trading within a wide range between $75ish and $105ish in the sessions ahead," Tony Sycamore, market analyst with IG in Sydney, said in a note.
On Monday, crude oil prices surged to a ŌĆŗsession high above $119 a ŌĆŗbarrel, their highest since ŌüĀJune 2022, and G7 officials have since gathered online to discuss a potential release of emergency oil stockpiles to soften the market blow.
French President Emmanuel ŌĆŗMacron will host a video call with other G7 country leaders on ŌĆŗWednesday to discuss ŌüĀthe impact of the conflict in the Middle East on energy and measures to address the situation.
War in Iran is currently cutting Gulf oil and oil products supply to the market by some ŌüĀ15 ŌĆŗmillion barrels per day which could raise crude prices to $150 per ŌĆŗbarrel, according to research and consultancy firm Wood Mackenzie.
Reflecting higher demand, U.S. crude, gasoline and distillate stocks fell last week, ŌĆŗmarket sources said, citing American Petroleum Institute figures on Tuesday.

item.title: Drone strike hits US diplomatic facility in Iraq, reports Washington Post
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 10 (Reuters) - A drone struck a major U.S. diplomatic facility in Iraq on Tuesday in suspected retaliation by pro-Tehran militias over the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, ŌĆŗthe Washington Post reported, citing an unidentified security official and an ŌĆīinternal U.S. State Department alert.
The strike hit the diplomatic support center, a logistical hub for U.S. diplomats near Baghdad airport and Iraqi military bases, the Post reported.
It was not clear from ŌĆŗthe report whether there were any injuries. The White House, U.S. State ŌĆŗDepartment and Pentagon did not immediately respond to requests for comment.
The ŌüĀPost reported that six drones were launched toward the compound in Baghdad, one ŌĆŗof which hit the U.S. facility while five were shot down. The security ŌĆŗofficial, who the Post said spoke on the condition of anonymity to discuss a sensitive security situation, was not aware of any casualties.

The attack was likely carried out by the Islamic Resistance ŌĆīin Iraq, ŌüĀan umbrella group of Iran-backed armed factions, the Post reported, citing the security official.
The U.S. and Israel began attacks on Iran on February 28. Iran has responded with its own strikes on Israel and on U.S. bases in Gulf countries. Iran's ŌĆŗU.N. ambassador said the ŌĆŗU.S.-Israeli strikes had ŌüĀkilled more than 1,300 civilians.
Israel says 11 civilians have been killed in Iranian attacks while the U.S. military says seven ŌĆŗservice members have been killed. Earlier on Tuesday, Reuters reported that ŌĆŗas many ŌüĀas 150 U.S. troops have been wounded in the war.
President Donald Trump has said the strikes were aimed to eliminate what he called imminent threats from Iran, citing ŌüĀits nuclear ŌĆŗand ballistic missile programs, its support for Hamas ŌĆŗand Hezbollah and its "menacing activities." Iran, which denies seeking a nuclear weapon, has called the attacks a ŌĆŗviolation of its sovereignty.

item.title: Australia grants asylum to two more from Iranian women's soccer delegation

SYDNEY, March 11 (Reuters) - Australia on Wednesday granted humanitarian visas ŌĆīto two more members of the Iranian women's soccer team who decided to stay in the country due to fears for their safety if they returned home.
