item.title: Carlyle to sell Colombian oil firm SierraCol to Philippine's Prime Infrastructure
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - Private equity group Carlyle (CG.O) , opens new tab has agreed to sell its Colombian oil producer SierraCol to Prime Infrastructure Capital, the infrastructure unit ŌĆŗof Filipino businessman Enrique K. Razon Jr., for an undisclosed ŌĆīsum, the U.S. company said on Wednesday.
Carlyle, which set up SierraCol in 2020 after buying assets from Occidental Petroleum (OXY.N) , opens new tab , had sought around $1.5 billion for the Colombian ŌĆŗfirm, sources had told Reuters in 2025.
Elsewhere in the oil ŌĆŗand gas sector, Carlyle in January reached a non-binding, initial ŌüĀagreement to buy most international assets from sanctioned Russian firm Lukoil (LKOH.MM) , opens new tab ŌĆŗand merge its European refining vehicle Moeve with Portuguese energy firm Galp's (GALP.LS) , opens new tab downstream ŌĆŗbusiness .
"This is where our track record is strong and I expect to continue that. We have a clear playbook for executing complex carve-outs and strengthening these businesses," ŌĆŗsaid co-head of Carlyle International Energy Partners (CIEP), Bob Maguire.
He said CIEP ŌĆŗhad no fixed views on how much investment to allocate to downstream or upstream ŌĆīacquisitions.
CIEP ŌüĀmanaging director Parminder Singh told Reuters that it has been tough to extract assets from the bigger players in the current market as majors are keen to boost their own oil and gas reserves ŌĆŗwhile retrenching on ŌĆŗlow-carbon projects.
Carlyle said ŌüĀit has invested around $1 billion in SierraCol since 2020, mainly spending on the firm's existing assets to ŌĆŗstabilise its net production at around 45,000 barrels of ŌĆŗoil equivalent ŌüĀper day and reduce operational emissions.
SierraCol's gross output of 77,000 barrels of oil equivalent per day makes up around 10% of Colombia's overall production.
SierraCol ŌüĀhad $205 ŌĆŗmillion in free cashflow for the 12 ŌĆŗmonths to October 2025 and net debt of $618 million, according to its website.
Prime Infrastructure runs ŌĆŗenergy, waste and water infrastructure.

item.title: Israeli strike hits Beirut apartment block, Lebanese media reports
item.description:
BEIRUT, March 11 (Reuters) - An Israeli strike hit an apartment block in central Beirut on Wednesday, Lebanon's state news ŌĆŗagency reported, further widening Israeli attacks in the ŌĆīcapital beyond the Hezbollah-controlled southern suburbs where heavy strikes continued.
Footage showed extensive damage to two floors of the apartment ŌĆŗblock in the Aicha Bakkar neighbourhood, and smoke ŌĆŗrising from the building.
There was no immediate comment ŌüĀfrom the Israeli military and no immediate reports ŌĆŗof casualties.
It would mark the second Israeli strike in ŌĆŗthe heart of Beirut in four days. On Sunday, an Israeli strike hit a hotel in the seafront Raouche neighbourhood. The Israeli ŌĆŗmilitary said that strike targeted five senior members ŌĆŗof Iran's Revolutionary Guards Quds Force.
Lebanese authorities say nearly 570 people ŌĆīhave ŌüĀbeen killed by Israeli strikes since Iran-backed Hezbollah opened fire on March 2 to avenge the killing of Supreme Leader Ayatollah Ali Khamenei at the start ŌĆŗof the U.S.-Israeli war ŌĆŗon Iran.
Israel ŌüĀkept up heavy strikes on the southern suburbs, known as Dahiyeh, overnight. The ŌĆŗIsraeli military has ordered residents of the ŌĆŗpredominantly Shi'ite ŌüĀMuslim Dahiyeh to leave, along with residents of a swathe of southern Lebanon and parts of the east.
Some ŌüĀ700,000 ŌĆŗpeople have been displaced in ŌĆŗLebanon, or more than a tenth of the population, the United Nations ŌĆŗhas reported.

item.title: UK police ban pro-Iranian London march due to 'extreme tensions'
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - British police said they had banned a pro-Iranian march due to take place in London on Sunday, citing the possibility of "extreme tensions" with ŌĆŗcounter-protesters and the risk posed by Tehran during the conflict in the ŌĆīMiddle East.
The Al Quds march in central London is organised annually by the Islamic Human Rights Commission, which the police said was supportive of the Iranian regime.
The threshold to ban a protest ŌĆŗis high in Britain, with the police saying this was the first ŌĆŗtime the power had been invoked in 14 years, but the ŌüĀrisks of public disorder were "so severe" it was right to block it. The ban ŌĆŗalso applies to any counter-protest marches.
The Islamic Human Rights Commission has previously said on ŌĆŗX that the march was being held in support of Palestinian liberation.
Police said that previous Al Quds marches have resulted in arrests for supporting terrorist organisations and anti-Semitic hate crimes, and said ŌĆŗthat even with the ban in place they were facing a "challenging, potentially violent weekend".
"We ŌĆŗhave taken into consideration the likely impact on protests of the volatile situation in the Middle ŌĆīEast, ŌüĀwith the Iranian regime attacking British allies and military bases overseas," the police statement published late on Tuesday said.
"We must also consider that the security services have been publicly clear about the threats we are facing on UK soil from the Iranian ŌĆŗregime."
British police have faced ŌĆŗheavy scrutiny of ŌüĀtheir handling of regular, large pro-Palestinian protests in London since the October 7, 2023 attacks by Hamas on Israel, with supporters ŌĆŗand opponents at odds over the right of free speech, the ŌĆŗsupport shown ŌüĀfor a proscribed group and the impact on the Jewish community.
Last week, British police arrested four men on suspicion of helping Iran's intelligence services carry out surveillance of people and ŌüĀlocations ŌĆŗlinked to the Jewish community in London.
Should the Islamic ŌĆŗHuman Rights Commission go ahead with a static assembly, which there is no law to ban, police said ŌĆŗit would be placed under strict conditions.

item.title: Two drones fall in vicinity of Dubai airport as Iran crisis shows no sign of easing
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Two drones came down in the vicinity of Dubai ŌĆŗInternational Airport (DXB) on Wednesday, injuring four people, Dubai's media ŌĆīoffice said, as attacks on infrastructure across the Gulf continued on the 12th day of the Iran crisis , which has caused widespread ŌĆŗdisruption to global air traffic.
"Authorities confirm that two ŌĆŗdrones fell in the vicinity of Dubai International ŌüĀAirport (DXB) a short while ago," the media office posted ŌĆŗon X, adding that air traffic is operating as ŌĆŗnormal.
The attack resulted in minor injuries to two Ghanaian nationals and one Bangladeshi national, as well as moderate injuries to one Indian ŌĆŗnational, it added.
The outbreak of the U.S.-Israel war against ŌĆŗIran has led to flight cancellations, rescheduling and re-routing for airlines ŌĆīacross ŌüĀthe globe, as most airspaces in the Middle East, including Qatar, remain shut over missile and drone concerns. It has also led to an energy crisis that has ŌĆŗled to ŌĆŗa spike in fuel ŌüĀprices.
UAE airlines like Dubai's Emirates and Abu Dhabi's Etihad have resumed some flights since ŌĆŗthe beginning of the crisis on February ŌĆŗ28 ŌüĀbut they are still operating below their capacity, with the attack on Wednesday marking a renewed hit to DXB, ŌüĀthe world's ŌĆŗbusiest airport for international passengers, ŌĆŗwhich last year handled nearly 100 million passengers.

item.title: Opposition candidates in Congo Republic's presidential election
item.description:
DAKAR, March 11 (Reuters) - Denis Sassou Nguesso , the 82-year-old president of Republic of Congo, is seeking another five-year term in an election on March 15, aiming to extend his more than four decades in office in ŌĆŗthe oil-rich Central African nation.
A former paratrooper, Sassou seized power in 1979 and has ŌĆīgoverned almost continuously since, except for a five-year hiatus in the 1990s.
He faces six opposition candidates in a race he is widely expected to win. The opposition remains fragmented, with established parties boycotting the vote and some prominent potential ŌĆŗcandidates either in prison or in exile.
Following are details on his challengers: JOSEPH KIGNOUMBI KIA MBOUNGOU
Joseph ŌĆŗKignoumbi Kia Mboungou, 73, a veteran lawmaker from the southwestern Lekoumou department and leader of the political ŌüĀparty La Cha├«ne, is running for president for the fifth time since 2002. He earned 0.62% ŌĆŗin the 2021 election.
His campaign has focused on what he describes as the need for political change ŌĆŗand economic diversification, highlighting his goals of reducing Congo's heavy reliance on oil and tackling poverty.
Uphrem Dave Mafoula, 43, an economist, is making his second consecutive presidential bid after running in 2021, when he was the youngest candidate and ŌĆŗsecured 0.52% of the vote.
His platform centres on governance reforms, job creation and reducing territorial inequalities.
Melaine Destin Gavet Elengo, 35, is the youngest contender in the race and a first-time presidential candidate.
An engineer ŌĆīin ŌüĀthe oil sector, he presents himself as part of a new generation seeking political renewal. His campaign emphasizes transparency, an independent justice system and inclusive development.
Mabio Mavoungou Zinga, 69, a retired customs inspector and former member of parliament, is running under the banner of the Alliance party, a coalition of opposition ŌĆŗgroups.
He has pledged to ŌĆŗtackle corruption and free ŌüĀjailed opposition leaders Jean-Marie Michel Mokoko and Andr├® Okombi Salissa, who were arrested after the disputed 2016 election and later sentenced to 20 years in ŌĆŗprison on charges of undermining state security. This is his first presidential race.
Vivien ŌüĀRomain Manangou, 43, an independent candidate and public law university lecturer, is also making his first presidential bid.
His campaign has centered on institutional reforms, improving public finances and promoting national unity.
Anguios Nganguia Engambe, around 60, president ŌüĀof the ŌĆŗParty for Action of the Republic, is running for president ŌĆŗfor the fourth consecutive time, having stood in 2009, 2016 and 2021, when he won just 0.18% of the vote.
His platform ŌĆŗfocuses on bridging political divisions and increasing political participation.

item.title: South Korea Presidential aide says AI cooperation with UAE could accelerate after conflict
item.description: SEOUL, March 11 (Reuters) - South Korea's senior presidential secretary for AI, Ha Jung-woo, said on Wednesday that artificial intelligence cooperation with the ŌĆŗUnited Arab Emirates could accelerate after the Middle East ŌĆīconflict ends, given the UAE's need to strengthen its defence capabilities.

item.title: Israel believes Iran's new leader was lightly wounded in attacks, senior official says
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - Israel's ŌĆŗintelligence assessment is that Iran's ŌĆīnew supreme leader, Mojtaba Khamenei , was lightly wounded in the Israeli-U.S. joint air war ŌĆŗagainst Iran and that is ŌĆŗwhy he has not been ŌüĀseen in public, a senior ŌĆŗIsraeli official told Reuters.
Iran's Revolutionary ŌĆŗGuards forced through the choice of Mojtaba Khamenei as the new supreme leader, ŌĆŗseeing him as a more ŌĆŗpliant version of his father who would back ŌĆītheir ŌüĀhardline policies.
Khamenei's selection may add up to a more aggressive stance abroad and sterner internal ŌĆŗrepression, said ŌĆŗthree ŌüĀsenior Iranian sources, a reformist former official and ŌĆŗanother insider.
Israeli Defense Minister ŌĆŗIsrael ŌüĀKatz said last week that any leader appointed by the current Iranian leadership ŌüĀwould "be ŌĆŗan unequivocal target ŌĆŗfor elimination."

item.title: Romanian to review US request to use local air base for Iran operations
item.description:
BUCHAREST, March 11 (Reuters) - Romanian President Nicusor Dan has convened the EU ŌĆīand NATO nation's top defence council on Wednesday to discuss whether to allow U.S. aircraft access to its military bases for support linked ŌĆŗto its Tehran operations , political sources said.
The council will meet ŌĆŗfor the first time this year to discuss the ŌüĀsecurity fallout from the conflict in the Middle East, ŌĆŗits impact on Romania's energy market and "the temporary deployment of military ŌĆŗcapability on Romanian territory."
That deployment, political sources said without elaborating, referred to a U.S. request to use the Mihail Kogalniceanu air base.
While some EU ŌĆŗcountries--such as France, Greece and Italy-- have sent warships to Cyprus ŌĆŗafter Iranian-made drones struck a British base on the island, others allow use ŌĆīof ŌüĀtheir military bases.
Most EU top officials have condemned Iranian strikes in the region and urged an end and diplomatic solution to the conflict.
The U.S. withdrew about ŌĆŗ1,000 troops from ŌĆŗRomania's Mihail ŌüĀKogalniceanu air base last year, as the U.S. focused on its own borders and the Indo-Pacific ŌĆŗregion. Another 1,000 U.S. troops remain in ŌĆŗRomania.
The permanent ŌüĀallied presence in Romania stands at around 3,500 NATO troops, including U.S. soldiers.
Romania shares a 650 km (400 mile) land border with ŌüĀUkraine, ŌĆŗover which Russian drones have flown ŌĆŗtowards Kyiv, while mines in the Black Sea from the conflict impact key ŌĆŗtrade and energy routes.

item.title: Russia says it shot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russian Defence Ministry said on ŌĆŗWednesday that its forces ŌĆīshot down two British-supplied Storm Shadow missiles over ŌĆŗthe past 24 ŌĆŗhours, according to the ŌüĀRIA news agency.
Earlier ŌĆŗUkraine said it had ŌĆŗused Storm Shadow missiles to strike a key plant ŌĆŗproducing missile components ŌĆŗin the Russian city of Bryansk ŌĆīon ŌüĀTuesday.
The governor of Bryansk region, Alexander Bogomaz, said that six ŌĆŗcivilians were ŌĆŗkilled ŌüĀand 42 injured in the ŌĆŗattack.
Separately, the Russian ŌĆŗdefence ŌüĀministry said its forces had captured the ŌüĀsettlement ŌĆŗof Chervona Zoria ŌĆŗin Ukraine's Sumy region.

item.title: Gasunie urges Dutch government to build strategic gas reserve
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - Dutch natural gas grid operator Gasunie on Wednesday urged ŌĆŗthe government to build a strategic gas ŌĆīreserve to ensure the Netherlands can withstand a prolonged disruption of supplies.
Gasunie said the Netherlands was protected against short-term ŌĆŗdisruptions due to cold weather, but not for ŌĆŗlong-term problems caused by, for instance, geopolitical tensions ŌüĀor sabotage.
"The current geopolitical climate demands realism and ŌĆŗpreparations for the unexpected to happen on the ŌĆŗlonger term," Gasunie director Hans Coenen said.
The gas company called for research on the use of the so-called cushion gas that ŌĆŗis available in the country's large gas storages ŌĆŗas a reserve.
Cushion gas is permanently stored in natural gas ŌĆīstorages ŌüĀto maintain adequate pressure.
The Netherlands was once one of Europe's major gas suppliers, but has turned into an importer after it shut down the large Groningen ŌĆŗfield due to ŌĆŗthe earthquakes ŌüĀthat gas extraction had caused there.
Most of the gas used in the Netherlands ŌĆŗnow comes from Norway, but an ŌĆŗincreasing part ŌüĀis supplied by the U.S. in the form of liquefied natural gas.
The country still has large underground ŌüĀgas ŌĆŗstorages, of which about 85% of capacity ŌĆŗwould be needed to meet yearly gas demand under regular circumstances, ŌĆŗGasunie said.

item.title: Dutch court scraps cap on number of flights at Amsterdam airport
item.description:
AMSTERDAM, March 11 (Reuters) - The Dutch Supreme ŌĆŗCourt on ŌĆīWednesday scrapped a government decision ŌĆŗto limit ŌĆŗthe number of flights ŌüĀat Amsterdam's ŌĆŗSchiphol airport, ŌĆŗone of Europe busiest hubs.
The court ŌĆŗsaid the ŌĆŗ2024 decision to limit ŌĆīthe ŌüĀnumber of flights to 478,000 per ŌĆŗyear, ŌĆŗmade ŌüĀin an effort ŌĆŗto cut ŌĆŗnoise ŌüĀpollution, had not been ŌüĀproperly ŌĆŗmotivated.

item.title: Exclusive: Foreign hacker in 2023 compromised Epstein files held by FBI
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - A foreign hacker compromised files relating to the FBIŌĆÖs investigation of the late sex offender Jeffrey Epstein during a break-in at the bureauŌĆÖs New York Field Office three years ago, according to ŌĆŗa source familiar with the matter and recently published Justice Department documents reviewed by Reuters.
The details of who accessed a server at the FBIŌĆÖs New York Field Office, ŌĆīincluding the allegation that a foreign hacker was involved, are being reported here for the first time.
In a statement, the FBI said what it described as a "cyber incident" was "an isolated one."
"The FBI restricted access to the malicious actor and rectified the network. The investigation remains ongoing, so we do not have further comments to provide at this time."
Although the source said the intrusion appeared to have been carried out by a cybercriminal rather than a foreign government, ŌĆŗthe incident underscores the files' potential intelligence value, one academic said. The legally mandated publication of U.S. Justice Department documents has exposed the dead financier's ties to prominent people in politics, finance, academia and business, ŌĆŗtriggering investigations in numerous countries around the world.
ŌĆ£Who wouldnŌĆÖt be going after the Epstein files if youŌĆÖre the Russians or somebody interested in kompromat?ŌĆØ said ŌüĀJon Lindsay, who researches the role of emerging technology in global security at the Georgia Institute of Technology. ŌĆ£If foreign intelligence agencies are not thinking seriously about the Epstein files as a target, then ŌĆŗI would be shocked.ŌĆØ
The breach was reported contemporaneously by CNN and Reuters on February 17; the connection to Epstein materials was made by the French magazine Marianne.
Epstein, a longtime associate of President Donald Trump, ŌĆŗpleaded guilty in 2008 to prostitution charges, including soliciting an underage girl. He was found hanged in his jail cell in 2019, in what was ruled a suicide, after being arrested again on federal charges of sex trafficking of minors.
The hack occurred after a server at the Child Exploitation Forensic Lab in the FBIŌĆÖs New York Field Office was inadvertently left vulnerable by Special Agent Aaron Spivack, who was trying to navigate the bureauŌĆÖs complex ŌĆŗprocedures for handling digital evidence, according to the source and the documents.
A timeline written by Spivack and included in the large cache of Epstein documents released earlier this year said the break-in happened ŌĆŗon February 12, 2023. It was discovered the following day when Spivack turned on his computer and discovered a text file warning him that his network had been compromised, according to that document.
Further investigation turned up traces ŌĆīof unusual activity ŌüĀon the server, the document said, adding that the activity "included combing through certain files pertaining to the Epstein investigation.ŌĆØ
The timeline does not say which specific files were accessed, whether the hacker downloaded the data, or who the hacker was. Reuters could not establish what, if any, overlap the affected data had with the Epstein documents published earlier this year or the files that remain under wraps.
Spivack, whose name appears elsewhere , opens new tab in the documents , opens new tab in connection with the Epstein investigation, did not return repeated messages seeking comment. Reuters was unable to reach the man identified in the documents as SpivackŌĆÖs lawyer, Richard J. Roberson, Jr. Seven FBI ŌĆŗagents identified in the documents as being involved ŌĆŗin the investigation into the incident did ŌüĀnot return messages.
In his statement to FBI investigators examining whether he was responsible for the breach, Spivack said he was being made "a scapegoat for the intrusion" and that conflicting bureau policies and faulty guidance around information technology were to blame. Reuters could not establish the result ŌĆŗof the bureau's internal investigation.
The person familiar with the breach said the intrusion was carried out by a foreign hacker who did not appear ŌĆŗto realize they had penetrated ŌüĀa law enforcement server. The hacker expressed disgust at the presence of child abuse images on the device and left a message threatening to turn its owner over to the FBI, the person said.
The source said bureau officials defused the situation by convincing the hacker that they actually were the FBI, in part by having the hacker join a video chat where they flashed their law enforcement credentials in front ŌüĀof a web ŌĆŗcamera.
Reuters could not determine - and the source said they did not know - who the hacker was, what country they ŌĆŗwere operating from, what they did with the material accessed, or whether any effort was made to identify or punish them for breaking into the FBIŌĆÖs server.
Many of the Justice Department's documents have been heavily redacted and others have been kept ŌĆŗsecret altogether despite a law mandating their full release last year . The Trump administration says it is withholding material that could compromise victimsŌĆÖ identities or jeopardize ongoing investigations.

item.title: As Iran war rages, Trump pivots to cost of living in Kentucky trip
item.description:
HEBRON, Kentucky, March 11 (Reuters) - Twelve days into the Iran war, U.S. President Donald Trump is refocusing on domestic issues, traveling ŌĆŗto Kentucky on Wednesday to promote his economic plans as Republicans urge him to address rising concern over the ŌĆīcost of living.
The president's campaign trip is his first since the start of the U.S.-Israeli military operation in Iran . It offers Trump a chance to sharpen his economic message ahead of the November midterm elections, when Republicans will be defending narrow majorities in both chambers of the U.S. Congress.
Trump is due to ŌĆŗdeliver remarks at two events in northern Kentucky and nearby Cincinnati, Ohio. White House press secretary Karoline Leavitt could not ŌĆŗsay why Trump had chosen to go to those places in particular, but the Kentucky district ŌüĀis also home to Republican U.S. Representative Thomas Massie, a Trump critic who has frequently broken with his party.
Massie led the high-profile ŌĆŗpush for more transparency from Trump's Justice Department's handling of files connected to the late convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
Trump has endorsed the ŌĆŗRepublican candidate who will face off against Massie in the party's primary in May.
In his public remarks, Trump is expected to focus on his efforts to lower some prescription drug costs, a key selling point for Republicans trying to address voters' economic concerns.
It is the latest in a series of speeches Trump ŌĆŗhas delivered around the country to sell the American public on his economic policies, but they have often been overshadowed by his ŌĆŗpolicies abroad, most recently the war in Iran, which has caused gas prices to soar.
Gas prices are up 61 cents on average in both Kentucky ŌĆīand nationwide ŌüĀcompared to last month, according to travel organization AAA.
"Short term oil prices, which will drop rapidly when the destruction of the Iran nuclear threat is over, is a very small price to pay," Trump posted earlier this week, "ONLY FOOLS WOULD THINK DIFFERENTLY!"
Bill Kunkel, a 67-year-old retired welder in Union, Kentucky, told Reuters he is concerned about how the Iran war and Trump's tariff program will continue to affect an ŌĆŗarea known for logistics and ŌĆŗmanufacturing.
"I voted for Trump because ŌüĀhe was going to get us out of all of these wars. That is one of the reasons why I agree with Massie - it can't just be a forever war," Kunkel added, referring to ŌĆŗMassie's criticism of Trump's decision to go to war against Iran.
Massie said in an interview that ŌĆŗhe welcomes Trump ŌüĀto his district as they share a lot of the same supporters.
ŌĆ£IŌĆÖve taken care to never insult the president, to never call him names. If I have a policy disagreement with him, I articulate it and keep it to that," he said.
Trump has endorsed Ed Gallrein, a ŌüĀformer Navy ŌĆŗSEAL officer and farmer Trump calls "high-quality," to take on Massie, a man Trump ŌĆŗcalls "unreliable." GallreinŌĆÖs campaign said he will attend the presidentŌĆÖs event.
"There is growing dissatisfaction with where Massie is, and part of it is not supporting the president," said ŌĆŗLarry Mazzuckelli, a retired federal government employee and political commentator from Union, Kentucky.

item.title: Kremlin says Britain was involved in Ukraine's missile strike on Russia
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - The Kremlin on Wednesday accused British specialists of involvement in a deadly ŌĆŗUkrainian strike on the Russian city of Bryansk that ŌĆīused British Storm Shadow missiles and said it would take Britain's role "into account".
The Bryansk regional governor said Tuesday's strike killed at least six civilians and injured ŌĆŗ37 in what he called a "terrorist missile attack", without saying ŌĆŗwhat was hit. Ukraine said it had struck a ŌüĀkey plant producing missile components. Moscow accused it of deliberately targeting ŌĆŗcivilians.
Asked by Reuters if there would be a military response to ŌĆŗthe use of British missiles against sovereign Russian territory, Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov said Russia would take into account the British involvement.
"It is obvious that the launch ŌĆŗof these missiles was impossible without British specialists," Peskov told ŌĆŗreporters. "We are aware of this, we know it well, and we naturally take it ŌĆīinto ŌüĀaccount."
"In order to prevent such barbaric actions by the Kyiv regime from continuing, the special military operation is being conducted," he said, adding that one of the goals of the operation was to demilitarise ŌĆŗUkraine.
There was no ŌĆŗimmediate comment ŌüĀfrom London, though Russia has repeatedly said that Ukraine needs Western expertise and Western-supplied targeting data and ŌĆŗsatellite imagery to fire advanced missiles deep into Russia.
Ukrainian ŌĆŗPresident ŌüĀVolodymyr Zelenskiy said that Kyiv had struck one of the most important military factories in Bryansk that produced electronics for Russian missiles.
Russian Foreign Ministry ŌüĀspokeswoman ŌĆŗMaria Zakharova said the strike was premeditated ŌĆŗand directed against civilians, and challenged the U.N. to assess what had occurred.

item.title: China 'does not agree' with attacks on Gulf countries, says foreign ministry
item.description: BEIJING, March 11 (Reuters) - China "does ŌĆŗnot agree" ŌĆīwith attacks on ŌĆŗGulf states ŌĆŗand condemns all ŌüĀindiscriminate ŌĆŗattacks on ŌĆŗcivilians and non-military targets, ŌĆŗsaid ŌĆŗGuo Jiakun, spokesperson ŌĆīat ŌüĀthe Chinese foreign ministry, at ŌĆŗa ŌĆŗregular ŌüĀpress conference ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗWednesday.

item.title: Thailand says 3 crew missing after ship hit by projectiles in Strait of Hormuz
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Twenty crew have been rescued ŌĆŗfrom a Thai ŌĆīship hit by unknown projectiles in the ŌĆŗStrait of Hormuz ŌĆŗbut three crew remain ŌüĀmissing, Thailand's Transport ŌĆŗMinistry said on Wednesday.
The ŌĆŗministry said the crew had abandoned the vessel ŌĆŗin a lifeboat ŌĆŗand were rescued by the ŌĆīOmani ŌüĀnavy. It said an explosion occurred in the stern of ŌĆŗthe ŌĆŗship ŌüĀand caused a fire in the ŌĆŗengine compartment, ŌĆŗwhere ŌüĀthe remaining three crew members had been ŌüĀworking.

item.title: Pope Leo laments death of civilians, children in Iran war
