VATICAN CITY, March 11 (Reuters) - Pope Leo on Wednesday ŌĆŗlamented the death ŌĆīof numerous civilians in the ongoing Iran war ŌĆŗand also expressed ŌĆŗcloseness to people in ŌüĀLebanon, saying the ŌĆŗcountry, targeted by Israeli ŌĆŗstrikes, was going through a "great trial."
Leo, the first ŌĆŗU.S. pope, called ŌĆŗon pilgrims in his weekly ŌĆīaudience ŌüĀin St. Peter's Square to pray for peace.
"Let us continue ŌĆŗto pray ŌĆŗfor ŌüĀpeace in Iran, and throughout ŌĆŗthe Middle East, ŌĆŗespecially ŌüĀfor the many civilian victims, including many ŌüĀinnocent ŌĆŗchildren," said ŌĆŗthe pontiff.

item.title: Thailand's Phuket airport closes runway after Air India Express plane malfunction
item.description:
BANGKOK, March 11 (Reuters) - Thailand's Phuket airport said on Wednesday it had temporarily closed its runway to remove a disabled Air India Express plane ŌĆŗafter it experienced a malfunction upon landing.
Air India Express Flight AXB938 ŌĆīarriving from Hyderabad suffered damage to its landing gear as it hit the runway, Phuket airport said in a statement.
The plane "experienced an issue with the nose wheel at ŌĆŗPhuket Airport," a spokesperson for Air India Express said, adding: "The ŌĆŗcrew followed all standard protocols, and guests were deplaned.
There were no ŌüĀcasualties and the runway was expected to reopen at 6 p.m. ŌĆŗlocal time (1100 GMT), the airport said.
An image posted on X by Thai state ŌĆŗbroadcaster MCOT showed part of the aircraft landing gear, with a wheel missing, in direct contact with the runway, with a long, swerving skid mark on the tarmac.
Air India Express, the ŌĆŗbudget arm of Air India, has a fleet of over 100 Boeing ŌĆŗand Airbus aircraft.
India's aviation watchdog reprimanded Air India Express in March last year for ŌĆīnot ŌüĀchanging the engine parts of an Airbus A320, as directed by the European Union's aviation safety agency, Reuters has reported . It was also criticised for falsifying records to show compliance.
Air India Express reported 95 technical defects from January to November last ŌĆŗyear, India's civil ŌĆŗaviation ministry said ŌüĀin December, the second highest among India's airlines over the period. The ministry did not provide details on the ŌĆŗnature of the faults.
Parent Air India, a full-service carrier, ŌĆŗhas come ŌüĀunder intense scrutiny in the last few months after a series of safety lapses and a deadly crash in in Ahmedabad last June that killed ŌüĀ260 ŌĆŗpeople.
Phuket is one of Thailand's busiest gateways, with ŌĆŗhundreds of flights daily, including from the Middle East, Europe and Asia, as well as ŌĆŗdomestic routes.

item.title: Kremlin says it is in constant touch with Iranian leaders
item.description:
MOSCOW, March 11 (Reuters) - Russia is constantly in touch ŌĆŗwith the Iranian leadership and ŌĆīwilling to contribute to efforts to stabilise the region, ŌĆŗthe Kremlin said on ŌĆŗWednesday.
"Here I can only say ŌüĀthat we are in ŌĆŗconstant contact with the Iranian ŌĆŗside and with the Iranian leadership," Kremlin spokesman Dmitry Peskov ŌĆŗtold reporters.
"As President (Vladimir) Putin ŌĆŗhas said, Russia is always ready ŌĆīto ŌüĀdo what it can to restore peace and stability in the region."
Putin and ŌĆŗForeign Minister ŌĆŗSergei ŌüĀLavrov have spoken with their Iranian counterparts ŌĆŗin recent days, ŌĆŗwhile ŌüĀPutin also held a phone call with U.S. President ŌüĀDonald ŌĆŗTrump on Monday.

item.title: Hungary's opposition Tisza party maintains lead as election nears, poll shows
item.description:
BUDAPEST, March 11 (Reuters) - The main Hungarian opposition party Tisza's lead over long-time Prime Minister Viktor Orban's Fidesz shrank slightly in a poll published on ŌĆŗWednesday, as campaigning enters its final month before an April ŌĆī12 election.
Nationalist Orban faces the biggest challenge to his rule in 16 years, although the outcome of the vote - coming against the backdrop of the Ukraine-Russia war ŌĆŗand building economic challenges - remains highly uncertain, with opinion polls showing ŌĆŗthat many voters are still undecided.
A poll by 21 Research ŌüĀCentre (21 Kutatokozpont), conducted between March 2 and 6 and released on ŌĆŗWednesday, showed Tisza leads by 14 percentage points ahead of Fidesz among ŌĆŗdecided voters, down from a 16-point lead in the agency's last poll in January.
Centre-right Tisza, led by former government insider Peter Magyar , had the support of 53% of decided ŌĆŗvoters, unchanged from January, according to 21 Research Centre, while 39% backed ŌĆŗFidesz, up from 37% in the previous poll.
The poll, published by news site 24.hu, ŌĆīshowed ŌüĀ38% support for Tisza among all voters, with Fidesz backed by 30%.
Based on the poll, Tisza could win 115 seats in Hungary's 199-seat parliament, and Fidesz could expect 78 seats. The far-right Our Homeland (Mi Hazank) party ŌĆŗwould be the ŌĆŗonly other party ŌüĀcrossing the 5% threshold to win seats.
Magyar has said his party would curb corruption, unlock billions of euros ŌĆŗin frozen European Union funds to boost the economy, ŌĆŗand firmly ŌüĀanchor Hungary in the EU and NATO.
Fidesz has pointed to other surveys that still show it on course to victory, though opponents - who criticise Orban ŌüĀfor ŌĆŗharming the rule of law and keeping ŌĆŗwarm ties with Russia - say these have mainly been conducted by institutes with financial or personal ŌĆŗties to the ruling party.

item.title: Sweden's Stegra seeks to raise over $2.3 billion, daily DI reports
item.description:
STOCKHOLM, March 11 (Reuters) - Swedish startup Stegra must raise more than 2 billion euros ($2.3 billion) to ŌĆŗcomplete construction of its green steel plant, ŌĆīmore than double its previous estimate, business daily Dagens Industri reported on Wednesday, citing unnamed sources.
The ŌĆŗcompany has appointed a new CFO and ŌĆŗshaken up its board , as it scrambles to ŌüĀraise more cash to finish its hydrogen-based ŌĆŗsteel plant in northern Sweden.
($1 = 0.8602 euros)

item.title: US ignites Iran war, but Gulf Arab states pay the price, Gulf sources say
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. may have pulled the trigger on the Iran war , but it is the oil-producing Gulf that will pay the price, Gulf sources and analysts say, signalling unease in ties between a region under Iranian attack and the superpower it relies on for protection.
Behind the scenes, resentment is mounting ŌĆŗin Gulf Arab capitals at being drawn into a war they neither initiated nor endorsed but are now paying for economically and militarily, with airports, hotels, ports and military and oil installations hit by Iranian strikes, said three ŌĆīregional sources, who declined to be identified as they were not authorised to speak publicly.
"It is not our war. We did not want this conflict, yet we are paying the price in our security and our economy," Ebtesam Al-Ketbi, President of the Emirates Policy Center, told Reuters.
That doesn't mean Iran is "innocent", she said. Gulf governments had assured Tehran they would not allow their territories or airspace to be used by Washington in the war. Yet Iran has unleashed waves of drone and missile strikes across the region despite those assurances, denting business confidence in the process.
While ŌĆŗdisquiet about U.S. President Donald Trump is growing over a conflict many believe he launched without consultation, some regional sources argue that having started the war, Washington should now see it through to eliminate what they see as a persistent ŌĆŗIranian threat on their doorstep.
ŌĆ£If America leaves the war now without achieving victory, it will be like abandoning an injured lion,ŌĆØ Ketbi said. ŌĆ£Iran will remain a threat to the region, capable of ŌüĀstriking again. And if the regime collapses, leaving a power vacuum, neighbouring states will suffer the consequences.ŌĆØ
Asked for comment, the White House said U.S. and Israeli strikes had reduced IranŌĆÖs retaliatory missile attacks by 90 percent, "crushing their ability to shoot these weapons or ŌĆŗproduce more".
White House spokeswoman Anna Kelly added that Trump was in close contact with Middle East partners and that IranŌĆÖs attacks on its neighbors underscore why the threat had to be eliminated.
There was no immediate response from Gulf states to requests for comment.
Iran's Supreme ŌĆŗLeader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, was killed on the first day of the war. Tehran responded by hitting Israel and Gulf states hosting U.S. military installations, effectively halting oil and gas shipments through the Strait of Hormuz -- a conduit for roughly a fifth of the worldŌĆÖs petroleum and LNG.
Across the region, airspace closures have resulted in airlines cancelling some 40,000 flights, the largest disruption to global air travel since the COVID-19 pandemic. Gulf tourism meanwhile is also taking a hit , putting at risk the region's carefully crafted image as a safe and high-end vacation hotspot.
Amid the turmoil, Gulf states have ŌĆŗsought to project calm and resolve. The president of the United Arab Emirates, Sheikh Mohammed bin Zayed Al Nahyan, said on Friday his nation was in a time of war but was well and told his enemies it was no easy prey , in his ŌĆŗfirst public comments since Iran fired missiles at the UAE.
At the same time, analysts say the war has left Gulf states reassessing both their security dependence on Washington and the prospect of eventually engaging Tehran on new regional security arrangements -- even as trust in Iran has collapsed.
For decades, ŌĆīrelations between Washington ŌüĀand the Gulf states rested on an implicit trade-off: Gulf energy and capital -- including hundreds of billions of dollars spent on U.S. arms, advanced technology and goods and services -- in return for U.S. protection, said Fawaz Gerges of the London School of Economics.
Gerges said the war had shaken those assumptions. Now, he said, Gulf states would accelerate efforts to diversify their foreign and security partnerships, realising "they cannot really rely on the United States to protect their energy, oil, gas, their people and their sovereignty.ŌĆØ
The Gulf's latest misgivings about Washington echo disquiet felt in Saudi Arabia at the U.S. failure to retaliate against Iran after drone and missile attack in 2019 on the kingdom's Abqaiq and Khurais oil facilities. Washington and Riyadh blamed the attacks on Tehran, which denied responsibility.
Abdulaziz Sager, Chairman of the Saudi-based ŌĆŗGulf Research Center, said Washington had failed to prepare safeguards ŌĆŗfor its regional allies or ensure oil and gas flows ŌüĀduring wartime, saying the cost on the Gulf states' economies is "horrendous".
Sager said the war had demonstrated the limits of relying solely on external security guarantees, particularly from the United States, adding that Gulf countries need to strengthen their own defense capabilities and prepare for future crises.
"External powers also tend to make decisions based on their own strategic interests rather than ŌĆŗthose of the Gulf. As a result, Gulf states will likely pursue a more cautious and balanced approach in their relations with both Iran and major international partners," added Sager.
In a ŌĆŗrare public rebuke, prominent Emirati businessman ŌüĀKhalaf Al Habtoor questioned the objectives of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran.
ŌĆ£If the strikes were aimed at containing Iran, did they take into account the regional consequences -- or was the cost of dragging the Gulf into a conflict it was not party to simply ignored?ŌĆØ said Al Habtoor, founder of the Al Habtoor Group.
Saudi Aramco CEO Amin Nasser warned on Tuesday that continued disruption to shipping through Hormuz would have ŌĆ£catastrophic consequencesŌĆØ for oil markets .
IranŌĆÖs Revolutionary Guards, however, said they would not allow ŌĆ£one litre of oilŌĆØ to be shipped from the Middle East if U.S. ŌüĀand Israeli attacks ŌĆŗcontinued. Trump has warned Washington would strike Iran harder if it blocked oil exports.
Sources close to Gulf Arab government circles say frustration with Trump privately runs ŌĆŗdeep across the region. Many believe he dragged the Gulf into a war shaped heavily by Israel, without sharing a plan and acting hastily and without fully weighing the political and economic fallout for allies.
One Gulf source familiar with U.S. policymaking said key decisions were crafted by a small inner circle around Trump operating ŌĆŗlargely outside traditional U.S. policy channels.
ŌĆ£They are businessmen and dealmakers, not career policymakers,ŌĆØ the source said, adding this approach left Gulf partners exposed to the consequences of this circle's decisions.

item.title: Meloni bets on a lottery to curb Italy's judicial clans
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy has tried random selection before.
Ancient Romans used to cast lots to decide who governed in distant provinces while Renaissance Florence pulled names from bags to avoid powerful families gaining a stranglehold on power.
Now Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni is looking to revive the centuries-old ŌĆŗtradition of "sortition" as a way to stop what she says are political factions lording it over Italy's long-troubled judiciary.
The plan to select magistrate members of judicial governing councils by lottery ŌĆīlies at the heart of a reform being put to referendum on March 22-23, pitting Meloni's conservative government against most of the nation's judges and prosecutors.
Recent opinion polls show the "no" camp, backed by the main opposition centre-left parties, has nudged ahead, threatening one of Meloni's signature initiatives which was championed by her predecessor Silvio Berlusconi before his death in 2023.
Berlusconi, who faced dozens of trials largely tied to his media empire after entering politics, accused the judiciary of being in the ŌĆŗhands of the left. Magistrates denied this, but Meloni vowed to take up his crusade after she won power in 2022.
"I think change is needed because justice is one of the ŌĆŗthree fundamental powers that keep Italy afloat, but it's also the one that we haven't managed to substantially reform since the 1980s," Meloni told RTL ŌüĀradio last week.
"If we don't succeed this time, we won't have another chance," she added, saying future governments would be unlikely to take on this sort of constitutional reform that almost always requires ŌĆŗa referendum that is hard to win in a divided country.
The referendum campaign is unfolding amid growing distrust between the judiciary and the ruling coalition, with "no" supporters arguing that Meloni's plan would undermine the justice ŌĆŗsystem and boost political interference.
They seized on remarks by Giusi Bartolozzi, the justice ministry's chief of staff, who urged voters in a TV interview this week to back the reform, saying it would allow Italians to "get rid of a judiciary that acts like a firing squad."
The government's reform has three main pillars.
First, it would separate the career paths of judges and public prosecutors, who currently enter through the same exam, can swap ŌĆŗjob roles and are governed by a single council (the CSM), which oversees appointments and promotions.
Second, it would split the CSM into two councils - one for judges and one for prosecutors. Third, it would create ŌĆŗa new disciplinary court to handle misconduct cases.
Under the proposal for the new-look CSMs, two thirds of seats would be reserved for working judges or prosecutors, and one third for lay members, such as law professors or ŌĆīlawyers, whose ŌüĀnames would be put forward by parliament.
All CSM members will ultimately be drawn by lot.
Meloni says this will prevent the sort of wheeler-dealing between various internal factions that recent scandals have revealed, and create a system in which council members do not have to "say thank you to anyone" after an election.
Critics counter that in institutions designed to safeguard independence, legitimacy comes from having leaders chosen on purpose, not picked at random.
"It is one thing to punish individuals who make mistakes; it is another to humiliate the entire judiciary by saying that you are not capable of electing your own representatives," said David Ermini, a ŌĆŗformer vice president of the CSM and an ŌĆŗex-lawmaker with the centre-left Democratic Party.
No one looms over this debate quite like Luca Palamara, a former CSM member whose spectacular downfall spurred the reform.
Wiretaps in 2019 exposed late-night meetings involving Palamara, senior judicial figures and politicians, with discussions focused on steering appointments ŌĆö especially to coveted and sensitive prosecutor posts.
Careers were clearly being fixed by cliques ŌĆŗand Palamara was expelled from the magistracy.
Now, in a twist that infuriates many of his former colleagues, Palamara is campaigning for a "Yes" vote.
"The introduction ŌĆŗof random selection would dismantle ŌüĀthe possibility of pre-selecting who sits on the CSM, thereby eliminating factional ties," he said, calling the reform "the one most feared" by those within the factions.
Some opponents say the proposed new selection rules risk promoting people who are ill-prepared or under-qualified, sweeping away any notion of merit and leaving everything up to chance. But Palamara said these concerns were overblown.
"We are talking about people who have passed a highly competitive public examination ŌüĀto enter the ŌĆŗprofession and who make extremely serious decisions every day - life sentences, delicate family situations," he said.
But even if the reform ŌĆŗpasses the referendum hurdle, history suggests that sortition may not achieve the desired result.
In both ancient Rome and Renaissance Florence, lotteries promised fair rotation in theory, but in practice elites learned to game the system, steering outcomes so that the biggest ŌĆŗprizes still tended to land in familiar hands.
"The mechanism of factionism would not end with drawing lots. Everyone selected might all come from the same faction," said Ermini.

item.title: Ukrainian trainers will help German army get ready to defend against Russia by 2029, chief says
item.description:
BERLIN, March 11 (Reuters) - Ukrainian military trainers will help ŌĆŗGermany's military meet a target of being ready to defend against any Russian attack on ŌĆīNATO by 2029, the German army chief said, in a role reversal after years of Western troops training Ukrainian forces.
Berlin and Kyiv agreed last month that Ukraine would send military instructors to German army schools to teach lessons they ŌĆŗhave learned from fighting against Russia's invasion.
"We have high expectations," Lieutenant General Christian Freuding, the head ŌĆŗof the German army, told Reuters in an interview. "The Ukrainian military is currently ŌüĀthe only one in the world with frontline experience against Russia."
Germany is not the only Western country ŌĆŗdrawing on Kyiv's experience: Ukrainian President Volodymyr Zelenskiy on Thursday pledged to provide assistance to the U.S. in ŌĆŗdealing with Iranian drones in the Middle East.
Freuding said Germany was the first country to reach an arrangement with Ukraine to provide military instructors but predicted others would follow suit.
Asked what the Ukrainian trainers would contribute, he referred to assessments ŌĆŗby German and other Western intelligence agencies that Russia could be ready for a large-scale attack on ŌĆŗthe NATO military alliance by 2029.
"That's almost the day after tomorrow. We have no time - the enemy doesn't wait for ŌĆīus ŌüĀto declare we're ready. So we have to use every possibility to prepare," Freuding said.
Russia has insisted it does not intend to attack NATO, but Western officials say such assurances cannot be taken at face value as Moscow made similar statements before invading Ukraine.
Freuding, who became army chief ŌĆŗlast October after previously coordinating ŌĆŗGermany's arms supplies to ŌüĀKyiv , said the initial contingent of Ukrainian trainers would likely number in the "middle double-digits" and stay for a few weeks at a time.
The Ukrainian instructors are ŌĆŗexpected to contribute expertise in artillery, engineering, armoured operations, drone usage and command ŌĆŗand control.
Since ŌüĀRussia's full-scale invasion of Ukraine in 2022, Germany has trained Ukrainian forces on weapons such as Marder infantry fighting vehicles, Leopard tanks, howitzers and air defence systems.
Freuding said Ukraine had built on these skills in combat, developing ŌüĀtactics ŌĆŗand capabilities in data-centric warfare - the military term for highly ŌĆŗdigitalised and interconnected operations - that cannot be found in textbooks.
"The fact that they are now coming to us as instructors reflects a ŌĆŗsecurity partnership on an equal footing," Freuding said.

item.title: ItalyŌĆÖs Meloni criticises US war on Iran as part of dangerous trend
item.description:
ROME, March 11 (Reuters) - Italy's Prime Minister ŌĆīGiorgia Meloni on Wednesday delivered her strongest criticism yet of the U.S.-Israeli war on Iran, describing it as part of a growing and dangerous trend of interventions "outside the scope of international law".
Her ŌĆŗremarks to parliament came after repeated accusations from the opposition that her ŌĆŗright-wing government had been too soft toward its allies. Most other ŌüĀEuropean nations, with the notable exception of Spain , have withheld direct critique of the U.S. ŌĆŗand Israeli attacks, largely calling for restraint.
Meloni - who has close ties with U.S. President ŌĆŗDonald Trump - also said Iran must not be allowed to develop nuclear weapons, as that would end the international non-proliferation framework with "dramatic repercussions for global security", leaving Italy and Europe exposed to a ŌĆŗpotential nuclear threat from Tehran.
The U.S. and Israel traded air strikes with Iran ŌĆŗacross the Middle East as the war stretched into its 12th day, a conflict that has halted ŌĆīthe ŌüĀflow of one-fifth of the world's oil and gas supplies.
Addressing parliament on the crisis, Meloni drew parallels between the Middle East conflict and Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine, which she said triggered a broader global destabilisation.
"It is in this context of ŌĆŗstructural crisis in the ŌĆŗinternational system, in ŌüĀwhich threats are becoming increasingly frightening and unilateral interventions outside the scope of international law are multiplying, that we must also ŌĆŗplace the American and Israeli intervention against the Iranian regime," she ŌĆŗtold the ŌüĀSenate.
Meloni said Rome was providing air-defense assets to Gulf countries hit by strikes from Tehran.
"This is not only because these are friendly nations and strategic partners of Italy, but also ŌüĀbecause ŌĆŗthere are tens of thousands of Italian citizens in ŌĆŗthat area whom we must protect, not to mention the fact that there are about 2,000 Italian ŌĆŗsoldiers stationed in the Gulf."

item.title: US navy aircraft transits Taiwan Strait ahead of Trump's planned China visit
item.description:
TAIPEI, March 11 (Reuters) - A U.S. Navy P-8A Poseidon aircraft transited the Taiwan Strait in international ŌĆŗairspace on Wednesday, in a move the ŌĆīU.S. 7th Fleet said "demonstrates the United States' commitment to a free and open Indo-Pacific."
The transit comes ahead of ŌĆŗU.S. President Donald Trump's planned visit to China ŌĆŗthis month as Washington and Beijing look ŌüĀto stabilise ties marred by issues including Taiwan, ŌĆŗtrade disputes and technology curbs.
"By operating within the ŌĆŗTaiwan Strait in accordance with international law, the United States upholds the navigational rights and freedoms of all nations," the ŌĆŗ7th Fleet said in a statement.
U.S. military ŌĆŗships or aircraft routinely pass through or above the waterway ŌĆīthat ŌüĀseparates democratically governed Taiwan from China - missions that always anger Beijing.
China claims democratically governed Taiwan as its own territory and says it has jurisdiction over ŌĆŗthe strait. ŌĆŗTaiwan and ŌüĀthe U.S. dispute that, saying the strait is an international waterway.
China has repeatedly ŌĆŗsaid the Taiwan issue is its ŌĆŗinternal affair ŌüĀand at the core of Beijing's interests - a red line that must not be crossed or ŌüĀtrampled ŌĆŗon.
Taiwan's government rejects China's sovereignty claims, ŌĆŗsaying that only Taiwan's people can decide their future.

item.title: A 'corporate welfare' claim vs Walmart and Amazon over worker benefits
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - (The opinions expressed here are those of the author, a correspondent for Reuters. This column is part of the Reuters Sustainable Finance Newsletter, which you can sign up for here )
Providing low-paid workers with government benefits like health insurance and food assistance is usually considered to be socially minded policy. But another way to look at the payments is as subsidies giving big businesses access to a large pool of labor able to work for less-than-living wages.
That's the critique of Sarah Anderson, Global Economy ŌĆŗProject Director at the Institute for Policy Studies, a progressive-leaning think tank in Washington, D.C. In a new report , opens new tab she reviewed pay practices of companies she dubbed "The Low-Wage 20," or the 20 largest employers of low-wage U.S. ŌĆīworkers.
Among her top findings: more than half of these firms reported 2024 median pay that would make a family of three eligible for food benefits under the federal Supplemental Nutrition Assistance Program, also known as SNAP, and for government-subsidized Medicaid health insurance benefits in most states. All the while, most of their CEOs earned hundreds of times as much per year.
The following transcript of our conversation has been edited for length and clarity. At the end I've published responses from the two top U.S. employers, Walmart and Amazon. (AMZN.O) , opens new tab
Question: Sarah, could you talk about how you were able to develop the calculations?
Answer: I have worked on executive compensation issues for a long, ŌĆŗlong time. And this is a report that really connects those high CEO pay levels to low-wage worker issues. These are all companies that are in the S&P 500 and they're the ones that have the lowest median pay levels ŌĆŗthat they've reported to the SEC (U.S. Securities and Exchange Commission) and that also have a majority of their workers in the United States.
Q: You found a lot of these employees were eligible for things ŌüĀlike Medicaid and SNAP food assistance, which are social benefits to lower-wage Americans.
A: We think that median pay number that the companies report themselves is a really good indicator of how many of their workers might be on Medicaid and SNAP. We don't have ŌĆŗthe exact figures because they're not required to report that, but there were a handful of state governments that have revealed specific data company by company, and Walmart and Amazon were at the top of all of those lists with really clear numbers in terms ŌĆŗof how many of their employees are.
These benefits are at risk right now. So it's a good time to be having this conversation because these workers who are already having to stretch to pay their bills are going to be facing even more hardship.
Q: If I were an executive for one of these companies, I'm sure I would respond by saying, well, a lot of these are part-time workers who don't want to be working full time. And, the calculations for public assistance are based on household size. Maybe it's not realistic to expect a head of household, a three-person household, to survive ŌĆŗon just one income in this day and age.
A: I would say that having a business model that is heavily reliant on part-time workers is a choice. It's a way to get around having to pay full benefits at a lot of these companies under ŌĆŗthe Affordable Care Act. Large companies have to provide affordable health insurance, or at least offer it to people working at least 30 hours a week. So if you can keep your workers, you know, at a lower hourly level, then you can get around that. I (also) question ŌĆīwhether people are ŌüĀworking part time because they want to work part time. A lot of people are having to work several part-time jobs to try to make ends meet.
You know, a huge share of the low-wage workforce are single parents. And for these companies to just abdicate their obligation to provide a living wage says a lot about their priorities.
Question: Surely mom-and-pop businesses face the same challenge. A lot of them can't pay their workers enough to get them above the thresholds for public assistance as well. What's the difference?
A: These should be the leading among the leading corporations of America. They're in the S&P 500. They set the standards for wages and other things. The other thing that just makes it so outrageous to so many people is to see the CEO pay levels at these companies. There's ŌĆŗno owner of a small mom and pop that is making ŌĆŗthe $18 million a year, which is the average (for CEOs) ŌüĀin our low-wage 20. It's a business model that is based on really extracting from the people often who are the frontline workers.
Q: Something that jumped out at me, among the large employers in Nevada (a state cited in the study) is that a couple of public sector organizations, a school district and a county that have a lot of their employees eligible for public assistance.
A: That's true. I have to ŌĆŗsay I was dismayed to see the U.S. Postal Service was on some of these lists as well. It just gets to my broader view on this that having these extreme ŌĆŗgaps between CEO pay and worker ŌüĀpay, where people don't feel like they're part of a team, it just doesn't bring out the best in people and that does have real costs in terms of lower employee morale and higher turnover rates.
Here is what a Walmart spokesman said about the study - "We offer a ladder of opportunity so people can build a career at Walmart, which includes a small percentage of our workforce that comes to us on public assistance. We hire them, train them and give them the chance to build a better life."
Here is what Amazon spokesperson Eileen Hards told me: ŌĆ£Amazon pay ŌüĀis among the ŌĆŗbest in the industry ŌĆō well over double the federal minimum wage and significantly more than other retailers. Pointing fingers at Amazon over SNAP and/or Medicaid is a ŌĆŗred herring when eligibility is based on total household income and size ŌĆō and not individual wages."
"For example, two employees who work at the same site and earn identical pay in the same state can have completely different SNAP or Medicaid eligibility depending on whether they support children, elderly parents, or are the sole earner in ŌĆŗtheir household. As weŌĆÖve said for years, what really needs to happen is a significant and large increase in the federal minimum wageŌĆöthat would be a big boost for American families.ŌĆØ

item.title: Morning Bid: From panic to patience
item.description:
March 11 - What matters in U.S. and global markets today
By Mike Dolan , opens new tab , Editor-At-Large, Finance and Markets
Both energy and stock markets appear to be in a holding pattern after the wild swings earlier this week as they look for any evidence that oil and gas supplies might ŌĆŗbe freed up in the Middle East.
For now, there are few signs on the ground to back up President TrumpŌĆÖs view that the war will ŌĆībe over very soon, with Tuesday seeing intense bombardments , shipping still blocked in the Strait of Hormuz, and Saudi AramcoŌĆÖs CEO warning of ŌĆ£catastrophic consequencesŌĆØ for the oil market if conflict drags on.
IŌĆÖll get into that and more below.
But first, check out my latest column on why an Iran oil shock could prove politically toxic in the U.S. even if GDP escapes largely unscathed.
And listen to today's episode of the Morning Bid podcast , where I discuss ŌĆŗoil-shock inflation fears and look ahead to today's big macro release - inflation data for the leadup to the crisis.
Subscribe to hear Reuters journalists discuss the biggest news ŌĆŗin markets and finance seven days a week.
Oil prices initially dropped below $90 per barrel on Wednesday on reports that the ŌüĀInternational Energy Agency had proposed the largest release of oil reserves in its history - more than double the size of the 182 million barrels released after the Ukraine invasion in 2022.
Oil then staged ŌĆŗa rebound , however, as traders digested the proposal. Goldman Sachs estimated a release the size of that in 2022 would offset 12 days of an estimated 15.4 million barrel per day ŌĆŗexport disruption in the Gulf.
As prices seesaw, though, it will be hoped that Monday's panic surge in crude markets to nearly $120 per barrel has set the parameters for prices - at least until the duration of the Iran conflict becomes clearer.
Wall Street was mixed on Tuesday as the intense bombardments in the Middle East - and further Iranian threats of a comprehensive oil blockade - dampened earlier hopes for a short-lived conflict. All major ŌĆŗindexes closed largely flat.
Global shares steadied somewhat on Wednesday as JapanŌĆÖs Nikkei gained 1.7% and South KoreaŌĆÖs KOSPI rose by 1.75%. European stocks opened lower, however, while U.S. stock futures held ŌĆŗsteady ahead of the bell.
Meantime, the dollar rebounded after slipping early on Wednesday, holding onto some of the early-week gains it recently shed as traders looked for signs of a quick conclusion to the war.
Later ŌĆītoday, traders ŌüĀwill get a read on where U.S. CPI was in February, just before the Iran attacks started late in the month. More important, though, will be the release on Friday of February PCE data, the FedŌĆÖs favoured measure of inflation. Core inflation measured by the latter was likely already running above 3% before the oil spike.
Elsewhere, OracleŌĆÖs shares surged 8% in extended trading after its latest results, which saw it predict boosted revenue estimates well into 2027 on the AI data center boom, helping to reassure investors about the massive scale of its capex. ŌĆŗThe results also serve as a reminder of ŌĆŗthe enduring AI theme in equities ŌüĀbeyond the day-to-day energy market ructions.
Ongoing angst about private credit funds and the quality of the loans contained within them is also rising, however. JPMorgan Chase has marked down the value of certain loans held by private credit groups and is tightening its lending to the sector, ŌĆŗthe FT reported on Wednesday
Chart of the day
U.S. consumer prices likely picked up in February as the cost of gasoline increased in ŌĆŗanticipation of an escalating ŌüĀwar in the Middle East, and with the conflict driving up oil prices, a further rise in inflation is expected in March.
The anticipated increase in the Consumer Price Index last month would also reflect the continued but staggered pass-through from President Donald Trump's sweeping tariffs, which he pursued under a law meant for use in national emergencies, that have since been struck down by the ŌüĀU.S. Supreme ŌĆŗCourt.
Today's events to watch
* U.S. February CPI (8:30 AM EDT)
* U.S. 10-year note auction
* Fed's Michelle Bowman speaks
* OPEC ŌĆŗMonthly Oil Market Report
Want to receive the Morning Bid in your inbox every weekday morning? Sign up for the newsletter here . You can find ROI on the Reuters website , opens new tab , and you can follow us on LinkedIn , opens new tab and X , opens new tab .
Opinions expressed are those of ŌĆŗthe author. They do not reflect the views of Reuters News, which, under the Trust Principles , opens new tab , is committed to integrity, independence, and freedom from bias.

item.title: Exclusive: Shell, TotalEnergies and others declare FM to their clients who take LNG from Qatar - sources say
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 - Several companies that buy liquefied natural ŌĆīgas (LNG) from QatarEnergy as portfolio players or offtakers ŌĆö including Shell (SHEL.L) , opens new tab , TotalEnergies (TTEF.PA) , opens new tab and some firms in Asia ŌĆö have declared force majeure ŌĆŗto customers they supply, three sources told Reuters ŌĆŗon Wednesday.
Qatar, the world's second-largest LNG exporter, announced ŌüĀa production halt at its 77 million tons ŌĆŗper annum (mtpa) facility last week and declared force majeure ŌĆŗon LNG shipments.
Shell , the world's largest LNG trader, declined to comment. TotalEnergies did not immediately respond to Reuters request for comment.
Both ŌĆŗcompanies have long-term partnerships with QatarEnergy and are partners ŌĆŗin the company's massive North Field expansion project which aims to ŌĆīboost ŌüĀcapacity by 2027.
Analysts estimate Shell takes 6.8 million tons per annum (mtpa) of Qatari LNG, while TotalEnergies takes 5.2 mtpa and sells them to their clients worldwide.
Qatari ŌĆŗEnergy Minister Saad ŌĆŗal-Kaabi told ŌüĀthe Financial Times last week that it would take "weeks to months" to return to ŌĆŗnormal deliveries, even if the war ended ŌĆŗtoday. ŌüĀThe company declared force majeure on LNG shipments on Wednesday.
Sources told Reuters last week that the FM notices ŌüĀsent ŌĆŗto clients stated that LNG deliveries ŌĆŗfor March will not be affected, with the impact being felt as ŌĆŗof April.

item.title: Return to Russian fuels would be a strategic blunder, von der Leyen says
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - Europe would be making a strategic ŌĆŗblunder if it sought to ŌĆīblunt soaring energy prices with a return to Russian ŌĆŗfossil fuels, European ŌĆŗCommission president Ursula von der ŌüĀLeyen said on Wednesday.
"In ŌĆŗthe current crisis, some argue ŌĆŗthat we should abandon our long-term strategy and even go ŌĆŗback to Russian fossil ŌĆŗfuels. This would be a strategic ŌĆīblunder," ŌüĀvon der Leyen said in a speech in European Parliament.
Von der Leyen ŌĆŗsaid the ŌĆŗEU ŌüĀwas preparing options to lower energy prices, ŌĆŗincluding the better ŌĆŗuse ŌüĀof power purchasing agreements, state aid measures and ŌüĀgas ŌĆŗprice subsidies or ŌĆŗcaps.

item.title: Israel sees no certainty IranŌĆÖs government will fall despite war
item.description:
JERUSALEM/DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli officials in closed discussions have acknowledged there is no certainty the war against Iran will lead to a collapse of its clerical government, a senior Israeli official told Reuters, with no sign ŌĆŗof an Iranian uprising amid the bombardment.
However, despite commentary by U.S. President Donald Trump that the war may finish soon , Israel's assessment is that Washington is not close to instructing an end ŌĆīto the conflict , two Israeli officials said.
The intense U.S. and Israeli bombing campaign killed the supreme leader, Ayatollah Ali Khamenei, as well as a host of senior military commanders, but it has also killed civilians and smashed homes and public buildings, angering many Iranians.
With missiles striking across Tehran and other cities, and with the Iranian authorities threatening deadly force against any who dares protest, Iranians who might otherwise come to the streets may also fear to do so until the war ends.
Still, Iran's long-term challenges ŌĆŗlook worse than ever, with ever harsher sanctions strangling the economy and little prospect of better times ahead for a population whose protests in January were crushed with thousands dead.
The senior Israeli official did not say what had led the country to assess that a collapse of Iran's ruling system was not ŌüĀa certainty.
On the day Israel launched its joint air war with the U.S., its Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said: "Our joint action will create the conditions for the brave Iranian people to take their destiny ŌĆŗinto their own hands."
He particularly spoke of Iran's main ethnic and linguistic minorities, the Kurds , Baluchis and Arabs, adding to reports that the U.S. or Israel may back uprisings by those groups.
However, in a statement on Tuesday, ŌĆŗNetanyahu repeated that though Israel's aspiration was to help Iranians "cast off the yoke of tyranny," that ultimately "it is up to them" - an apparent acknowledgement that a rising does not look imminent.
Israel and the U.S. have not issued a joint public statement setting out clear unified war aims, or articulating the conditions under which they might decide to end the campaign.
On Monday, Trump described the war as "very complete, pretty much", but on Tuesday the White House said it would only end when Trump determined its ŌĆŗobjectives were met and that Iran was in a state of unconditional surrender.
In a closedŌĆædoor briefing with foreign diplomats on Tuesday, Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Saar declined to set a timeframe for the military campaign - ŌĆŗchiming with the government's assessment that Trump was not close to ending it.
Saar acknowledged in the briefing that IranŌĆÖs government could survive the war but expressed confidence it would collapse later, sources said.
Speaking to reporters on Tuesday, Saar said ŌĆīthe war ŌüĀwould proceed until Israel and the U.S. determined the time had come to end hostilities, but that Israel did not seek "endless war".
Assaf Orion, a former head of strategy with the Israeli military and a fellow at the Washington Institute for Near East Policy, said weakening Iranian military capabilities appeared a more direct, tangible and measurable war aim.
"Creating conditions for regime change is indirect and hence more difficult to fathom," he said, adding that while the military campaign appeared to be planned for weeks, any uprising against Iran's ruling system might take months or years.
On Wednesday Iran's police chief Ahmadreza Radan warned ŌĆŗthat "anyone taking to the streets at the enemy's ŌĆŗrequest will be confronted as an enemy, not ŌüĀa protester. All our security forces have their fingers on the trigger".
While many Iranians want change and some openly celebrated the death of Khamenei, whose security forces killed thousands of anti-government protesters just weeks ago, there has been no sign of protest since the war began.
"I hate this regime. I want it to ŌĆŗgo, but under bombardment there are no streets left to protest in," said Ali, 26, from Tehran.
Although Tehran's streets are far quieter than usual, banks, petrol ŌĆŗstations and shops are all ŌüĀoperating as usual, though with reduced hours and tighter rationing of fuel. All government offices are open.
"Every day and every night there is bombing. My apartment building and its windows shake," said a Tehran resident who asked to retain anonymity for fear of reprisals.
"Despite discontent with the regime, the sense of Iranian pride and patriotism is growing and people are expressing hatred for Reza Pahlavi, Trump and Netanyahu," the resident added, naming the son of ŌüĀIran's former shah, ŌĆŗwho had urged military intervention.
However, the bombing has destroyed airports and ports as well as other civilian infrastructure, adding to the ŌĆŗproblems Iran's government may face in reviving a stagnant economy that has angered much of the population.
"Iran prefers the war to continue because if it ends, protests will start due to economic problems and shortcomings that the war has also worsened," said a university ŌĆŗteacher in Tehran who also requested anonymity.

item.title: Spain permanently withdraws ambassador as rift with Israel deepens
item.description:
MADRID, March 11 (Reuters) - Spain permanently withdrew its ambassador to Israel on Tuesday as a ŌĆŗdiplomatic standoff worsened between the two countries ŌĆīover Spain's opposition to the U.S.-Israeli attacks on Iran .
The ambassador was summoned back to Spain last September amid a diplomatic ŌĆŗrow over Spanish measures banning aircraft and ships ŌĆŗcarrying weapons to Israel from its ports ŌüĀor airspace due to Israel's military offensive in ŌĆŗGaza, which Israeli Foreign Minister Gideon Sa'ar denounced ŌĆŗas antisemitic.
On Tuesday, Spain published an announcement in its official gazette that the ambassador's position had been terminated. Spain's Foreign ŌĆŗMinistry said its embassy in Tel Aviv ŌĆŗwill be led by a charge d'affaires for the foreseeable ŌĆīfuture.
The ŌüĀmove marks the latest escalation in diplomatic relations between the two countries, which have been heavily strained since Israel launched its assault on the Gaza ŌĆŗStrip in ŌĆŗOctober of ŌüĀ2023.
Israel's embassy in Spain is also run by a charge d'affaires after the ŌĆŗcountry summoned its ambassador last May ŌĆŗin protest ŌüĀat Spain's decision to recognise a Palestinian state.
Tensions have heightened since the U.S.-Israeli strikes on Iran, ŌüĀwith ŌĆŗSa'ar accusing Spain in early March ŌĆŗof "standing with tyrants" for opposing the war.

item.title: Iran's new supreme leader 'lightly injured' but still active, Iranian official tells Reuters
item.description:
DUBAI, March 11 (Reuters) - Newly-appointed Supreme ŌĆŗLeader Mojtaba Khamenei ŌĆīwas lightly injured but ŌĆŗis continuing ŌĆŗto operate, an Iranian ŌüĀofficial ŌĆŗtold Reuters on ŌĆŗWednesday.
The official did not give details ŌĆŗabout ŌĆŗwhen he was injured ŌĆīor ŌüĀwhy he had not made any ŌĆŗstatement ŌĆŗto ŌüĀthe public since ŌĆŗbeing appointed ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀnew leader.

item.title: Drones hit east Congo's biggest city, killing French aid worker, rebels and UN say
item.description:
March 11 (Reuters) - Drone strikes hit Goma in east Congo ŌĆŗon Wednesday, killing at least three people including a French aid worker, the ŌĆīfirst such attack in the city since AFC/M23 rebels seized it last year, according to the group and the U.N.
The AFC/M23 blamed the attack on the Congolese army, saying in a post on X that Kinshasa ŌĆŗhad launched drones against a densely populated urban area in the lakeside city.
A spokesperson ŌĆŗfor the rebels said in a separate post that three people had died, ŌüĀincluding a foreign humanitarian worker.
A senior official for U.N. children's agency UNICEF told Reuters ŌĆŗthat one of its employees, a French national, was killed.
French President Emmanuel Macron and Hadja Lahbib, ŌĆŗEuropean Commissioner for equality, preparedness and crisis management, condemned the attack in posts on X, calling for respect for international humanitarian law and saying aid workers must never be targeted.
A spokesperson for Congo's army declined to ŌĆŗcomment.
A spokesperson for Congo's government did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
A Reuters ŌĆŗjournalist in Goma reported hearing two loud explosions at around 4 a.m. (0200 GMT). The blasts shook windows and ŌĆīdoors ŌüĀand were followed by the sound of ambulance sirens.
A senior AFC/M23 official told Reuters the house struck by one of the drones had been rented by UNICEF employees and is close to a residence used by former Congolese President Joseph Kabila in a neighbourhood that houses several ŌĆŗprominent political and business ŌĆŗfigures.
The official said a ŌüĀsecond drone had targeted the residence of AFC/M23 political coordinator Corneille Nangaa, but fell into Lake Kivu.
The strikes come after weeks of intensifying ŌĆŗdrone operations on both sides of the conflict.
AFC/M23 military spokesperson Willy Ngoma ŌĆŗwas killed in ŌüĀa drone attack by the Congolese army near the strategic mining town of Rubaya, some 60 km from Goma, on February 24.
The rebels have also claimed a series of drone attacks on the airport ŌüĀserving ŌĆŗthe northeastern city of Kisangani in recent weeks.
Fighting has ŌĆŗcontinued in eastern Congo on several fronts, despite mediation efforts by parties including Qatar and the U.S.

item.title: Israeli settler violence rises in West Bank under Iran war curbs
item.description:
ABU FALAH, West Bank, March 11 (Reuters) - Israeli settlers in the occupied West Bank are taking advantage of curbs on movement imposed during the war on Iran to attack Palestinians, with military roadblocks ŌĆŗpreventing ambulances reaching victims quickly, rights groups and medics say.
Settlers have killed at least five Palestinians in the West Bank since the United States and Israel began airstrikes against Iran ŌĆīon February 28, according to the Palestinian health ministry. A sixth man died after inhaling teargas fired during an attack, according to Israeli rights group B'Tselem.
Israel's military blocked many West Bank roads with iron gates and mounds of earth on the first day of the war, and has largely shut crossings with Israel.
The Israeli military says the curbs are preemptive measures while it is carrying out airstrikes on Iran and against Lebanese group Hezbollah, which has fired missiles at Israel in solidarity with ŌĆŗTehran.
Palestinians in remote West Bank villages say the roadblocks have left them increasingly exposed to settler violence.
The Israeli military has also continued to carry out the raids it frequently conducts in Palestinian ŌĆŗcities and towns during peacetime to arrest Palestinians, often without charge, they say.
A spokesperson for the Yesha Council, which represents Jewish settlements, did not immediately respond ŌüĀto a Reuters request for comment on the attacks.
Malak Beirat's husband, Thaer, was one of two Palestinians who residents and the Palestinian health ministry say were shot dead by settlers before dawn on Sunday ŌĆŗin Abu Falah, north of the city of Ramallah.
"Thaer loved life. I never expected he would die," said Beirat, fighting back tears as she sat with her two children.
Witnesses told Reuters that when over 100 settlers ŌĆŗgathered on the outskirts of Abu Falah, a local WhatsApp group rallied men to protect the small village. The initial confrontation involved stone throwing, but armed settlers arrived later and began shooting, they said.
Beirat's husband was shot dead while trying to protect a house from attack, a man who helped defend the village said.
Blood could still be seen on Monday in olive groves at the scene of the attack, where villagers have erected two Palestinian flags at the spots where the ŌĆŗtwo men were killed - one for each victim.
A third Palestinian died after the attack. B'Tselem said his death was probably caused by the effect of teargas fired by Israeli troops deployed to the village during the ŌĆŗattack.
The Israeli military says an investigation has been launched into the incident and that it condemns "violence of any kind".
Medics say the new roadblocks have led to delays in reaching injured Palestinians.
"There are obstacles - and even ŌĆīattacks by settlers ŌüĀand the military on the (medical) crews," said Ahmed Jibril, spokesman for the Palestinian Red Crescent ambulance service.
There have been over 109 reports of settler violence against Palestinians in the West Bank since the start of the war with Iran including shootings, physical assaults, property damage, and threats, said Israeli monitoring group Yesh Din.
All the reported killings of Palestinians by settlers this year were in the last week, B'Tselem said.
Settlers shot dead Amir Muhammad Shanaran in a village near the city of Hebron on Saturday, and brothers Muhammad and Fahim ŌĆśAzem were shot dead in Qaryut southeast of the city of Nablus last Monday, the Palestinian health ministry said.
"Taking advantage ŌĆŗof the war, armed settler militias, often operating with ŌĆŗsupport from the army, continue to attack and ŌüĀharass Palestinian communities across the West Bank in an effort to force them out," B'Tselem said.
In three of the settler shootings, the settlers were wearing Israeli army uniform, Yesh Din said. The Israeli military did not immediately respond to a request for comment.
Palestinians accuse the military of protecting settlers rather than villagers. Israel's ŌĆŗmilitary denies this.
Israeli indictments of settler violence are rare. At the end of 2025, Yesh Din said that of the hundreds of cases of settler ŌĆŗviolence it had documented since ŌüĀthe Hamas-led attacks on Israel in October 2023 that led to the Gaza war , only 2% resulted in indictments.
The United Nations says nearly 700 Palestinians were displaced by settler violence from the start of 2025 through early February 2026.
Israel's government has expanded settlements in a construction push that Finance Minister Bezalel Smotrich says is aimed at burying the idea of a Palestinian state.
Right-wing Israeli minister Yossi Dagan announced on Wednesday the establishment of a new settlement ŌüĀin a strategic ŌĆŗposition in the mountain overlooking Nablus, one of 22 new settlements announced by the Israeli government last May.
Palestinians have long sought an ŌĆŗindependent state in the West Bank, Gaza and East Jerusalem, territories Israel captured and occupied in the 1967 Middle East war.
Over 700,000 settlers live in East Jerusalem and the West Bank among more than 3 million Palestinians, according to a European Union report in 2024.
Most ŌĆŗof the world considers Israel's settlement activity in the West Bank illegal under international law relating to military occupations. Israel disputes this view.

item.title: EU envoys approve sanctions on 19 Iranian officials, entities over rights violations
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 11 (Reuters) - European Union envoys have approved new ŌĆŗsanctions targeting 19 ŌĆīIranian officials and entities responsible for serious human rights ŌĆŗviolations, the bloc's ŌĆŗforeign policy chief, Kaja ŌüĀKallas, said on Wednesday.
"As ŌĆŗthe Iran war continues, ŌĆŗthe EU will protect its interests and pursue those ŌĆŗresponsible for domestic repression," ŌĆŗKallas said on X, adding ŌĆīthat "it ŌüĀalso sends a message to Tehran that IranŌĆÖs future cannot be built ŌĆŗon ŌĆŗrepression".
The ŌüĀsanctions, which have been in the ŌĆŗmaking since before ŌĆŗthe ŌüĀbeginning of the war, will require final approval ŌüĀfrom ŌĆŗthe Council ŌĆŗof the EU.

item.title: Israeli dentist returns to identifying war victims in Iran conflict
item.description:
JERUSALEM, March 11 (Reuters) - After helping to identify the remains of the last Israeli hostage in Gaza, dentist Esi Sharon-Sagie hoped her work naming the dead killed ŌĆŗin more than two years of war was finally over. Weeks later she was called back to duty.
On February 28, a ŌĆīU.S.-Israel air assault in Iran ignited a war now engulfing the Middle East and Sharon-Sagie is again seeking to give a name to those whose bodies are too badly mutilated for visual identification techniques.
On March 1, she was called to the site of an Iranian missile attack on the town of Beit Shemesh in central Israel.
"We were informed that a missile ŌĆŗkilled nine people that weren't able to get into a shelter in time. And the minute we heard that the disaster happened, ŌĆŗwe had to go to do the identification," said Sharon-Sagie.
Among the dead were a brother and two sisters, 13, 15 ŌüĀand 16 years old.
Although she has been volunteering with police since 2010, Sharon-Sagie says that everything changed on ŌĆŗOctober 7, 2023, when Palestinian militant group Hamas launched a surprise attack on Israel.
Health authorities struggled to cope with the hundreds killed in Israel's single deadliest ŌĆŗday. Identification of about 1,200 fatalities, some badly burned, was not completed for many months.
Forensic odontology is used to identify the remains of people whose bodies are in a condition that makes other methods, like visual recognition or fingerprinting, impossible. For instance, a charred or decomposed body can be identified by matching X-rays with past dental records.
Sharon-Sagie, who ŌĆŗis on duty day and night, often had to summon the strength to overcome exhaustion. On one occasion, at the end of an hours-long shift, ŌĆŗshe was asked to help with one more victim. She looked at the woman's slim hand, and rings.
"I spoke with her in my heart with no sound. I ŌĆīlooked at ŌüĀher and I said: 'now you're going to help me because I have no strength anymore. You'll help me and I'll help you. I'll bring you back to your family'," said Sharon-Sagie.
A year later she helped to identify one of the masterminds of the October 7 attack.
Yahya Sinwar , then the leader of Hamas, was killed by Israeli fighters in southern Gaza in October 2024.
Sinwar's body was transferred to Israel, which still held his ŌĆŗmedical records from his time in ŌĆŗan Israeli prison, where he was ŌüĀjailed for killing Israeli soldiers and Palestinians whom Hamas suspected were spying for Israel.
"He had brain surgery in Israel many, many years ago. So we had all his information," said Sharon-Sagie.
For many in Israel, the pain of the ŌĆŗOctober 7 attack could only be alleviated with the release of the last remaining hostages from Hamas captivity ŌĆŗin Gaza.
That relief finally ŌüĀcame on January 26 when the remains of the last of the 251 hostages were found in the Palestinian enclave.
Sharon-Sagie was on the team that checked scores of bodies buried in a cemetery in northern Gaza in the search for police officer, Ran Gvili, who fought Palestinian militants at an Israeli kibbutz.
As a volunteer with the police ŌüĀforce, it ŌĆŗwas a complex moment for Sharon-Sagie.
"I was surprised it was actually happening. I felt my ŌĆŗhands trembling, I was very moved. When we did the identification and finished signing the report that this is him, at first I was happy but seconds after that I understood ŌĆŗthat it is over, he is not coming back to Israel alive," she said.

item.title: OPEC confirms big Saudi oil production hike ahead of Iran war, holds forecasts steady
item.description:
LONDON, March 11 (Reuters) - OPEC said on ŌĆŗWednesday that Saudi Arabia sharply increased ŌĆīoil production in February ahead of U.S. and Israeli strikes on Iran and ŌĆŗkept its forecasts for relatively ŌĆŗstrong global oil demand growth this ŌüĀyear.
Saudi Arabia increased its oil ŌĆŗoutput and exports as part of ŌĆŗa contingency plan in case any U.S. strike on Iran disrupts supplies from the Middle ŌĆŗEast, sources familiar with the ŌĆŗplan had said in February.
The attack came on ŌĆīFebruary ŌüĀ28 and the resulting conflict has disrupted oil exports, forced production stoppages and sent prices soaring.
OPEC, in a ŌĆŗmonthly report ŌĆŗon its ŌüĀwebsite, said that Saudi Arabia told OPEC its ŌĆŗoil supply to the market ŌĆŗin ŌüĀFebruary was 10.111 million barrels per day while production was 10.882 million ŌüĀbpd. ŌĆŗSaudi Arabia reported its ŌĆŗproduction at 10.10 million bpd for January.

item.title: EU countries give final approval to 2040 target
item.description:
BRUSSELS, March 5 (Reuters) - European ŌĆīUnion countries gave the final approval on Thursday to a new climate target to slash greenhouse gas emissions 90% by 2040, pressing ahead with the bloc's ambitious climate agenda despite political resistance.
The new climate target ŌĆŗis a hard-fought political compromise, struck by governments and EU lawmakers last year. ŌĆŗIt is more ambitious than most major economies' emissions-cutting commitments, including China's.
In ŌüĀpractice, the target will require an 85% emissions reduction from European industries against 1990 ŌĆŗlevels. The EU will pay developing countries via carbon credits, so they cut emissions on ŌĆŗEurope's behalf to make up the rest, to reach 90%.
The EU agreed the target last year following months of wrangling between countries, such as Spain, which say worsening droughts and wildfires justified more ambitious ŌĆŗgoals, and those like Poland and Italy, which sought to soften the emissions cuts, ŌĆŗarguing that struggling industries cannot afford the upfront investments.
A reinforced majority of EU countries' ministers gave the ŌĆīfinal ŌüĀformal sign-off to the legally binding goal at a meeting in Brussels. The Czech Republic, Slovakia, Poland and Hungary opposed it. The climate target will now pass into EU law.
As part of the agreement, the EU will also consider the option in future to ŌĆŗuse international carbon credits ŌĆŗto meet a ŌüĀfurther 5% of its 2040 emissions reductions - potentially further softening the domestic efforts required.
The deal also delays the launch of a politically ŌĆŗsensitive new EU carbon market by one year, to 2028 - a ŌĆŗmove designed ŌüĀto win over countries sceptical of the climate goal.
The target, which is designed to keep Europe on track to meet its pledge to reach net-zero emissions by 2050, falls short of the ŌüĀ90% domestic ŌĆŗemissions cut recommended by the EU's climate science ŌĆŗadvisers.
It is also weaker than Brussels' original plan for the goal, reflecting disagreement between EU governments over the speed and ŌĆŗcost of their green agenda.

item.title: U.S. military tells civilians to avoid port facilities where Iranian navy operating
item.description:
WASHINGTON, March 11 (Reuters) - The U.S. military on ŌĆŗWednesday warned civilians ŌĆīto leave port facilities where Iranian ŌĆŗnaval forces are ŌĆŗoperating, adding that U.S. ŌüĀforces could ŌĆŗnot guarantee their ŌĆŗsafety.
"Iranian dockworkers, administrative personnel, and commercial vessel crews ŌĆŗshould avoid ŌĆŗIranian naval vessels and military ŌĆīequipment," ŌüĀU.S. Central Command said in a statement. The ŌĆŗUnited States ŌĆŗhas ŌüĀbeen carrying out strikes ŌĆŗagainst Iranian naval ŌĆŗvessels-- ŌüĀsinking or destroying 60 so ŌüĀfar.

item.title: Turkey opposition leader says Imamoglu trial is purely political
item.description:
ISTANBUL, March 11 (Reuters) - The trial of Istanbul's jailed mayor, Ekrem Imamoglu, is a purely political affair ŌĆŗaimed at preventing him from challenging President Tayyip Erdogan in the next election, Turkey's main opposition leader told ŌĆīReuters in an interview on Wednesday.
Imamoglu, 55, appeared in court on Monday in the opening hearing of a sweeping corruption case involving more than 400 defendants linked to the Istanbul municipality.
Prosecutors accuse him of leading a criminal organisation for profit through tender-rigging and bribery, charges he and his Republican People's Party (CHP) deny.
"Let ŌĆŗme put it plainly. In Turkey, what is happening is not a trial in the normal sense of the word. ŌĆŗIt is not a legal process but a political one," CHP leader Ozgur Ozel said.
The judges handling ŌüĀthe case have been tasked "not with conducting a fair trial but with delivering the decision that Erdogan wants", he said.
Erdogan's government denies ŌĆŗinfluencing the judiciary and says Turkey's courts are independent. The investigation into the Istanbul municipality was led by Akin Gurlek, who last month was ŌĆŗappointed justice minister.
Imamoglu has been held in pre-trial detention for nearly a year amid an unprecedented judicial crackdown on the CHP that rights groups and opposition figures say has damaged the democratic credentials of NATO member Turkey.
Ozel said he did not expect Imamoglu to be released from detention during ŌĆŗhis trial, adding that such an outcome would require greater social pressure within Turkey, international pressure, or a further drop in Erdogan's ŌĆŗpoll ratings.
Ozel said Erdogan wanted to keep Imamoglu in prison because, if released, he could campaign across the country ahead of elections that the opposition ŌĆīleader ŌüĀbelieves will take place next year, ahead of schedule.
"If Imamoglu gets out, he could travel all over Turkey and launch a major campaign. That is why Erdogan wants to keep him inside," he said.
Opinion polls show Imamoglu performing strongly against Erdogan in any presidential race, while party polling suggests a tight race between the secularist CHP and ErdoganŌĆÖs Islamist-rooted AKP, with the CHP holding a narrow lead.
Ozel said ŌĆŗTurkish democracy had sharply deteriorated ŌĆŗunder Erdogan, who has led ŌüĀTurkey as prime minister or president since 2003.
"Turkey's democracy has probably gone back 50 or 60 years," he said. "We have fallen into what I would call the third division in the world."
Ozel also said ŌĆŗhe expected pressure on the opposition to intensify as elections approach.
"Things will get worse, and [Erdogan] will ŌĆŗbecome more aggressive. ŌüĀWe are open to every kind of attack, both through legal channels and through other means," he said, adding that the opposition was not afraid and believed it would defeat Erdogan and his ruling AK Party at the ballot box.
Ozel said he believed Erdogan was preparing for ŌüĀan early ŌĆŗelection in September or October 2027 rather than waiting until the scheduled 2028 ŌĆŗvote because under the constitution an earlier ballot could allow the president to run again.
"We want an election as soon as possible," Ozel added. "If the election is held ŌĆŗin June 2028, Erdogan cannot run. If it is held early, he can."
